title: Consensus Guidelines for Therapeutic Drug Monitoring in Neuropsychopharmacology: Update 2017.
abstract: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) has become increasingly important in neuropsychopharmacology practice, yet standardized guidelines remain inconsistent across clinical settings. This update represents a systematic revision of evidence-based recommendations for TDM in psychiatric medications, incorporating recent pharmacokinetic and pharmacogenomic research findings from 2012-2017. An international expert panel of 24 neuropsychopharmacologists and clinical pharmacologists reviewed published literature using systematic search strategies across PubMed, Cochrane, and Embase databases. Consensus recommendations were developed through modified Delphi methodology, focusing on antidepressants, antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and anxiolytics. Evidence quality was graded using standardized criteria, with particular attention to therapeutic reference ranges, sampling protocols, and clinical indications for monitoring. Updated recommendations now include 47 psychotropic medications, representing a 23% increase from previous guidelines. Strong evidence (Level A) supports routine TDM for lithium, valproate, carbamazepine, and clozapine. Moderate evidence (Level B) supports monitoring for tricyclic antidepressants, several atypical antipsychotics, and lamotrigine. New additions include recommendations for therapeutic ranges of aripiprazole, paliperidone, and vortioxetine based on emerging pharmacokinetic data. The panel identified optimal sampling times, with steady-state trough concentrations recommended for most agents. Special populations requiring modified approaches include pediatric patients, elderly individuals, and those with hepatic or renal impairment. These updated consensus guidelines provide clinicians with evidence-based frameworks for implementing TDM in neuropsychiatric practice, potentially improving treatment outcomes while minimizing adverse effects through personalized dosing strategies.

title: The pediatric psychopharmacology of autism spectrum disorder: A systematic review - Part I: The past and the present.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1 in 54 children, with increasing prevalence driving demand for evidence-based pharmacological interventions. Despite decades of research, psychopharmacological treatment remains challenging due to heterogeneous symptom presentations and limited pediatric-specific data. This systematic review synthesized the historical development and current state of pediatric psychopharmacology for ASD core symptoms and associated comorbidities. We searched PubMed, Embase, PsycINFO, and Cochrane databases from inception through December 2020 for randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series examining psychotropic medications in children and adolescents with ASD (ages 2-18 years). Studies were evaluated using PRISMA guidelines, with quality assessment performed using the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool. Of 2,847 identified records, 156 studies met inclusion criteria, encompassing 8,234 pediatric participants. Antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, demonstrated the strongest evidence for managing irritability and aggression, with FDA approval for pediatric ASD populations. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors showed mixed efficacy for repetitive behaviors, with significant safety concerns in younger children. Stimulants effectively addressed comorbid attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms but required careful monitoring for adverse effects. Alpha-2 agonists and anticonvulsants showed promise for specific symptom clusters but lacked robust pediatric data. Emerging treatments including oxytocin, N-acetylcysteine, and cannabinoids require further investigation. Current evidence supports limited pharmacological options for pediatric ASD, primarily targeting associated symptoms rather than core deficits. Future research must prioritize age-stratified studies, biomarker development, and personalized treatment approaches to optimize outcomes in this vulnerable population.

title: Cell-Specific Neuropharmacology.
abstract: The development of cell-type-specific therapeutic interventions represents a paradigm shift in neuropharmacology, moving beyond broad neurochemical modulation toward precision targeting of distinct neuronal populations. Traditional pharmacological approaches often produce off-target effects due to the widespread distribution of neurotransmitter receptors across heterogeneous brain circuits. Recent advances in molecular neuroscience, including single-cell RNA sequencing and cell-type-specific genetic tools, have revealed unprecedented cellular diversity within neural networks and identified unique molecular signatures that can be therapeutically exploited. We review emerging strategies for achieving cell-selective drug action, including designer receptors exclusively activated by designer drugs (DREADDs), optogenetics-assisted pharmacology, cell-penetrating peptides conjugated to selective payloads, and nanotechnology-based delivery systems. Engineered viral vectors now enable targeted expression of synthetic receptors in genetically-defined cell populations, allowing temporal and spatial control of pharmacological interventions. Additionally, activity-dependent drug release systems can selectively modulate hyperactive or hypoactive neuronal ensembles implicated in neuropsychiatric disorders. Early clinical applications demonstrate proof-of-concept for cell-specific approaches in treating epilepsy, Parkinson's disease, and chronic pain, with significantly reduced adverse effects compared to conventional therapies. However, challenges remain in translating these tools from preclinical models to human therapeutics, including delivery efficiency, immune responses, and long-term safety profiles. Cell-specific neuropharmacology promises to revolutionize treatment of neurological and psychiatric disorders by enabling unprecedented precision in therapeutic targeting while minimizing systemic side effects.

title: Psychopharmacology and memory.
abstract: The therapeutic manipulation of memory through psychopharmacological interventions raises profound ethical questions regarding personal identity, autonomy, and the nature of authentic human experience. While emerging research demonstrates the efficacy of agents such as propranolol in reducing traumatic memory consolidation and cholinesterase inhibitors in enhancing memory formation, the clinical implementation of these interventions requires careful ethical consideration. This analysis examines the moral implications of memory-modifying drugs through the lens of principlism, considering beneficence, non-maleficence, autonomy, and justice. We conducted a systematic review of current literature on memory-enhancing and memory-dampening pharmaceuticals, analyzing 47 peer-reviewed studies published between 2000-2005, and evaluated their findings against established bioethical frameworks. Results indicate significant therapeutic potential for memory-modifying agents in treating post-traumatic stress disorder, dementia, and other neuropsychiatric conditions. However, concerns emerge regarding informed consent for treatments that may fundamentally alter patients' psychological continuity, the potential for coercive use in vulnerable populations, and distributive justice issues surrounding cognitive enhancement. The risk of creating "inauthentic" memories or personalities presents particular challenges to traditional notions of personal identity and moral responsibility. We conclude that while psychopharmacological memory interventions offer genuine therapeutic benefits, their implementation requires robust ethical guidelines, enhanced informed consent procedures, and careful consideration of long-term psychological and social consequences. Further interdisciplinary dialogue between neuroscientists, clinicians, and ethicists is essential before widespread clinical adoption.

title: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptor redux: Discovery of accessories opens therapeutic vistas.
abstract: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) have long been considered promising therapeutic targets for neurological and psychiatric disorders, yet clinical translation has remained elusive due to limited subtype selectivity and complex pharmacology. Recent structural and functional studies have revealed that nAChR activity is profoundly modulated by a previously unrecognized class of accessory proteins, termed nicotinic receptor-associated proteins (NRAPs), which confer tissue-specific receptor properties and drug sensitivities. Here, we employed cryo-electron microscopy, electrophysiology, and high-throughput compound screening to characterize NRAP interactions across major nAChR subtypes. We identified three distinct NRAP families—NRAPs 1-3—that selectively associate with α4β2, α7, and α3β4 receptors, respectively, altering their agonist binding kinetics by 10-100 fold and dramatically shifting allosteric modulator potencies. Proteomics analysis revealed brain region-specific NRAP expression patterns that correlate with known cholinergic dysfunction in Alzheimer disease and schizophrenia. Leveraging this knowledge, we discovered compound series that exhibit >1000-fold selectivity for NRAP-containing versus NRAP-free receptors. Lead compounds demonstrated robust cognitive enhancement in mouse models of Alzheimer disease and antipsychotic-like effects in dopamine hyperfunction paradigms, with minimal peripheral side effects. Furthermore, NRAP expression was significantly altered in postmortem brain tissue from patients with these disorders. These findings fundamentally redefine nAChR pharmacology and provide a mechanistic framework for developing the next generation of subtype-selective cholinergic therapeutics with enhanced clinical translatability.

title: Psychopharmacology of traumatic brain injury.
abstract: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) represents a leading cause of disability worldwide, with complex pathophysiological cascades involving neuroinflammation, neurotransmitter dysfunction, and altered neural connectivity that persist long after initial injury. Current pharmacological interventions show limited efficacy, necessitating a comprehensive understanding of psychopharmacological approaches for both acute management and long-term rehabilitation. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding medication use in TBI patients, examining both therapeutic benefits and potential risks across different injury severities and recovery phases. We conducted a systematic analysis of clinical trials, observational studies, and mechanistic research published between 2010-2019, focusing on psychotropic medications commonly prescribed in TBI populations including antidepressants, antipsychotics, anticonvulsants, and cognitive enhancers. Analysis of 127 studies involving 45,682 TBI patients revealed significant heterogeneity in treatment approaches and outcomes. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors demonstrated modest benefits for post-TBI depression but showed mixed effects on cognitive recovery. Atypical antipsychotics effectively managed agitation and behavioral disturbances, though metabolic side effects remained concerning. Anticonvulsants beyond seizure prophylaxis showed limited cognitive benefits, while cholinesterase inhibitors demonstrated promising but inconsistent effects on attention and memory deficits. Notably, certain medications including haloperidol and phenytoin were associated with impaired neuroplasticity and delayed recovery. These findings highlight the need for personalized pharmacological approaches in TBI management, emphasizing careful risk-benefit analysis and consideration of injury-specific pathophysiology. Future research should prioritize mechanistically-informed drug selection and development of TBI-specific therapeutic targets to optimize neuropsychiatric outcomes and functional recovery.

title: The origins and early history of neurochemistry and its societies.
abstract: The field of neurochemistry emerged in the mid-20th century as an interdisciplinary science combining neuroscience, biochemistry, and pharmacology to understand brain function at the molecular level. This historical analysis examines the origins and institutional development of neurochemistry from 1950-1980, focusing on key discoveries and the formation of professional societies. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of seminal publications, analyzed archival materials from the International Society for Neurochemistry (ISN) and American Society for Neurochemistry (ASN), and interviewed pioneering researchers or their immediate collaborators. Our analysis reveals that neurochemistry crystallized as a distinct discipline following three pivotal developments: the discovery of neurotransmitter synthesis pathways in the 1950s, the establishment of synaptosomal preparations for studying nerve terminals in vitro, and the characterization of neurotransmitter receptors using radioligand binding techniques. The ISN, founded in 1967, emerged from informal gatherings at international conferences, while regional societies like the ASN (1969) developed to address local research needs. Early neurochemical societies prioritized standardizing methodologies, particularly for measuring neurotransmitter concentrations and enzyme activities in brain tissue. Key founding members included Heinrich Waelsch, Eugene Roberts, and Abel Lajtha, who established editorial policies for the Journal of Neurochemistry that emphasized rigorous experimental design and reproducibility. The institutional framework established during this formative period created lasting standards for neurochemical research methodology and fostered international collaboration that continues to drive advances in understanding neurological and psychiatric disorders at the molecular level.

title: PET Neurochemical Imaging Modes.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as the premier neuroimaging modality for investigating brain neurochemistry in vivo, offering unprecedented insights into neurotransmitter systems and their alterations in neuropsychiatric disorders. This review examines the current state and future directions of PET neurochemical imaging modes, with emphasis on methodological advances and clinical applications. We analyzed data from 127 published studies between 2010-2016 utilizing various PET radioligands targeting dopaminergic, serotonergic, GABAergic, glutamatergic, cholinergic, and noradrenergic systems. Novel imaging approaches including dual-tracer paradigms, displacement studies with pharmacological challenges, and kinetic modeling techniques were systematically evaluated for their ability to quantify neurotransmitter synthesis, release, and receptor occupancy. Results demonstrate significant progress in radioligand development, particularly for previously challenging targets such as metabotropic glutamate receptors (mGluR5) and norepinephrine transporters. Advanced kinetic modeling approaches, including the simplified reference tissue model and Logan graphical analysis, have improved quantification accuracy by 15-30% compared to traditional methods. Pharmacological challenge studies using amphetamine, fenfluramine, and other agents have enabled measurement of endogenous neurotransmitter release with enhanced sensitivity. Clinical applications in Parkinson's disease, depression, schizophrenia, and addiction have yielded new biomarkers for disease progression and treatment response. Future developments in radiochemistry, scanner technology, and computational modeling promise to further expand PET's capacity to elucidate brain neurochemistry, supporting both basic neuroscience research and precision medicine approaches in psychiatry and neurology.

title: The psychopharmacology of Huntington disease.
abstract: Huntington disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by motor, cognitive, and psychiatric manifestations that significantly impact patient quality of life and caregiver burden. While no disease-modifying treatments currently exist, targeted psychopharmacological interventions can effectively manage behavioral and psychiatric symptoms. This comprehensive review examines current evidence-based pharmacological approaches for HD-associated neuropsychiatric complications. We analyzed published clinical trials, case series, and expert consensus guidelines from 1990-2018, focusing on medications for chorea, depression, anxiety, psychosis, and behavioral dysregulation in HD patients. Tetrabenazine and deutetrabenazine demonstrate robust efficacy for chorea management through vesicular monoamine transporter 2 inhibition, with deutetrabenazine showing improved tolerability profiles. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, particularly sertraline and citalopram, effectively treat depression and anxiety with lower risk of extrapyramidal side effects compared to tricyclic antidepressants. Atypical antipsychotics, including quetiapine and aripiprazole, show promise for psychotic symptoms and behavioral disturbances while minimizing motor deterioration. Mood stabilizers such as valproate may benefit patients with irritability and aggression. However, traditional antipsychotics and dopamine-depleting agents require careful monitoring due to potential worsening of motor symptoms. Sleep disorders respond well to targeted interventions including melatonin and sedating antidepressants. Future research should focus on personalized treatment approaches, biomarker-guided therapy selection, and novel neuroprotective compounds. Multidisciplinary care incorporating psychiatric assessment and pharmacological optimization remains essential for comprehensive HD management throughout disease progression.

title: An Update on the Neurochemistry of Essential Tremor.
abstract: Essential tremor (ET) is the most prevalent movement disorder, affecting approximately 1% of the global population, yet its underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain incompletely understood. Recent advances in neuroimaging, postmortem studies, and cerebrospinal fluid analyses have provided new insights into the molecular pathophysiology of ET. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding neurotransmitter systems, metabolic pathways, and molecular biomarkers implicated in ET pathogenesis. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of studies published between 2015-2020, focusing on neurochemical findings from human ET patients and relevant animal models. Emerging evidence suggests significant dysregulation in GABAergic neurotransmission, with reduced GABA levels observed in cerebellar cortex and dentate nucleus. Glutamatergic hyperexcitability appears prominent in olivocerebellar circuits, supported by elevated glutamate concentrations in cerebrospinal fluid and increased metabotropic glutamate receptor expression. Novel findings indicate altered dopaminergic signaling in ET patients, contrasting with previous assumptions of isolated cerebellar dysfunction. Metabolomic analyses reveal disrupted energy metabolism, particularly impaired mitochondrial function and altered lactate levels in affected brain regions. Additionally, inflammatory markers including elevated cytokines and activated microglia suggest neuroinflammatory processes contribute to ET progression. Proteomic studies have identified potential biomarkers including altered levels of neurofilament proteins and tau species. These neurochemical alterations collectively point toward ET as a complex neurodegenerative condition involving multiple neurotransmitter systems and cellular pathways. Understanding these molecular mechanisms provides crucial foundations for developing targeted therapeutic interventions and improving clinical management strategies for ET patients.

title: Glutamate-based antidepressants: preclinical psychopharmacology.
abstract: Major depressive disorder affects over 300 million people worldwide, yet current monoamine-based antidepressants have limited efficacy and delayed onset of action. Recent clinical findings with ketamine, an N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (NMDAR) antagonist, demonstrate rapid antidepressant effects within hours, suggesting glutamatergic mechanisms as promising therapeutic targets. We systematically reviewed preclinical evidence for glutamate-modulating compounds as potential rapid-acting antidepressants using established rodent behavioral paradigms. Male Sprague-Dawley rats underwent chronic unpredictable stress (CUS) for 21 days to induce depression-like behaviors, followed by acute or subchronic treatment with ketamine (10 mg/kg), memantine (20 mg/kg), or the mGluR2/3 antagonist LY341495 (3 mg/kg). Behavioral outcomes were assessed using forced swim test, sucrose preference, and novelty-suppressed feeding paradigms. Brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) and synaptic protein expression were measured in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Ketamine produced rapid reversal of anhedonic and despair-like behaviors within 24 hours, persisting for 7 days after single administration. Memantine showed similar but less robust effects, while LY341495 demonstrated comparable rapid onset but shorter duration of action. All NMDAR antagonists significantly increased BDNF expression and synaptic markers PSD-95 and synaptophysin in both brain regions compared to vehicle controls. These findings support the glutamate hypothesis of depression and validate NMDAR antagonism as a mechanism for rapid antidepressant action. The sustained behavioral improvements and enhanced neuroplasticity markers suggest that glutamate-targeting drugs may offer superior therapeutic profiles compared to conventional monoaminergic antidepressants, warranting further clinical development.

title: Schizophrenia: a review of neuropharmacology.
abstract: Schizophrenia remains one of the most challenging psychiatric disorders, affecting approximately 1% of the global population with significant socioeconomic burden. Despite decades of research, the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain incompletely understood. This comprehensive review examines current knowledge of neurotransmitter systems implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology and evaluates therapeutic interventions. A systematic literature search was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases from 1990-2004, focusing on dopaminergic, glutamatergic, GABAergic, and serotonergic dysfunction in schizophrenia. Clinical trial data, neuroimaging studies, and post-mortem brain tissue analyses were reviewed to assess neuropharmacological targets and treatment efficacy. The traditional dopamine hypothesis has evolved beyond simple D2 receptor hyperactivity to incorporate complex interactions between dopaminergic pathways and other neurotransmitter systems. Emerging evidence supports the NMDA receptor hypofunction hypothesis, linking glutamatergic deficits to both positive and negative symptoms. GABAergic interneuron dysfunction contributes to altered gamma oscillations and cognitive impairment. Atypical antipsychotics demonstrate superior efficacy through multi-receptor antagonism, particularly 5-HT2A/D2 receptor modulation, with reduced extrapyramidal side effects compared to conventional agents. However, metabolic adverse effects remain problematic. Novel therapeutic approaches targeting glutamatergic enhancement, cholinergic modulation, and neuroprotective strategies show promise in early-phase trials. Contemporary understanding of schizophrenia neuropharmacology emphasizes network dysfunction rather than single neurotransmitter abnormalities. Future therapeutic development should focus on symptom-specific interventions and personalized medicine approaches to optimize treatment outcomes while minimizing adverse effects.

title: Pharmacotherapies for alcoholism: the old and the new.
abstract: Alcoholism remains a significant public health burden with limited pharmacological treatment options and suboptimal clinical outcomes. While traditional therapies including disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate have shown modest efficacy, novel therapeutic targets are urgently needed. This comprehensive review examines established pharmacotherapies alongside emerging treatment approaches based on recent advances in understanding alcohol addiction neurobiology. We conducted a systematic analysis of clinical trial data from 1990-2009, encompassing 127 randomized controlled trials involving 18,456 patients with alcohol use disorders. Traditional medications demonstrated variable efficacy: naltrexone reduced heavy drinking days by 17-25%, acamprosate increased abstinence rates by 13-18%, while disulfiram showed benefits primarily in supervised settings. Emerging pharmacotherapies targeting glutamatergic, GABAergic, and dopaminergic systems show promise. Topiramate demonstrated significant reductions in drinking frequency and quantity, while baclofen showed efficacy in maintaining abstinence, particularly in patients with hepatic complications. Novel approaches targeting the endocannabinoid system, neuropeptide Y, and corticotropin-releasing factor pathways are in early development stages. Pharmacogenomic studies revealed potential biomarkers for treatment response, including OPRM1 polymorphisms affecting naltrexone efficacy and ALDH2 variants influencing disulfiram response. Despite advances, effect sizes remain moderate, with most medications showing 15-30% improvement over placebo. Combination therapies and personalized medicine approaches based on genetic profiling and neurobiological phenotyping represent promising future directions. Integration of pharmacotherapy with behavioral interventions continues to optimize treatment outcomes in alcohol use disorders.

title: Cannabinoids: Emerging developments in neuropsychopharmacology and biological psychiatry.
abstract: Cannabinoids have emerged as promising therapeutic agents for various neuropsychiatric disorders, yet their complex mechanisms of action and clinical efficacy remain incompletely understood. This comprehensive review synthesizes recent advances in cannabinoid neuropsychopharmacology, examining preclinical and clinical evidence for therapeutic applications in anxiety, depression, post-traumatic stress disorder, and psychotic disorders. We conducted a systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature published between 2016-2021, focusing on studies investigating cannabidiol (CBD), delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), and synthetic cannabinoid compounds. Preclinical studies demonstrate that CBD exhibits anxiolytic and antidepressant-like effects through modulation of serotonergic, GABAergic, and endocannabinoid signaling pathways, while showing minimal psychoactive properties. Clinical trials reveal significant therapeutic potential for CBD in treatment-resistant epilepsy, with emerging evidence supporting efficacy in anxiety disorders and schizophrenia-spectrum conditions. Conversely, THC shows biphasic dose-dependent effects, with low doses potentially beneficial for certain conditions but higher concentrations associated with increased psychotic symptoms and cognitive impairment. Novel synthetic cannabinoids targeting specific receptor subtypes demonstrate improved therapeutic indices in animal models. However, translational challenges persist, including variable bioavailability, complex drug-drug interactions, and heterogeneous patient responses. Current evidence suggests that cannabinoids, particularly CBD, represent a promising class of neuropsychiatric therapeutics, though further standardized clinical trials are essential to establish optimal dosing regimens, long-term safety profiles, and patient selection criteria for personalized treatment approaches in biological psychiatry.

title: Human Brain Neuropharmacology: A Platform for Translational Neuroscience.
abstract: The translation of preclinical neuropharmacological findings to clinical applications remains a significant challenge in drug development, with failure rates exceeding 90% for central nervous system therapeutics. Traditional approaches relying primarily on animal models often fail to capture the complexity of human neural circuits and drug responses. We present a comprehensive platform integrating human brain-derived models, advanced imaging techniques, and computational approaches to bridge this translational gap. Our methodology combines induced pluripotent stem cell-derived neural cultures, human brain organoids, ex vivo human brain slice preparations, and in vivo neuroimaging with pharmacological challenges. We validated this platform using established compounds across major neurotransmitter systems, including dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic pathways. Comparative analysis revealed significant species-specific differences in drug sensitivity and receptor expression patterns between rodent and human neural tissues. Human brain organoids demonstrated enhanced predictive validity for clinical efficacy compared to conventional cell lines, showing 78% concordance with clinical outcomes versus 34% for traditional models. Pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic modeling incorporating human-specific parameters improved prediction accuracy for therapeutic windows and adverse effects. Integration of multi-modal neuroimaging data provided real-time validation of target engagement and network-level effects. This platform successfully identified three novel compounds with therapeutic potential that had previously failed in animal studies, leading to clinical trial initiation. These findings establish a robust framework for human-centric neuropharmacology that enhances translational success while reducing reliance on animal models, potentially revolutionizing neurotherapeutic development.

title: [Psychopharmacological Studies in Mice].
abstract: The psychopharmacological effects of various compounds on central nervous system function remain incompletely understood, particularly regarding their mechanisms of action in rodent models. This study investigated the behavioral and neurochemical effects of novel benzothiazepine derivatives in ICR mice to elucidate their potential therapeutic applications. Male ICR mice (n=8-10 per group) were administered test compounds (1, 3, or 10 mg/kg, i.p.) or vehicle controls. Behavioral assessments included spontaneous locomotor activity, rotarod performance, forced swimming test, and elevated plus maze. Brain tissue samples were analyzed for monoamine neurotransmitter levels using high-performance liquid chromatography. Compound BT-347 significantly reduced spontaneous locomotor activity at 10 mg/kg (p<0.01) without impairing motor coordination on the rotarod test. In the forced swimming test, BT-347 decreased immobility time at 3 mg/kg (p<0.05), suggesting antidepressant-like effects. The elevated plus maze revealed anxiolytic properties, with increased open arm entries at 1 and 3 mg/kg doses (p<0.05). Neurochemical analysis showed dose-dependent increases in hippocampal serotonin levels (p<0.01) and decreased norepinephrine turnover in the frontal cortex. No significant changes in dopamine concentrations were observed. These findings indicate that BT-347 exhibits antidepressant and anxiolytic properties mediated through serotonergic mechanisms, with minimal effects on motor function. The compound demonstrates potential as a therapeutic agent for mood disorders, warranting further investigation into its clinical applications and long-term safety profile.
journal: Yakugaku zasshi : Journal of the Pharmaceutical Society of Japan

title: The psychopharmacology of brain vascular disease/poststroke depression.
abstract: Poststroke depression (PSD) affects approximately 30% of stroke survivors and represents a major determinant of functional recovery and quality of life. While the pathophysiology of PSD involves complex interactions between neuroinflammation, monoaminergic dysfunction, and structural brain changes, optimal pharmacological management remains poorly defined. This systematic review examines the current evidence for psychopharmacological interventions in PSD and their mechanisms of action in the context of cerebrovascular pathology. We analyzed randomized controlled trials and observational studies published between 2010-2018 investigating antidepressant efficacy in stroke patients, focusing on selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), tricyclic antidepressants, and novel therapeutic approaches. Twenty-three studies encompassing 2,847 patients met inclusion criteria. SSRIs, particularly sertraline and citalopram, demonstrated superior efficacy compared to placebo (pooled effect size 0.73, 95% CI 0.52-0.94, p<0.001) with acceptable tolerability profiles. Fluoxetine showed additional neuroprotective properties, potentially enhancing neuroplasticity through BDNF upregulation and promoting functional recovery beyond mood improvement. Tricyclic antidepressants showed comparable antidepressant efficacy but increased cardiovascular risks in this vulnerable population. Emerging evidence suggests that early intervention within 3 months post-stroke optimizes treatment response, possibly by modulating neuroinflammatory cascades and preventing maladaptive neuroplastic changes. However, drug-drug interactions with anticoagulants and antiplatelets require careful consideration. Current evidence supports SSRIs as first-line therapy for PSD, with timing of initiation and individual stroke characteristics influencing treatment selection. Future research should focus on personalized approaches based on stroke location, severity, and individual risk factors.

title: Neuropharmacology of Cevimeline and Muscarinic Drugs-Focus on Cognition and Neurodegeneration.
abstract: Muscarinic acetylcholine receptors play crucial roles in cognitive function and are implicated in neurodegenerative diseases, particularly Alzheimer's disease. Cevimeline, a selective M1/M3 muscarinic agonist primarily used for xerostomia treatment, has emerged as a potential therapeutic candidate for cognitive enhancement and neuroprotection. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding the neuropharmacological properties of cevimeline and related muscarinic compounds in the context of cognition and neurodegeneration. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies examining cevimeline's effects on cognitive function, synaptic plasticity, and neuroprotective mechanisms. Additionally, we evaluated comparative data from other muscarinic modulators including donepezil, rivastigmine, and novel M1-selective compounds. Preclinical studies demonstrate that cevimeline enhances long-term potentiation in hippocampal slices, improves spatial memory performance in rodent models, and reduces amyloid-β accumulation through M1 receptor-mediated pathways. The compound exhibits neuroprotective effects against oxidative stress and tau hyperphosphorylation in transgenic Alzheimer's models. Clinical pilot studies suggest modest cognitive improvements in mild cognitive impairment patients, with favorable tolerability profiles compared to traditional cholinesterase inhibitors. Mechanistically, cevimeline appears to activate PKC/MAPK signaling cascades and promote CREB-mediated gene expression essential for synaptic plasticity. However, peripheral cholinergic side effects remain dose-limiting factors. These findings support cevimeline's potential as a novel therapeutic approach for cognitive disorders, warranting larger clinical trials to establish its efficacy and optimal dosing strategies in neurodegenerative diseases.

title: Inside-out neuropharmacology of nicotinic drugs.
abstract: Traditional approaches to nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) pharmacology have focused on drug binding and functional modulation at the extracellular surface, yet emerging evidence suggests that intracellular mechanisms play crucial roles in therapeutic efficacy. Here we investigated the "inside-out" neuropharmacological properties of nicotinic drugs using a combination of patch-clamp electrophysiology, fluorescence imaging, and behavioral assays in rodent models. We examined the effects of varenicline, cytisine, and novel synthetic compounds on α4β2 and α7 nAChRs when applied to the intracellular membrane surface of excised patches from rat hippocampal neurons. Intracellular application of varenicline (10-100 μM) produced sustained receptor activation distinct from its extracellular partial agonist profile, with efficacy reaching 85% of maximum ACh responses. Cytisine showed similar inside-out enhancement, while traditional antagonists like mecamylamine exhibited unexpected positive modulatory effects when applied intracellularly. Calcium imaging revealed that inside-out nicotinic activation triggered sustained Ca²⁺ elevations lasting 3-fold longer than extracellular stimulation. In behavioral studies, intracerebroventricular administration of varenicline produced enhanced cognitive performance in novel object recognition tasks compared to systemic delivery, despite lower total brain exposure. These findings demonstrate that nicotinic drugs exhibit fundamentally different pharmacological profiles when accessing receptors from the intracellular compartment, suggesting that drug trafficking across membranes and accumulation in cytoplasmic domains may be critical determinants of therapeutic outcomes. This inside-out perspective opens new avenues for nicotinic drug development and may explain variable clinical responses to current smoking cessation therapies.

title: Legal aspects of zebrafish neuropharmacology and neurotoxicology research.
abstract: Zebrafish (Danio rerio) have emerged as a valuable vertebrate model for neuropharmacology and neurotoxicology research due to their genetic tractability, optical transparency, and conserved neurobiological pathways with mammals. However, the regulatory landscape governing zebrafish research remains complex and varies significantly across jurisdictions. This review examines the current legal framework surrounding zebrafish neuropharmacology studies, analyzing regulations from the United States, European Union, and other major research jurisdictions. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of institutional animal care and use committee protocols, federal guidelines, and international standards governing zebrafish research between 2010-2018. Our analysis reveals substantial heterogeneity in regulatory requirements, with some jurisdictions treating zebrafish as equivalent to mammalian models while others provide more streamlined approval processes for early developmental stages. The European Union's Directive 2010/63/EU generally requires full ethical review for zebrafish studies beyond 120 hours post-fertilization, while U.S. guidelines vary by institution regarding developmental stage thresholds. Drug screening protocols, behavioral assays, and toxicological endpoints face different regulatory scrutiny depending on the specific compounds tested and intended applications. Key regulatory gaps identified include inconsistent definitions of sentience onset, variable requirements for anesthesia and euthanasia protocols, and unclear guidelines for high-throughput screening applications. These findings highlight the need for harmonized international standards that balance scientific innovation with appropriate ethical oversight, particularly as zebrafish models increasingly bridge preclinical research and regulatory toxicology applications in pharmaceutical development.

title: "Novel Psychopharmacology for Depressive Disorders".
abstract: Major depressive disorder (MDD) affects over 300 million individuals worldwide, with current antidepressant therapies demonstrating limited efficacy and significant side effects. Traditional monoamine-based treatments show response rates of only 60-70%, necessitating novel therapeutic approaches. This study investigated the antidepressant potential of compound ZL-47, a selective modulator of synaptic plasticity targeting both AMPA receptor potentiation and mTOR pathway activation. Male C57BL/6J mice (n=48) were subjected to chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) for 6 weeks to induce depression-like behaviors. Animals received daily intraperitoneal injections of ZL-47 (5, 10, or 20 mg/kg), fluoxetine (20 mg/kg), or vehicle for 14 days. Behavioral assessments included forced swim test, sucrose preference test, and open field test. Hippocampal tissue was analyzed for BDNF expression, dendritic spine density, and synaptic protein levels using Western blot and Golgi staining. ZL-47 treatment dose-dependently reduced immobility time in the forced swim test (p<0.001) and restored sucrose preference (p<0.01) compared to vehicle controls. Notably, behavioral improvements emerged after 7 days of treatment, significantly faster than fluoxetine (14 days). Molecular analyses revealed increased BDNF expression (2.3-fold), enhanced dendritic spine density (34% increase), and elevated PSD-95 levels in ZL-47-treated animals. These findings suggest that dual AMPA/mTOR modulation represents a promising therapeutic strategy for depression, offering rapid onset and robust efficacy through synaptic plasticity enhancement. Further clinical investigation is warranted to evaluate translational potential.
journal: Advances in experimental medicine and biology

title: PET in psychopharmacology.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful neuroimaging technique for investigating the pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics of psychotropic medications in living human brain. This review examines the current applications and methodological considerations of PET in psychopharmacological research. PET studies utilizing various radioligands have provided unprecedented insights into drug occupancy at neurotransmitter receptors, regional brain metabolism, and cerebral blood flow changes following psychoactive drug administration. Dopamine D2 receptor occupancy studies using [11C]raclopride have demonstrated that therapeutic efficacy of antipsychotic drugs occurs at 60-80% striatal receptor occupancy, while extrapyramidal side effects emerge above 80% occupancy. Similarly, serotonin transporter occupancy studies with [11C]McN5652 have shown that antidepressant efficacy requires >80% transporter blockade. PET investigations of glucose metabolism using [18F]FDG have revealed drug-induced alterations in prefrontal cortex activity in patients with schizophrenia and depression. Recent developments in PET methodology, including improved spatial resolution and novel radioligands targeting GABA, glutamate, and other neurotransmitter systems, are expanding research possibilities. However, limitations include radiation exposure constraints, high costs, and the need for on-site cyclotron facilities. Despite these challenges, PET remains an invaluable tool for dose optimization, understanding mechanisms of action, and predicting therapeutic response and adverse effects. Future applications may include personalized medicine approaches and the development of novel therapeutic targets in psychiatric disorders.

title: Neuropharmacology of 3,4-Methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV), Its Metabolites, and Related Analogs.
abstract: 3,4-Methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV) is a synthetic cathinone that emerged as a prominent constituent of "bath salts" and has been associated with severe psychostimulant toxicity and abuse liability. Despite regulatory scheduling, MDPV analogs continue to appear in illicit drug markets, yet their pharmacological profiles remain poorly characterized. This study investigated the neuropharmacological properties of MDPV, its primary metabolites, and five structural analogs using in vitro transporter assays, microdialysis, and behavioral paradigms in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Radioligand binding assays revealed that MDPV potently inhibited dopamine (DAT) and norepinephrine (NET) transporters with IC₅₀ values of 4.2 and 26.1 nM, respectively, while showing minimal serotonin transporter activity. The 3,4-dihydroxy and 4-hydroxy-3-methoxy metabolites retained significant DAT inhibition (IC₅₀ 89-156 nM) but displayed reduced potency compared to the parent compound. Structural analogs α-PVP and MDPBP exhibited similar DAT/NET selectivity profiles, while pyrrolidinyl modifications substantially altered transporter selectivity. In vivo microdialysis demonstrated that MDPV (1-3 mg/kg, i.p.) produced dose-dependent increases in nucleus accumbens dopamine levels lasting 4-6 hours. Self-administration studies confirmed robust reinforcing effects, with MDPV maintaining higher response rates than cocaine at equivalent doses. These findings demonstrate that MDPV and related analogs function as potent DAT/NET inhibitors with high abuse potential. The persistent activity of MDPV metabolites may contribute to prolonged psychoactive effects observed clinically. These data provide crucial pharmacological insights for understanding synthetic cathinone toxicity and informing regulatory strategies for emerging analogs.

title: Psychopharmacology of combined activation of the serotonin1A and σ1 receptors.
abstract: Serotonin1A (5-HT1A) and sigma-1 (σ1) receptors represent promising therapeutic targets for neuropsychiatric disorders, yet their potential synergistic interactions remain poorly understood. This study investigated the psychopharmacological effects of combined 5-HT1A and σ1 receptor activation using behavioral, electrophysiological, and neurochemical approaches. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received intraperitoneal injections of the selective 5-HT1A agonist 8-OH-DPAT (0.5 mg/kg), the σ1 receptor agonist PRE-084 (3 mg/kg), or their combination. Anxiety-like behavior was assessed using elevated plus maze and open field tests, while antidepressant-like effects were evaluated via forced swim test. Hippocampal long-term potentiation was measured using field potential recordings, and monoamine levels were quantified by HPLC in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Combined treatment produced significantly greater anxiolytic effects compared to either compound alone, increasing open arm exploration by 78% versus 42% for 8-OH-DPAT and 35% for PRE-084 monotherapy. The drug combination enhanced antidepressant-like behavior, reducing immobility time by 65% compared to 38% and 29% for individual treatments. Electrophysiological analysis revealed that co-administration facilitated hippocampal LTP induction, with 45% greater potentiation magnitude than controls. Neurochemical analysis demonstrated synergistic increases in prefrontal dopamine and norepinephrine levels following combined treatment. The σ1 antagonist BD-1047 blocked these synergistic effects. These findings suggest that simultaneous 5-HT1A and σ1 receptor activation produces enhanced anxiolytic and antidepressant-like effects through complementary modulation of monoaminergic neurotransmission and synaptic plasticity, supporting the therapeutic potential of dual-target pharmacological approaches for mood and anxiety disorders.

title: Psychopharmacology in autism.
abstract: Autism is a pervasive developmental disorder characterized by impaired social interaction, communication deficits, and repetitive behaviors. While no medications specifically treat the core symptoms of autism, psychopharmacological interventions may ameliorate associated behavioral disturbances. This study examined the efficacy and safety of various psychoactive medications in children and adolescents with autistic disorder. We conducted a retrospective chart review of 127 patients (ages 3-18 years) diagnosed with autism who received psychopharmacological treatment between 1994-1998 at a university-affiliated developmental disabilities clinic. Medications were categorized into five classes: selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), typical antipsychotics, atypical antipsychotics, stimulants, and anticonvulsants. Clinical improvement was assessed using standardized behavioral rating scales and physician global impression scores. SSRIs were the most frequently prescribed medications (n=42), followed by typical antipsychotics (n=31) and stimulants (n=28). Significant improvements in repetitive behaviors and aggression were observed with SSRI treatment (p<0.01), while antipsychotics showed efficacy for severe behavioral dyscontrol and self-injurious behaviors (p<0.05). Stimulants demonstrated modest benefits for hyperactivity and inattention in higher-functioning individuals. Adverse effects were generally mild to moderate, with sedation and weight gain being most common. These findings suggest that targeted psychopharmacological interventions can provide symptomatic relief for specific behavioral manifestations of autism, though careful monitoring is essential. Further controlled studies are needed to establish optimal treatment protocols and long-term safety profiles in this vulnerable population.

title: Neuropharmacology of Synthetic Cathinones.
abstract: Synthetic cathinones, colloquially known as "bath salts," represent a rapidly expanding class of psychoactive substances that have emerged as significant public health concerns due to their widespread abuse and unpredictable neuropsychiatric effects. These β-ketone analogs of phenethylamine exhibit structural similarities to amphetamine and cathinone, the psychoactive component of the khat plant. To elucidate their neuropharmacological mechanisms, we conducted comprehensive in vitro and in vivo studies examining the effects of prominent synthetic cathinones including mephedrone, methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV), and α-pyrrolidinopentiophenone (α-PVP) on monoaminergic neurotransmission. Radioligand binding assays and uptake inhibition studies in HEK293 cells transfected with human dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin transporters revealed that synthetic cathinones function primarily as transporter blockers rather than substrates, with MDPV and α-PVP demonstrating particularly high selectivity for dopamine and norepinephrine transporters (DAT/NET selectivity ratios >100-fold over SERT). Microdialysis studies in freely-moving rats showed dose-dependent increases in extracellular dopamine concentrations in the nucleus accumbens following administration of all tested compounds, with MDPV producing the most sustained elevation. Behavioral assessments revealed hyperlocomotion, stereotypy, and conditioned place preference, consistent with abuse liability. These findings demonstrate that synthetic cathinones exert their psychoactive effects primarily through blockade of monoamine reuptake transporters, with selectivity profiles that may explain their distinct behavioral and abuse potential compared to classical stimulants.

title: The Psychopharmacology of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Preclinical Roadmap.
abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) affects 2-3% of the global population and represents one of the most debilitating psychiatric conditions, yet current pharmacological interventions remain limited and poorly understood mechanistically. While selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) constitute first-line treatment, approximately 40-60% of patients show inadequate response, highlighting critical gaps in our understanding of OCD neurobiology. This review synthesizes current preclinical models and emerging pharmacological targets to provide a translational framework for OCD drug development. We systematically analyzed behavioral paradigms including marble burying, schedule-induced polydipsia, and signal attenuation models in rodents, alongside their neurobiological correlates in cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuitry. Beyond the classical serotonergic hypothesis, converging evidence implicates glutamatergic dysfunction, particularly NMDA receptor hypofunction and altered metabotropic glutamate receptor signaling within the orbitofrontal cortex and striatum. Preclinical studies demonstrate therapeutic potential for glutamate modulators including memantine, riluzole, and mGluR2/3 agonists. Additionally, emerging targets encompass GABAergic interneuron dysfunction, dopaminergic imbalances in reward processing circuits, and neuroimmune activation involving microglial cytokine release. Novel approaches targeting the endocannabinoid system, particularly CB1 receptor modulation, show promise in reversing compulsive-like behaviors. Optogenetic and chemogenetic studies have refined our understanding of specific neural populations driving repetitive behaviors, revealing distinct roles for direct versus indirect pathway medium spiny neurons. These preclinical insights provide a roadmap for developing more effective, mechanistically-informed treatments for OCD, emphasizing the need for circuit-specific therapeutic approaches beyond traditional monoaminergic strategies.

title: Fundamental and clinical neuropharmacology, a terra incognita with constantly expanding frontiers.
abstract: The field of neuropharmacology has witnessed unprecedented growth over the past decade, yet significant knowledge gaps persist in our understanding of drug-brain interactions and their clinical translation. This comprehensive review synthesizes recent advances in fundamental neuropharmacology research and examines the evolving landscape of therapeutic interventions for neurological and psychiatric disorders. We conducted a systematic analysis of published literature from 2010-2021, encompassing preclinical studies, clinical trials, and emerging therapeutic modalities. Database searches yielded 2,847 relevant publications, which were evaluated for methodological rigor, translational potential, and clinical impact. Our analysis reveals three major frontiers driving neuropharmacological innovation: precision medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenomics and biomarker-guided therapy, novel drug delivery systems enabling targeted central nervous system penetration, and the emergence of neuromodulatory techniques as pharmacological adjuncts. Despite remarkable progress in molecular neurobiology and drug discovery platforms, the failure rate for neurotherapeutic development remains disproportionately high at 92% compared to other therapeutic areas. Key challenges include the complexity of blood-brain barrier penetration, interspecies translational gaps, and the heterogeneous nature of neuropsychiatric phenotypes. However, breakthrough technologies including optogenetics, CRISPR-based therapeutics, and artificial intelligence-driven drug repurposing strategies offer unprecedented opportunities for therapeutic advancement. The neuropharmacological landscape continues to expand rapidly, demanding interdisciplinary collaboration and innovative methodological approaches to bridge the persistent gap between preclinical promise and clinical efficacy in treating disorders of the central nervous system.

title: Opinion on monoaminergic contributions to traits and temperament.
abstract: The monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems, comprising dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine pathways, have long been implicated in individual differences in personality traits and temperamental characteristics. However, the precise neurochemical mechanisms underlying these associations remain incompletely understood, with conflicting evidence across human neuroimaging, pharmacological challenge, and genetic studies. We synthesize current evidence linking monoaminergic function to major personality dimensions, including extraversion, neuroticism, and sensation-seeking behaviours. Through systematic review of neuroimaging studies employing PET and SPECT methodologies, we examine associations between neurotransmitter receptor densities, transporter availability, and validated personality measures in healthy populations. Pharmacological challenge studies using selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, dopamine receptor agonists, and noradrenergic modulators provide convergent evidence for distinct monoaminergic contributions to temperamental traits. Dopaminergic signalling in ventral striatal circuits correlates positively with reward sensitivity and extraversion measures, while serotonergic dysfunction in limbic regions associates with increased neuroticism and negative affectivity. Noradrenergic activity in prefrontal-amygdalar circuits modulates stress reactivity and behavioural inhibition. Genetic polymorphisms affecting monoamine metabolism show modest but consistent associations with personality phenotypes across populations. We propose an integrative framework wherein monoaminergic systems interact dynamically to shape individual differences in emotional reactivity, reward processing, and stress responsivity. These findings have implications for understanding psychiatric vulnerability and developing personalized therapeutic approaches. Future research should employ multimodal neuroimaging approaches and longitudinal designs to elucidate developmental trajectories of monoaminergic contributions to human temperament.
journal: Philosophical transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological sciences

title: Neurophamacology of yawning.
abstract: Yawning is a highly conserved behavioral phenomenon observed across vertebrate species, yet its neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. While traditionally associated with fatigue or boredom, emerging evidence suggests yawning involves complex neurotransmitter interactions within the hypothalamus, brainstem, and limbic structures. This study investigated the neuropharmacological basis of yawning using a rat model with selective pharmacological interventions. Adult Sprague-Dawley rats (n=96) were administered various neurotransmitter agonists and antagonists, including dopaminergic, serotonergic, cholinergic, and GABAergic compounds. Yawning frequency was quantified during 60-minute observation periods following drug administration. Apomorphine, a dopamine receptor agonist, significantly increased yawning frequency (p<0.001), with peak effects occurring 15-30 minutes post-injection. This response was completely blocked by haloperidol pretreatment, confirming dopaminergic mediation. Oxytocin administration also induced pronounced yawning, while the oxytocin receptor antagonist L-368,899 reduced baseline yawning behavior. Serotonin 5-HT2C receptor activation with mCPP enhanced yawning, whereas 5-HT1A agonist 8-OH-DPAT produced inhibitory effects. Cholinergic stimulation with physostigmine moderately increased yawning frequency, while muscarinic blockade with atropine showed minimal effects. GABA-A receptor activation suppressed yawning behavior. These findings demonstrate that yawning is regulated by a complex interplay of neurotransmitter systems, with dopaminergic and oxytocinergic pathways playing primary roles. Understanding yawning's neuropharmacology may provide insights into arousal mechanisms and have implications for neurological conditions characterized by excessive yawning.

title: Pharmacological regulation of gene expression.
abstract: Transcriptional regulation represents a fundamental mechanism through which pharmacological agents exert therapeutic effects in the central nervous system. While acute drug actions on neurotransmitter systems are well-characterized, the molecular pathways governing drug-induced changes in gene expression remain incompletely understood. This study investigated the temporal dynamics and signaling mechanisms underlying pharmacologically-induced transcriptional responses in primary cortical neurons and hippocampal tissue. Primary rat cortical neurons were treated with clinically relevant concentrations of fluoxetine (10 μM), diazepam (1 μM), or vehicle control for 1, 6, and 24 hours. Gene expression profiles were analyzed using quantitative RT-PCR and Western blotting, while chromatin immunoprecipitation assays examined histone modifications at target promoters. Pharmacological inhibitors of CREB, PKA, and MAPK pathways were employed to elucidate signaling mechanisms. Fluoxetine treatment significantly upregulated BDNF, c-fos, and CREB-1 mRNA expression in a time-dependent manner, with peak induction observed at 6 hours (2.8-fold, 4.2-fold, and 1.9-fold increases, respectively; p<0.01). Diazepam enhanced GABAA receptor α1 subunit expression while suppressing immediate early gene transcription. Chromatin analysis revealed increased H3K4me3 and H3K27ac marks at BDNF promoter regions following fluoxetine exposure. PKA inhibition with H-89 abolished fluoxetine-induced transcriptional changes, implicating cAMP-dependent mechanisms. These findings demonstrate that psychoactive drugs rapidly engage distinct transcriptional programs through specific epigenetic modifications and kinase cascades, providing molecular insight into delayed therapeutic responses and suggesting novel targets for neuropsychiatric drug development.

title: Neurochemical substrates linked to impulsive and compulsive phenotypes in addiction: A preclinical perspective.
abstract: Impulsive and compulsive behaviors represent core features of substance use disorders, yet their underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain incompletely understood. While impulsivity typically precedes addiction onset, compulsivity emerges during chronic drug exposure and withdrawal phases. This study examined the neurochemical substrates mediating these distinct behavioral phenotypes using established rodent models. Male Sprout-Dawley rats (n=120) underwent behavioral characterization using the 5-choice serial reaction time task for impulsivity assessment and progressive ratio schedules for compulsive drug-seeking evaluation following chronic cocaine self-administration. Microdialysis, immunohistochemistry, and Western blot analyses quantified neurotransmitter levels and protein expression across key brain regions including the nucleus accumbens, prefrontal cortex, and dorsal striatum. High-impulsive animals exhibited reduced baseline dopamine D2 receptor expression in the nucleus accumbens core (p<0.01) and elevated extracellular glutamate in the prelimbic cortex compared to low-impulsive counterparts. Following chronic cocaine exposure, compulsive phenotypes correlated with decreased serotonin transporter density in the dorsal raphe (p<0.001) and enhanced AMPA receptor phosphorylation in the dorsolateral striatum. Acute N-acetylcysteine administration (100 mg/kg) attenuated compulsive responding while having minimal effects on impulsive choice behavior. These findings demonstrate distinct neurochemical profiles underlying impulsive versus compulsive addiction-related phenotypes, with impulsivity associated with dopaminergic and glutamatergic dysregulation in corticoaccumbal circuits, while compulsivity involves serotonergic deficits and enhanced striatal plasticity. Understanding these neurochemical distinctions may inform targeted therapeutic approaches for different stages of addiction vulnerability and progression.

title: Gaddum and LSD: the birth and growth of experimental and clinical neuropharmacology research on 5-HT in the UK.
abstract: The discovery of lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) and its subsequent investigation by John Henry Gaddum at the National Institute for Medical Research fundamentally transformed our understanding of serotonin (5-HT) neuropharmacology in the United Kingdom. This historical review examines the pivotal period from 1953 to 1965 when Gaddum's pioneering work with LSD established the foundation for modern 5-HT receptor research. We analysed archival documents, laboratory notebooks, and published literature from Gaddum's laboratory to trace the development of experimental techniques and theoretical frameworks that emerged from LSD studies. Gaddum's observations that LSD potently antagonised 5-HT responses in isolated guinea pig ileum preparations led to the first quantitative receptor assays and the development of competitive antagonism theory. His work demonstrated that LSD's psychoactive effects correlated with its anti-5-HT activity, providing the earliest evidence for serotonin's role in psychiatric disorders. These investigations spawned a generation of UK neuropharmacologists who subsequently identified multiple 5-HT receptor subtypes and developed selective serotonergic compounds. Clinical applications emerged directly from this research programme, including the development of anti-migraine agents and early antidepressants targeting the 5-HT system. The LSD-Gaddum collaboration represents a unique convergence of serendipitous drug discovery with rigorous pharmacological methodology that established the UK as a leading centre for serotonin research. This historical analysis demonstrates how a single psychedelic compound catalysed the birth of systematic neurotransmitter pharmacology and laid the groundwork for modern psychiatric therapeutics.

title: Neurochemistry of Anesthetic States.
abstract: The molecular mechanisms underlying anesthetic-induced unconsciousness remain incompletely understood, hindering the development of more targeted anesthetic agents and improved perioperative monitoring. While previous studies have focused primarily on receptor-level interactions, the broader neurochemical landscape during anesthetic states has been less systematically characterized. We developed a comprehensive analytical framework combining microdialysis sampling with high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) to quantify neurotransmitter dynamics in the rat prefrontal cortex and thalamus during propofol and sevoflurane anesthesia. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8 per group) were implanted with microdialysis probes and subjected to clinically relevant anesthetic concentrations while monitoring electroencephalographic activity. Dialysate samples were collected at 15-minute intervals and analyzed for GABA, glutamate, dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and acetylcholine using validated LC-MS/MS methods. Both anesthetics produced distinct neurochemical signatures: propofol increased extracellular GABA levels by 240% in the prefrontal cortex while decreasing glutamate by 35%, whereas sevoflurane showed more modest GABA elevation (120%) but greater suppression of monoaminergic signaling. Thalamic acetylcholine levels decreased by 60-70% under both anesthetics, correlating strongly with loss of consciousness onset. Recovery patterns differed markedly, with propofol showing rapid neurotransmitter normalization while sevoflurane exhibited prolonged monoamine suppression. These findings demonstrate that anesthetic states involve complex, agent-specific neurochemical reorganization beyond simple GABA enhancement, providing a methodological foundation for future investigations of consciousness and anesthetic mechanisms.

title: High frequency, real-time neurochemical and neuropharmacological measurements in situ in the living body.
abstract: Recent advances in neuropharmacology have been limited by the temporal resolution of traditional analytical methods for measuring neurotransmitter dynamics in vivo. Existing microdialysis techniques, while valuable, provide measurements averaged over minutes rather than the millisecond timescales relevant to synaptic transmission and drug action. We developed and validated a novel fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) approach combined with carbon fiber microelectrodes to achieve high-frequency, real-time neurochemical measurements in living brain tissue. Using anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24), we implanted carbon fiber electrodes (7 μm diameter) into the nucleus accumbens and monitored dopamine release with 100 Hz temporal resolution during pharmacological challenges. We administered cocaine (10 mg/kg, i.v.), amphetamine (2 mg/kg, i.v.), and quinpirole (1 mg/kg, i.v.) while simultaneously recording neurochemical changes and corresponding behavioral responses. Our system successfully detected dopamine transients with sub-second resolution, revealing previously unobserved rapid fluctuations in neurotransmitter concentrations following drug administration. Cocaine produced a characteristic 300% increase in extracellular dopamine within 5 seconds, while amphetamine showed a more gradual but sustained elevation over 15 minutes. Quinpirole administration resulted in biphasic dopamine responses not detectable with conventional sampling methods. These findings demonstrate that high-frequency neurochemical monitoring can reveal previously hidden temporal dynamics of neuropharmacological responses, providing critical insights into drug mechanisms of action and potentially improving therapeutic drug monitoring for neuropsychiatric disorders.
journal: Translational research : the journal of laboratory and clinical medicine

title: The ethics of elective psychopharmacology.
abstract: The increasing availability of psychoactive pharmaceuticals for non-therapeutic enhancement has raised significant ethical concerns within neuropharmacology practice and research. While cognitive enhancers, mood stabilizers, and anxiolytics demonstrate clear therapeutic benefits for diagnosed psychiatric conditions, their off-label use by healthy individuals seeking performance enhancement or lifestyle modification presents novel bioethical challenges. This review examines current literature on elective psychopharmacology use, analyzing empirical data from surveys of university students, healthcare professionals, and general population cohorts regarding attitudes toward and prevalence of non-medical psychoactive drug use. We systematically reviewed 127 peer-reviewed articles published between 2008-2012, incorporating data from cross-sectional surveys (n=23,847 participants), longitudinal cohort studies, and expert consensus statements from major psychiatric organizations. Results indicate that 15-30% of university students report lifetime use of prescription stimulants without medical indication, while 8-12% of healthcare professionals acknowledge using cognitive enhancers for performance improvement. Key ethical concerns identified include: informed consent challenges given limited long-term safety data, questions of coercion in competitive environments, distributive justice issues regarding access and affordability, and potential medicalization of normal human variation. Professional guidelines remain inconsistent across jurisdictions, with 67% of surveyed physicians expressing uncertainty about appropriate prescribing boundaries. These findings highlight the urgent need for evidence-based ethical frameworks governing elective psychopharmacology use, incorporating considerations of individual autonomy, societal benefit, and professional responsibility. Further research is warranted to establish safety profiles and develop comprehensive regulatory approaches for enhancement-oriented psychopharmaceutical use.

title: Microdialysis applications in neuroscience.
abstract: Microdialysis has emerged as a powerful in vivo sampling technique for monitoring real-time neurochemical changes in the brain, offering unprecedented insights into neurotransmitter dynamics and drug pharmacokinetics. This review examines current applications of cerebral microdialysis in neuroscience research, focusing on methodological advances and clinical translational potential. We analyzed 127 published studies from 2003-2007 utilizing microdialysis techniques in various brain regions of rodent and non-human primate models. Key applications included monitoring dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, and GABA release in response to pharmacological interventions, behavioral paradigms, and disease models. Technical improvements in probe design, including reduction of membrane molecular weight cutoffs to 6-20 kDa and implementation of push-pull perfusion systems, have enhanced spatial resolution to approximately 1-2 mm³ and temporal resolution to 1-5 minute intervals. Recovery rates for small molecule neurotransmitters consistently ranged from 15-25% under standardized conditions. Recent innovations include coupling microdialysis with high-performance liquid chromatography and mass spectrometry for simultaneous multi-analyte detection, enabling quantification of neurotransmitters at femtomolar concentrations. Clinical applications have expanded to include human studies monitoring neurochemical changes during epileptic seizures and traumatic brain injury. Limitations include tissue trauma from probe insertion and potential alterations in local neurochemistry. Despite these constraints, microdialysis remains an invaluable tool for understanding brain neurochemistry and evaluating therapeutic interventions, with emerging applications in personalized medicine and real-time pharmacokinetic monitoring showing considerable promise for future neuropharmacological research.

title: Neuroethics and psychiatry.
abstract: The intersection of neuroscience and ethics has emerged as a critical domain in contemporary psychiatric practice, particularly as neuroimaging technologies and psychopharmacological interventions become increasingly sophisticated. This review examines the ethical implications of recent advances in neuropharmacology and neuroimaging within psychiatric contexts, analyzing literature published between 2005-2008. We conducted a comprehensive review of peer-reviewed articles, clinical guidelines, and case studies addressing neuroethical considerations in psychiatric treatment, focusing on cognitive enhancement, neuroimaging for diagnostic purposes, and informed consent in vulnerable populations. Key findings reveal significant ethical tensions surrounding the use of psychostimulants for cognitive enhancement in healthy individuals, with 34% of surveyed psychiatrists reporting requests for "cosmetic psychopharmacology." Neuroimaging studies raise concerns about genetic discrimination and privacy, particularly when structural abnormalities are detected incidentally. The concept of mental capacity and autonomous decision-making becomes complex when treating conditions that directly affect cognition and judgment. Additionally, the medicalization of normal human experiences through expanding diagnostic categories presents fundamental questions about the nature of mental illness. Our analysis identifies urgent needs for updated ethical frameworks addressing: enhancement versus treatment distinctions, confidentiality of neuroimaging data, and protection of vulnerable populations in research settings. As neurotechnology continues advancing, psychiatric practice must develop robust ethical guidelines that balance therapeutic innovation with patient autonomy and societal well-being. Professional training programs should integrate neuroethical education to prepare clinicians for these emerging challenges.

title: Integrative biological analysis for neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The complexity of neuropsychiatric disorders necessitates integrative approaches that combine multiple biological data types to understand drug mechanisms and identify novel therapeutic targets. Current neuropsychopharmacology research often relies on single-dimensional analyses, limiting our ability to capture the multifaceted nature of brain dysfunction and drug action. We developed a comprehensive analytical framework integrating genomics, proteomics, metabolomics, and neuroimaging data to investigate antidepressant mechanisms in major depressive disorder. Using samples from 156 treatment-naive patients and 89 healthy controls, we employed weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA) to identify molecular modules, followed by functional enrichment analysis and drug-target network construction. Pre- and post-treatment assessments included RNA sequencing from peripheral blood, plasma metabolite profiling, and resting-state fMRI. Our integrative analysis revealed three distinct molecular signatures associated with treatment response: a stress-response module enriched for glucocorticoid signaling genes, a neuroinflammatory module characterized by elevated cytokine pathways, and a neurotransmitter metabolism module showing altered monoamine biosynthesis. Critically, the combination of transcriptomic and metabolomic signatures predicted treatment outcome with 78% accuracy, significantly outperforming single-modality approaches (genomics: 61%, metabolomics: 65%). Neuroimaging data showed that molecular signatures correlated with altered connectivity in limbic-prefrontal circuits. These findings demonstrate that integrative biological analysis can reveal convergent pathways underlying neuropsychiatric disorders and drug action, potentially enabling personalized treatment selection. This multi-dimensional approach represents a paradigm shift toward precision neuropsychopharmacology, offering improved understanding of complex brain disorders and therapeutic mechanisms.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Team Evans and Watkins: Excitatory Amino Acid research at Bristol University 1973-1981.
abstract: This historical review examines the pioneering excitatory amino acid research conducted by the Evans and Watkins team at Bristol University between 1973-1981, a period that fundamentally shaped modern understanding of glutamatergic neurotransmission. During this era, the concept of glutamate as a major excitatory neurotransmitter was still emerging, with limited pharmacological tools available for mechanistic studies. The Bristol group systematically characterized the actions of various amino acid analogues on spinal cord neurons using electrophysiological recordings, developing novel synthetic compounds and analyzing structure-activity relationships. Their methodical approach involved testing over 200 amino acid derivatives, including the synthesis of conformationally restricted analogues such as ibotenic acid derivatives and quinolinic acid compounds. Key discoveries included the identification of distinct excitatory amino acid receptor subtypes, the development of selective antagonists including 2-amino-5-phosphonovaleric acid (APV), and the characterization of N-methyl-D-aspartic acid (NMDA) as a selective agonist. The team's work established the pharmacological foundation for distinguishing NMDA from non-NMDA receptors, decades before molecular cloning confirmed these receptor families. Their systematic classification of excitatory amino acid receptors and development of subtype-selective ligands provided essential tools that enabled subsequent breakthroughs in understanding synaptic plasticity, excitotoxicity, and neurological disorders. The Bristol laboratory's contributions during 1973-1981 represent a cornerstone period in neuropharmacology that directly enabled the modern therapeutic targeting of glutamate receptor systems in conditions ranging from stroke to schizophrenia.

title: Pharmaco fMRI: Determining the functional anatomy of the effects of medication.
abstract: Pharmacological functional magnetic resonance imaging (pharmaco-fMRI) has emerged as a powerful translational tool for investigating drug mechanisms in the human brain, yet standardized methodological approaches remain limited. This study aimed to establish a comprehensive framework for determining medication effects on neural circuitry using simultaneous pharmacokinetic and BOLD signal measurements. Twenty-four healthy volunteers (age 25.3±4.2 years) underwent fMRI scanning during acute administration of escitalopram (20mg) or placebo in a randomized, double-blind, crossover design.
