We conducted a comprehensive analysis of emerging endocannabinoid mediators including N-acylethanolamines, 2-acylglycerols, and related lipid species, evaluating their receptor binding profiles, metabolic pathways, and physiological functions through systematic literature review and meta-analysis of preclinical studies published between 2005-2012. Our analysis identified at least 12 distinct endocannabinoid-related molecules with varying affinities for CB1, CB2, TRPV1, and orphan receptors. Notable compounds include palmitoylethanolamide (PEA), oleoylethanolamide (OEA), and virodhamine, each demonstrating unique tissue distribution patterns and pharmacokinetic properties. These molecules exhibit diverse mechanisms including anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective, and metabolic regulatory effects that extend beyond traditional cannabinoid receptor activation. Several compounds modulate endocannabinoid tone through inhibition of fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) or monoacylglycerol lipase (MAGL). The endocannabinoid system represents a far more complex signaling network than initially conceived, with multiple molecular players contributing to cannabinoid-mediated neurotransmission. Understanding these diverse pathways may reveal novel therapeutic targets for neuropsychiatric disorders, chronic pain, and metabolic dysfunction.

title: Tobacco psychopharmacology and public health policy: it takes a community.
abstract: The public health burden of tobacco use remains substantial despite decades of policy interventions, suggesting that traditional approaches may benefit from enhanced understanding of nicotine's neuropharmacological mechanisms and their interaction with social-environmental factors. This review synthesizes current evidence on tobacco dependence neurobiological pathways and examines how community-level interventions can leverage pharmacological insights to improve cessation outcomes. We conducted a systematic analysis of randomized controlled trials (n=47) published between 2005-2011 that combined pharmacotherapeutic approaches with community-based interventions, alongside neuroimaging studies (n=23) examining nicotine's effects on reward circuitry in social contexts. Pharmacological treatments showed significantly enhanced efficacy when implemented through community networks compared to clinical settings alone (OR=1.64, 95% CI 1.31-2.05, p<0.001). Varenicline and bupropion demonstrated superior outcomes in community pharmacy programs versus standard medical care (quit rates 28.3% vs 19.7%, p=0.003). Neuroimaging data revealed that social environmental cues modulate nicotinic receptor availability and dopaminergic signaling in ways that influence medication response patterns. Community-delivered nicotine replacement therapy combined with peer support networks achieved 6-month abstinence rates of 31.2% compared to 22.1% for individual treatment approaches. These findings suggest that effective tobacco control requires integration of neuropharmacological knowledge with community-level implementation strategies. The complex interplay between nicotine's neurobiological effects and social determinants of smoking behavior necessitates collaborative approaches spanning clinical pharmacology, public health infrastructure, and community engagement to optimize population-level cessation outcomes.

title: Laboratory of receptor pharmacology.
abstract: G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) represent the largest class of drug targets, yet their complex pharmacological profiles remain incompletely understood. We investigated the functional selectivity and allosteric modulation properties of metabotropic glutamate receptor subtype 5 (mGluR5) using novel pharmacological tools in recombinant cell systems. Chinese hamster ovary cells stably expressing human mGluR5 were treated with the orthosteric agonist DHPG (10-100 μM) and the negative allosteric modulator MPEP (1-10 μM) either alone or in combination. Intracellular calcium mobilization was measured using Fura-2 fluorescence imaging, while cAMP accumulation was quantified by radioimmunoassay. Receptor internalization was assessed through confocal microscopy following fluorescent ligand binding. DHPG produced concentration-dependent increases in both calcium release (EC₅₀ = 15.2 ± 2.1 μM) and cAMP inhibition (EC₅₀ = 23.7 ± 3.4 μM). MPEP demonstrated pathway-selective antagonism, completely blocking calcium signaling while only partially inhibiting cAMP responses. Co-application studies revealed that MPEP binding caused a 4.2-fold rightward shift in DHPG calcium dose-response curves but only 1.8-fold shift for cAMP inhibition. Receptor internalization assays showed that MPEP reduced agonist-induced endocytosis by 65% at 30 minutes post-treatment. These findings demonstrate that mGluR5 exhibits significant functional selectivity between calcium and cAMP signaling pathways, with allosteric modulators capable of biasing receptor responses toward specific downstream effectors. Understanding such pathway-selective pharmacology may enable development of more precise therapeutics targeting neurological and psychiatric disorders involving glutamatergic dysfunction.

title: Pharmacological treatment in behavioural medicine: the importance of neurochemistry, molecular biology and mechanistic hypotheses.
abstract: Behavioural disorders in companion animals have traditionally been treated empirically, with limited understanding of underlying neurobiological mechanisms. This review examines the application of neurochemical principles and molecular biology to improve therapeutic outcomes in veterinary behavioural medicine. We analysed treatment responses in 156 dogs and 43 cats presenting with anxiety disorders, compulsive behaviours, and aggression over a 24-month period. Animals were treated with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (fluoxetine, sertraline), tricyclic antidepressants (clomipramine), or anxiolytics (alprazolam) based on mechanistic hypotheses derived from neurochemical dysfunction patterns. Therapeutic response was evaluated using standardised behavioural scoring systems and owner questionnaires at 4, 8, and 12 weeks post-treatment initiation. Dogs with separation anxiety showed 73% improvement with fluoxetine compared to 45% with traditional anxiolytics (P<0.01). Compulsive disorders responded favourably to clomipramine (68% improvement) versus fluoxetine (41%, P<0.05). Cats with urine marking behaviour demonstrated 82% response rates to fluoxetine. Molecular analysis of cerebrospinal fluid in a subset of animals revealed altered serotonin metabolite ratios correlating with treatment response (r=0.67, P<0.001). These findings demonstrate that mechanism-based therapeutic approaches, grounded in neurochemical understanding, significantly improve treatment outcomes compared to empirical methods. The integration of molecular biology principles into veterinary behavioural medicine represents a paradigm shift towards evidence-based therapeutics. Future research should focus on identifying specific biomarkers to guide individualised treatment protocols and optimise drug selection in companion animal behavioural disorders.

title: Neuropharmacology of cognition and memory: a unifying theory of neuromodulator imbalance in psychiatry and amnesia.
abstract: Cognitive dysfunction and memory impairments represent core features across diverse psychiatric disorders and amnestic syndromes, yet the underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. Current fragmented approaches focusing on individual neurotransmitter systems have failed to provide a comprehensive framework explaining the overlapping symptomatology observed in conditions ranging from schizophrenia and depression to Alzheimer's disease and temporal lobe amnesia. We propose a unifying hypothesis based on neuromodulator imbalance theory, wherein disrupted ratios between acetylcholine, dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin create distinct cognitive phenotypes through their differential effects on hippocampal-cortical circuits. This theoretical framework synthesizes existing literature demonstrating that memory consolidation requires optimal cholinergic-dopaminergic balance in the hippocampus, while executive attention depends on noradrenergic-serotonergic equilibrium in prefrontal regions. We postulate that psychiatric disorders represent specific neuromodulator imbalance patterns: excessive dopamine with deficient acetylcholine in psychosis, reduced norepinephrine with elevated serotonin in depression, and global cholinergic decline with compensatory monoaminergic hyperactivation in dementia. This model predicts that therapeutic interventions should target neuromodulator ratios rather than individual systems, explaining why combination therapies often prove more effective than monotherapy. Furthermore, the theory suggests that cognitive enhancement strategies should focus on restoring optimal neuromodulator balance within hippocampal-cortical networks. If validated, this framework could revolutionize psychiatric pharmacotherapy by shifting focus from symptom-based treatment to mechanism-based neuromodulator rebalancing, potentially improving both cognitive outcomes and quality of life across multiple neuropsychiatric conditions.

title: Antidepressant-related sexual dysfunction - perspectives from neuroimaging.
abstract: Antidepressant-induced sexual dysfunction (AISD) affects 30-70% of patients treated with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), yet the underlying neural mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study employed functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to investigate brain activation patterns during visual sexual stimuli in patients experiencing AISD compared to healthy controls and depressed patients with preserved sexual function. Twenty-four medication-free depressed patients, 22 SSRI-treated patients with sexual dysfunction, 18 SSRI-treated patients without sexual dysfunction, and 26 healthy controls underwent fMRI scanning while viewing erotic visual stimuli. Sexual function was assessed using the Changes in Sexual Functioning Questionnaire and Arizona Sexual Experience Scale. Blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signal changes were analyzed in regions of interest including the anterior cingulate cortex, insula, hypothalamus, and reward-related structures. SSRI-treated patients with sexual dysfunction showed significantly reduced activation in the ventral striatum, anterior cingulate cortex, and hypothalamic regions compared to all other groups (p<0.05, corrected). Additionally, these patients demonstrated increased activation in the lateral prefrontal cortex, suggesting compensatory cognitive control mechanisms. Correlation analyses revealed significant negative associations between SSRI plasma concentrations and BOLD signal in reward-processing regions (r=-0.58, p<0.01). These findings provide the first neuroimaging evidence that AISD involves disrupted reward processing and altered hypothalamic function during sexual arousal. The results suggest that serotonergic modulation of dopaminergic reward circuits may underlie SSRI-induced sexual side effects, offering potential targets for therapeutic intervention and informing treatment strategies for this prevalent clinical problem.

title: CNS Targets for multi-functional drugs in the treatment of Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases.
abstract: The progressive nature of Alzheimer's disease (AD) and Parkinson's disease (PD) involves multiple neurotransmitter systems and pathological mechanisms, suggesting that multi-target therapeutic approaches may provide superior clinical outcomes compared to single-target strategies. This review examines the rationale for developing multi-functional drugs that simultaneously modulate several CNS targets implicated in neurodegeneration. We analyzed the molecular pathways common to both diseases, including cholinergic dysfunction, oxidative stress, mitochondrial impairment, and protein aggregation mechanisms. Key targets identified include acetylcholinesterase, monoamine oxidase-B, NMDA receptors, and neuroprotective pathways involving BDNF and antioxidant systems. Literature analysis revealed that compounds with dual cholinesterase and MAO-B inhibition, such as ladostigil, demonstrate enhanced neuroprotective effects in preclinical models compared to selective inhibitors. Similarly, memantine's combined NMDA antagonism and neuroprotective properties provide broader therapeutic benefits than traditional approaches. Multi-target compounds targeting iron chelation, antioxidant activity, and neurotransmitter modulation, including M30 and related derivatives, showed promising results in cellular and animal models of both AD and PD. The evidence suggests that rationally designed multi-functional drugs addressing the complex pathophysiology of neurodegenerative diseases may offer improved therapeutic efficacy while potentially reducing polypharmacy-related complications. However, the development of such compounds requires careful optimization to achieve appropriate pharmacokinetic properties and target selectivity. Future clinical trials should prioritize multi-target agents that address the multifactorial nature of neurodegeneration.

title: Mass spectrometry imaging, an emerging technology in neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: Mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) represents a transformative analytical approach for studying drug distribution and neurochemical alterations in brain tissue with unprecedented spatial resolution. Traditional neuropsychopharmacological methods rely on tissue homogenization or microdialysis, which lack the ability to simultaneously map multiple compounds across distinct brain regions. Here, we demonstrate the application of matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI) MSI to examine the spatial distribution of antipsychotic drugs and endogenous neurotransmitters in rodent brain sections following acute and chronic haloperidol treatment. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) received either vehicle or haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg, i.p.) for 1 or 21 days. Brain sections (12 μm) were analyzed using MALDI-MSI with 50 μm spatial resolution across striatal, cortical, and hippocampal regions. Following acute treatment, haloperidol showed preferential accumulation in the striatum (2.3-fold higher than cortex, p<0.001) with concurrent reduction in dopamine levels (45% decrease, p<0.01). Chronic treatment revealed altered distribution patterns, with increased cortical penetration and region-specific changes in GABA and glutamate concentrations. Notably, MSI detected previously uncharacterized metabolites of haloperidol localized to white matter tracts. These findings demonstrate MSI's capability to simultaneously map drug pharmacokinetics and neurochemical consequences with spatial precision unattainable by conventional methods. This technology offers new insights into region-specific drug actions and resistance mechanisms, potentially informing more targeted therapeutic strategies. MSI represents a paradigm shift toward comprehensive, spatially-resolved neuropsychopharmacological analysis that bridges molecular pharmacology with systems-level brain function.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Neurochemical and physiological correlates of a critical period of respiratory development in the rat.
abstract: During early postnatal development, the mammalian respiratory control system undergoes significant maturation that is critical for establishing stable breathing patterns. Previous studies have identified the second postnatal week in rats as a vulnerable period characterized by increased susceptibility to respiratory instability and apnea. To elucidate the underlying mechanisms, we examined neurochemical and physiological changes in the brainstem respiratory network during postnatal days 8-16 in Sprague-Dawley rat pups. Respiratory parameters were monitored using whole-body plethysmography under normoxic and hypoxic conditions. Brainstem tissue was analyzed for neurotransmitter levels using high-performance liquid chromatography, and respiratory neuron activity was recorded from medullary slice preparations. Our results demonstrate that respiratory frequency and tidal volume exhibit significant variability between P10-P13, coinciding with a 40% reduction in GABA concentrations within the pre-Bötzinger complex compared to P8 levels. Serotonin levels showed a parallel 35% decrease during this period, while glutamate concentrations remained stable. Electrophysiological recordings revealed decreased inspiratory burst amplitude and increased cycle-to-cycle variability in respiratory neurons during P10-P13. Hypoxic ventilatory responses were markedly attenuated during this critical window, with recovery time increased by 60% compared to P8 animals. These findings suggest that the developmental reorganization of GABAergic and serotonergic neurotransmission within respiratory control circuits underlies the physiological instability observed during the second postnatal week, providing insight into mechanisms of respiratory vulnerability during early mammalian development.

title: A review of monoaminergic neuropsychopharmacology in zebrafish.
abstract: The zebrafish (Danio rerio) has emerged as a powerful vertebrate model for investigating monoaminergic neurotransmission and its pharmacological modulation. This review synthesizes current knowledge of dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic systems in zebrafish and their relevance to neuropsychopharmacological research. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published studies examining monoamine neurotransmitter distribution, receptor pharmacology, and behavioral responses to psychoactive compounds in zebrafish larvae and adults. Immunohistochemical and in situ hybridization studies reveal conserved monoaminergic neuronal populations in zebrafish brain regions analogous to mammalian structures, including dopaminergic neurons in the posterior tuberculum, serotonergic neurons in the raphe, and noradrenergic neurons in the locus coeruleus homolog. Pharmacological studies demonstrate that zebrafish exhibit dose-dependent behavioral responses to selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, dopamine receptor agonists and antagonists, and norepinephrine transporter inhibitors that parallel mammalian responses. High-throughput behavioral assays, including novel tank exploration, shoaling behavior, and prepulse inhibition paradigms, provide robust readouts for monoaminergic function. Genetic approaches using morpholinos and mutant lines further validate the functional conservation of monoaminergic pathways. The transparency of zebrafish larvae enables real-time visualization of neurotransmitter dynamics using genetically-encoded biosensors. These findings establish zebrafish as a tractable model system for screening neuroactive compounds, investigating monoamine-related psychiatric disorders, and elucidating fundamental mechanisms of monoaminergic neurotransmission with high translational relevance to human neuropsychopharmacology.

title: Serotonin receptors of type 6 (5-HT6): what can we expect from them?
abstract: The serotonin-6 (5-HT6) receptor represents one of the most recently characterized members of the serotonin receptor family, exhibiting unique pharmacological properties and distinctive central nervous system expression patterns. Unlike other serotonin receptor subtypes, 5-HT6 receptors are predominantly localized in brain regions associated with cognitive function, including the hippocampus, cortex, and striatum, suggesting potential therapeutic applications in neuropsychiatric disorders. This review examines current knowledge regarding 5-HT6 receptor pharmacology, distribution, and therapeutic potential based on preclinical and early clinical investigations. Molecular cloning studies have revealed that 5-HT6 receptors are positively coupled to adenylyl cyclase through Gαs proteins, leading to increased cyclic adenosine monophosphate levels. Behavioral studies using selective 5-HT6 receptor antagonists, including SB-271046, SB-399885, and Ro 04-6790, have demonstrated significant procognitive effects in various animal models of learning and memory, particularly in tasks involving spatial navigation and object recognition. These compounds have shown efficacy in reversing cognitive deficits induced by cholinergic antagonists, NMDA receptor blockade, and aging. Furthermore, 5-HT6 receptor modulation appears to influence neurotransmitter systems beyond serotonin, including acetylcholine, dopamine, and GABA pathways. Clinical trials with 5-HT6 receptor antagonists in Alzheimer's disease and schizophrenia have yielded mixed but promising results. The unique pharmacological profile and anatomical distribution of 5-HT6 receptors position them as attractive targets for developing novel therapeutic interventions for cognitive impairment associated with neurodegenerative diseases, schizophrenia, and other neuropsychiatric conditions requiring enhanced cognitive function.

title: Neuronal and glial cell lines as model systems for studying P2Y receptor pharmacology.
abstract: P2Y receptors are G-protein coupled receptors activated by extracellular nucleotides that play crucial roles in neurotransmission, glial activation, and neuroinflammation. While native tissues provide physiological relevance, immortalized cell lines offer advantages for systematic pharmacological studies including homogeneous receptor expression and reproducible experimental conditions. We characterized P2Y receptor subtypes in commonly used neuronal and glial cell lines to evaluate their utility as model systems for neuropharmacological research. RT-PCR analysis and immunocytochemistry were performed on PC12, SH-SY5Y, N2a neuroblastoma cells, C6 glioma cells, and primary rat astrocytes. Functional responses were assessed using calcium imaging, cAMP measurements, and inositol phosphate accumulation following stimulation with selective P2Y agonists including ADP, UTP, and UDP. PC12 cells predominantly expressed P2Y₁ and P2Y₁₂ receptors with robust calcium responses to ADP (EC₅₀ = 145 ± 23 nM). SH-SY5Y cells showed high P2Y₂ expression and strong UTP-evoked responses (EC₅₀ = 78 ± 15 nM). C6 glioma cells expressed multiple P2Y subtypes (P2Y₁, P2Y₂, P2Y₄) with mixed pharmacological profiles, while primary astrocytes showed predominant P2Y₁ receptor-mediated responses. N2a cells exhibited minimal P2Y receptor expression and weak functional responses. These findings demonstrate that specific cell lines can serve as reliable models for distinct P2Y receptor subtypes, with PC12 cells optimal for P2Y₁/P2Y₁₂ studies and SH-SY5Y cells suitable for P2Y₂ receptor pharmacology. This characterization provides a framework for selecting appropriate cellular models in P2Y receptor drug discovery and mechanistic studies.

title: Chemogenetics drives paradigm change in the investigation of behavioral circuits and neural mechanisms underlying drug action.
abstract: The emergence of chemogenetic tools has revolutionized neuroscience research by enabling precise temporal and spatial control over neural circuit activity in behaving animals. Traditional approaches to investigating neural mechanisms underlying drug action have relied heavily on pharmacological interventions, lesion studies, and electrophysiology, which often lack the specificity required to dissect complex behavioral circuits. This review examines how Designer Receptors Exclusively Activated by Designer Drugs (DREADDs) and other chemogenetic systems have transformed our understanding of drug-induced behaviors and addiction-related neural pathways. We analyzed recent literature from 2015-2020 documenting chemogenetic applications in addiction research, focusing on studies investigating reward circuits, drug-seeking behaviors, and relapse mechanisms. Our analysis reveals that chemogenetic approaches have enabled researchers to establish direct causal relationships between specific neural populations and drug-related behaviors with unprecedented precision. Key findings include the identification of distinct roles for different dopaminergic cell populations in reward processing, the characterization of cortico-striatal circuits mediating drug craving, and the discovery of novel therapeutic targets for addiction treatment. Chemogenetic studies have demonstrated that traditional pharmacological approaches often produced confounding effects by simultaneously activating multiple neural pathways. The ability to selectively manipulate genetically-defined cell populations has revealed previously unknown functional heterogeneity within classical reward circuits. These technological advances represent a paradigm shift from correlational to causal investigations in behavioral neuropharmacology, providing new insights into the neural basis of drug action and opening promising avenues for developing more targeted therapeutic interventions for substance use disorders.

title: Current and Future Neuropharmacological Options for the Treatment of Essential Tremor.
abstract: Essential tremor (ET) is the most prevalent movement disorder, affecting up to 5% of individuals over 65 years of age, yet current therapeutic options remain limited and often inadequately address symptom severity. This comprehensive review examines existing neuropharmacological treatments and evaluates emerging therapeutic strategies for ET management. We conducted a systematic analysis of clinical trials, case series, and preclinical studies published between 2000-2020, focusing on mechanisms of action, efficacy profiles, and adverse event rates. Current first-line treatments include propranolol and primidone, which provide meaningful tremor reduction in approximately 60% of patients but are associated with significant side effects including cognitive impairment, fatigue, and cardiovascular complications. Second-line agents such as topiramate, gabapentin, and benzodiazepines demonstrate modest efficacy but limited tolerability. Emerging therapeutic approaches show considerable promise, including selective GABA-A receptor modulators, T-type calcium channel blockers, and novel glutamatergic modulators targeting cerebellar circuits. Perampanel, a selective AMPA receptor antagonist, demonstrated 40% tremor reduction in recent phase II trials with favorable tolerability. Additionally, precision medicine approaches utilizing genetic biomarkers and neuroimaging-guided therapy selection are being investigated to optimize treatment selection. Deep brain stimulation remains highly effective for refractory cases, though invasiveness limits widespread application. Future directions emphasize development of cerebellar-specific targets, combination therapies, and personalized treatment algorithms. These advances suggest that more effective, tolerable pharmacological options for ET management may emerge within the next decade, potentially transforming outcomes for millions of affected individuals worldwide.

title: Pharmacotherapy of mental illness--a historical analysis.
abstract: The treatment of mental illness has undergone dramatic transformation over the past century, evolving from crude interventions to sophisticated pharmacological approaches. This historical analysis examines the development of psychopharmacology from 1900 to 2000, focusing on major therapeutic breakthroughs and their impact on clinical practice. A comprehensive literature review was conducted using MEDLINE, PsycINFO, and historical medical databases, supplemented by examination of pharmaceutical industry records and regulatory documents from the FDA and European agencies. Key therapeutic milestones were identified and analyzed within their historical context, including social, scientific, and economic factors influencing drug development. The analysis reveals five distinct epochs: the pre-pharmacological era (1900-1950), characterized by physical treatments; the serendipitous discovery period (1950-1970), marked by the introduction of chlorpromazine, lithium, and tricyclic antidepressants; the rational drug design era (1970-1985), featuring benzodiazepines and second-generation antipsychotics; the selective receptor targeting period (1985-1995), highlighted by SSRIs and atypical antipsychotics; and the genomics-informed era (1995-2000), emphasizing pharmacogenetics and novel targets. Each period demonstrated progressively improved efficacy-to-side effect ratios and expanded therapeutic indications. However, fundamental limitations persist, including incomplete understanding of psychiatric pathophysiology and significant treatment-resistant populations. This historical perspective reveals that while pharmacotherapy has revolutionized mental health treatment, future advances will likely require integration of genetic, neurobiological, and psychosocial approaches to achieve truly personalized psychiatric medicine.

title: Antipsychotic drugs: evolving mechanisms of action with improved therapeutic benefits.
abstract: The introduction of atypical antipsychotic agents has revolutionized the treatment of schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, offering enhanced efficacy with reduced extrapyramidal side effects compared to conventional dopamine D2 receptor antagonists. This review examines the evolving understanding of antipsychotic mechanisms and their therapeutic implications. We analyzed preclinical and clinical data from 1995-2003 regarding receptor binding profiles, neurotransmitter interactions, and clinical outcomes of second-generation antipsychotics including clozapine, risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, and ziprasidone. Atypical antipsychotics demonstrate complex pharmacological profiles involving serotonin 5-HT2A/2C antagonism, variable dopamine D2 occupancy, and interactions with histaminergic, adrenergic, and cholinergic systems. The 5-HT2A/D2 receptor occupancy ratio appears critical for the atypical profile, with optimal therapeutic windows of 65-80% 5-HT2A and 60-80% D2 occupancy. These agents show superior efficacy against negative symptoms and cognitive deficits, with reduced liability for tardive dyskinesia and hyperprolactinemia. Emerging evidence suggests modulation of glutamatergic transmission and neuroprotective effects may contribute to their therapeutic advantages. Fast dissociation from D2 receptors, as demonstrated by clozapine and quetiapine, correlates with reduced extrapyramidal symptoms. The evolving mechanistic understanding of atypical antipsychotics reveals multireceptor targeting as key to their improved therapeutic profile. Future drug development should focus on optimizing receptor selectivity patterns and exploring novel targets including metabotropic glutamate and nicotinic receptors to further enhance treatment outcomes in psychotic disorders.
journal: Current drug targets. CNS and neurological disorders

title: A primer on confidence intervals in psychopharmacology.
abstract: The interpretation of clinical trial results in psychopharmacology relies heavily on statistical significance testing, yet confidence intervals (CIs) provide crucial additional information often overlooked in practice. This educational review examines the application and interpretation of confidence intervals in psychopharmacology research, highlighting their advantages over sole reliance on P-values. We analyzed 200 randomized controlled trials published in major psychiatry journals between 2010-2014, evaluating reporting practices and interpretation of statistical findings. Additionally, we present worked examples using data from recent antidepressant and antipsychotic trials to illustrate proper CI interpretation. Our analysis revealed that only 34% of reviewed studies reported confidence intervals for primary efficacy outcomes, and fewer than 12% adequately discussed clinical significance in relation to CI boundaries. Studies reporting CIs were more likely to provide nuanced interpretations of treatment effects and discuss clinical relevance of observed differences. Through practical examples, we demonstrate how 95% CIs reveal the precision of treatment effect estimates, help distinguish between statistically significant and clinically meaningful differences, and provide insights into study power that P-values alone cannot convey. For instance, a statistically significant 2.1-point improvement on the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale with a 95% CI of 0.2-4.0 suggests uncertain clinical benefit, whereas a CI of 1.8-2.4 indicates more precise estimation of a modest but consistent effect. Confidence intervals should be routinely reported and interpreted alongside P-values in psychopharmacology research to enhance clinical decision-making and improve the translation of statistical findings into clinical practice.

title: How human electrophysiology informs psychopharmacology: from bottom-up driven processing to top-down control.
abstract: Recent advances in human electrophysiology have provided unprecedented insights into the neural mechanisms underlying psychopharmacological interventions, bridging the gap between cellular-level drug actions and complex behavioral outcomes. This review synthesizes current knowledge of how pharmacological agents modulate cortical information processing, from sensory-driven responses to executive control networks. We examined electrophysiological data from 47 studies investigating acute and chronic effects of major psychoactive drug classes, including dopaminergic, serotonergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic compounds, using event-related potentials (ERPs), steady-state responses, and spectral analyses in healthy volunteers and clinical populations. Bottom-up sensory processing, indexed by early ERP components (P50, N100, P300), showed consistent modulation by dopamine D2 antagonists and NMDA receptor modulators, with attenuation of sensory gating deficits in schizophrenia patients following antipsychotic treatment. Top-down control mechanisms, reflected in late ERP components and frontal theta oscillations, were selectively enhanced by noradrenergic and cholinergic agents, correlating with improved cognitive performance. Serotonergic manipulations primarily affected emotional processing networks, as evidenced by altered P300 amplitudes to affective stimuli. Cross-frequency coupling analyses revealed that pharmacological interventions restore disrupted communication between cortical networks in psychiatric disorders. These findings demonstrate that human electrophysiology provides critical translational biomarkers for drug development, enabling mechanistic understanding of therapeutic efficacy and facilitating personalized treatment approaches. Integration of electrophysiological measures into clinical trials may accelerate identification of novel therapeutic targets and optimize dosing strategies for neuropsychiatric medications.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Pharmacotherapy of alcohol dependence: a review of the clinical data.
abstract: Alcohol dependence represents a major public health concern affecting millions worldwide, yet pharmacological treatment options remain limited. Current therapeutic approaches have shown modest efficacy, highlighting the need for comprehensive evaluation of available agents. This review systematically examines clinical trial data for FDA-approved medications and emerging pharmacotherapies in alcohol dependence treatment. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of randomized controlled trials published between 1994-2004, focusing on naltrexone, acamprosate, and disulfiram, as well as investigational compounds including topiramate, ondansetron, and baclofen. Primary outcome measures included relapse rates, time to first drink, percentage of drinking days, and treatment retention. Naltrexone demonstrated consistent efficacy in reducing heavy drinking episodes and craving intensity across multiple studies, with effect sizes ranging from 0.2-0.4. Acamprosate showed superior efficacy in maintaining abstinence, particularly in European trials, with 13-23% improvement over placebo in abstinence rates at 6 months. Disulfiram effectiveness was highly dependent on supervised administration and patient compliance. Among investigational agents, topiramate showed promising results in reducing drinking frequency and promoting abstinence, while ondansetron demonstrated selective efficacy in early-onset alcoholics. Treatment response varied significantly based on patient characteristics, including genetic polymorphisms in opioid and dopamine systems, drinking patterns, and psychosocial factors. Current pharmacotherapies provide meaningful but modest benefits for alcohol dependence treatment. Optimal outcomes likely require personalized approaches combining medications with psychosocial interventions. Future research should focus on identifying predictors of treatment response and developing novel therapeutic targets.

title: The discovery of chemical neurotransmitters.
abstract: The identification and characterization of chemical neurotransmitters represents one of the most fundamental advances in modern neuroscience, yet the historical progression of these discoveries has not been comprehensively analyzed from a neuropharmacological perspective. We conducted a systematic review of primary literature spanning 1904-1980 to examine the methodological evolution underlying neurotransmitter discovery and validation. Key discoveries were analyzed chronologically, including acetylcholine (Dale, 1914; Loewi, 1921), norepinephrine (von Euler, 1946), dopamine (Carlsson, 1958), serotonin (Brodie & Shore, 1957), and GABA (Roberts & Frankel, 1950). We evaluated the experimental approaches that established the classical criteria for neurotransmitter identification: anatomical localization, calcium-dependent release, receptor binding, and physiological mimicry through exogenous application. Our analysis reveals three distinct methodological phases: early bioassay-dependent studies (1904-1940), the introduction of neurochemical techniques (1940-1965), and the emergence of receptor pharmacology (1965-1980). The acetylcholine-atropine and norepinephrine-phenoxybenzamine paradigms established the foundation for competitive antagonist studies, while the development of reserpine and monoamine oxidase inhibitors provided critical pharmacological tools for catecholamine research. Notably, the discovery timeline correlates strongly with technological advances in chromatography, fluorescence microscopy, and radioligand binding assays. These historical insights demonstrate that neurotransmitter discovery was fundamentally dependent on parallel advances in analytical chemistry and pharmacological methodology. Understanding this progression provides essential context for contemporary neurotransmitter research and highlights the continuing importance of technological innovation in neuropharmacological discovery.

title: New approaches to chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting: from neuropharmacology to clinical investigations.
abstract: Chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) remains one of the most distressing side effects of cancer treatment, significantly impacting patient quality of life and treatment compliance. Despite advances in antiemetic therapy, breakthrough nausea and delayed-phase vomiting continue to affect 30-60% of patients receiving moderately to highly emetogenic chemotherapy regimens. Recent neuropharmacological research has identified novel therapeutic targets beyond the traditional 5-HT3 and NK1 receptor pathways. This review examines emerging mechanisms including GABA-ergic modulation in the chemoreceptor trigger zone, cannabinoid CB1/CB2 receptor activation, and ghrelin receptor antagonism in the dorsal vagal complex. We analyzed preclinical studies using cisplatin-induced pica models in rats and ferret vomiting paradigms, alongside Phase II clinical trial data from 847 patients across six multicenter studies conducted between 2003-2005. Novel compounds targeting metabotropic glutamate receptor 5 (mGluR5) demonstrated 40-65% reduction in acute emetic episodes compared to standard ondansetron therapy in animal models. Preliminary human trials of the mGluR5 antagonist MPEP showed significant improvement in delayed nausea scores (p<0.001) and reduced rescue antiemetic use by 45% when combined with standard prophylaxis. Dual neurokinin receptor modulators and peripherally-restricted cannabinoid agonists also showed promise in early-phase studies. These findings suggest that multi-targeted approaches exploiting diverse neurotransmitter systems may overcome current therapeutic limitations. Further investigation of these mechanisms could lead to more effective, personalized antiemetic strategies for cancer patients receiving emetogenic chemotherapy.

title: Stereochemistry in clinical medicine: a neurological perspective.
abstract: The stereochemical properties of pharmaceutical compounds have profound implications for neurological therapeutics, yet clinical appreciation of these effects remains limited. Stereoisomers of neurotropic drugs can exhibit dramatically different pharmacokinetic profiles, receptor affinities, and therapeutic indices, necessitating careful consideration in clinical practice. This review examines the stereochemical basis of drug action in neurological disorders and analyzes current prescribing patterns across major therapeutic classes. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of stereochemical studies in neuropharmacology from 1980-2002, supplemented by analysis of prescribing data from 847 neurological patients treated at three tertiary care centers. Key therapeutic areas examined included anticonvulsants, antiparkinsonian agents, antidepressants, and analgesics. Our analysis revealed that 73% of commonly prescribed neurological medications contain chiral centers, yet only 42% are administered as single enantiomers. Notable examples include the superior efficacy of S-citalopram over racemic citalopram in depression, the reduced toxicity profile of levetiracetam's pure enantiomer compared to its racemate, and the contrasting pharmacological profiles of dextro- versus levo-amphetamine in attention disorders. Clinical outcomes analysis demonstrated 23% fewer adverse effects and 31% improved therapeutic response rates when pure enantiomers were utilized compared to racemic mixtures. These findings underscore the critical importance of stereochemical considerations in neurological prescribing practices. Enhanced awareness of stereochemical principles among clinicians could significantly improve therapeutic outcomes while reducing medication-related morbidity in neurological patients.
journal: Journal of clinical neuroscience : official journal of the Neurosurgical Society of Australasia

title: Clinical practice with anti-dementia drugs: A revised (third) consensus statement from the British Association for Psychopharmacology.
abstract: Current clinical practice with anti-dementia drugs remains heterogeneous despite established national guidelines, necessitating updated consensus recommendations from expert practitioners. The British Association for Psychopharmacology convened a multidisciplinary expert panel to review contemporary evidence and develop revised clinical practice guidelines for anti-dementia medications. A comprehensive literature review was conducted covering publications from 2012-2016, supplemented by expert consensus methodology involving structured discussions among 15 specialists in old age psychiatry, neurology, and clinical pharmacology. The panel evaluated evidence for acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) and memantine across different dementia subtypes, considering efficacy, tolerability, and practical implementation issues. Key recommendations include: initiation of acetylcholinesterase inhibitors in mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease should follow standardized protocols with systematic monitoring using validated cognitive and functional assessments; memantine demonstrates benefit as monotherapy in moderate-to-severe Alzheimer's disease and as combination therapy with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors; treatment discontinuation should be considered only after careful assessment of individual risk-benefit ratios; dose optimization strategies should account for patient-specific factors including comorbidities and concomitant medications; emerging evidence supports limited use of these agents in dementia with Lewy bodies and Parkinson's disease dementia, though with enhanced monitoring for adverse effects. The consensus emphasizes individualized treatment approaches, systematic outcome monitoring, and regular treatment review. These updated recommendations provide evidence-based guidance for optimizing anti-dementia drug therapy in routine clinical practice while acknowledging limitations in current therapeutic options.

title: Psychoneuroimmunology meets neuropsychopharmacology: translational implications of the impact of inflammation on behavior.
abstract: Growing evidence implicates inflammatory processes in the pathophysiology of psychiatric disorders, yet the mechanistic links between peripheral immune activation and central nervous system dysfunction remain poorly understood. We investigated the translational relevance of inflammation-induced behavioral changes using complementary approaches in rodent models and human subjects. Male C57BL/6 mice received lipopolysaccharide (LPS; 0.83 mg/kg, i.p.) or saline, followed by behavioral testing and neurochemical analysis. Parallel studies examined cytokine levels and mood symptoms in 45 healthy adults receiving typhoid vaccination versus saline control in a double-blind, randomized design. LPS administration in mice produced significant increases in immobility time in the forced swim test (p<0.001) and reduced sucrose preference (p<0.01), accompanied by elevated IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-6 in plasma and hippocampus. Microdialysis revealed decreased extracellular dopamine in the nucleus accumbens (-32%, p<0.05) and reduced serotonin in the prefrontal cortex (-28%, p<0.05) 4-6 hours post-LPS. These neurochemical changes were prevented by pretreatment with the anti-inflammatory agent minocycline (50 mg/kg). In human subjects, typhoid vaccination increased circulating IL-6 levels 3-fold (p<0.001) and induced significant mood deterioration and cognitive impairment on tasks of working memory and attention. Importantly, individual differences in IL-6 response predicted the magnitude of behavioral symptoms (r=0.67, p<0.001). These findings demonstrate conserved mechanisms linking immune activation to monoaminergic dysfunction and behavioral pathology across species, supporting the therapeutic potential of anti-inflammatory strategies in psychiatry and highlighting inflammation as a viable target for novel neuropsychopharmacological interventions.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Drugs used to treat Parkinson's disease, present status and future directions.
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) affects approximately 1% of individuals over 60 years of age and represents one of the most prevalent neurodegenerative disorders worldwide. Current pharmacological interventions primarily target the dopaminergic system to compensate for progressive loss of substantia nigra neurons, yet significant limitations persist regarding long-term efficacy and motor complications. This comprehensive review examined existing therapeutic approaches and emerging treatment strategies through systematic analysis of clinical trials, preclinical studies, and epidemiological data published between 1990-2008. We evaluated the efficacy, safety profiles, and mechanisms of action for established medications including levodopa, dopamine agonists, monoamine oxidase-B inhibitors, and catechol-O-methyltransferase inhibitors. Additionally, we assessed novel therapeutic targets including adenosine A2A receptor antagonists, glutamate modulators, and neuroprotective agents currently in clinical development. Our analysis reveals that while levodopa remains the gold standard for symptomatic relief, its long-term use is associated with motor fluctuations and dyskinesias in up to 50% of patients within 5-10 years of treatment. Dopamine agonists demonstrate comparable efficacy with reduced dyskinesia risk but increased psychiatric side effects. Emerging non-dopaminergic approaches show promise for addressing motor symptoms while potentially offering neuroprotective benefits. Future therapeutic directions should focus on combination strategies targeting multiple pathways, personalized medicine approaches based on genetic profiling, and disease-modifying treatments that address underlying neurodegeneration rather than solely providing symptomatic relief. Integration of these advances may significantly improve long-term outcomes for PD patients.

title: Diagnosis and management of chronic daily headache.
abstract: Chronic daily headache (CDH) affects approximately 4-5% of the global population and represents a significant burden on healthcare systems worldwide. The heterogeneous nature of CDH, encompassing chronic migraine, chronic tension-type headache, hemicrania continua, and new daily persistent headache, presents substantial diagnostic and therapeutic challenges. This comprehensive review synthesizes current evidence-based approaches to CDH diagnosis and management, analyzing data from 47 clinical trials and observational studies published between 2000-2010. We examined diagnostic criteria refinements, neuroimaging findings, and treatment outcomes across different CDH subtypes. Systematic evaluation revealed that medication overuse headache occurs in 60-80% of CDH patients, representing the primary modifiable risk factor. Neuroimaging studies demonstrate altered pain processing networks in the brainstem and thalamus, supporting central sensitization mechanisms. First-line preventive treatments include topiramate (50-100mg daily), amitriptyline (25-75mg daily), and propranolol (80-160mg daily), showing 50% response rates in 40-60% of patients. Botulinum toxin type A injections demonstrated significant efficacy in chronic migraine patients, with mean headache day reductions of 8.4 days per month compared to placebo. Behavioral interventions, including cognitive-behavioral therapy and biofeedback, provide additional therapeutic benefit when combined with pharmacotherapy. Multidisciplinary treatment approaches incorporating medication withdrawal protocols, preventive pharmacotherapy, and non-pharmacological interventions yield superior outcomes compared to monotherapy. Early recognition and appropriate management of CDH can substantially improve patient quality of life and reduce healthcare utilization, emphasizing the importance of specialized headache care.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of novel and selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists: WAY-181187 and WAY-208466.
abstract: The 5-HT6 receptor represents an emerging therapeutic target for cognitive enhancement and treatment of neurodegenerative disorders. While selective 5-HT6 receptor antagonists have been extensively characterized, few potent and selective agonists are available for research. We evaluated the neuropharmacological profiles of two novel compounds, WAY-181187 and WAY-208466, as potential selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists. Radioligand binding studies using [³H]LSD in HEK293 cells expressing human 5-HT6 receptors demonstrated high binding affinity for both compounds (Ki = 8.2 nM for WAY-181187; Ki = 12.7 nM for WAY-208466). Functional activity was assessed using cAMP accumulation assays, revealing full agonist activity at 5-HT6 receptors with EC50 values of 15.3 nM and 23.1 nM, respectively. Selectivity screening against 85 neurotransmitter receptors, ion channels, and transporters showed >100-fold selectivity for 5-HT6 receptors over other targets. In vivo microdialysis studies in freely moving rats demonstrated that both compounds (1-10 mg/kg, i.p.) dose-dependently increased acetylcholine release in the frontal cortex and hippocampus, effects blocked by the selective 5-HT6 antagonist SB-271046. Neither compound significantly altered extracellular dopamine, norepinephrine, or serotonin levels at behaviorally active doses. In the novel object recognition test, both WAY-181187 and WAY-208466 enhanced cognitive performance in young adult rats, with peak effects observed at 3 mg/kg. These findings establish WAY-181187 and WAY-208466 as highly selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists with procognitive properties, providing valuable pharmacological tools for investigating 5-HT6 receptor function and therapeutic potential.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: The roles of dopamine and serotonin in decision making: evidence from pharmacological experiments in humans.
abstract: Decision-making processes involve complex interactions between neurotransmitter systems, particularly dopamine and serotonin pathways. While animal studies have established fundamental roles for these systems in reward processing and behavioral control, their specific contributions to human decision-making remain incompletely understood. We conducted a double-blind, placebo-controlled study examining the effects of selective pharmacological manipulation of dopamine and serotonin systems on decision-making performance in healthy volunteers. Forty-eight participants completed three experimental sessions, receiving either placebo, the dopamine precursor L-DOPA (100mg), or the serotonin reuptake inhibitor citalopram (20mg) in randomized order. Decision-making was assessed using the Iowa Gambling Task (IGT) and a probabilistic reversal learning paradigm. Computational modeling was applied to extract learning rate parameters and decision consistency measures. L-DOPA administration significantly enhanced performance on the IGT, increasing advantageous deck selections by 23% compared to placebo (p<0.01) and improving learning rates for positive outcomes (p<0.05). Conversely, citalopram impaired reversal learning performance, reducing adaptability to contingency changes (p<0.01) while increasing perseverative responding. Computational analysis revealed that dopaminergic enhancement primarily affected reward prediction error signaling, whereas serotonergic modulation influenced behavioral flexibility and response inhibition. These findings provide direct evidence for dissociable roles of dopamine and serotonin in human decision-making: dopamine facilitates reward-based learning and approach behavior, while serotonin modulates behavioral control and adaptation to changing environmental contingencies. Understanding these neurotransmitter-specific contributions has important implications for treating decision-making impairments in psychiatric and neurological disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Neuroglialpharmacology: white matter pathophysiologies and psychiatric treatments.
abstract: Emerging evidence suggests that white matter abnormalities and glial dysfunction play critical roles in psychiatric disorders, challenging traditional neuron-centric models of mental illness. While most psychotropic medications target neuronal synapses, their therapeutic mechanisms may involve previously unrecognized effects on oligodendrocytes, astrocytes, and myelin integrity. We conducted a comprehensive review of white matter pathophysiology in major psychiatric disorders and examined potential glial targets for therapeutic intervention. Diffusion tensor imaging studies consistently demonstrate reduced fractional anisotropy and altered white matter microstructure in schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and major depression, particularly in fronto-limbic circuits. Post-mortem analyses reveal oligodendrocyte loss, decreased myelin basic protein expression, and disrupted myelin ultrastructure across these conditions. Preclinical studies demonstrate that chronic stress and inflammation impair oligodendrocyte maturation and myelination through dysregulation of neurotrophic factors and cytokine signaling. Notably, established psychiatric medications exhibit previously unappreciated effects on glial cells: lithium promotes oligodendrocyte survival and myelin gene expression, antipsychotics modulate glial glutamate transport, and antidepressants enhance oligodendrocyte precursor proliferation. Novel therapeutic approaches targeting white matter pathology show promise, including phosphodiesterase inhibitors that promote myelination and anti-inflammatory agents that protect oligodendrocytes from cytokine-induced damage. These findings support a paradigm shift toward "neuroglialpharmacology" - the development of treatments that simultaneously target neuronal and glial components of psychiatric pathophysiology. Understanding white matter dysfunction may lead to more effective therapeutic strategies and biomarkers for treatment response in psychiatric disorders.

title: Past and future of neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The field of neuropsychopharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation since its inception in the 1950s with the discovery of chlorpromazine's antipsychotic properties. This review examines the historical development of psychotropic medications and projects future directions based on emerging neuroscientific understanding. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of pharmaceutical developments from 1952-1989, reviewing clinical trials, mechanistic studies, and therapeutic outcomes across major drug classes including antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, and mood stabilizers. The evolution from serendipitous discoveries to rational drug design is traced through landmark medications: chlorpromazine and haloperidol for psychosis, imipramine and subsequent tricyclics for depression, benzodiazepines for anxiety, and lithium for bipolar disorder. Each class revealed fundamental neurotransmitter systems - dopaminergic, serotonergic, noradrenergic, and GABAergic - establishing the neurochemical basis of mental illness. Recent advances in receptor subtype characterization, second messenger systems, and brain imaging techniques now enable more targeted therapeutic approaches. Emerging areas include selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors showing improved tolerability profiles, atypical antipsychotics with reduced extrapyramidal effects, and novel anxiolytics acting beyond the benzodiazepine receptor. Future developments will likely focus on neuropeptide systems, glutamate modulation, and personalized medicine approaches based on genetic polymorphisms affecting drug metabolism. The integration of molecular biology, neuroimaging, and pharmacokinetic modeling promises more efficacious treatments with fewer adverse effects, representing a paradigm shift toward precision neuropsychopharmacology.

title: Two novel combined drug treatments for women with hypoactive sexual desire disorder.
abstract: Hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) affects approximately 10% of women and represents the most common form of female sexual dysfunction, yet effective pharmacological treatments remain limited. Current monotherapies targeting dopaminergic, noradrenergic, or melanocortin pathways have shown modest efficacy with significant side effects. This study investigated two novel combination therapies designed to synergistically enhance sexual motivation through complementary neurotransmitter systems. In a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, 180 premenopausal women diagnosed with HSDD received either placebo, bupropion (150mg) plus buspirone (10mg) twice daily (Combination A), or low-dose testosterone gel (300μg) plus bremelanotide nasal spray (1.25mg as needed) (Combination B) for 12 weeks. Primary outcomes included changes in sexual desire using the Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) desire domain and sexual distress via the Female Sexual Distress Scale-Revised. Secondary measures assessed sexual satisfaction, frequency of satisfying sexual events, and neurohormonal markers including plasma testosterone, prolactin, and cortisol. Both combination treatments significantly improved FSFI desire scores compared to placebo (Combination A: 3.8±0.7 vs 2.1±0.6, p<0.001; Combination B: 4.2±0.8 vs 2.1±0.6, p<0.001). Sexual distress decreased significantly in both active treatment groups. Combination B showed superior improvements in sexual satisfaction and arousal domains. Common adverse effects included mild nausea and headache, with no serious safety concerns. These findings suggest that rationally designed combination therapies targeting multiple neurochemical pathways may offer enhanced therapeutic efficacy for HSDD compared to monotherapy approaches.

title: Neuropharmacology of memory consolidation and reconsolidation: Insights on central cholinergic mechanisms.
abstract: Memory consolidation and reconsolidation represent critical phases in the formation and maintenance of long-term memories, yet the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying these processes remain incompletely understood. The cholinergic system, particularly through muscarinic and nicotinic receptor subtypes, has emerged as a key modulator of memory processing. Here, we investigated the differential roles of central cholinergic mechanisms in memory consolidation versus reconsolidation using a contextual fear conditioning paradigm in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received bilateral hippocampal or medial prefrontal cortex infusions of the muscarinic antagonist scopolamine (10 μg/side), the nicotinic antagonist mecamylamine (5 μg/side), or vehicle at specific time points relative to training or memory reactivation. Post-training scopolamine administration significantly impaired consolidation when delivered within 6 hours but not 24 hours after acquisition, demonstrating a time-dependent consolidation window. Conversely, scopolamine infusion immediately following memory reactivation disrupted subsequent recall, indicating cholinergic involvement in reconsolidation. Mecamylamine showed similar but less pronounced effects, with regional specificity favoring hippocampal over prefrontal involvement. Interestingly, co-administration of the acetylcholinesterase inhibitor physostigmine (0.5 μg/side) rescued scopolamine-induced impairments in both consolidation and reconsolidation phases. These findings demonstrate that central cholinergic signaling, primarily through muscarinic receptors, is essential for both the initial stabilization and subsequent restabilization of contextual memories. Our results provide novel insights into the temporal dynamics and receptor specificity of cholinergic modulation in memory processing, with potential implications for therapeutic interventions targeting memory-related disorders.

title: Towards a clinical methodology for neuropsychopharmacological research.
abstract: Neuropsychopharmacological research faces significant methodological challenges that limit the translation of preclinical findings to clinical applications. Current approaches often lack standardized protocols for cognitive assessment, biomarker validation, and patient stratification, resulting in inconsistent outcomes across studies. This review synthesizes methodological frameworks from 127 neuropsychopharmacological studies published between 2002-2006, identifying critical gaps in clinical trial design and proposing standardized approaches for future research. We analyzed study designs, outcome measures, statistical approaches, and reporting standards across major psychiatric and neurological conditions including depression, schizophrenia, and dementia. Key findings reveal substantial heterogeneity in cognitive battery selection (>40 different instruments used), inconsistent biomarker implementation (only 23% of studies included biological markers), and inadequate consideration of pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic relationships in 67% of reviewed trials. Placebo response rates varied dramatically (18-65%) across similar patient populations, suggesting insufficient attention to patient selection criteria and study duration optimization. We propose a standardized clinical methodology framework incorporating: (1) validated cognitive assessment batteries matched to specific neuropsychiatric domains, (2) mandatory inclusion of pharmacokinetic sampling and relevant biomarkers, (3) stratified randomization based on genetic polymorphisms affecting drug metabolism, and (4) adaptive trial designs allowing for interim efficacy analyses. Implementation of these methodological improvements could significantly enhance the reliability and translational value of neuropsychopharmacological research, ultimately accelerating the development of more effective therapeutic interventions for psychiatric and neurological disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacologia Hungarica : a Magyar Pszichofarmakologiai Egyesulet lapja = official journal of the Hungarian Association of Psychopharmacology

title: Neuropsychopharmacology at the new millennium: new industry directions.
abstract: The neuropharmaceutical industry stands at a critical juncture as we approach the new millennium, with traditional drug discovery paradigms yielding diminishing returns and emerging technologies offering unprecedented opportunities for innovation. This review examines current industry trends and forecasts future directions in neuropsychopharmacology based on comprehensive analysis of pharmaceutical pipeline data, patent filings, and R&D investment patterns from 1995-1999. We surveyed 47 major pharmaceutical companies and biotechnology firms to assess strategic priorities, technological adoption, and resource allocation in CNS drug development. Our analysis reveals a fundamental shift away from serendipitous drug discovery toward rational, target-based approaches. Genomics-driven strategies now account for 34% of early-stage CNS programs, compared to 8% in 1995. High-throughput screening methodologies have increased compound evaluation capacity 50-fold, while combinatorial chemistry has expanded chemical diversity exponentially. Investment in neurodegenerative disease research has grown 180%, reflecting demographic pressures and improved understanding of molecular pathogenesis. Concurrently, psychiatric drug development increasingly emphasizes novel receptor subtypes and non-monoaminergic targets, with 23 distinct mechanisms currently in Phase II/III trials. Pharmacogenomic applications are emerging, with genetic screening protocols implemented in 15% of current CNS studies. These data indicate that neuropsychopharmacology is entering an era of unprecedented molecular precision and therapeutic specificity. Success in the coming decade will depend on effective integration of genomic technologies, innovative clinical trial designs, and collaborative academia-industry partnerships to address the growing global burden of neuropsychiatric disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Neurobiology and neuroimmunology of Tourette's syndrome: an update.
abstract: Tourette's syndrome (TS) is a complex neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by chronic motor and vocal tics, with emerging evidence suggesting significant neuroimmune dysfunction. This review synthesizes recent advances in understanding the neurobiological and neuroimmunological mechanisms underlying TS pathophysiology. We examined current literature on cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuit abnormalities, neurotransmitter dysregulation, and immune system involvement in TS. Neuroimaging studies consistently demonstrate altered dopaminergic transmission within the basal ganglia, particularly reduced D2 receptor binding in the caudate nucleus and putamen. Post-mortem analyses reveal decreased interneuron populations and altered GABA-ergic signaling in striatal regions. Compelling evidence now implicates autoimmune mechanisms, with elevated anti-neuronal antibodies detected in subsets of TS patients, particularly those with post-infectious exacerbations. Molecular mimicry between streptococcal antigens and basal ganglia proteins appears to trigger cross-reactive immune responses, leading to neuroinflammation and tic symptomatology. Cytokine profiling demonstrates elevated pro-inflammatory markers including TNF-α, IL-12, and IL-17 in TS patients compared to controls. Additionally, genetic studies have identified susceptibility loci affecting both neurotransmitter metabolism and immune function, including variants in dopamine transporter and complement pathway genes. These findings suggest TS emerges from complex interactions between genetic vulnerability, environmental triggers, and aberrant neuroimmune responses. Understanding these mechanisms has important therapeutic implications, as immunomodulatory treatments show promise in treatment-refractory cases. Future research should focus on identifying biomarkers for immune-mediated TS subtypes and developing targeted neuroimmune interventions.
journal: Cellular and molecular life sciences : CMLS

title: NMDA antagonists: antiepileptic-neuroprotective drugs with diversified neuropharmacological profiles.
abstract: The N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor has emerged as a critical target for therapeutic intervention in epilepsy and neurodegeneration. Excessive glutamatergic neurotransmission mediated by NMDA receptors contributes to seizure propagation and excitotoxic neuronal death. This study evaluated the antiepileptic and neuroprotective properties of three structurally distinct NMDA antagonists: MK-801 (dizocilpine), CPP, and memantine in both in vitro and in vivo models. Antiepileptic activity was assessed using maximal electroshock seizure (MES) and pentylenetetrazol (PTZ) tests in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-12 per group). Neuroprotection was evaluated in primary cortical cell cultures exposed to glutamate excitotoxicity and in a rat model of middle cerebral artery occlusion. NMDA receptor binding affinity and ion channel properties were characterized using radioligand binding assays and patch-clamp electrophysiology. All three compounds demonstrated dose-dependent anticonvulsant activity, with MK-801 showing the lowest ED50 values (0.3 mg/kg, MES; 0.8 mg/kg, PTZ). In vitro, compounds reduced glutamate-induced neuronal death by 45-78% at concentrations of 1-10 μM. In the ischemia model, pretreatment with NMDA antagonists reduced infarct volume by 35-60%. Electrophysiological analysis revealed distinct channel blocking kinetics: MK-801 exhibited use-dependent, voltage-independent block, while memantine showed voltage-dependent, rapidly reversible inhibition. These findings demonstrate that NMDA receptor antagonists possess diversified pharmacological profiles that confer both antiepileptic and neuroprotective properties, suggesting their potential therapeutic utility in various neurological disorders involving glutamate-mediated excitotoxicity.

title: Beyond the reward hypothesis: alternative functions of nucleus accumbens dopamine.
abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAc) has been traditionally viewed as a critical component of the brain's reward circuitry, with dopaminergic signaling mediating hedonic responses and reinforcement learning. However, accumulating evidence suggests that NAc dopamine serves broader functions beyond simple reward processing. We conducted a comprehensive review of recent electrophysiological, neurochemical, and behavioral studies examining dopamine release patterns in the NAc across diverse behavioral contexts. Microdialysis and fast-scan cyclic voltammetry data reveal that NAc dopamine neurons respond not only to rewarding stimuli, but also to aversive events, novel stimuli, and behavioral switching paradigms. Single-unit recordings demonstrate that dopamine release in the NAc core and shell subregions correlates with movement initiation, effort-based decision making, and attention allocation rather than solely with hedonic valuation. Pharmacological manipulations using selective D1 and D2 receptor antagonists indicate that NAc dopamine is necessary for behavioral activation and the energizing of goal-directed behaviors, independent of their rewarding properties. Lesion studies further support a role for NAc dopamine in processing salient stimuli regardless of valence and in facilitating adaptive responses to environmental changes. These findings suggest that NAc dopamine functions as a general motivational signal that promotes behavioral engagement and facilitates learning about predictive relationships in the environment. Understanding these alternative functions has important implications for interpreting dopamine's role in psychiatric disorders and developing more targeted therapeutic interventions for conditions involving motivational dysfunction.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of an atypical antipsychotic, NRA0562.
abstract: The development of atypical antipsychotics with improved efficacy and reduced extrapyramidal side effects remains a critical goal in schizophrenia treatment. NRA0562, a novel benzisoxazole derivative, was characterized for its neuropharmacological properties and compared to risperidone and haloperidol. Radioligand binding assays were performed using rat brain membranes to determine receptor affinities. In vivo studies employed conditioned avoidance response (CAR), catalepsy, and apomorphine-induced stereotypy paradigms in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Microdialysis was used to measure dopamine and serotonin levels in the prefrontal cortex and striatum following acute administration. NRA0562 demonstrated high affinity for dopamine D2 (Ki = 2.1 nM) and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors (Ki = 0.8 nM) with a 5-HT2A/D2 ratio of 0.38. The compound showed moderate affinity for α1-adrenergic (Ki = 15 nM) and histamine H1 receptors (Ki = 45 nM), with minimal binding to muscarinic receptors. In behavioral studies, NRA0562 effectively blocked CAR (ED50 = 0.25 mg/kg) and apomorphine-induced stereotypy (ED50 = 0.18 mg/kg) while producing minimal catalepsy at therapeutic doses, yielding a favorable separation index of 12.8 compared to haloperidol (2.1). Microdialysis revealed preferential enhancement of cortical dopamine release over striatal dopamine, consistent with atypical antipsychotic properties. These findings suggest NRA0562 possesses a promising neuropharmacological profile with potential for treating positive and negative symptoms of schizophrenia while minimizing motor side effects, warranting further clinical investigation.

title: Effect of short-term intake of high- and low-concentrations of sucrose solution on the neurochemistry of male and female mice.
abstract: Dietary sugar intake has been linked to alterations in brain neurochemistry and behavior, yet the acute effects of different sucrose concentrations on neurotransmitter systems remain poorly understood. This study investigated the short-term neurochemical responses to high- and low-concentration sucrose solutions in male and female C57BL/6J mice. Adult mice (n=48, equal sex distribution) were randomly assigned to receive either 5% (low), 20% (high), or 0% (control) sucrose solutions for 7 days via drinking bottles. High-performance liquid chromatography was used to quantify dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, and their metabolites in the prefrontal cortex, striatum, and hippocampus. Plasma glucose and corticosterone levels were also measured. High-concentration sucrose significantly increased striatal dopamine levels in both sexes (p<0.01), with males showing greater elevations than females. Serotonin concentrations decreased in the hippocampus following high sucrose intake in females only (p<0.05), while prefrontal cortex norepinephrine increased in males receiving both sucrose concentrations (p<0.05). Low-concentration sucrose produced minimal neurochemical changes compared to controls. Plasma corticosterone was elevated in all sucrose-treated groups, with no sex differences observed. These findings demonstrate that even short-term exposure to high-concentration sucrose solutions induces rapid, sex-specific alterations in brain monoamine neurotransmitter systems. The differential responses between males and females suggest that sex hormones may modulate sugar-induced neurochemical adaptations, providing insight into the early neurobiological mechanisms underlying sugar consumption effects on brain function.

title: Commentary: physician-scientist's frustrations fester.
abstract: The declining pipeline of physician-scientists in neuropharmacology research represents a critical threat to translational medicine and drug development. This commentary examines the mounting frustrations experienced by clinician-researchers attempting to maintain dual careers in patient care and basic neuroscience research. We surveyed 247 physician-scientists across 18 academic medical centers between 2007-2008, focusing on career satisfaction, funding challenges, and institutional support. Structured interviews were conducted with department chairs and senior faculty to identify systemic barriers. Results revealed that 68% of respondents reported decreased research productivity over the past five years, with 43% considering abandoning research entirely. Primary frustrations included inadequate protected research time (87%), declining NIH funding success rates (74%), and insufficient institutional startup packages (62%). Notably, physician-scientists spent an average of 23% more time on administrative tasks compared to PhD researchers, while generating comparable grant funding and publications. Early-career physician-scientists (within 10 years of residency) were particularly vulnerable, with 56% expressing regret about their career choice. Department chairs cited patient care revenue pressures and reduced research space allocation as primary institutional constraints. The data demonstrate a perfect storm of factors undermining physician-scientist careers: compressed training timelines, delayed research independence, funding uncertainty, and competing clinical demands. Without systematic interventions including enhanced mentorship programs, improved salary support mechanisms, and institutional commitment to protected research time, the neuropharmacology field risks losing its essential translational workforce, potentially devastating future therapeutic advances for neuropsychiatric disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: In-vivo brain dialysis of neurotransmitters.
abstract: In-vivo brain microdialysis has emerged as a powerful technique for monitoring extracellular neurotransmitter concentrations in the brains of freely-moving animals. This method represents a significant advancement over traditional approaches such as brain homogenization and push-pull perfusion, offering improved spatial resolution and reduced tissue damage. We implanted concentric dialysis probes (2 mm membrane length, 20,000 Da cutoff) into the striatum and nucleus accumbens of male Sprague-Dawley rats and perfused artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 2 μL/min.
