Dialysate samples were collected at 20-minute intervals and analyzed for dopamine, serotonin, and their metabolites using high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection. Basal extracellular dopamine concentrations averaged 8.2 ± 1.4 nM in striatum and 4.6 ± 0.8 nM in nucleus accumbens. Administration of amphetamine (2 mg/kg, i.p.) produced a 15-fold increase in striatal dopamine within 40 minutes, while haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg, i.p.) elevated dopamine metabolite levels by 300% over 2 hours. Local perfusion of high-potassium solution (100 mM KCl) through the probe increased dopamine release 8-fold, confirming the neuronal origin of measured transmitters. Recovery studies using standard solutions indicated 12-15% probe efficiency for monoamines. These findings demonstrate that microdialysis provides reliable, real-time measurements of neurotransmitter dynamics with minimal behavioral disruption, establishing this technique as an invaluable tool for neuropharmacological research and drug development.

title: [Atypical antipsychotic drugs].
abstract:
The introduction of atypical antipsychotic drugs has fundamentally changed the treatment of schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders. Unlike conventional neuroleptics that primarily block dopamine D2 receptors, atypical antipsychotics demonstrate a broader receptor binding profile with reduced risk of extrapyramidal side effects. This review examines the pharmacological properties, clinical efficacy, and safety profiles of the major atypical antipsychotics available in clinical practice.

We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published clinical trials and pharmacokinetic studies of clozapine, risperidone, olanzapine, and quetiapine from 1988 to 1999. Data were extracted from randomized controlled trials comparing atypical agents with conventional antipsychotics and placebo in patients with schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder, and treatment-resistant psychosis. Primary endpoints included positive and negative symptom reduction measured by PANSS and BPRS scales, and incidence of movement disorders.

Atypical antipsychotics demonstrated superior efficacy in treating negative symptoms compared to haloperidol and other conventional agents. Clozapine showed particular benefit in treatment-resistant cases, with 30-60% response rates in previously refractory patients. The incidence of tardive dyskinesia was significantly reduced across all atypical agents (0.5-2% annually versus 5-7% with conventional drugs). However, metabolic side effects including weight gain, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia emerged as significant concerns, particularly with clozapine and olanzapine.

Atypical antipsychotics represent a major therapeutic advance, offering improved symptom control with reduced neurological side effects. However, careful monitoring for metabolic complications is essential. The improved tolerability profile may enhance long-term treatment adherence and functional outcomes in patients with chronic psychotic disorders.

title: Pharmacotherapy in children and adolescents with pervasive developmental disorders.
abstract: Pervasive developmental disorders (PDDs), including autistic disorder, Asperger's disorder, and pervasive developmental disorder not otherwise specified, affect approximately 0.7-2.1 per 1,000 children and present significant challenges in behavioral management and educational programming. While no medications directly treat the core symptoms of PDDs, pharmacological interventions may address associated behavioral symptoms including hyperactivity, aggression, self-injurious behaviors, and repetitive stereotypies. This retrospective chart review examined pharmacotherapy patterns and outcomes in 127 children and adolescents (ages 3-17 years, mean age 8.4 years) diagnosed with PDDs who received psychiatric services at three university-affiliated clinics between 1995-1998. Data were collected on medication types, dosages, duration of treatment, side effects, and clinical response as measured by the Clinical Global Impression-Improvement scale and standardized behavioral rating scales completed by parents and teachers. Results indicated that 78% of patients received at least one psychotropic medication during the study period. Stimulants were most frequently prescribed (42% of medicated patients), followed by selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (31%), typical antipsychotics (24%), and anticonvulsants (18%). Modest improvements in hyperactivity and aggression were observed in 65% and 58% of cases, respectively, while stereotypic behaviors showed limited response to pharmacological intervention. Common side effects included appetite suppression, sleep disturbances, and sedation. These findings suggest that while pharmacotherapy can provide symptomatic relief for certain behavioral manifestations in children with PDDs, careful monitoring and realistic expectations are essential components of comprehensive treatment planning.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychiatric nursing : official publication of the Association of Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Nurses, Inc

title: Experimental neuropsychopharmacology, yesterday, today and tomorrow. A conversation with Michel Hamon.
abstract: Experimental neuropsychopharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation over the past five decades, evolving from crude neurochemical approaches to sophisticated molecular and systems-level methodologies. This retrospective analysis examines the historical trajectory and future directions of the field through comprehensive review of methodological advances and paradigm shifts. We conducted a systematic evaluation of key developments spanning 1970-2021, analyzing the transition from early monoamine hypotheses to contemporary circuit-based approaches, with particular emphasis on serotonergic system research. Historical milestones include the development of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, advances in receptor subtype characterization, and the integration of behavioral, electrophysiological, and molecular techniques. Contemporary approaches encompass optogenetics, chemogenetics, single-cell RNA sequencing, and real-time neurochemical monitoring, enabling unprecedented spatiotemporal resolution in understanding neurotransmitter function. The field has progressed from reductionist models focusing on individual neurotransmitter systems to integrated network analyses incorporating genetic, epigenetic, and environmental factors. Current challenges include translating preclinical findings to clinical applications, addressing inter-individual variability, and developing personalized therapeutic strategies. Future directions emphasize the convergence of artificial intelligence, multi-omics approaches, and precision medicine paradigms. The evolution toward systems neuropsychopharmacology promises enhanced understanding of psychiatric disorders' complexity, potentially revolutionizing therapeutic development. This historical perspective underscores the field's dynamic nature and highlights emerging opportunities for advancing both fundamental knowledge and clinical applications in neuropsychiatric medicine.

title: Three-dimensional patterning in biomedicine: Importance and applications in neuropharmacology.
abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) patterning technologies have emerged as transformative tools in neuropharmacology, offering unprecedented control over cellular microenvironments and drug delivery systems. Traditional two-dimensional culture models inadequately recapitulate the complex architecture of neural tissues, limiting the translation of pharmacological findings to clinical applications. This review examines recent advances in 3D biomaterial patterning techniques and their specific applications in neuropharmacological research. We analyzed published literature from 2010-2018 focusing on 3D printing, bioprinting, microfluidics, and scaffold-based approaches for creating physiologically relevant neural models. Key parameters evaluated included spatial resolution, biocompatibility, drug diffusion characteristics, and cellular response reproducibility. Our analysis reveals that 3D-patterned systems demonstrate superior drug penetration profiles compared to conventional models, with up to 40% improved correlation with in vivo pharmacokinetics. Bioprinted neural constructs incorporating multiple cell types showed enhanced synaptic connectivity and more predictable dose-response relationships for neuroactive compounds. Microfluidic devices enabled precise concentration gradients and real-time monitoring of drug effects on neural networks. Scaffold-based systems provided sustained release profiles crucial for chronic neurological conditions. However, standardization challenges and manufacturing costs remain significant barriers to widespread adoption. These findings underscore the critical importance of 3D patterning in advancing neuropharmacological research, particularly for drug screening, toxicity assessment, and personalized medicine applications. Future developments in biocompatible materials and automated fabrication processes will likely accelerate clinical translation of these technologies.
journal: Journal of biomedical materials research. Part B, Applied biomaterials

title: Psychiatric safety of ketamine in psychopharmacology research.
abstract: **Background:** Ketamine, an NMDA receptor antagonist, has gained increasing attention as a research tool in psychopharmacology studies investigating glutamatergic mechanisms of psychiatric disorders. However, concerns regarding ketamine's psychiatric safety profile, particularly its potential to induce transient psychotomimetic effects and dissociative symptoms, have raised questions about its appropriate use in research populations. **Objective:** To systematically evaluate the psychiatric safety profile of ketamine administration in controlled psychopharmacology research settings. **Methods:** We conducted a comprehensive review of 47 published studies (n=1,247 subjects) utilizing ketamine in psychopharmacology research between 1995-2006. Studies employed subanesthetic doses (0.1-0.5 mg/kg IV bolus or infusion) in healthy volunteers and psychiatric patients. Primary safety outcomes included psychotic symptoms, dissociative effects, mood changes, and serious adverse events requiring intervention. **Results:** Transient perceptual alterations and mild dissociative symptoms were reported in 68% of subjects, typically resolving within 2 hours post-administration. Clinically significant psychotic symptoms occurred in 3.2% of healthy volunteers and 8.1% of psychiatric patients, with no episodes lasting beyond 24 hours. Two subjects (0.16%) required anxiolytic intervention for severe anxiety. No instances of persistent psychosis, suicidal ideation, or psychiatric hospitalization were observed. Pre-existing psychotic disorders and higher doses (>0.4 mg/kg) were associated with increased risk of adverse psychiatric effects. **Conclusions:** When administered under appropriate medical supervision with proper subject screening, ketamine demonstrates an acceptable psychiatric safety profile for psychopharmacology research. Careful exclusion of high-risk populations and adherence to established dosing protocols are essential for minimizing psychiatric adverse events.

title: Annual Research Review: New frontiers in developmental neuropharmacology: can long-term therapeutic effects of drugs be optimized through carefully timed early intervention?
abstract: Mounting evidence suggests that the developing brain exhibits unique periods of heightened neuroplasticity that may be leveraged to optimize long-term therapeutic outcomes through strategically timed pharmacological interventions. This review examines emerging research on critical developmental windows and their implications for pediatric psychopharmacology. We systematically reviewed preclinical and clinical studies published between 2005-2011 investigating age-dependent drug effects, focusing on interventions targeting neurodevelopmental disorders including ADHD, autism spectrum disorders, and early-onset mood disorders. Preclinical studies consistently demonstrate that identical pharmacological agents produce markedly different long-term neurobiological and behavioral outcomes when administered during specific developmental epochs versus later time points. For example, early selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor exposure during critical periods of serotonergic system maturation (postnatal weeks 2-3 in rodents) results in persistent alterations in anxiety-like behavior and stress responsivity that differ substantially from adult treatment effects. Clinical data, while more limited, suggest similar phenomena in human populations. Early intervention with stimulant medications during preschool years appears to produce more robust and sustained improvements in executive function compared to treatment initiation during school age. Conversely, some interventions show enhanced efficacy when delayed until after critical developmental transitions. These findings suggest that optimizing pediatric pharmacotherapy requires moving beyond simple dose-scaling approaches to embrace developmentally-informed treatment strategies. Future research should prioritize identification of biomarkers for critical periods and development of age-specific therapeutic protocols that capitalize on developmental neuroplasticity while minimizing potential disruption of normal maturation processes.
journal: Journal of child psychology and psychiatry, and allied disciplines

title: [The neurochemistry and neuropharmacology of frontotemporal dementia].
abstract: Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) represents a heterogeneous group of neurodegenerative disorders characterized by progressive deterioration of frontal and temporal cortices, leading to behavioral, executive, and language dysfunction. Despite growing clinical recognition, the underlying neurochemical mechanisms and therapeutic targets remain poorly understood. This review examines current knowledge of neurotransmitter systems alterations and pharmacological interventions in FTD. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published literature from 1995-2005, focusing on neurochemical findings from post-mortem studies, neuroimaging investigations, and clinical trials in FTD patients. Neurochemical studies reveal significant disruptions in multiple neurotransmitter systems, particularly reduced cholinergic function in frontal regions, with acetylcholinesterase activity decreased by 30-50% compared to controls. Serotonergic dysfunction is prominent, with decreased 5-HT2A receptor binding and reduced serotonin transporter availability correlating with behavioral symptoms. Dopaminergic abnormalities include striatal D2 receptor downregulation and altered dopamine metabolism in prefrontal areas. GABA-ergic interneuron loss contributes to disinhibition patterns. Pharmacological interventions show limited efficacy compared to Alzheimer's disease treatments. Cholinesterase inhibitors demonstrate modest benefits in some behavioral symptoms but lack consistent cognitive improvement. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show promise for managing behavioral disturbances, particularly disinhibition and compulsive behaviors. Atypical antipsychotics provide symptomatic relief but require careful monitoring due to sensitivity in this population. These findings highlight the complex neurochemical pathology underlying FTD and emphasize the need for targeted therapeutic approaches addressing specific neurotransmitter dysfunction patterns rather than broad-spectrum interventions.

title: I Believe I Can Fly!: Use of Drosophila as a Model Organism in Neuropsychopharmacology Research.
abstract: The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster has emerged as a powerful model organism for neuropsychopharmacological research, offering unique advantages including genetic tractability, conserved neurotransmitter systems, and well-characterized behaviors. Despite extensive use in basic neuroscience, systematic evaluation of Drosophila's utility for testing psychoactive compounds remains limited. We conducted a comprehensive assessment of behavioral and molecular responses to clinically relevant neuropsychiatric drugs in wild-type Canton-S flies and mutant strains with altered dopaminergic and serotonergic signaling. Adult flies were exposed to therapeutic concentrations of antidepressants (fluoxetine, imipramine), antipsychotics (haloperidol, risperidone), anxiolytics (diazepam), and psychostimulants (cocaine, amphetamine) via feeding protocols. Behavioral outcomes were measured using locomotor activity assays, negative geotaxis climbing tests, and novel courtship paradigms. Drug effects on neurotransmitter levels were quantified using HPLC analysis of whole-brain extracts. Pharmacological treatments produced dose-dependent behavioral changes remarkably similar to mammalian responses, including psychostimulant-induced hyperlocomotion, antipsychotic-mediated motor suppression, and anxiolytic effects on stress-related behaviors. Neurotransmitter analyses revealed drug-specific alterations in dopamine, serotonin, and GABA levels consistent with known mechanisms of action. Genetic mutants showed predictable alterations in drug sensitivity, validating target specificity. These findings demonstrate that Drosophila recapitulates key aspects of mammalian psychopharmacology, supporting its use as a cost-effective, high-throughput screening platform for neuropsychiatric drug discovery and mechanistic studies of psychoactive compounds.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Drug development in neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The development of novel therapeutic agents for neuropsychiatric disorders remains a critical challenge in modern medicine, with traditional drug discovery approaches yielding limited success rates and prolonged development timelines. Current methodologies rely heavily on serendipitous discoveries and phenotypic screening rather than targeted molecular approaches. This review examines emerging strategies in neuropsychopharmacology drug development, analyzing data from 847 compounds in clinical development between 2000-2007 across major pharmaceutical databases and regulatory submissions. We evaluated success rates, target selectivity, and translational efficiency from preclinical models to clinical efficacy. Our analysis reveals that only 12.3% of neuropsychiatric compounds entering Phase I trials ultimately received regulatory approval, compared to 18.7% for other therapeutic areas. Novel approaches targeting glutamatergic, GABAergic, and neuromodulatory systems showed improved preclinical-to-clinical translation rates (23.1%) compared to traditional monoaminergic targets (8.4%). Compounds utilizing biomarker-guided development and incorporating pharmacogenomic considerations demonstrated 2.7-fold higher success rates in Phase II trials. Integration of advanced neuroimaging techniques and molecular diagnostics during early development phases correlated with improved clinical outcomes. These findings suggest that neuropsychopharmacology drug development requires fundamental paradigm shifts toward mechanism-based approaches, enhanced translational models, and personalized medicine strategies. Future success will likely depend on adopting systems-level understanding of neural circuits, implementing robust biomarker strategies, and developing more predictive preclinical models that better recapitulate human neuropsychiatric pathophysiology.
journal: European archives of psychiatry and clinical neuroscience

title: The Texas Children's Medication Algorithm Project: revision of the algorithm for pharmacotherapy of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.
abstract: **Background:** The Texas Children's Medication Algorithm Project (CMAP) was developed to provide evidence-based treatment guidelines for pediatric psychiatric disorders. Since the initial attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) algorithm publication in 2001, new clinical trial data and medication approvals have necessitated algorithmic revision. **Objective:** To update the ADHD pharmacotherapy algorithm based on recent efficacy and safety data. **Methods:** A multidisciplinary expert panel reviewed randomized controlled trials, meta-analyses, and FDA safety communications published between 2001-2005. The panel included child psychiatrists, pediatricians, pharmacologists, and health services researchers from academic medical centers across Texas. Evidence quality was systematically rated, and treatment recommendations were developed through consensus methodology. **Results:** The revised algorithm maintains stimulants as first-line therapy but incorporates new formulations including extended-release methylphenidate preparations and mixed amphetamine salts. Atomoxetine is positioned as an alternative first-line option for patients with contraindications to stimulants or comorbid conditions. The algorithm addresses cardiovascular screening recommendations following FDA safety advisories and includes specific dosing guidelines for preschool-age children based on the Preschool ADHD Treatment Study findings. Treatment-resistant cases now include combination therapy options and alpha-2 agonists as adjunctive treatments. Enhanced monitoring parameters for growth, cardiovascular effects, and psychiatric symptoms are specified. **Conclusions:** The updated Texas CMAP ADHD algorithm reflects current evidence-based practices while maintaining practical clinical utility. Implementation of these guidelines may improve treatment outcomes and safety monitoring in pediatric ADHD management across diverse clinical settings.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry

title: Targeted disruption of cocaine-activated nucleus accumbens neurons prevents context-specific sensitization.
abstract: Repeated cocaine exposure induces behavioral sensitization characterized by enhanced locomotor responses that become associated with environmental contexts. The nucleus accumbens (NAc) is critical for cocaine's rewarding properties, but the specific neuronal populations mediating context-dependent sensitization remain unclear. Here we used immediate early gene mapping combined with targeted neuronal ablation to identify and eliminate cocaine-activated NAc neurons responsible for contextual sensitization. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats received cocaine (15 mg/kg, i.p.) or saline for five consecutive days in distinct environmental contexts, followed by a 7-day withdrawal period. Fos immunohistochemistry revealed that repeated cocaine exposure activated a stable population of medium spiny neurons in the NAc shell, comprising approximately 12% of total neurons. Using focal diphtheria toxin injections guided by Fos expression patterns, we selectively ablated cocaine-activated neuronal ensembles in the NAc shell. Rats with targeted lesions showed normal acute cocaine responses and developed sensitization when tested in a novel environment, but completely failed to express sensitized locomotor responses when re-exposed to the cocaine-paired context. Electrophysiological recordings confirmed that ablated neurons exhibited enhanced AMPA/NMDA ratios characteristic of cocaine-induced synaptic plasticity. Control lesions targeting randomly distributed NAc neurons had no effect on contextual sensitization. These findings demonstrate that a discrete population of cocaine-activated NAc shell neurons encodes contextual memories associated with drug exposure and is both necessary and sufficient for the expression of context-specific behavioral sensitization, providing a potential cellular target for treating drug-associated relapse.

title: Psychopharmacological treatment of schizophrenia: what do we have, and what could we get?
abstract: Despite decades of research, current antipsychotic medications for schizophrenia primarily target dopamine D2 receptors and provide limited efficacy against negative symptoms and cognitive deficits, while causing significant side effects including extrapyramidal symptoms and metabolic dysfunction. This comprehensive review examines the current state of schizophrenia pharmacotherapy and evaluates emerging therapeutic approaches based on novel neurobiological targets. We analyzed clinical trial data from 1990-2012 for approved antipsychotics and conducted a systematic review of phase II/III trials investigating non-dopaminergic mechanisms. Current first- and second-generation antipsychotics demonstrate modest efficacy (effect sizes 0.4-0.6) primarily for positive symptoms, with atypical agents offering improved tolerability profiles but persistent metabolic risks. Approximately 30% of patients show inadequate response to conventional treatment, and functional outcomes remain poor despite symptom control. Promising alternative approaches include glutamate NMDA receptor modulators (glycine transport inhibitors, D-amino acid oxidase inhibitors), which show preliminary efficacy for negative symptoms and cognition in early trials. Cholinergic α7 nicotinic receptor agonists demonstrate potential for cognitive enhancement, while metabotropic glutamate receptor modulators and phosphodiesterase inhibitors show preclinical promise. Additionally, adjunctive anti-inflammatory agents and antioxidants targeting oxidative stress pathways have yielded encouraging preliminary results. The future of schizophrenia treatment likely lies in personalized medicine approaches combining multiple mechanisms targeting the disorder's complex pathophysiology. Novel glutamatergic and cholinergic strategies represent the most promising near-term advances for addressing unmet clinical needs in negative symptoms and cognitive dysfunction.

title: The golden years: a tribute to Erminio Costa.
abstract: The field of neuropharmacology has been profoundly shaped by the pioneering contributions of Erminio Costa over five decades of research excellence. This tribute reviews Costa's seminal discoveries that fundamentally transformed our understanding of neurotransmitter systems and their pharmacological modulation. We analyzed Costa's most influential publications from 1960-2010, examining their impact on current therapeutic approaches and research methodologies. Costa's early work elucidated the mechanisms of monoamine metabolism, particularly the role of monoamine oxidase inhibitors in psychiatric disorders. His laboratory's identification of GABA as the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter revolutionized our understanding of neuronal inhibition and led to the development of benzodiazepine pharmacology. Perhaps most notably, Costa's research group discovered the molecular basis of benzodiazepine action at GABA-A receptors, revealing how these compounds enhance GABAergic transmission through allosteric modulation. This work provided the foundation for modern anxiolytic and anticonvulsant therapies. Costa's investigations into dopaminergic systems significantly advanced schizophrenia research, while his studies on acetylcholine metabolism contributed to early Alzheimer's disease therapeutic strategies. His methodological innovations, including neurochemical assays and receptor binding techniques, became standard tools in neuropharmacological research. Costa's mentorship produced numerous leading neuroscientists who continue advancing the field. His interdisciplinary approach, combining biochemistry, pharmacology, and behavioral studies, established paradigms still employed today. Costa's legacy encompasses not only fundamental discoveries that enabled breakthrough medications for neuropsychiatric disorders, but also the scientific rigor and collaborative spirit that define modern neuropharmacology research.

title: Listening to the brain: microelectrode biosensors for neurochemicals.
abstract: Real-time monitoring of neurochemical dynamics in the living brain remains a fundamental challenge in neuroscience research. Traditional microdialysis techniques, while valuable, suffer from poor temporal resolution and tissue damage, limiting their utility for studying rapid neurotransmitter fluctuations during behavior and pathological states. Here we review recent advances in microelectrode biosensor technology that enable direct, real-time detection of neurochemicals with unprecedented spatial and temporal resolution. We examine three primary biosensor approaches: enzyme-linked amperometric sensors for glutamate, glucose, and acetylcholine detection; aptamer-based electrochemical sensors for dopamine and serotonin monitoring; and novel carbon fiber electrodes modified with selective recognition elements. Validation studies in anesthetized rats demonstrate detection limits in the low micromolar range with sub-second temporal resolution and minimal tissue disruption. In behaving animals, these biosensors successfully recorded phasic dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens during reward-seeking behavior and monitored glutamate dynamics in the hippocampus during spatial learning tasks. Comparative analyses with established techniques, including fast-scan cyclic voltammetry and microdialysis, confirm the reliability and selectivity of these biosensor platforms. The technology shows particular promise for investigating neurochemical dysfunction in disease models, with preliminary studies in Parkinson's and Alzheimer's disease paradigms revealing distinct neurotransmitter signatures. These miniaturized biosensors represent a significant technological advancement, offering researchers powerful tools to decode the chemical language of neural communication with unprecedented precision and enabling new insights into brain function and dysfunction.

title: Are You Ready for Change?
abstract: The implementation of novel psychopharmacological interventions in inpatient psychiatric settings requires careful assessment of nursing staff readiness and institutional capacity for change. Despite evidence supporting personalized medication management approaches, adoption rates remain inconsistent across mental health facilities. This mixed-methods study examined factors influencing nurses' readiness to implement precision psychiatry protocols in acute care settings. A convenience sample of 247 psychiatric nurses from six Midwestern hospitals completed the Readiness for Organizational Change Scale and participated in focus groups between March and September 2017. Participants had a mean age of 34.2 years (SD=8.1) and averaged 7.4 years of psychiatric nursing experience. Quantitative analysis revealed that nurses with higher educational levels (BSN or above) demonstrated significantly greater change readiness scores compared to those with associate degrees (p<0.001). Years of experience showed a curvilinear relationship with readiness, peaking among nurses with 5-10 years of practice. Qualitative themes included concerns about medication complexity, confidence in pharmacogenomic testing interpretation, and perceived administrative support. Nurses reported feeling more prepared when provided with structured educational programs and decision-support tools. Organizational culture emerged as the strongest predictor of change readiness (β=0.43, p<0.001). These findings suggest that successful implementation of advanced psychopharmacological protocols requires targeted educational interventions, particularly for less experienced nurses and those in less supportive organizational environments. Healthcare administrators should prioritize culture transformation initiatives alongside technical training to optimize adoption of evidence-based psychiatric medication management practices.
journal: Journal of psychosocial nursing and mental health services

title: A history of the Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (1957-2004).
abstract: The Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) was established in 1957 as the premier international scientific organization dedicated to advancing research in neuropsychopharmacology and biological psychiatry. This historical analysis examines the evolution, scientific contributions, and global impact of CINP during its first 47 years (1957-2004). Comprehensive archival research was conducted using CINP congress proceedings, institutional records, published abstracts, and interviews with founding members and past presidents. Meeting attendance records, publication databases, and scientific program analyses were systematically reviewed to assess the organization's growth and scientific output. From its founding congress in Rome with 85 participants, CINP expanded to over 2,400 members representing 65 countries by 2004. The organization convened 14 biennial world congresses during this period, with total attendance increasing from 85 to over 3,000 participants. Analysis of congress programs revealed a progressive shift from early psychoactive drug discovery (1960s) to molecular mechanisms and neurotransmitter systems (1970s-1980s), followed by integration of neuroimaging, genetics, and evidence-based therapeutics (1990s-2004). CINP fostered international collaboration through standardized research methodologies, consensus statements on psychotropic drug classification, and establishment of regional divisions in Europe, Asia-Pacific, and the Americas. The organization played a crucial role in legitimizing neuropsychopharmacology as a distinct scientific discipline, facilitating knowledge transfer between basic and clinical research, and promoting evidence-based approaches to psychiatric treatment during the foundational decades of modern biological psychiatry.

title: Changes in cerebrospinal fluid neurochemistry during pregnancy.
abstract: Pregnancy involves profound physiological adaptations that may influence central nervous system neurochemistry, yet little is known about how pregnancy affects cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) neurotransmitter concentrations. We investigated changes in CSF neurochemistry across pregnancy trimesters and compared these to non-pregnant controls. Twenty-four healthy pregnant women underwent lumbar puncture during routine spinal anesthesia for cesarean delivery (n=8 each trimester) and were compared to twelve age-matched non-pregnant controls undergoing elective procedures. CSF samples were analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography for monoamine metabolites including 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), homovanillic acid (HVA), and 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycol (MHPG), as well as gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and glutamate concentrations. Pregnant women showed significantly elevated CSF 5-HIAA concentrations compared to controls (148.2 ± 22.1 vs 98.7 ± 18.4 nmol/L, p<0.001), with progressive increases across trimesters. HVA levels were reduced in third trimester subjects compared to controls (187.3 ± 31.2 vs 234.8 ± 28.9 nmol/L, p<0.05). CSF GABA concentrations were elevated throughout pregnancy (42.3 ± 8.1 vs 28.6 ± 6.2 nmol/L, p<0.01), while glutamate levels remained unchanged. MHPG showed no significant differences across groups. These findings demonstrate that pregnancy is associated with specific alterations in CNS serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems, potentially reflecting adaptive mechanisms for maternal behavioral changes and fetal neuroprotection. Understanding these neurochemical adaptations may provide insights into pregnancy-related mood disorders and inform therapeutic approaches during the perinatal period.

title: Chapter 11 - Solid lipid nanoparticles for brain tumors therapy: State of the art and novel challenges.
abstract: The blood-brain barrier (BBB) presents a formidable obstacle for effective chemotherapeutic delivery to brain tumors, with less than 2% of systemically administered drugs reaching the central nervous system. Solid lipid nanoparticles (SLNs) have emerged as a promising drug delivery system that can potentially overcome BBB limitations through enhanced permeation and retention effects. This review examines current advances in SLN technology for brain tumor therapy and identifies critical challenges for clinical translation. We analyzed preclinical studies from 2005-2009 investigating SLN formulations loaded with anticancer agents including paclitaxel, doxorubicin, and temozolomide. SLNs demonstrated superior pharmacokinetic profiles compared to free drugs, with 3-8 fold increases in brain accumulation and extended circulation half-lives. In vitro studies showed enhanced cytotoxicity against glioblastoma cell lines, while in vivo experiments using orthotopic tumor models revealed significant improvements in median survival times (40-65% increase) with reduced systemic toxicity. Surface modification with transferrin, lactoferrin, and cell-penetrating peptides further enhanced BBB penetration through receptor-mediated transcytosis. However, several challenges remain unresolved, including particle size optimization for maximum brain uptake, prevention of rapid clearance by the reticuloendothelial system, and scaling manufacturing processes for clinical use. Additionally, the heterogeneous nature of brain tumor vasculature and variable BBB integrity across different tumor regions complicate targeted delivery strategies. Future research must focus on personalized SLN design based on individual tumor characteristics and the development of real-time imaging techniques to monitor nanoparticle distribution and therapeutic efficacy.

title: Discrete neurochemical coding of distinguishable motivational processes: insights from nucleus accumbens control of feeding.
abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAc) has been implicated in motivational control of feeding behavior, yet the specific neurochemical mechanisms that distinguish between different motivational processes remain poorly understood. Previous studies have suggested that dopamine and GABA systems within the NAc may differentially regulate appetitive versus consummatory phases of feeding. Here, we examined the distinct neurochemical profiles associated with food-seeking versus food-consumption behaviors using targeted microinjections and in vivo microdialysis in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Bilateral microinjections of the GABA-A agonist muscimol (0.25 μg) into the NAc shell dramatically increased food intake by 340% compared to vehicle controls, while the same treatment in the NAc core produced no significant effect. Conversely, intra-shell injections of the dopamine D1 antagonist SCH-23390 (1.0 μg) selectively impaired food-seeking behaviors without affecting consummatory responses once feeding was initiated. Microdialysis revealed that muscimol treatment increased extracellular dopamine levels by 180% specifically during active feeding periods, while SCH-23390 had no effect on dopamine release but reduced acetylcholine efflux by 45%. Double-label Fos immunohistochemistry demonstrated distinct patterns of neuronal activation, with GABA manipulations primarily affecting ventromedial shell neurons and dopamine manipulations engaging dorsolateral shell populations. These findings provide evidence for anatomically and neurochemically discrete coding mechanisms within the NAc shell that independently regulate motivational drive versus hedonic consumption. Understanding this neurochemical compartmentalization may inform therapeutic approaches for disorders involving dysregulated feeding motivation.

title: Looking at neurotransmitters in the microscope.
abstract:
Advanced microscopic techniques have revolutionized our understanding of neurotransmitter dynamics in the central nervous system, yet comprehensive visualization of multiple neurotransmitter systems simultaneously remains technically challenging. Traditional immunohistochemical approaches provide limited temporal resolution and require tissue fixation, potentially altering neurotransmitter distribution patterns. This study employed novel multicolor fluorescence microscopy combined with real-time imaging to characterize spatial and temporal dynamics of dopamine, serotonin, and GABA in live hippocampal and striatal tissue preparations.

We utilized transgenic mice expressing fluorescent protein-tagged neurotransmitter transporters (DAT-YFP, SERT-CFP, GAT1-RFP) and applied high-speed confocal microscopy with specialized perfusion chambers maintaining physiological conditions. Neurotransmitter release was stimulated using electrical field stimulation and pharmacological agents including amphetamine, fluoxetine, and bicuculline. Image acquisition at 100ms intervals allowed quantitative analysis of neurotransmitter uptake and release kinetics across different brain regions.

Our results demonstrate distinct spatiotemporal patterns of neurotransmitter distribution, with dopaminergic terminals showing rapid, phasic release patterns in striatal regions, while serotonergic terminals exhibited more sustained, tonic activity in hippocampal areas. GABA dynamics varied significantly between interneuron subtypes, with parvalbumin-positive interneurons displaying faster kinetics than somatostatin-positive cells. Pharmacological manipulation revealed region-specific sensitivity to transporter inhibition, with striatal dopamine showing greater sensitivity to DAT blockade compared to hippocampal regions.

These microscopic visualization techniques provide unprecedented insight into neurotransmitter system interactions and offer powerful tools for investigating neuropsychiatric disorders. The methodology establishes a foundation for future studies examining neurotransmitter dysfunction in disease models and therapeutic interventions.

title: The future of acute care and prevention in headache.
abstract: Headache disorders represent a major public health burden, with migraine and tension-type headache affecting over 40% of the global population. Current therapeutic approaches often rely on non-specific analgesics and prophylactic agents with limited efficacy and significant adverse effects. This review examines emerging pharmacological strategies and preventive interventions that may revolutionize headache management in the coming decade. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent clinical trials and preclinical studies investigating novel therapeutic targets, including calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) antagonists, 5-HT1F receptor agonists, and neuronal nitric oxide synthase inhibitors. Additionally, we evaluated the potential of personalized medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenomic profiling and biomarker identification. Current evidence suggests that CGRP receptor antagonists demonstrate superior efficacy compared to traditional triptans in acute migraine treatment, with reduced cardiovascular contraindications. Lasmiditan, a selective 5-HT1F receptor agonist, shows promise for patients with coronary artery disease who cannot use conventional vasoconstrictive agents. Preventive strategies incorporating continuous monitoring devices and predictive algorithms may enable pre-emptive treatment initiation before headache onset. Pharmacogenomic testing for CYP2D6 polymorphisms could optimize prophylactic medication selection and dosing. The integration of targeted molecular therapies, personalized treatment algorithms, and real-time monitoring systems represents a paradigm shift toward precision medicine in headache care. These advances promise to improve treatment outcomes while minimizing adverse effects, ultimately reducing the substantial socioeconomic impact of headache disorders.
journal: Neurological sciences : official journal of the Italian Neurological Society and of the Italian Society of Clinical Neurophysiology

title: 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline antagonizes a rise in brain dopamine metabolism, glutamate release in frontal cortex and locomotor hyperactivity produced by MK-801 but not the disruptions of prepulse inhibition, and impairment of working memory in rat.
abstract: N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with NMDA antagonists like MK-801 producing schizophrenia-like behavioral and neurochemical alterations in rodents. 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (1MeTIQ), an endogenous amine with neuroprotective properties, has shown potential therapeutic effects in various neurological disorders. This study investigated whether 1MeTIQ could attenuate MK-801-induced behavioral and neurochemical changes in rats. Male Wistar rats received MK-801 (0.3 mg/kg, i.p.) with or without 1MeTIQ (25 mg/kg, i.p.) pretreatment. Locomotor activity was assessed in automated chambers, prepulse inhibition (PPI) of acoustic startle was measured, and working memory was evaluated using the T-maze alternation task. Brain dopamine metabolism was determined by HPLC analysis of striatal DOPAC and HVA levels, while glutamate release in frontal cortex was measured using in vivo microdialysis. MK-801 administration significantly increased locomotor activity, dopamine turnover, and cortical glutamate efflux, while disrupting PPI and impairing working memory performance. 1MeTIQ pretreatment effectively prevented MK-801-induced locomotor hyperactivity, normalized dopamine metabolism, and blocked excessive glutamate release in frontal cortex. However, 1MeTIQ failed to restore MK-801-disrupted PPI or improve working memory deficits. These findings suggest that 1MeTIQ selectively modulates certain aspects of NMDA receptor hypofunction, particularly those involving dopaminergic and glutamatergic neurotransmission, while cognitive and sensorimotor gating impairments remain unaffected. The differential protective profile of 1MeTIQ indicates distinct neurobiological mechanisms underlying various NMDA antagonist-induced behavioral phenotypes.

title: Achieving synaptically relevant pulses of neurotransmitter using PDMS microfluidics.
abstract: Precise spatiotemporal control of neurotransmitter application remains a fundamental challenge in studying synaptic transmission and plasticity. Traditional perfusion systems lack the temporal resolution to mimic physiological neurotransmitter transients, which typically last microseconds to milliseconds in the synaptic cleft. Here we describe a novel microfluidic approach using polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) devices to deliver brief, localized pulses of neurotransmitter with synaptic-like kinetics. We fabricated multilayer PDMS chips containing microchannels (10-50 μm width) positioned within 20 μm of cultured hippocampal neurons. Pneumatic valves enabled rapid switching between control and neurotransmitter-containing solutions with rise times of 2-8 ms. Using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from CA1 pyramidal neurons, we applied glutamate (1-100 μM) and GABA (10-500 μM) pulses ranging from 5-100 ms duration. Glutamate applications evoked AMPA and NMDA receptor-mediated currents with kinetics closely resembling miniature excitatory postsynaptic currents, including rapid desensitization phases. GABA pulses generated GABA_A receptor responses with physiological decay kinetics. The spatial precision allowed selective activation of dendritic versus somatic receptors, and temporal control enabled investigation of short-term plasticity mechanisms including paired-pulse facilitation and depression. Pharmacological experiments confirmed receptor subtype-specific responses. This microfluidic platform provides unprecedented control over neurotransmitter delivery, enabling investigation of synaptic mechanisms under near-physiological conditions. The system offers significant advantages over conventional application methods for studying receptor kinetics, synaptic integration, and plasticity in both cultured neurons and acute brain slices.

title: Optofluidic control of rodent learning using cloaked caged glutamate.
abstract: Precise spatiotemporal control of neurotransmitter release remains a critical challenge for understanding neural circuit function and behavior. Here, we present an optofluidic approach using "cloaked" caged glutamate compounds that enable selective neurotransmitter delivery with minimal off-target effects during learning paradigms. We synthesized novel ruthenium-based caged glutamate derivatives with red-shifted activation spectra and reduced tissue scattering properties, then developed a miniaturized fiber-optic microfluidic device for concurrent compound delivery and optical uncaging in freely moving rodents. Adult C57BL/6 mice were implanted with optofluidic probes targeting the dorsal hippocampus CA1 region and trained in a Morris water maze task. Controlled glutamate release was triggered during specific learning phases using 660 nm light pulses. Electrophysiological recordings confirmed spatially restricted glutamate uncaging within a 200 μm radius, with peak concentrations reaching 50-100 μM lasting 2-3 seconds. Behavioral analysis revealed that optically triggered glutamate release during memory encoding phases significantly enhanced spatial learning performance compared to control groups receiving either vehicle or caged compound without light activation (p < 0.001, n = 12 per group). Post-training immunohistochemistry showed increased c-Fos expression and enhanced long-term potentiation in stimulated regions. Importantly, the cloaked compounds exhibited 95% reduced background activation compared to conventional caged glutamate, minimizing artifacts. This optofluidic platform provides unprecedented precision for dissecting glutamatergic signaling contributions to learning and memory, with broad applications for investigating causal relationships between neurotransmitter dynamics and behavior.
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

title: Dynamic of neurochemical alterations in striatum, hippocampus and cortex after the 6-OHDA mesostriatal lesion.
abstract: Parkinson's disease is characterized by progressive degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra, leading to striatal dopamine depletion and motor dysfunction. While the primary pathology affects the nigrostriatal pathway, emerging evidence suggests broader neurochemical alterations may occur in interconnected brain regions. The present study investigated the temporal dynamics of neurochemical changes in striatum, hippocampus, and cortex following unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) lesions of the medial forebrain bundle in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received stereotaxic injections of 6-OHDA (8 μg/4 μl) or vehicle and were sacrificed at 1, 2, 4, and 8 weeks post-lesion (n=8-10/group). High-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection was used to quantify dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and their metabolites in dissected brain regions. 6-OHDA lesions produced >90% dopamine depletion in ipsilateral striatum by 1 week, which remained stable throughout the study period. Unexpectedly, hippocampal dopamine levels showed a biphasic response, with initial 40% reduction at 1 week followed by partial recovery by 4 weeks. Frontal cortex exhibited delayed dopamine loss (25% reduction) emerging only at 4-8 weeks post-lesion. Serotonin levels increased progressively in striatum (+35% at 8 weeks) and hippocampus (+20% at 4-8 weeks), while norepinephrine remained unchanged across all regions. These findings demonstrate that 6-OHDA mesostriatal lesions trigger complex, time-dependent neurochemical reorganization extending beyond the primary target region, suggesting compensatory mechanisms that may have therapeutic implications for Parkinson's disease treatment strategies.
journal: International journal of developmental neuroscience : the official journal of the International Society for Developmental Neuroscience

title: Love and attachment: the psychobiology of social bonding.
abstract: Social bonding represents a fundamental aspect of human behavior with profound implications for mental health and wellbeing. While the neurobiological mechanisms underlying pair bonding have been extensively studied in prairie voles, the translational relevance to human attachment remains unclear. This review synthesizes current evidence examining the psychobiology of love and attachment across species, with particular emphasis on oxytocin and vasopressin signaling pathways. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of neuroimaging, pharmacological, and genetic studies published between 1990-2008, focusing on the neural circuitry and neurochemical systems mediating social bonding behaviors. Converging evidence indicates that the mesolimbic dopamine system, in concert with oxytocinergic and vasopressinergic neurotransmission, mediates the formation and maintenance of pair bonds. Functional neuroimaging studies in humans reveal activation of reward-related brain regions, including the ventral tegmental area and nucleus accumbens, during early romantic love. Genetic polymorphisms in oxytocin and vasopressin receptor genes correlate with individual differences in attachment styles and relationship quality. Additionally, intranasal oxytocin administration enhances trust, empathy, and social recognition in healthy individuals. These findings suggest that social bonding utilizes evolutionarily conserved neural mechanisms originally involved in maternal behavior and social recognition. Understanding the neurobiological substrates of attachment has important implications for treating psychiatric disorders characterized by social dysfunction, including autism spectrum disorders, social anxiety, and borderline personality disorder. Future therapeutic interventions targeting the oxytocin system may prove beneficial for enhancing social functioning in clinical populations.

title: Alexander George Karczmar (1917-2017).
abstract: Alexander George Karczmar (1917-2017) was a pioneering figure in neuropharmacology whose seven-decade career fundamentally shaped our understanding of cholinergic neurotransmission and neural plasticity. Born in Poland and educated at the University of Edinburgh, Karczmar immigrated to the United States where he established himself as a leading authority on acetylcholine systems and their pharmacological modulation. His early work at the University of Illinois College of Medicine elucidated the mechanisms of anticholinesterase compounds, laying critical groundwork for understanding organophosphate toxicity and therapeutic applications of cholinesterase inhibitors. Karczmar's research encompassed diverse areas including synaptic transmission, neuronal development, and the role of cholinergic systems in learning and memory. He made seminal contributions to characterizing nicotinic and muscarinic receptor subtypes, their distribution patterns, and functional significance in both central and peripheral nervous systems. His laboratory pioneered electrophysiological techniques for studying cholinergic neurotransmission and developed novel pharmacological tools that remain widely used today. Beyond his scientific achievements, Karczmar was renowned as an educator and mentor, training numerous researchers who became leaders in neuropharmacology. He authored over 400 peer-reviewed publications and several influential textbooks, including "Explorations in Functions of the Nervous System" which became a cornerstone reference. Karczmar's interdisciplinary approach, combining molecular pharmacology with systems neuroscience, established methodological frameworks that continue to guide contemporary research in cholinergic neurobiology and therapeutic development for neurological disorders.

title: Differential effects of acute administration of SCH-23390, a D₁ receptor antagonist, and of ethanol on swimming activity, anxiety-related responses, and neurochemistry of zebrafish.
abstract: Dopaminergic neurotransmission plays a crucial role in regulating locomotor activity and anxiety-related behaviors across vertebrate species. While ethanol's effects on these behaviors are well-documented, the specific contribution of D₁ dopamine receptors remains incompletely understood. This study investigated the comparative effects of SCH-23390, a selective D₁ receptor antagonist, and ethanol on behavioral and neurochemical parameters in adult zebrafish (Danio rerio), an established model for neuropharmacological research. Adult zebrafish (n=96) were randomly assigned to treatment groups receiving acute administration of SCH-23390 (0.1, 0.5, or 1.0 mg/L), ethanol (0.25%, 0.5%, or 1.0%), or vehicle control. Swimming activity was assessed using automated video tracking, while anxiety-related responses were measured in a novel tank diving test. Whole-brain samples were analyzed for dopamine, serotonin, and their metabolites using high-performance liquid chromatography. SCH-23390 dose-dependently reduced swimming velocity and total distance traveled, with significant effects observed at 0.5 and 1.0 mg/L (p<0.01). Ethanol initially increased locomotor activity at lower concentrations but produced sedation at 1.0%. In the anxiety test, SCH-23390 increased bottom-dwelling time and reduced transitions to upper tank areas, indicating heightened anxiety-like behavior. Ethanol showed biphasic anxiolytic-to-anxiogenic effects with increasing concentration. Neurochemically, SCH-23390 elevated dopamine turnover ratios, while ethanol decreased serotonin levels dose-dependently. These findings demonstrate that D₁ receptor blockade produces distinct behavioral and neurochemical profiles compared to ethanol, supporting the utility of zebrafish for dissecting dopaminergic mechanisms underlying locomotion and anxiety.

title: A short historical review of SCNP with special reference to the UKU.
abstract: The Scandinavian College of Neuropsychopharmacology (SCNP) has played a pivotal role in advancing European psychiatric research since its establishment in 1961. This historical review examines the evolution of SCNP's contributions to neuropsychopharmacology, with particular emphasis on the development and validation of the UKU Side Effect Rating Scale. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published literature, conference proceedings, and archival materials from SCNP meetings between 1961-2013, focusing on methodological innovations and collaborative research initiatives. The UKU scale, developed through SCNP's multicenter collaboration in the 1980s, emerged as a response to the need for standardized assessment of psychotropic medication side effects in clinical trials. Our analysis reveals that SCNP facilitated over 200 collaborative studies across Nordic countries, with the UKU scale being adopted in more than 150 international clinical trials by 2013. The scale's comprehensive 48-item structure, covering psychological, neurological, autonomic, and other side effects, has demonstrated robust psychometric properties across diverse patient populations. SCNP's emphasis on rigorous methodology and cross-cultural validation contributed significantly to the scale's widespread acceptance by regulatory agencies including the EMA and FDA. The college's innovative approach to fostering international collaboration through annual meetings, standardized training programs, and research networks has established a model for scientific cooperation in neuropsychopharmacology. The UKU scale remains one of the most widely used instruments for side effect assessment, reflecting SCNP's enduring impact on evidence-based psychiatric practice and drug development methodologies.

title: Subcellular probes for neurochemical recording from multiple brain sites.
abstract: Spatiotemporal monitoring of neurochemical dynamics across multiple brain regions remains a critical challenge in understanding neural circuit function and dysfunction. Traditional neurochemical recording techniques lack the spatial resolution necessary to distinguish between subcellular compartments and often provide limited multiplexing capabilities. Here, we present a novel microfluidic platform integrating carbon fiber microelectrodes with subcellular positioning capabilities for simultaneous neurochemical detection across multiple brain sites. Our device features an array of 16 independently addressable carbon fiber probes (diameter: 7 μm) positioned with nanometer precision using integrated microactuators. We validated the platform using acute brain slices from adult C57BL/6 mice, targeting dopamine and serotonin release in the striatum, nucleus accumbens, and prefrontal cortex simultaneously. Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry enabled detection of neurotransmitter release events with 100 ms temporal resolution and subcellular spatial precision. The platform successfully recorded stimulus-evoked dopamine release (peak concentration: 1.2 ± 0.3 μM) and detected heterogeneous release patterns across different subcellular domains within individual neurons. Cross-correlation analysis revealed distinct temporal coupling between neurotransmitter release sites, with stronger correlations observed between proximal recording locations (r = 0.73 ± 0.08) compared to distal sites (r = 0.31 ± 0.12). Signal-to-noise ratios improved 3.2-fold compared to conventional single-electrode approaches. This multiplexed subcellular recording platform provides unprecedented spatial and temporal resolution for investigating neurochemical signaling mechanisms, offering new insights into neurotransmitter release dynamics and their role in neural network function.

title: Academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry.
abstract: The relationship between academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry has evolved significantly over the past two decades, raising concerns about potential conflicts of interest and their impact on research integrity and clinical practice. This study examined the extent and nature of financial relationships between academic psychiatrists and pharmaceutical companies through a comprehensive analysis of disclosure statements, research funding patterns, and publication trends from 1995-2005. We surveyed 847 academic psychiatrists from 125 medical schools across North America and Europe, analyzing their industry relationships, research outputs, and clinical recommendations. Additionally, we reviewed 2,340 published studies in major psychiatric journals to assess funding sources and potential bias indicators. Results revealed that 78% of surveyed academic psychiatrists reported some form of financial relationship with pharmaceutical companies, including research grants (52%), consulting fees (41%), speaker honoraria (38%), and equity holdings (12%). Department chairs and senior faculty showed higher rates of industry involvement (89%) compared to junior faculty (65%). Publications with industry funding were 2.3 times more likely to report outcomes favorable to the sponsor's product compared to independently funded studies. However, methodological quality scores did not differ significantly between industry-funded and independent research. These findings highlight the pervasive nature of academic-industry relationships in psychiatry and suggest the need for enhanced disclosure requirements, institutional oversight mechanisms, and standardized conflict-of-interest policies to maintain research objectivity while preserving beneficial collaborations that advance psychiatric therapeutics.

title: Changes in striatal electroencephalography and neurochemistry induced by kainic acid seizures are modified by dopamine receptor antagonists.
abstract: The kainic acid model of temporal lobe epilepsy provides a valuable tool for investigating seizure-induced neurochemical alterations in basal ganglia circuits. Previous studies have demonstrated that limbic seizures significantly affect striatal dopaminergic transmission, though the functional consequences remain poorly understood. The present study examined whether dopamine receptor blockade could modify seizure-induced changes in striatal electrophysiology and neurochemistry following systemic kainic acid administration. Adult male Wistar rats (n=48) received intraperitoneal injections of kainic acid (12 mg/kg) alone or in combination with the D1 receptor antagonist SCH-23390 (0.5 mg/kg) or the D2 receptor antagonist sulpiride (50 mg/kg). Striatal electroencephalographic activity was recorded continuously for 4 hours post-injection, and tissue samples were analyzed for dopamine, DOPAC, and HVA concentrations using high-performance liquid chromatography. Kainic acid alone produced characteristic limbic seizures accompanied by significant increases in striatal dopamine turnover (DOPAC/DA ratio: 187% of control, p<0.01) and enhanced theta frequency power in striatal EEG recordings. Pretreatment with SCH-23390 attenuated both the neurochemical (142% of control) and electrophysiological responses, while sulpiride showed more modest effects. Neither antagonist significantly altered seizure severity scores or latency to onset. These findings suggest that D1 receptors play a critical role in mediating seizure-induced striatal activation and may represent an important target for understanding basal ganglia involvement in epileptogenesis. The differential effects of D1 versus D2 receptor blockade indicate distinct roles for dopamine receptor subtypes in seizure-related striatal dysfunction.

title: Psychopharmacology of tic disorders in children and adolescents.
abstract: Tic disorders, including Tourette syndrome, affect 1-3% of school-age children and can significantly impair academic performance, social functioning, and quality of life. While behavioral interventions remain first-line treatment for mild cases, pharmacological intervention is often necessary for moderate to severe tics or when comorbid conditions are present. This comprehensive review examines current evidence for psychopharmacological treatments of tic disorders in pediatric populations. We conducted a systematic literature review of randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series published between 2000-2011, focusing on efficacy, tolerability, and safety profiles of anti-tic medications in children and adolescents. Traditional antipsychotics, particularly haloperidol and pimozide, demonstrate robust efficacy with 70-80% of patients showing significant tic reduction, but are limited by extrapyramidal side effects and potential cardiac complications. Atypical antipsychotics, including risperidone, aripiprazole, and ziprasidone, show comparable efficacy with improved tolerability profiles, though weight gain and metabolic effects remain concerning. Alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, guanfacine) provide moderate tic suppression with favorable safety profiles, making them particularly suitable for children with comorbid ADHD. Emerging treatments including topiramate, baclofen, and botulinum toxin injections show promise in preliminary studies. Treatment selection should be individualized based on tic severity, comorbid conditions, family preferences, and side effect tolerability. Future research should focus on head-to-head comparisons, long-term outcomes, and identification of predictive biomarkers to optimize treatment selection in pediatric tic disorders.

title: Psychopharmacology: finding one's way.
abstract: The development of effective psychotropic medications has been largely serendipitous, with many breakthrough discoveries arising from unexpected clinical observations rather than rational drug design. This review examines the historical progression of psychopharmacology from the 1950s to present, analyzing the relationship between mechanistic understanding and therapeutic efficacy. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of major psychotropic drug classes, including antipsychotics, antidepressants, and anxiolytics, examining their discovery pathways, proposed mechanisms of action, and clinical validation timelines. Database searches of MEDLINE from 1950-1994 identified 847 relevant publications, which were categorized by drug class and discovery methodology. Our analysis reveals that 78% of currently approved psychotropic medications were discovered through phenotypic screening or clinical accident rather than target-based approaches. The monoamine hypothesis of depression emerged only after tricyclic antidepressants and MAOIs demonstrated clinical efficacy, while dopamine receptor antagonism was characterized years after chlorpromazine's antipsychotic properties were established. Despite limited mechanistic insight, these empirically-derived treatments have provided substantial clinical benefit and generated testable hypotheses about psychiatric pathophysiology. However, the gap between therapeutic efficacy and mechanistic understanding has hindered the development of more selective agents with improved side effect profiles. Future progress in psychopharmacology will likely require integration of emerging neuroscience technologies, including molecular biology and neuroimaging, with traditional pharmacological approaches to bridge the divide between empirical observation and rational drug design.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: [Therapeutic drug monitoring in neuropsychopharmacology : Summary of the consensus guidelines 2017 of the TDM task force of the AGNP].
abstract: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) has gained increasing recognition as a valuable tool for optimizing psychopharmacological treatment in neuropsychiatric disorders. The AGNP (Arbeitsgemeinschaft für Neuropsychopharmakologie und Pharmakopsychiatrie) TDM task force has developed comprehensive consensus guidelines to standardize the clinical application of TDM in neuropsychopharmacology. These guidelines were systematically reviewed and updated based on current evidence from pharmacokinetic studies, clinical trials, and expert consensus. The task force evaluated therapeutic reference ranges, clinical indications, and methodological considerations for TDM across major psychotropic drug classes including antidepressants, antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and anticonvulsants used in psychiatric practice. Strong recommendations (Level A) were established for TDM in cases of non-response, suspected non-adherence, drug-drug interactions, and vulnerable populations such as elderly patients or those with hepatic impairment. Level B recommendations were formulated for dose optimization and monitoring of potentially toxic compounds. The guidelines emphasize the importance of standardized sampling procedures, analytical methods, and clinical interpretation of plasma concentrations. Specific therapeutic ranges were defined for 58 psychotropic substances, with detailed recommendations for clinical decision-making based on measured concentrations. The consensus guidelines provide evidence-based recommendations to enhance the rational use of TDM in clinical practice, potentially improving treatment outcomes while minimizing adverse effects. Implementation of these standardized approaches may contribute to more personalized and effective neuropsychopharmacological treatment strategies in routine clinical care.

title: Neuropsychopharmacologic challenge in biological psychiatry.
abstract: The use of neuropsychopharmacologic challenge paradigms has emerged as a powerful research tool for investigating the pathophysiology of psychiatric disorders and elucidating neurotransmitter system dysfunction. This review examines the current applications and methodological considerations of challenge tests in biological psychiatry research. We analyzed data from 47 controlled challenge studies published between 1985-1993, encompassing serotonergic, dopaminergic, noradrenergic, and GABAergic probe compounds in patients with major depression, schizophrenia, panic disorder, and obsessive-compulsive disorder compared to healthy controls. Challenge agents included fenfluramine, m-chlorophenylpiperazine (m-CPP), apomorphine, yohimbine, and flumazenil, with outcome measures including neuroendocrine responses, behavioral ratings, and physiological parameters. Serotonergic challenges revealed blunted prolactin responses in 68% of depressed patients compared to controls (p<0.01), while enhanced behavioral sensitivity to m-CPP was observed in 73% of panic disorder patients. Dopaminergic probes demonstrated exaggerated growth hormone responses in medication-free schizophrenic patients, suggesting receptor hypersensitivity. Noradrenergic challenge with yohimbine produced greater anxiety responses and cardiovascular reactivity in panic patients versus controls. These findings demonstrate that pharmacologic challenge paradigms can reveal specific neurotransmitter abnormalities underlying different psychiatric conditions. Challenge studies provide a unique window into central nervous system function and represent a valuable complement to neuroimaging and postmortem approaches in biological psychiatry research. Standardization of protocols and careful attention to confounding variables will enhance the utility of these methods for understanding psychiatric pathophysiology.

title: Interaction Between the Trace Amine-Associated Receptor 1 and the Dopamine D2 Receptor Controls Cocaine's Neurochemical Actions.
abstract: Cocaine's reinforcing effects are primarily mediated through blockade of the dopamine transporter, yet the molecular mechanisms underlying individual vulnerability to cocaine addiction remain poorly understood. The trace amine-associated receptor 1 (TAAR1) has emerged as a potential modulator of dopaminergic signaling, but its interaction with dopamine D2 receptors in cocaine's neurochemical actions has not been thoroughly characterized. We investigated the functional interaction between TAAR1 and D2 receptors using heterologous expression systems, primary striatal cultures, and in vivo microdialysis in rats. Co-immunoprecipitation and proximity ligation assays demonstrated direct physical interaction between TAAR1 and D2 receptors in striatal neurons. Functional studies revealed that TAAR1 activation attenuated D2 receptor-mediated inhibition of adenylyl cyclase activity and reduced D2 receptor surface expression through enhanced internalization. In primary striatal cultures, the selective TAAR1 agonist RO5256390 significantly reduced cocaine-induced dopamine overflow, while TAAR1 knockdown using siRNA enhanced this response. In vivo microdialysis experiments showed that intra-striatal administration of RO5256390 reduced cocaine-evoked dopamine release by 40% compared to vehicle controls. Conversely, TAAR1 knockout mice exhibited enhanced cocaine-induced dopamine efflux and increased locomotor sensitization following repeated cocaine administration. These findings demonstrate that TAAR1 forms functional heteromeric complexes with D2 receptors that negatively modulate dopaminergic signaling and cocaine's neurochemical effects. The TAAR1-D2 receptor interaction represents a novel regulatory mechanism that may contribute to individual differences in cocaine sensitivity and could serve as a therapeutic target for substance use disorders.

title: Consensus statements on the clinical uses of pregabalin for Hong Kong.
abstract:
**Background:** Pregabalin, a gamma-aminobutyric acid analogue, has demonstrated efficacy across multiple neuropsychiatric conditions, yet clinical practice patterns vary significantly across Asia-Pacific regions. Limited regional consensus exists regarding optimal prescribing practices for pregabalin in Hong Kong's diverse patient population. This study aimed to develop evidence-based consensus statements for pregabalin use in Hong Kong clinical practice.

**Methods:** A modified Delphi process was conducted involving 28 psychiatrists, neurologists, and pain specialists from major Hong Kong medical centers between March-September 2019. Three rounds of structured questionnaires addressed pregabalin's clinical applications, dosing strategies, safety considerations, and patient selection criteria. Literature review encompassed randomized controlled trials, meta-analyses, and regional prescribing data. Consensus was defined as ≥75% expert agreement.

**Results:** Strong consensus emerged for pregabalin use in generalized anxiety disorder (89% agreement), neuropathic pain (96% agreement), and adjunctive epilepsy treatment (82% agreement). Recommended starting doses were 75mg twice daily for anxiety disorders and 150mg daily for neuropathic pain, with gradual titration based on clinical response. Experts agreed on careful monitoring for sedation, dizziness, and weight gain, particularly in elderly patients (93% agreement). Consensus supported avoiding abrupt discontinuation and exercising caution in patients with substance abuse history (86% agreement). Cultural factors influencing medication adherence and stigma concerns were emphasized for Hong Kong populations.

**Conclusions:** These consensus statements provide standardized guidance for pregabalin prescribing in Hong Kong, emphasizing individualized treatment approaches while considering local clinical contexts and patient characteristics. Implementation may improve treatment outcomes and reduce prescribing variability across regional healthcare systems.
journal: Asia-Pacific psychiatry : official journal of the Pacific Rim College of Psychiatrists

title: Pharmacological findings on the biochemical bases of memory processes: a general view.
abstract: The molecular mechanisms underlying memory formation and consolidation remain incompletely understood, despite decades of research into the biochemical processes governing synaptic plasticity. Recent pharmacological approaches have provided valuable insights into the cellular cascades involved in learning and memory. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of memory-related biochemical pathways using selective pharmacological agents in hippocampal slice preparations and behavioral paradigms in adult male Wistar rats. Long-term potentiation (LTP) was induced in CA1 pyramidal neurons while applying specific inhibitors targeting protein kinase A (H-89), protein kinase C (chelerythrine), calcium/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II (KN-62), and mitogen-activated protein kinases (PD98059). Behavioral experiments utilized Morris water maze and passive avoidance tasks with intrahippocampal drug administration. Results demonstrated that PKA inhibition significantly impaired both early and late phases of LTP (p<0.01), while PKC blockade selectively affected the maintenance phase. CaMKII inhibition disrupted LTP induction and spatial memory acquisition, whereas MAPK pathway interference primarily affected memory consolidation processes occurring 3-6 hours post-training. Protein synthesis inhibitors (cycloheximide, anisomycin) abolished long-term memory formation without affecting short-term retention. These findings support a sequential activation model where multiple kinase cascades contribute distinct temporal components to memory processes. The data suggest that memory formation involves coordinated activation of parallel biochemical pathways, with early phases depending on post-translational modifications and later phases requiring de novo protein synthesis. Understanding these mechanisms may facilitate development of cognitive enhancers for memory disorders.

title: Time-dependent behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic dysregulation in female C57BL/6 mice caused by chronic high-fat diet intake.
