abstract: Chronic high-fat diet (HFD) consumption is associated with metabolic dysfunction and altered neurobehavioral phenotypes, yet the temporal progression of these changes remains poorly characterized in female rodents. This study investigated time-dependent effects of chronic HFD exposure on behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic parameters in female C57BL/6 mice. Eight-week-old female mice were randomly assigned to either standard chow (10% fat) or HFD (60% fat) for 2, 8, or 16 weeks (n=12 per group). Body weight, glucose tolerance, and plasma leptin levels were assessed at each timepoint. Behavioral testing included open field, elevated plus maze, and forced swim tests. Neurochemical analysis examined monoamine concentrations in the prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and hypothalamus using HPLC. HFD-fed mice exhibited significant weight gain by week 2 (p<0.01), with progressive glucose intolerance and elevated leptin levels at later timepoints. Anxiety-like behavior emerged at 8 weeks, evidenced by reduced open arm exploration (p<0.05) and decreased center zone activity (p<0.01). Depressive-like behavior was observed only at 16 weeks, with increased immobility time in forced swim tests (p<0.01). Neurochemical alterations included reduced serotonin in the hippocampus at 8 weeks (p<0.05) and decreased dopamine in the prefrontal cortex at 16 weeks (p<0.01). Hypothalamic norepinephrine levels were elevated throughout all timepoints in HFD groups (p<0.05). These findings demonstrate that chronic HFD consumption produces sequential metabolic, anxiety-related, and depressive-like phenotypes in female mice, accompanied by region-specific monoaminergic disruptions.

title: Effects of soybean peptide on immune function, brain function, and neurochemistry in healthy volunteers.
abstract: Growing evidence suggests that bioactive peptides derived from dietary proteins may influence both peripheral immune function and central nervous system activity. Soybean-derived peptides have demonstrated immunomodulatory properties in vitro, but their effects on brain function and neurochemistry in humans remain poorly understood. We conducted a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial to evaluate the effects of soybean peptide supplementation on immune markers, cognitive performance, and neurochemical parameters in healthy adults. Sixty volunteers (aged 22-45 years) were randomly assigned to receive either 3g of soybean peptide or placebo daily for 8 weeks. Immune function was assessed using natural killer cell activity, lymphocyte proliferation assays, and cytokine levels (IL-2, IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ). Cognitive performance was evaluated using standardized neuropsychological tests, while brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), serotonin, and dopamine metabolites were measured in serum. Compared to placebo, soybean peptide supplementation significantly enhanced natural killer cell activity (p<0.01) and increased IL-2 production (p<0.05), while reducing pro-inflammatory TNF-α levels (p<0.03). Participants receiving soybean peptides showed improved performance on working memory tasks (p<0.02) and attention-switching paradigms (p<0.04). Serum BDNF concentrations increased by 18% in the treatment group versus 3% in placebo (p<0.01), accompanied by elevated serotonin metabolite levels. These findings suggest that soybean peptide supplementation may provide dual benefits by enhancing immune function and supporting cognitive performance through modulation of neurotrophic factors and neurotransmitter systems in healthy individuals.

title: The neurochemical mobile with non-linear interaction matrix: an exploratory computational model.
abstract: Current understanding of neurochemical interactions in psychiatric disorders remains limited by traditional linear models that fail to capture the complex, dynamic relationships between neurotransmitter systems. The present study introduces a novel computational framework termed the "neurochemical mobile" to model non-linear interactions between dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, GABA, and glutamate systems in the context of major depressive disorder and schizophrenia. We developed a mathematical model incorporating weighted interaction matrices with time-dependent coefficients, allowing for bidirectional influences and cascade effects between neurotransmitter pathways. The model was parameterized using published pharmacokinetic data from 847 patients across 23 clinical studies involving antipsychotic and antidepressant treatments. Monte Carlo simulations were performed to explore system stability under various perturbation scenarios representing drug interventions. Results demonstrated that the non-linear model successfully predicted 73% of clinically observed drug response patterns, compared to 41% accuracy for traditional linear models. The framework revealed previously unrecognized bistable states in dopamine-serotonin interactions and identified critical threshold effects in GABA-glutamate balance that correlated with treatment resistance patterns. Sensitivity analysis indicated that small perturbations in norepinephrine signaling could trigger large-scale system reorganization, potentially explaining individual variability in antidepressant response. These findings suggest that the neurochemical mobile model provides a more realistic representation of CNS pharmacodynamics and may inform development of personalized treatment algorithms. Further validation in prospective clinical trials is warranted to assess the model's predictive utility for optimizing psychiatric medication selection.

title: How to design PET experiments to study neurochemistry: application to alcoholism.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) represents a powerful neuroimaging technique for investigating neurochemical alterations in psychiatric disorders, yet methodological considerations critically influence data quality and interpretability. This review examines optimal experimental design principles for PET neurochemistry studies, with specific application to alcohol use disorders (AUD). We analyzed published PET studies of AUD from 2000-2013, focusing on dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems, and synthesized best practices for radiotracer selection, participant characterization, and data acquisition protocols. Key design considerations include careful selection of appropriate radiotracers based on target specificity and kinetic properties, standardization of abstinence periods to minimize acute alcohol effects, implementation of rigorous exclusion criteria for psychiatric comorbidities, and utilization of reference tissue models when arterial blood sampling is not feasible. For AUD studies, we recommend minimum 7-14 day abstinence periods, assessment of alcohol craving and withdrawal symptoms, and consideration of genetic polymorphisms affecting neurotransmitter metabolism. Optimal statistical approaches should account for multiple comparisons while maintaining sufficient power, typically requiring 15-20 subjects per group for medium effect sizes. Integration of PET data with behavioral measures and treatment outcomes enhances clinical relevance. Common pitfalls include inadequate control for head motion, insufficient consideration of partial volume effects, and failure to validate kinetic modeling assumptions. These methodological guidelines provide a framework for designing robust PET neurochemistry studies that advance understanding of addiction neurobiology and facilitate development of targeted therapeutic interventions.
journal: The Yale journal of biology and medicine

title: The psychopharmacological management of RLS in psychiatric conditions: a review of the literature.
abstract: Restless legs syndrome (RLS) is a common neurological disorder characterized by uncomfortable sensations in the legs and an irresistible urge to move them, particularly during periods of rest. The prevalence of RLS is significantly elevated among patients with psychiatric conditions, including depression, anxiety disorders, and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, creating complex treatment challenges for psychiatric nurses and clinicians. This comprehensive literature review examined the current evidence base for psychopharmacological management of RLS in psychiatric populations. A systematic search of PubMed, CINAHL, and PsycINFO databases was conducted for English-language articles published between 2000 and 2012, focusing on pharmacological interventions for RLS in patients with comorbid psychiatric disorders. Sixty-three studies met inclusion criteria, including randomized controlled trials, case series, and observational studies. Dopamine agonists, particularly pramipexole and ropinirole, demonstrated efficacy for RLS symptoms while showing potential benefits for comorbid depression. However, augmentation phenomena and impulse control disorders emerged as significant concerns. Gabapentin and pregabalin showed promise in patients with anxiety-related conditions, while iron supplementation proved beneficial in cases of documented deficiency. Antidepressant selection required careful consideration, as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and tricyclics frequently exacerbated RLS symptoms. The evidence suggests that successful management of RLS in psychiatric populations requires individualized treatment approaches that consider drug interactions, contraindications, and the potential for psychiatric medications to worsen RLS symptoms. Future research should focus on developing evidence-based treatment algorithms specific to psychiatric comorbidities.
journal: Journal of the American Psychiatric Nurses Association

title: Affinity labeling the dopamine transporter ligand binding site.
abstract: The dopamine transporter (DAT) plays a critical role in regulating synaptic dopamine levels and represents a primary target for psychostimulants and antidepressants. Despite extensive pharmacological characterization, the molecular architecture of the DAT ligand binding site remains poorly understood. We employed photoaffinity labeling coupled with mass spectrometry to identify amino acid residues within the cocaine and dopamine binding domains of the human DAT. A series of photoreactive analogs were synthesized, including [125I]RTI-82, a cocaine analog containing a photoactivatable azide group, and [3H]azido-dopamine. These compounds retained high binding affinity (Ki = 15-45 nM) and selectivity for DAT over norepinephrine and serotonin transporters. Following UV irradiation of membrane preparations from HEK293 cells stably expressing human DAT, covalent incorporation was assessed by SDS-PAGE and autoradiography. Proteolytic digestion and MALDI-TOF analysis of labeled DAT identified specific crosslinking sites within transmembrane domains 1, 3, 6, and 8. Competition experiments with unlabeled ligands confirmed specificity of labeling to the primary binding site. Mutation of photolabeled residues Phe76, Asp79, Phe320, and Phe319 to alanine resulted in 10-100 fold decreases in cocaine binding affinity, validating their functional importance. These findings provide direct molecular evidence for a binding pocket model involving multiple transmembrane domains and establish a foundation for structure-based drug design targeting the dopamine transporter. The photoaffinity approach described here should prove useful for mapping ligand interactions in other neurotransmitter transporters.

title: Cosmetic psychopharmacology and the President's Council on Bioethics.
abstract: The concept of "cosmetic psychopharmacology," introduced by Kramer in 1993, describes the use of psychoactive medications to enhance normal psychological functioning rather than treat psychiatric illness. As selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and other psychotropic agents demonstrate efficacy beyond their approved indications, questions arise regarding their appropriate use in healthy individuals seeking cognitive or emotional enhancement. This analysis examines the deliberations of the President's Council on Bioethics regarding enhancement technologies, with particular focus on psychopharmacological interventions in non-pathological populations. We reviewed transcripts from Council meetings between 2002-2005, position papers, and published recommendations concerning human enhancement, specifically addressing mood and cognitive enhancement through pharmaceutical means. The Council's discussions revealed significant ethical tensions between therapeutic and enhancement applications of psychoactive drugs. While acknowledging potential benefits such as improved quality of life and performance optimization, the Council emphasized concerns including medicalization of normal human variation, distributive justice issues, authenticity of chemically-mediated personality changes, and long-term safety in healthy populations. The Council distinguished between restoration of normal function and enhancement beyond typical human ranges, though acknowledged the difficulty in operationally defining this boundary. The analysis reveals that cosmetic psychopharmacology presents unique challenges to traditional medical ethics frameworks. The Council's recommendations suggest need for enhanced regulatory oversight, professional guidelines addressing enhancement uses, and continued public discourse on the appropriate role of psychopharmacological enhancement in society. These deliberations provide crucial guidance for clinicians navigating requests for enhancement-oriented prescribing.

title: Dementia: the neurochemical basis of putative transmitter orientated therapy.
abstract: The progressive cognitive decline characteristic of dementia has been increasingly linked to specific neurotransmitter system dysfunction, prompting investigation into targeted pharmacological interventions. This review examines the neurochemical basis underlying putative transmitter-oriented therapeutic approaches for dementia, with particular emphasis on Alzheimer's disease and multi-infarct dementia. We analyzed postmortem brain tissue studies, cerebrospinal fluid biochemical markers, and clinical trial data from cholinergic, noradrenergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic intervention studies conducted between 1985-1991. Cholinergic system dysfunction remains the most consistently documented abnormality, with acetylcholine esterase activity reduced by 60-90% in cortical and hippocampal regions of Alzheimer patients. Choline acetyltransferase deficits correlate significantly with cognitive impairment severity (r=0.78, p<0.001). Noradrenergic dysfunction, evidenced by locus coeruleus neuronal loss and reduced cortical norepinephrine levels, appears more prominent in multi-infarct dementia. Clinical trials with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, including physostigmine and tetrahydroaminoacridine, demonstrate modest but statistically significant improvements in cognitive test scores, though practical benefits remain limited. Noradrenergic enhancement with L-DOPA or norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors shows inconsistent results. Serotonergic interventions have yielded marginal cognitive benefits but may improve behavioral symptoms. Current evidence suggests that effective dementia therapy will likely require combination approaches targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems simultaneously, rather than single-system interventions. The complex, multifactorial nature of dementia-related neurochemical dysfunction necessitates continued investigation into novel pharmacological strategies addressing the underlying pathophysiological heterogeneity.

title: Emerging roles for novel antipsychotic medications in the treatment of schizophrenia.
abstract: The introduction of atypical antipsychotic agents has fundamentally altered the therapeutic landscape for patients with schizophrenia. Unlike conventional neuroleptics that primarily antagonize dopamine D2 receptors, these novel compounds exhibit complex pharmacological profiles involving serotonin 5-HT2A receptor antagonism and variable dopamine receptor affinity. This review examines the clinical efficacy and neurochemical mechanisms of risperidone, olanzapine, and quetiapine in treating both positive and negative symptoms of schizophrenia. A comprehensive analysis of randomized controlled trials conducted between 1990-1997 was performed, encompassing 2,847 patients across 23 studies. Outcome measures included the Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale (PANSS), extrapyramidal symptom ratings, and cognitive assessments. Results demonstrate that atypical antipsychotics produce superior improvements in negative symptoms compared to haloperidol (p<0.001), with effect sizes ranging from 0.3 to 0.7. Extrapyramidal side effects occurred in 8-15% of patients receiving novel agents versus 45% with conventional antipsychotics. Cognitive function, particularly working memory and executive functioning, showed modest but statistically significant enhancement with atypical compounds. The 5-HT2A/D2 receptor occupancy ratio appears critical for therapeutic efficacy while minimizing motor side effects. Prolactin elevation was markedly reduced except with risperidone at higher doses. These findings suggest that novel antipsychotic medications represent a paradigm shift in schizophrenia treatment, offering improved symptom control with reduced neurological complications. Future research should focus on optimizing dosing strategies and identifying patient subgroups most likely to benefit from specific atypical agents.
journal: The Psychiatric clinics of North America

title: Psychopharmacological possibilities in the acute disaster setting.
abstract: The acute psychological aftermath of disasters presents unique challenges for emergency mental health interventions, yet systematic evidence for psychopharmacological approaches in these settings remains limited. Traditional psychiatric medications may require modification in dosing, selection, and monitoring when deployed in disaster scenarios characterized by disrupted infrastructure, limited medical supervision, and heterogeneous patient populations. This review synthesizes available evidence regarding the use of psychotropic medications in acute disaster settings, examining both efficacy and practical considerations. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using MEDLINE, PsycINFO, and disaster medicine databases from 1990-2003, supplemented by clinical reports from major disasters including the World Trade Center attacks, Oklahoma City bombing, and international humanitarian crises. Benzodiazepines demonstrated rapid anxiolytic effects but raised concerns regarding dependency risk and cognitive impairment in populations requiring intact decision-making capabilities. Low-dose atypical antipsychotics showed promise for severe acute stress reactions and agitation, with risperidone and olanzapine offering favorable side effect profiles. Traditional antidepressants proved less useful due to delayed onset of action, though trazodone provided effective short-term sleep induction. Practical considerations included medication storage stability, drug interactions with concurrent medical treatments, and the need for simplified dosing regimens. Emergency psychiatric protocols should emphasize short-acting agents with established safety profiles, minimal drug interactions, and rapid onset of action. Future research should focus on developing evidence-based guidelines specific to disaster psychiatry, including optimal medication selection algorithms and training protocols for non-psychiatric medical personnel likely to provide initial pharmacological interventions.
journal: The Psychiatric clinics of North America

title: History of modern psychopharmacology: a personal view with an emphasis on antidepressants.
abstract: The development of modern psychopharmacology has transformed psychiatric treatment over the past five decades, with antidepressant medications representing one of the most significant therapeutic advances. This review examines the historical evolution of antidepressant pharmacotherapy from the serendipitous discovery of imipramine and monoamine oxidase inhibitors in the 1950s through the introduction of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in the 1980s and 1990s. We analyzed pivotal clinical trials, regulatory milestones, and conceptual shifts in understanding depression neurobiology that shaped drug development strategies. The tricyclic era established the monoamine hypothesis and demonstrated that depression could be effectively treated pharmacologically, though significant side effects limited tolerability. The subsequent development of SSRIs, including fluoxetine, sertraline, and paroxetine, represented a paradigm shift toward more selective receptor targeting with improved safety profiles. Clinical trial data spanning four decades reveal a progressive increase in treatment response rates from 60% with early tricyclics to 65-70% with newer agents, while dropout rates decreased from 30-40% to 15-20%. The introduction of atypical antidepressants targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems, such as venlafaxine and nefazodone, reflects growing appreciation for depression's neurochemical complexity. These advances have fundamentally altered psychiatric practice, enabling outpatient management of severe depression and reducing hospitalization rates by approximately 50% since 1970. Future developments will likely focus on personalized medicine approaches and novel targets beyond traditional monoamine systems to address the substantial proportion of treatment-resistant patients.

title: Neuropsychopharmacology: are we ready for a paradigm shift?
abstract: The field of neuropsychopharmacology has traditionally relied on monoaminergic theories of psychiatric disorders, yet mounting evidence suggests fundamental limitations in current therapeutic approaches. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of contemporary pharmacological paradigms by reviewing efficacy data from 847 clinical trials published between 1980-1995, examining mechanistic studies of 23 major psychotropic compounds, and analyzing treatment response patterns in 12,400 patients across major depressive disorder, schizophrenia, and anxiety disorders. Our analysis reveals that classical monoamine-based medications demonstrate complete remission rates of only 35-45% across psychiatric conditions, with significant interindividual variability in treatment response. Emerging evidence points toward complex interactions between neurotransmitter systems, including glutamatergic, GABAergic, and neuropeptide pathways previously considered secondary targets. Novel compounds targeting NMDA receptor modulation, sigma receptors, and neuroactive steroids show promising preliminary efficacy profiles distinct from traditional agents. Furthermore, pharmacogenomic studies indicate that genetic polymorphisms in cytochrome P450 enzymes and neurotransmitter receptors account for 40-60% of observed therapeutic variability. Advances in neuroimaging and molecular biology have identified new potential therapeutic targets, including immediate early genes, second messenger cascades, and synaptic plasticity mechanisms. These findings suggest that neuropsychopharmacology may be approaching a critical transition point requiring fundamental reconceptualization of drug development strategies. We propose that future therapeutic approaches must integrate multi-target mechanisms, personalized medicine principles, and novel non-monoaminergic pathways to achieve meaningful advances in psychiatric treatment outcomes.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Rats can learn a temporal task in a single session.
abstract: Previous studies have demonstrated that temporal discrimination learning in rodents typically requires multiple training sessions over several days. However, the minimum time required for rats to acquire temporal discrimination remains unclear. Here, we investigated whether rats could learn a temporal discrimination task within a single experimental session using a novel operant conditioning paradigm. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) were trained in operant chambers equipped with two levers and a house light. In the temporal bisection task, rats received food reinforcement for pressing the left lever after short stimulus durations (2-4 s) and the right lever after long stimulus durations (6-8 s). Training consisted of 200 trials presented in a single 3-hour session with immediate feedback. Temporal discrimination was assessed using psychometric curve analysis and bisection point calculations. Results showed that rats achieved significant temporal discrimination within the first session, with mean accuracy reaching 78.3±4.2% by trial 150. The bisection point stabilized at 4.8±0.3 s, indicating successful categorization of temporal intervals. Learning curves revealed rapid acquisition during the first 100 trials, with performance plateauing thereafter. Control experiments using non-temporal cues confirmed that rats were responding to duration rather than other stimulus properties. These findings demonstrate that rats possess remarkable capacity for rapid temporal learning, achieving functional temporal discrimination in a single session when provided with immediate feedback and appropriate reinforcement contingencies. This paradigm offers advantages for studying temporal cognition mechanisms while minimizing confounding effects of prolonged training protocols.

title: Serial neurochemical measurement of cerebrospinal fluid during the human sexual response cycle.
abstract: The neurochemical basis of human sexual response remains poorly understood due to methodological limitations in accessing central nervous system neurotransmitter dynamics during sexual activity. Previous studies have relied on peripheral measurements or post-coital sampling, providing limited insight into real-time neurochemical changes. We developed a novel microdialysis protocol to continuously monitor cerebrospinal fluid neurotransmitter concentrations in the lumbar space during controlled sexual stimulation in healthy volunteers. Eight male and six female participants (age 22-34 years) underwent lumbar microdialysis catheter placement under local anaesthesia. CSF samples were collected at 2-minute intervals during baseline, arousal, plateau, orgasm, and resolution phases, as defined by physiological and self-reported measures. High-performance liquid chromatography was used to quantify dopamine, serotonin, noradrenaline, oxytocin, and β-endorphin concentrations. Dopamine levels increased progressively from baseline through plateau phase (340% increase, p<0.001), with peak concentrations occurring during orgasm (520% above baseline). Noradrenaline showed similar patterns but with earlier onset during arousal. Serotonin concentrations remained stable until orgasm, then increased significantly during resolution (180% above baseline, p<0.01). Oxytocin demonstrated a biphasic response with moderate elevation during arousal and dramatic increases during orgasm (650% above baseline). β-endorphin levels peaked during orgasm and remained elevated throughout resolution. These findings provide the first direct evidence of dynamic neurotransmitter changes in human CSF during sexual response, supporting hypotheses regarding dopaminergic reward mechanisms and oxytocinergic pair-bonding systems while revealing novel serotonergic involvement in post-orgasmic satisfaction.

title: Zebrafish assessment of cognitive improvement and anxiolysis: filling the gap between in vitro and rodent models for drug development.
abstract: Traditional drug discovery for neuropsychiatric disorders relies heavily on in vitro assays followed by rodent studies, creating a significant gap in translational efficiency and cost-effectiveness. Zebrafish (Danio rerio) have emerged as a promising intermediate model organism, offering advantages of genetic tractability, optical transparency, and conserved neurotransmitter systems relevant to cognition and anxiety. This review examines the current state of zebrafish behavioral assays for evaluating cognitive enhancement and anxiolytic compounds, analyzing their predictive validity compared to established rodent models. We synthesized data from 47 studies published between 2005-2011 that employed zebrafish for neuropharmacological screening, focusing on learning paradigms, anxiety-related behaviors, and pharmacological interventions. Zebrafish demonstrate robust performance in spatial learning tasks, habituation protocols, and avoidance conditioning that parallel rodent cognitive assessments. Novel tank diving, light-dark preference, and shoaling behaviors provide reliable anxiety-related endpoints with good pharmacological sensitivity to benzodiazepines, SSRIs, and other anxiolytic compounds. Importantly, zebrafish models showed 78% concordance with rodent studies for known anxiolytic agents and 71% agreement for cognitive enhancers, including positive responses to cholinesterase inhibitors and nootropics. The high-throughput capacity, reduced costs, and ethical advantages of zebrafish models make them valuable for early-stage compound screening. However, limitations include simplified behavioral repertoires and questions regarding emotional complexity compared to mammals. Integration of zebrafish assays into drug discovery pipelines could significantly improve efficiency in identifying promising neuropsychiatric therapeutics while reducing reliance on higher-order animal models.

title: [Neuropharmacology yesterday, today and tomorrow].
abstract: The development of neuropharmacology as a distinct scientific discipline over the past four decades has fundamentally transformed our understanding of central nervous system function and therapeutic intervention. This review examines the historical progression, current state, and future directions of neuropharmacological research, with particular emphasis on Soviet contributions to the field. We analyzed published literature from 1950-1990, focusing on major breakthrough discoveries in neurotransmitter systems, receptor pharmacology, and psychoactive drug development. Historical analysis reveals that early neuropharmacological investigations centered on cholinergic and adrenergic systems, later expanding to include dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic pathways. Contemporary research demonstrates sophisticated understanding of receptor subtypes, second messenger systems, and synaptic plasticity mechanisms. Current therapeutic approaches for neuropsychiatric disorders show improved efficacy and reduced side effects compared to earlier compounds. Emerging methodologies including radioligand binding studies, electrophysiological techniques, and neurochemical assays have revolutionized drug discovery and mechanism elucidation. Future developments appear promising, with advancing technologies in molecular biology and neuroimaging suggesting potential for more selective pharmacological interventions. Projected research directions include investigation of neuropeptide systems, development of allosteric modulators, and exploration of neurotrophic factors as therapeutic targets. The integration of computational modeling with traditional pharmacological approaches may accelerate drug development processes. Soviet neuropharmacological research has contributed significantly to global scientific progress, particularly in areas of stress-response mechanisms and adaptogenic substances. Continued interdisciplinary collaboration will be essential for addressing complex neurological and psychiatric disorders in the coming decades.
journal: Fiziologicheskii zhurnal SSSR imeni I. M. Sechenova

title: Neuropharmacologic correlates of deglutition: lessons from fictive swallowing.
abstract: The neural mechanisms underlying deglutition remain poorly understood despite the clinical importance of swallowing disorders. Previous studies of intact swallowing have been limited by the complexity of coordinating respiratory, laryngeal, and esophageal musculature during the rapid deglutitive sequence. We employed an in vitro brainstem preparation from decerebrate cats to examine the neuropharmacologic basis of fictive swallowing, allowing direct manipulation of neurotransmitter systems without confounding peripheral feedback. Fictive swallowing was induced by electrical stimulation of the superior laryngeal nerve while recording from hypoglossal, recurrent laryngeal, and phrenic nerve roots. Bath application of serotonin (5-HT, 10-50 μM) significantly enhanced the amplitude and duration of fictive swallowing motor patterns, while the 5-HT2 receptor antagonist ketanserin (10 μM) abolished this facilitation. Norepinephrine (10-30 μM) produced variable effects, with low concentrations facilitating and higher concentrations suppressing swallowing motor output. The GABA-A receptor agonist muscimol (5-20 μM) dose-dependently reduced swallowing burst amplitudes and increased cycle duration, while bicuculline (10 μM) enhanced motor output. Substance P (1-10 μM) reliably triggered fictive swallowing and increased the excitability of the central pattern generator. These findings demonstrate that serotonergic and tachykininergic systems provide facilitatory drive to the swallowing network, while GABAergic inhibition modulates pattern timing and intensity. The results suggest that pharmacologic manipulation of these neurotransmitter systems may offer therapeutic approaches for treating dysphagia, particularly in patients with central nervous system disorders affecting brainstem swallowing circuits.

title: In vivo neurochemical correlates of cognitive processes: methodological and conceptual challenges.
abstract: The investigation of neurochemical substrates underlying cognitive processes has become increasingly sophisticated with the development of in vivo monitoring techniques, yet significant methodological and conceptual challenges remain. This review examines current approaches for studying real-time neurochemical changes during cognitive tasks, including microdialysis, voltammetry, and emerging neuroimaging methods. We analyzed methodological limitations across 127 published studies from 1990-1998 that employed in vivo neurochemical monitoring during learning, memory, and attention paradigms in both animal models and human subjects. Temporal resolution emerged as a critical constraint, with conventional microdialysis providing sample intervals of 5-20 minutes while cognitive processes occur on millisecond to second timescales. Spatial resolution limitations prevent precise localization of neurochemical changes to specific neural circuits. Additionally, the invasive nature of direct monitoring techniques raises concerns about tissue damage and behavioral artifacts. Conceptually, establishing causal relationships between neurochemical fluctuations and cognitive performance remains challenging, as correlational data predominate. The heterogeneity of neurotransmitter systems and their complex interactions further complicates interpretation of neurochemical-cognitive correlations. Recent advances in fast-scan cyclic voltammetry and no-net-flux microdialysis show promise for improved temporal resolution, while combination approaches integrating multiple measurement techniques may better capture the multidimensional nature of neurochemical signaling. Future progress will require continued methodological innovation, standardized protocols, and theoretical frameworks that account for the dynamic, distributed nature of neurochemical contributions to cognition. These developments are essential for translating basic neurochemical findings into therapeutic interventions for cognitive disorders.

title: Differential neuropsychopharmacological influences of naturally occurring tropane alkaloids anisodamine versus scopolamine.
abstract: Tropane alkaloids have long been recognized for their potent anticholinergic properties, yet differential effects among naturally occurring compounds remain poorly characterized. While scopolamine's neuropsychopharmacological profile is well-established, anisodamine, a structurally related tropane alkaloid derived from Anisodus tanguticus, has received limited investigation despite traditional medicinal use. This study compared the behavioral and neurochemical effects of anisodamine and scopolamine in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received intraperitoneal injections of anisodamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg), scopolamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg), or saline vehicle (n=8-10 per group). Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity monitoring, novel object recognition testing, and elevated plus maze evaluation. Neurochemical analyses measured acetylcholine and dopamine levels in hippocampus, striatum, and prefrontal cortex using HPLC. Both compounds dose-dependently impaired novel object recognition and increased locomotor activity compared to controls. However, anisodamine produced significantly less severe cognitive deficits at equivalent doses (p<0.05) and showed reduced anxiogenic effects in the elevated plus maze. Neurochemically, while both alkaloids decreased hippocampal acetylcholine levels, anisodamine caused less pronounced reductions (42% vs 68% decrease at 1.0 mg/kg, p<0.01). Striatal dopamine levels were differentially affected, with scopolamine producing greater increases than anisodamine. These findings suggest anisodamine exhibits a more selective anticholinergic profile with potentially fewer cognitive side effects, warranting further investigation as a therapeutic alternative to conventional anticholinergic agents.

title: The psychopharmacology algorithm project at the Harvard South Shore Program: An algorithm for core symptoms of autism spectrum disorder in adults.
abstract:
**Background:** Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1% of adults, yet evidence-based psychopharmacological interventions for core symptoms remain limited. The Harvard South Shore Program developed a systematic treatment algorithm to address repetitive behaviors, social communication deficits, and sensory processing abnormalities in adults with ASD. This study evaluated the clinical effectiveness and tolerability of our structured pharmacological approach.

**Methods:** We conducted a retrospective cohort analysis of 127 adults with ASD (mean age 28.4±8.2 years) treated at our specialized clinic between 2017-2019. The algorithm employed a stepwise approach: first-line selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors for repetitive behaviors and anxiety, followed by low-dose atypical antipsychotics for severe behavioral symptoms, and adjunctive medications targeting specific domains. Primary outcomes included Clinical Global Impression-Improvement scores and standardized behavioral assessments. Treatment response, time to response, and adverse events were analyzed using descriptive statistics and regression models.

**Results:** Overall response rates were 68% for repetitive behaviors, 52% for social communication symptoms, and 71% for anxiety-related behaviors. Sertraline (mean dose 87±23 mg/day) demonstrated optimal efficacy for repetitive behaviors with minimal side effects. Aripiprazole (mean dose 8.2±3.1 mg/day) showed significant benefit for severe behavioral symptoms in 58% of patients. Common adverse effects included gastrointestinal symptoms (23%) and initial sedation (18%). Mean time to clinically meaningful improvement was 8.3±4.2 weeks.

**Conclusions:** This systematic algorithm provides a practical framework for treating core ASD symptoms in adults, demonstrating reasonable efficacy with acceptable tolerability. Larger randomized controlled trials are needed to validate these preliminary findings and optimize treatment sequencing.

title: The endocannabinoid system: a new molecular target for the treatment of tobacco addiction.
abstract: Tobacco addiction remains a major public health concern with limited therapeutic options and high relapse rates. The endocannabinoid system (ECS), comprising CB1 and CB2 receptors, endogenous ligands anandamide and 2-arachidonoylglycerol, and metabolizing enzymes, has emerged as a potential target for addiction treatment due to its modulatory role in reward pathways and withdrawal symptoms. We investigated the therapeutic potential of ECS manipulation in nicotine addiction using preclinical models and preliminary clinical data. Male Sprague-Dawley rats were trained to self-administer nicotine and treated with the CB1 receptor antagonist rimonabant (0.3-3.0 mg/kg), the fatty acid amide hydrolase inhibitor URB597 (0.1-1.0 mg/kg), or vehicle. Additionally, we analyzed smoking cessation rates from a 12-week randomized controlled trial of rimonabant (20 mg/day) versus placebo in 346 treatment-seeking smokers. Rimonabant significantly reduced nicotine self-administration by 45-60% and attenuated cue-induced reinstatement without affecting food intake at therapeutic doses. URB597 enhanced anandamide levels and reduced somatic withdrawal signs including weight loss and anxiety-like behavior. In the clinical trial, rimonabant-treated subjects showed superior abstinence rates at 12 weeks compared to placebo (23.1% vs 12.4%, p<0.05) with modest weight gain prevention. Common adverse effects included nausea and mood alterations. These findings suggest that pharmacological modulation of the endocannabinoid system represents a promising therapeutic approach for tobacco addiction, warranting further clinical investigation with improved safety profiles.

title: Effects of dopamine D2/D3 receptor antagonism on human planning and spatial working memory.
abstract: Dopamine D2/D3 receptors play a critical role in executive functions, yet their specific contributions to planning and spatial working memory remain poorly understood. Dysregulation of these cognitive processes is implicated in several neuropsychiatric disorders, including schizophrenia and Parkinson's disease, where dopaminergic dysfunction is prominent. This study investigated the effects of selective D2/D3 receptor antagonism on planning ability and spatial working memory performance in healthy human subjects. Twenty-four healthy volunteers (aged 20-35 years) participated in a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study. Participants received either sulpiride (400mg) or placebo and completed computerized versions of the Tower of London task (planning) and the n-back spatial working memory task. Neuropsychological assessments were conducted 2-3 hours post-administration when plasma levels were optimal. Sulpiride significantly impaired planning performance, evidenced by increased solution times (p<0.01) and reduced accuracy on complex problems requiring 4-5 moves (p<0.05). Spatial working memory was also negatively affected, with decreased accuracy in the 2-back (p<0.05) and 3-back conditions (p<0.01) compared to placebo. Reaction times were significantly slower across all working memory conditions under D2/D3 antagonism (p<0.001). These findings demonstrate that D2/D3 receptors are essential for optimal planning and spatial working memory performance in humans. The results provide important insights into dopaminergic mechanisms underlying executive dysfunction in psychiatric disorders and may inform therapeutic strategies targeting cognitive symptoms.

title: Building a virtual archive using brain architecture and Web 3D to deliver neuropsychopharmacology content over the Internet.
abstract: The rapid expansion of neuropsychopharmacology research has created an urgent need for comprehensive digital repositories that can effectively organize and deliver complex neuroscientific content to researchers worldwide. Traditional web-based archives lack the spatial context necessary to understand drug-brain interactions and neural pathway mechanisms. We developed a novel virtual archive system that integrates three-dimensional brain architecture with Web 3D technologies to create an immersive platform for accessing neuropsychopharmacology data. The system was built using Virtual Reality Modeling Language (VRML) and X3D standards, incorporating high-resolution anatomical brain models derived from MRI datasets. Interactive modules were designed to visualize drug binding sites, neurotransmitter pathways, and pharmacokinetic data within their neuroanatomical context. Content management features allow researchers to upload experimental data, imaging studies, and molecular structures directly to relevant brain regions. Beta testing with 45 neuroscience researchers across 12 institutions demonstrated significant improvements in content accessibility and comprehension compared to traditional database interfaces. Users reported 73% faster location of relevant studies and 68% better understanding of spatial relationships between pharmacological targets and brain structures. Navigation efficiency increased by 81% when searching for region-specific drug effects. The virtual archive successfully delivered over 15,000 peer-reviewed articles, 3,200 molecular structures, and 800 neuroimaging datasets through the 3D interface. This Web 3D approach represents a paradigm shift in scientific data presentation, offering enhanced visualization capabilities that bridge the gap between complex neuropsychopharmacological concepts and their practical applications in research and education.
journal: Computer methods and programs in biomedicine

title: Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia: neural substrates and psychopharmacological implications.
abstract: Affect recognition deficits represent a core feature of schizophrenia, contributing significantly to functional disability and social impairment. These deficits appear to emerge early in the illness course and persist despite symptomatic improvement with antipsychotic treatment. We conducted a comprehensive review of neuroimaging and pharmacological studies examining the neural substrates underlying facial emotion recognition deficits in schizophrenia and evaluated current therapeutic approaches. Functional neuroimaging studies consistently demonstrate hypoactivation in key limbic and paralimbic regions, including the amygdala, fusiform gyrus, and superior temporal sulcus, during emotion processing tasks in patients compared to healthy controls. Structural MRI investigations reveal gray matter reductions in these same regions, suggesting both functional and anatomical abnormalities contribute to impaired affect recognition. Post-mortem studies implicate disrupted GABAergic interneuron function and altered dopaminergic modulation within emotion-processing circuits. Current antipsychotic medications show limited efficacy in ameliorating these deficits, with typical antipsychotics potentially exacerbating emotion recognition through extrapyramidal side effects that impair facial expression processing. Atypical antipsychotics demonstrate modest improvements, possibly through enhanced serotonergic modulation and reduced motor side effects. Emerging pharmacological targets include NMDA receptor modulators, cholinesterase inhibitors, and oxytocin, which show preliminary promise in small clinical trials. Social cognitive training programs combined with pharmacological interventions may offer the most comprehensive approach. Understanding the neurobiological basis of affect recognition deficits is crucial for developing targeted interventions that address this fundamental aspect of schizophrenia pathophysiology and improve long-term functional outcomes.

title: Integral methods for automatic quantification of fast-scan-cyclic-voltammetry detected neurotransmitters.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) has emerged as a powerful technique for real-time detection of neurotransmitter release in brain tissue, yet current analysis methods rely heavily on manual peak identification and quantification, limiting throughput and introducing operator bias. Here we developed and validated automated integral-based algorithms for quantifying dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine signals from FSCV data. Our approach utilizes background-subtracted cyclic voltammograms integrated over specific potential windows corresponding to oxidation and reduction peaks of target neurotransmitters. We tested the algorithms on datasets from ex vivo rat brain slices (n=24) and in vivo recordings from anesthetized rats (n=12), comparing performance against traditional manual analysis and peak-fitting methods. The integral method demonstrated superior signal-to-noise ratios (2.3-fold improvement, p<0.001) and reduced analysis time by 85% compared to manual quantification. Cross-validation analysis revealed excellent correlation with manual measurements for dopamine (r=0.94), serotonin (r=0.89), and norepinephrine (r=0.91). The algorithm successfully detected transient neurotransmitter events with temporal resolution of 100 ms and concentration sensitivity down to 50 nM. Automated analysis maintained accuracy across various experimental conditions including different stimulation parameters, electrode surface areas, and tissue preparations. Implementation in open-source software enables standardized, reproducible FSCV data analysis. This integral-based approach represents a significant advancement in neurotransmitter detection methodology, facilitating high-throughput analysis of neurochemical dynamics while minimizing subjective interpretation. The method's robustness and efficiency will accelerate neuropharmacological research requiring quantitative assessment of neurotransmitter kinetics.

title: Effects of triiodothyronine and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity in rat brain: regional differences.
abstract: The thyroid hormone triiodothyronine (T3) has been shown to enhance antidepressant efficacy when used as an adjunctive treatment, though the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study investigated the regional effects of T3 and fluoxetine, alone and in combination, on serotonin 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor function in rat brain. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received daily injections of saline, T3 (25 μg/kg), fluoxetine (10 mg/kg), or T3 plus fluoxetine for 14 days. Autoreceptor sensitivity was assessed using in vivo microdialysis coupled with local perfusion of selective agonists 8-OH-DPAT (5-HT1A) and CP-93129 (5-HT1B) in the dorsal raphe nucleus, hippocampus, and frontal cortex. Extracellular 5-HT levels were measured by HPLC with electrochemical detection. Fluoxetine alone produced modest desensitization of 5-HT1A autoreceptors in the dorsal raphe after 14 days, while 5-HT1B autoreceptor sensitivity remained unchanged. T3 monotherapy had no significant effect on autoreceptor function in any region examined. However, combined T3 and fluoxetine treatment resulted in enhanced desensitization of both 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptors, with the most pronounced effects observed in the dorsal raphe and frontal cortex compared to hippocampus. The combination therapy reduced agonist-induced inhibition of 5-HT release by 45-60% compared to fluoxetine alone. These findings suggest that T3 augmentation of antidepressant therapy may involve acceleration of serotonin autoreceptor desensitization, potentially explaining the enhanced therapeutic onset observed clinically. Regional differences in autoreceptor plasticity may contribute to the varied neurochemical effects of thyroid hormone-antidepressant combinations.

title: Dorsal raphe vs. median raphe serotonergic antagonism. Anatomical, physiological, behavioral, neuroendocrinological, neuropharmacological and clinical evidences: relevance for neuropharmacological therapy.
abstract: The dorsal raphe nucleus (DRN) and median raphe nucleus (MRN) represent distinct serotonergic subsystems with opposing functional roles in mood regulation, anxiety, and stress responses. While previous research has focused primarily on global serotonergic mechanisms, emerging evidence suggests these raphe nuclei exhibit antagonistic properties with significant implications for neuropsychiatric disorders. We conducted a comprehensive review of anatomical tract-tracing studies, electrophysiological recordings, behavioral assessments following selective lesions, neuroendocrine measurements, and pharmacological manipulations targeting DRN versus MRN serotonergic projections in rodent models. Additionally, we analyzed clinical neuroimaging and pharmacological response data from patients with major depression and anxiety disorders. Anatomical studies revealed distinct projection patterns: DRN neurons predominantly innervate forebrain limbic structures including amygdala and prefrontal cortex, while MRN projections favor hippocampus and septum. Electrophysiological data demonstrated opposing responses to stress, with DRN activation correlating with anxiety-like behaviors and MRN stimulation producing anxiolytic effects. Selective DRN lesions reduced depression-like behaviors in forced swim and sucrose preference tests, whereas MRN lesions exacerbated these measures. Neuroendocrine analyses showed DRN activation increased corticosterone levels while MRN stimulation attenuated HPA axis responses. Pharmacological studies using 5-HT1A receptor manipulations revealed differential sensitivity between nuclei. Clinical PET imaging demonstrated hyperactivity in DRN-innervated regions in depressed patients. These findings support a DRN-MRN antagonism model wherein therapeutic interventions should consider selective targeting of raphe subsystems rather than global serotonergic modulation for optimized treatment outcomes.

title: Bibliometric analysis of biomedical publications on SSRI during 1980-2000.
abstract: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have emerged as first-line pharmacotherapy for major depressive disorder and anxiety disorders since their introduction in the late 1980s. Despite their widespread clinical adoption, comprehensive bibliometric analyses of SSRI research patterns remain limited. This study examined the evolution and characteristics of biomedical publications on SSRIs from 1980-2000 to identify research trends and knowledge gaps. We conducted a systematic search of MEDLINE, PsycINFO, and EMBASE databases using Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) terms for fluoxetine, sertraline, paroxetine, fluvoxamine, and citalopram. Publications were analyzed for temporal distribution, journal categories, study design, therapeutic indications, and geographic origin. A total of 3,247 publications met inclusion criteria, with exponential growth from 12 articles in 1985 to 394 in 2000. Psychiatry journals published 42% of articles, followed by general medicine (23%) and neuroscience (18%) journals. Randomized controlled trials comprised 31% of publications, while case reports and observational studies accounted for 45%. Major depressive disorder was the primary focus in 58% of studies, with anxiety disorders (22%), obsessive-compulsive disorder (12%), and off-label uses (8%) representing other major areas. North American institutions contributed 47% of publications, European centers 35%, and Asian countries 12%. The bibliometric analysis reveals rapid expansion of SSRI research during this period, with increasing methodological rigor and diversification of therapeutic applications. However, geographic disparities in research output highlight the need for more global representation in SSRI clinical research.

title: S(-)Propranolol as a discriminative stimulus and its comparison to the stimulus effects of cocaine in rats.
abstract: The β-adrenergic antagonist propranolol exists as two enantiomers with differential pharmacological properties, with S(-)propranolol exhibiting greater β-blocking activity and potential membrane-stabilizing effects. While the discriminative stimulus properties of racemic propranolol have been characterized, the specific contribution of the S(-) enantiomer remains unclear. Additionally, given propranolol's sodium channel blocking properties at higher doses, we investigated whether S(-)propranolol shares discriminative stimulus effects with cocaine, another sodium channel blocker. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were trained to discriminate S(-)propranolol (10 mg/kg, IP) from saline in a two-lever operant conditioning paradigm using food reinforcement. Following acquisition, dose-response curves were generated for S(-)propranolol (1.0-30 mg/kg), and substitution tests were conducted with cocaine (3.0-30 mg/kg), R(+)propranolol (3.0-30 mg/kg), and the β-antagonist atenolol (1.0-10 mg/kg). S(-)Propranolol produced dose-dependent discrimination with an ED50 of 5.2 mg/kg. Cocaine failed to substitute for S(-)propranolol at any dose tested, producing maximum drug-lever responding of only 23% at the highest dose. R(+)propranolol produced partial substitution (maximum 65% drug-lever responding), while atenolol showed minimal substitution (maximum 18%). The β-agonist isoproterenol (0.3-3.0 mg/kg) did not antagonize the S(-)propranolol discriminative stimulus. These results suggest that S(-)propranolol produces distinct interoceptive cues that are not mediated solely by β-adrenergic blockade and do not overlap significantly with cocaine's discriminative stimulus effects, despite shared sodium channel blocking properties.

title: Methodological approaches to exploring epileptic disorders in the human brain in vitro.
abstract: The investigation of human epileptic tissue remains challenging due to limited availability and methodological constraints. We developed and validated a comprehensive in vitro approach for studying epileptogenic mechanisms using surgically resected human brain tissue from patients with medically refractory epilepsy. Fresh tissue specimens from temporal lobe (n=24) and neocortical (n=18) resections were obtained within 30 minutes of surgical removal and maintained in oxygenated artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 32°C. Slice preparations (400 μm thickness) were prepared using a vibrating microtome and subjected to electrophysiological recordings using multi-electrode arrays and patch-clamp techniques. Neuronal viability was assessed through propidium iodide staining and measurement of ATP levels over 8-hour periods. Spontaneous epileptiform activity was recorded in 67% of hippocampal slices and 43% of neocortical preparations. Application of 4-aminopyridine (100 μM) reliably induced seizure-like events in previously quiescent tissue, with characteristic high-frequency oscillations (80-200 Hz) preceding ictal discharges. Pharmacological testing with standard antiepileptic drugs (phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate) demonstrated concentration-dependent suppression of epileptiform activity, validating the model's utility for drug screening. Neuronal viability remained above 85% for up to 6 hours post-resection when optimal storage conditions were maintained. This methodology provides a robust platform for investigating human epileptogenic mechanisms and evaluating novel therapeutic interventions using authentic pathological tissue, offering significant advantages over animal models for translational epilepsy research.

title: Oral famotidine: a potential treatment for children with autism.
abstract: Recent clinical observations have suggested that children with autism may exhibit heightened sensitivity to histaminergic activity, potentially contributing to behavioral manifestations and cognitive dysfunction characteristic of this disorder. Gastric hyperacidity and gastrointestinal disturbances are frequently reported comorbidities in autistic children, leading us to hypothesize that H2 receptor antagonism might provide therapeutic benefit through both peripheral and central mechanisms. We propose that famotidine, a selective H2 receptor antagonist with established safety in pediatric populations, may ameliorate autistic symptoms by modulating histamine-mediated neurotransmission in limbic and cortical regions while simultaneously addressing gastrointestinal dysfunction. Preclinical studies have demonstrated that histamine H2 receptors are widely distributed throughout the central nervous system, particularly in areas involved in learning, memory, and emotional regulation. Furthermore, histamine has been implicated in the regulation of dopaminergic and serotonergic pathways, neurotransmitter systems known to be dysregulated in autism spectrum disorders. The blood-brain barrier permeability of famotidine, while limited, may be sufficient to achieve clinically relevant central nervous system concentrations. We hypothesize that oral famotidine administration at standard gastric dosing (0.5-1.0 mg/kg twice daily) could reduce stereotypic behaviors, improve social interaction, and enhance cognitive function in autistic children through dual peripheral and central H2 receptor blockade. This hypothesis warrants investigation through controlled clinical trials to evaluate both efficacy and optimal dosing strategies for this potentially novel therapeutic application.

title: Evaluation of metaquant microdialysis for measurement of absolute concentrations of amphetamine and dopamine in brain: a viable method for assessing pharmacokinetic profile of drugs in the brain.
abstract: Accurate measurement of absolute drug and neurotransmitter concentrations in brain tissue remains a significant challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional microdialysis techniques provide relative concentration changes but cannot reliably determine absolute extracellular concentrations due to unknown recovery rates. This study evaluated the metaquant microdialysis approach for measuring absolute concentrations of amphetamine and dopamine in rat striatum, addressing critical limitations in pharmacokinetic assessment of psychostimulants. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8 per group) were implanted with microdialysis probes in the striatum. The metaquant method utilized retrodialysis with varying concentrations of deuterated internal standards to determine probe recovery and calculate absolute concentrations. Animals received intraperitoneal amphetamine (2.5 mg/kg) while dialysate samples were collected every 20 minutes for 4 hours. Amphetamine and dopamine concentrations were analyzed using LC-MS/MS. Recovery rates remained stable throughout the experiment (amphetamine: 18.2 ± 2.1%; dopamine: 15.7 ± 1.8%). Absolute extracellular amphetamine concentrations peaked at 2.3 ± 0.4 μM at 60 minutes post-injection, while dopamine levels increased from baseline 0.008 ± 0.002 μM to maximum 0.045 ± 0.008 μM at 80 minutes. The pharmacokinetic profile demonstrated amphetamine half-life of 127 ± 18 minutes in striatal extracellular fluid. These findings demonstrate that metaquant microdialysis provides reliable measurement of absolute brain concentrations, offering superior pharmacokinetic characterization compared to conventional microdialysis methods. This approach represents a significant advancement for neuropharmacological studies requiring precise quantification of drug and neurotransmitter levels in discrete brain regions.

title: Reduced computational models of serotonin synthesis, release, and reuptake.
abstract: The serotonergic system plays a crucial role in mood regulation, sleep, and cognitive function, making it a primary target for antidepressant therapies. However, the complex dynamics of serotonin (5-HT) synthesis, release, and reuptake at the synaptic level remain computationally challenging to model comprehensively. We developed reduced-order mathematical models to simulate 5-HT neurotransmission dynamics while maintaining biological accuracy and computational efficiency. Our approach incorporated three coupled differential equations describing tryptophan hydroxylase-mediated synthesis, vesicular release mechanisms, and serotonin transporter (SERT)-mediated reuptake kinetics. Model parameters were derived from experimental data including enzyme kinetics, transporter affinities, and synaptic geometry measurements. We validated our models against published electrophysiological recordings from dorsal raphe neurons and chronoamperometric measurements of extracellular 5-HT concentrations in rat brain slices. The reduced models accurately reproduced key experimental observations, including biphasic 5-HT clearance kinetics (fast phase: τ = 0.8 ± 0.2 s; slow phase: τ = 12.3 ± 3.1 s) and dose-dependent responses to SERT inhibitors. Computational performance analysis showed 150-fold reduction in simulation time compared to detailed biophysical models while maintaining less than 8% error in peak 5-HT concentrations. Sensitivity analysis revealed that SERT density and vesicular release probability were the most critical parameters influencing steady-state 5-HT levels. These simplified models provide valuable tools for investigating serotonergic dysfunction in psychiatric disorders and optimizing pharmacological interventions targeting the 5-HT system in silico.

title: The Domino Effect: Ed Domino's early studies of Psychoactive Drugs.
abstract: Edward F. Domino's pioneering research in the 1950s and 1960s fundamentally shaped our understanding of psychoactive drug mechanisms and established methodological frameworks still employed in contemporary neuropharmacology. This historical review examines Domino's seminal contributions to psychedelic research, particularly his groundbreaking studies with phencyclidine (PCP), ketamine, and lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD), which laid the foundation for modern NMDA receptor theory and dissociative anesthetic development. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of Domino's published works from 1955-1975, reviewing his experimental protocols, subject populations, and key findings through examination of archived laboratory records, published manuscripts, and contemporary conference proceedings. Domino's early human studies with ketamine demonstrated its unique dissociative properties and established dosing parameters that remain clinically relevant today. His systematic investigation of PCP revealed its complex pharmacological profile and potential for abuse, contributing to its eventual classification as a controlled substance. Perhaps most significantly, his methodical approach to studying LSD's effects on consciousness and perception helped establish standardized protocols for psychedelic research, including controlled set-and-setting parameters and objective measurement scales. These early investigations directly influenced the development of the glutamate hypothesis of schizophrenia and modern understanding of NMDA receptor function in psychiatric disorders. Domino's rigorous scientific methodology and ethical frameworks established during this formative period of psychoactive drug research continue to inform contemporary clinical trials investigating psychedelics for treatment-resistant depression, PTSD, and other psychiatric conditions.

title: Synthesis and pharmacological evaluation of 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines.
abstract: Novel 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinoline derivatives were synthesized and evaluated for their potential neuropharmacological activities. The tetrahydroisoquinoline scaffold has garnered significant interest due to its presence in numerous biologically active compounds, particularly those targeting dopaminergic and adrenergic systems. A series of twenty-two compounds (8a-v) were prepared via Pictet-Spengler cyclization followed by phenoxy substitution at the 4-position, while eight chromeno-fused derivatives (12a-h) were synthesized through intramolecular cyclization of appropriately substituted precursors. All compounds were characterized by ¹H NMR, ¹³C NMR, IR spectroscopy, and mass spectrometry. In vitro binding assays were performed against dopamine D₁ and D₂ receptors, α₁- and α₂-adrenergic receptors, and 5-HT₂A receptors using rat brain membrane preparations. Selected compounds were further evaluated in behavioral models including locomotor activity, rotarod performance, and forced swim tests in male Wistar rats. Compounds 8f, 8m, and 12c exhibited notable affinity for D₂ receptors (Ki = 45-89 nM) with good selectivity over D₁ receptors. Compound 8m demonstrated significant antidepressant-like activity in the forced swim test without impairing motor coordination. The chromeno-fused derivatives showed moderate α₁-adrenergic antagonist activity. Structure-activity relationship analysis revealed that electron-withdrawing substituents on the phenoxy ring enhanced D₂ receptor affinity. These findings suggest that 4-phenoxy-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives represent promising lead compounds for developing novel antipsychotic and antidepressant agents with improved therapeutic profiles.

title: An integrative dynamic model of brain energy metabolism using in vivo neurochemical measurements.
abstract: Brain energy metabolism plays a crucial role in neuronal function, yet the dynamic relationships between glucose utilization, oxygen consumption, and neurotransmitter cycling remain incompletely understood. We developed an integrative computational model that incorporates real-time neurochemical measurements to simulate energy metabolism in the awake rat brain. Using microdialysis probes implanted in the striatum, we continuously monitored extracellular glucose, lactate, and glutamate concentrations during baseline conditions and following pharmacological challenges with 3-nitropropionic acid (10 mg/kg, i.p.) and glucose (2 g/kg, i.v.). Simultaneously, we measured local field potentials and tissue oxygen tension using chronically implanted electrodes. Our dynamic model integrated astrocyte-neuron lactate shuttle kinetics, mitochondrial respiratory chain function, and glutamate-glutamine cycling based on Michaelis-Menten enzyme kinetics and diffusion parameters derived from experimental data. The model successfully predicted 89% of variance in observed lactate fluctuations and 76% of glucose changes during metabolic perturbations. Following 3-nitropropionic acid administration, the model accurately reproduced the 34% decrease in tissue oxygen and corresponding 2.1-fold increase in lactate/glucose ratio observed experimentally. Sensitivity analyses revealed that astrocytic glucose transporter capacity and neuronal oxidative metabolism were the primary determinants of system stability. These findings demonstrate that integrative modeling combined with in vivo neurochemical monitoring provides novel insights into brain bioenergetics and may facilitate development of therapeutic strategies for metabolic disorders affecting the central nervous system.

title: Identifying novel protein-protein interactions using co-immunoprecipitation and mass spectroscopy.
abstract: Protein-protein interactions play critical roles in neuronal signaling cascades and synaptic function, yet many functionally relevant interactions remain uncharacterized. Traditional biochemical approaches have limitations in identifying novel binding partners, particularly those with transient or low-affinity interactions. We developed an optimized protocol combining co-immunoprecipitation with high-resolution mass spectrometry to systematically identify previously unknown protein interactions in neuronal tissue. Using rat hippocampal tissue, we performed immunoprecipitation of three well-characterized synaptic proteins (PSD-95, NMDA receptor subunit NR1, and CaMKII-α) followed by tryptic digestion and LC-MS/MS analysis. Our approach incorporated stringent washing conditions, negative controls using non-immune IgG, and quantitative label-free proteomics to distinguish specific interactions from non-specific binding. We identified 47 high-confidence protein interactions, including 23 novel binding partners not previously reported in protein interaction databases. Notably, we discovered interactions between PSD-95 and two previously uncharacterized postsynaptic proteins, confirmed by reciprocal co-immunoprecipitation and validated through GST pull-down assays. Network analysis revealed that 34% of identified proteins clustered into functional modules related to synaptic vesicle trafficking and calcium signaling. Western blot validation confirmed 89% of selected interactions across independent tissue preparations. This methodology provides a robust platform for systematic identification of protein complexes in neuronal tissue and reveals the modular organization of synaptic protein networks. The approach can be readily adapted to study protein interactions under different physiological conditions or disease states, advancing our understanding of molecular mechanisms underlying neuronal function.

title: Contemporary approaches to pharmacotherapy in Tourette's syndrome and obsessive-compulsive disorder.
abstract: Tourette's syndrome (TS) and obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) represent challenging neuropsychiatric conditions in children and adolescents, with emerging evidence suggesting shared pathophysiological mechanisms involving dopaminergic and serotonergic systems. This comprehensive review examines current pharmacotherapeutic approaches for these frequently comorbid disorders based on published clinical trials and case series from 1985-1995. We analyzed treatment outcomes from 47 studies encompassing 1,234 pediatric patients with TS and/or OCD. Traditional neuroleptics including haloperidol and pimozide remain first-line agents for tic suppression, demonstrating 60-80% efficacy in reducing motor and vocal tics. However, significant extrapyramidal side effects limit their utility, particularly in younger patients. Emerging atypical antipsychotics show promise with improved tolerability profiles. For OCD symptoms, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) including fluoxetine, sertraline, and fluvoxamine demonstrate superior efficacy compared to tricyclic antidepressants, with 40-60% of patients showing clinically significant improvement. Clomipramine retains importance for treatment-resistant cases despite anticholinergic burden. Combination therapy addressing both tic and obsessive-compulsive symptoms requires careful consideration of drug interactions and cumulative side effects. Novel approaches including risperidone and clonazepam warrant further investigation. Current evidence supports individualized treatment selection based on symptom severity, comorbidity patterns, and tolerability considerations. Future research should focus on optimizing dosing strategies, identifying predictors of treatment response, and developing targeted interventions for this complex patient population requiring specialized nursing care and monitoring.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychiatric nursing : official publication of the Association of Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Nurses, Inc

title: Mercury, selenium and neurochemical biomarkers in different brain regions of migrating common loons from Lake Erie, Canada.
abstract: Aquatic birds such as common loons (Gavia immer) are exposed to elevated levels of mercury (Hg) through piscivorous feeding habits, with potential neurotoxic effects that may be modulated by selenium (Se) co-exposure. This study examined the relationships between Hg and Se accumulation and neurochemical alterations in different brain regions of migrating common loons collected from Lake Erie. Twenty-four adult loons were collected during spring migration (April-May 2009) and analyzed for total Hg and Se concentrations in cerebrum, cerebellum, and brainstem tissues. Neurochemical biomarkers including acetylcholinesterase (AChE), monoamine oxidase (MAO), and lipid peroxidation (LPO) were measured using spectrophotometric assays. Mercury concentrations ranged from 0.84-4.12 μg/g wet weight across brain regions, with highest levels in cerebellum, while Se concentrations ranged from 0.32-1.18 μg/g wet weight. Significant negative correlations were observed between Hg concentrations and AChE activity in cerebrum (r = -0.67, p < 0.01) and cerebellum (r = -0.54, p < 0.05). MAO activity was significantly reduced in brainstem tissues with Hg levels exceeding 2.0 μg/g wet weight. Selenium showed protective effects against Hg-induced lipid peroxidation when Se:Hg molar ratios exceeded 1:1. These findings suggest that Hg accumulation in migrating loons causes region-specific neurochemical alterations that may affect cholinergic and monoaminergic neurotransmission. The protective role of Se against oxidative damage highlights the importance of considering Se status when assessing Hg neurotoxicity in wild bird populations.

title: Effects of zona incerta lesions on striatal neurochemistry and behavioral asymmetry in 6-hydroxydopamine-lesioned rats.
abstract: The zona incerta (ZI) has emerged as a potential modulator of basal ganglia function, yet its role in Parkinson's disease pathophysiology remains poorly understood. This study investigated whether ZI lesions influence striatal neurochemistry and motor asymmetries in the 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) rat model of parkinsonism. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received unilateral 6-OHDA lesions of the medial forebrain bundle, followed by either sham surgery or electrolytic ZI lesions two weeks later. Behavioral asymmetry was assessed using amphetamine-induced rotation testing at 2, 4, and 6 weeks post-6-OHDA lesioning. High-performance liquid chromatography was used to measure dopamine, DOPAC, and HVA concentrations in striatal tissue. Immunohistochemistry quantified tyrosine hydroxylase-positive neurons in the substantia nigra. 6-OHDA lesions produced robust ipsilateral rotational behavior and >85% depletion of striatal dopamine. ZI lesions significantly reduced amphetamine-induced rotations by 42% compared to sham-operated controls (p<0.01) without affecting the extent of nigral cell loss. Striatal dopamine levels were modestly but significantly higher in ZI-lesioned animals (1.8±0.3 ng/mg vs 1.1±0.2 ng/mg tissue, p<0.05), while DOPAC and HVA concentrations remained unchanged. These findings suggest that the ZI influences striatal dopaminergic function and motor asymmetries independently of nigral cell survival in parkinsonian rats. The ZI may represent a novel therapeutic target for modulating basal ganglia output and reducing motor symptoms in Parkinson's disease through mechanisms distinct from dopamine replacement strategies.

title: New vistas in immunopsychopharmacology.
abstract: The emerging field of immunopsychopharmacology represents a paradigm shift in understanding the bidirectional communication between the central nervous system and immune system, with profound implications for psychotropic drug development. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding immune-mediated mechanisms underlying psychiatric disorders and novel therapeutic approaches targeting neuroimmune pathways. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies published between 1990-1998, focusing on cytokine alterations in major depression, schizophrenia, and anxiety disorders, as well as immunomodulatory effects of conventional psychotropic medications. Literature review revealed consistent elevation of pro-inflammatory cytokines, particularly interleukin-1β, tumor necrosis factor-α, and interleukin-6, in patients with major depressive disorder compared to healthy controls.
