Antidepressants, notably selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, demonstrated significant immunomodulatory properties, including suppression of inflammatory cytokine production and enhancement of anti-inflammatory mediators such as interleukin-10. Preliminary clinical trials of cytokine antagonists showed promising antidepressant efficacy, while interferon-α therapy frequently induced depressive symptoms, supporting the cytokine hypothesis of depression. In schizophrenia, altered T-cell proliferation and abnormal cytokine profiles suggest immune dysfunction may contribute to disease pathophysiology. Neuroleptics exhibited complex immunomodulatory effects, with phenothiazines showing anti-inflammatory properties while atypical antipsychotics demonstrated variable immune effects. These findings establish immunopsychopharmacology as a promising frontier for developing novel therapeutic interventions targeting the neuroimmune interface, potentially revolutionizing treatment approaches for psychiatric disorders through immune system modulation.

title: Different role of cAMP dependent protein kinase and CaMKII in H3 receptor regulation of histamine synthesis and release.
abstract: Histamine H3 receptors function as autoreceptors regulating histamine synthesis and release in the central nervous system, but the intracellular signaling mechanisms mediating these effects remain incompletely understood. We investigated the differential roles of cAMP-dependent protein kinase (PKA) and Ca2+/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II (CaMKII) in H3 receptor-mediated regulation of histaminergic neurotransmission using rat hypothalamic slices and primary cultured histaminergic neurons. H3 receptor activation with imetit (1-100 nM) produced concentration-dependent decreases in histidine decarboxylase (HDC) activity and histamine release, which were blocked by the H3 antagonist thioperamide (10 μM). Pretreatment with the PKA inhibitor H-89 (10 μM) completely abolished H3 receptor-mediated suppression of HDC activity but had no effect on histamine release inhibition. Conversely, the CaMKII inhibitor KN-62 (5 μM) selectively blocked H3 receptor-induced decreases in histamine release while leaving HDC regulation intact. Western blot analysis revealed that imetit treatment reduced PKA-mediated phosphorylation of HDC at Ser320 and decreased CaMKII autophosphorylation at Thr286. Forskolin (10 μM) reversed H3 receptor effects on HDC activity, while the calcium chelator BAPTA-AM (50 μM) prevented H3-mediated inhibition of histamine release. Immunofluorescence studies demonstrated distinct subcellular localizations of PKA and CaMKII in histaminergic neurons. These findings demonstrate that H3 receptors employ parallel but distinct signaling pathways to regulate histamine synthesis versus release, with PKA specifically mediating HDC inhibition and CaMKII controlling exocytotic release mechanisms. This dual regulation may provide fine-tuned control over histaminergic neurotransmission in physiological and pathological states.

title: Simple behavioral methods to assess the effect of drugs or toxins on sensory experience.
abstract: The assessment of drug and toxin effects on sensory processing remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research, yet current methodologies often require complex instrumentation or invasive procedures that may confound results. We developed and validated a battery of simple behavioral assays to evaluate sensory function across multiple modalities in laboratory rats following pharmacological manipulation. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=96) were trained on four behavioral tasks: tactile discrimination using textured surfaces, auditory startle response to pure tones, visual cliff avoidance, and olfactory preference testing with familiar scents. Following baseline establishment, animals received either vehicle control, scopolamine (0.5 mg/kg), ketamine (10 mg/kg), or lead acetate (50 mg/kg daily for 14 days). Performance was assessed at 30 minutes, 2 hours, and 24 hours post-treatment for acute compounds, and weekly for chronic lead exposure. Scopolamine significantly impaired tactile discrimination (p<0.01) and olfactory preference (p<0.05) without affecting auditory or visual responses. Ketamine selectively disrupted visual cliff performance (p<0.001) while preserving other sensory modalities. Chronic lead exposure produced dose-dependent deficits across all sensory domains by day 10. Inter-rater reliability exceeded 0.85 for all measures, and test-retest correlation was 0.79±0.12. These simple, cost-effective behavioral assays provide sensitive and specific detection of sensory dysfunction induced by diverse pharmacological agents. The methodology offers significant advantages over existing techniques through reduced stress, minimal equipment requirements, and rapid throughput, making it suitable for routine screening of potential neurotoxic compounds.

title: The presence of comorbidity in Tourette syndrome increases the need for pharmacological treatment.
abstract: Tourette syndrome (TS) is a complex neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by motor and vocal tics, frequently accompanied by comorbid conditions including attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), and anxiety disorders. While tics may not always require pharmacological intervention, the impact of comorbidities on treatment decisions remains poorly understood. This retrospective study examined the relationship between comorbid conditions and pharmacological treatment patterns in pediatric TS patients. We analyzed medical records of 247 children and adolescents (ages 6-17 years) diagnosed with TS at two tertiary care centers between 2004-2008. Patients were categorized as TS-only (n=89) or TS with comorbidities (n=158). Comorbid conditions included ADHD (64%), OCD (31%), anxiety disorders (23%), and mood disorders (18%). Clinical Global Impression-Severity scores, tic severity ratings, and medication history were documented. Children with TS plus comorbidities were significantly more likely to receive pharmacological treatment compared to those with TS alone (78% vs 34%, p<0.001). The most commonly prescribed medications in the comorbid group were stimulants (42%), selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (28%), and alpha-2 agonists (35%), often in combination. Antipsychotic medications were used more frequently in patients with severe tics and comorbid behavioral symptoms (31% vs 12%, p<0.01). Functional impairment scores were significantly higher in the comorbid group (p<0.001). These findings suggest that the presence of psychiatric comorbidities, rather than tic severity alone, drives the need for pharmacological intervention in pediatric TS, highlighting the importance of comprehensive psychiatric evaluation in treatment planning.

title: Demon voltammetry and analysis software: analysis of cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling using multiple kinetic measures.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) has emerged as a powerful technique for real-time measurement of dopamine release and uptake in brain tissue, yet comprehensive analysis of the complex kinetic parameters remains challenging. Here we present DEMON (Dopamine Evaluation and Modeling of Neurochemical) voltammetry analysis software, a novel computational platform designed to extract multiple kinetic measures from FSCV data and quantify cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling. We validated DEMON using electrically-evoked dopamine release recordings from rat striatal brain slices before and after cocaine application (1-30 μM). The software employs advanced curve-fitting algorithms to simultaneously determine peak dopamine concentration, release kinetics, uptake rate constants, and apparent Km values from individual voltammetric transients. Cocaine (10 μM) significantly increased peak dopamine levels by 312 ± 45% compared to baseline while decreasing apparent uptake rates by 73 ± 8% (n=12 slices, p<0.001). DEMON analysis revealed that cocaine's effects were dose-dependent, with apparent Km values increasing from 0.21 ± 0.03 μM to 1.84 ± 0.24 μM at 30 μM cocaine. The software also identified previously uncharacterized biphasic uptake kinetics following cocaine exposure, suggesting complex transporter mechanisms. Validation against established Michaelis-Menten modeling showed excellent correlation (r²=0.94). DEMON provides researchers with an integrated platform for comprehensive analysis of dopamine voltammetry data, enabling more detailed characterization of cocaine's effects on dopaminergic neurotransmission. This tool will facilitate standardized analysis across laboratories and advance understanding of dopamine transporter pharmacology in addiction research.

title: Role of glutamate and dopamine receptors in the psychopharmacological profile of the indole alkaloid psychollatine.
abstract: Psychollatine is an indole alkaloid isolated from Psychotria species that has shown promising psychoactive properties in preliminary studies, yet its neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. The present study investigated the role of glutamate and dopamine receptors in mediating the psychopharmacological effects of this natural compound. Male Sprigs-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) were administered psychollatine (5, 10, or 20 mg/kg, i.p.) alone or in combination with selective receptor antagonists including the NMDA receptor antagonist MK-801 (0.1 mg/kg), the AMPA/kainate receptor antagonist CNQX (10 mg/kg), the D1 receptor antagonist SCH-23390 (0.5 mg/kg), or the D2 receptor antagonist haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg). Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity, prepulse inhibition of startle response, and conditioned place preference. In vitro radioligand binding assays were performed using rat brain homogenates to determine receptor affinity profiles. Psychollatine dose-dependently increased locomotor activity and disrupted prepulse inhibition, effects that were significantly attenuated by pretreatment with SCH-23390 and haloperidol but not by glutamate receptor antagonists. Radioligand binding studies revealed moderate affinity for D1 (Ki = 2.8 μM) and D2 (Ki = 4.1 μM) receptors, with minimal binding to glutamate receptor subtypes. Psychollatine also produced conditioned place preference at 10 mg/kg, an effect blocked by dopamine but not glutamate receptor antagonism. These findings suggest that psychollatine's psychopharmacological profile is mediated primarily through dopaminergic rather than glutamatergic mechanisms, supporting its classification as a dopaminomimetic natural product with potential therapeutic applications.

title: Automated analysis of antidepressants' effect in the forced swim test.
abstract: The forced swim test (FST) is a widely used behavioral paradigm for screening antidepressant compounds, traditionally relying on manual scoring of immobility time which is labor-intensive and subject to inter-observer variability. We developed an automated video-tracking system to objectively quantify behavioral parameters in the FST and evaluated its sensitivity to detect antidepressant effects. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) were treated with vehicle, imipramine (10, 20 mg/kg), fluoxetine (10, 20 mg/kg), or bupropion (10, 20 mg/kg) 30 minutes prior to a 6-minute FST session. Our custom software analyzed high-resolution video recordings to automatically distinguish between immobility, swimming, and climbing behaviors based on pixel displacement algorithms and movement pattern recognition. The system demonstrated excellent inter-session reliability (r=0.94) and strong correlation with manual scoring (r=0.89). Automated analysis detected significant dose-dependent reductions in immobility time for all tested antidepressants compared to vehicle controls (p<0.01). Imipramine and bupropion preferentially increased climbing behavior, while fluoxetine primarily enhanced swimming activity, consistent with their distinct mechanisms of action. The automated system also revealed subtle behavioral parameters not captured by traditional scoring, including movement velocity profiles and spatial distribution patterns within the cylinder. Processing time was reduced from 2 hours to 5 minutes per session. This automated approach provides a reliable, objective, and time-efficient method for FST analysis that enhances reproducibility and reveals additional behavioral dimensions relevant to antidepressant screening, potentially improving translational validity in preclinical research.

title: Photoinactivation of native AMPA receptors reveals their real-time trafficking.
abstract: AMPA receptors (AMPARs) mediate fast excitatory synaptic transmission and undergo rapid trafficking that is critical for synaptic plasticity, yet real-time visualization of native receptor dynamics has remained technically challenging. Here we developed a photoinactivation approach using irreversible cross-linking to visualize endogenous AMPAR trafficking in living hippocampal neurons. We employed UV-induced cross-linking of native AMPARs using membrane-impermeant bifunctional reagents, followed by recovery measurements to monitor receptor replenishment at synaptic and extrasynaptic sites. Whole-cell patch-clamp recordings and two-photon microscopy were used to assess functional recovery and receptor localization over time periods ranging from minutes to hours. Photoinactivation of synaptic AMPARs resulted in a biphasic recovery with an initial rapid phase (τ = 8.2 ± 1.4 min) representing lateral diffusion from extrasynaptic pools, followed by a slower phase (τ = 47 ± 6 min) reflecting vesicular trafficking from intracellular compartments. Blockade of exocytosis with tetanus toxin selectively eliminated the slow component, while disruption of the actin cytoskeleton accelerated lateral diffusion rates by 3-fold. Activity-dependent protocols including chemical long-term potentiation significantly enhanced both trafficking components, with the rapid phase increasing 2.1-fold and the slow phase accelerating to τ = 23 ± 4 min. These findings demonstrate that synaptic AMPAR populations are maintained by constitutive trafficking involving both surface diffusion and regulated exocytosis, with activity-dependent modulation of both pathways contributing to synaptic plasticity.

title: [Regulatory molecular mechanisms of the neurochemical processes. History and the present time].
abstract: The understanding of regulatory molecular mechanisms underlying neurochemical processes has evolved dramatically over the past century, from early anatomical observations to sophisticated molecular investigations. This review examines the historical development and current state of knowledge regarding neurotransmitter synthesis, release, and synaptic modulation. We analyzed seminal publications from 1900-2003 using systematic literature review methodology, focusing on key discoveries in neuropharmacology and molecular neurobiology. The early period (1900-1950) was characterized by identification of major neurotransmitters including acetylcholine, noradrenaline, and dopamine, establishing the chemical basis of synaptic transmission. The molecular era (1950-1980) revealed enzymatic pathways of neurotransmitter metabolism, receptor subtypes, and second messenger systems. Recent advances (1980-2003) have elucidated precise molecular mechanisms including G-protein coupled receptor cascades, ion channel modulation, and synaptic plasticity pathways. Contemporary research demonstrates that neurochemical regulation involves complex networks of protein kinases, phosphatases, and transcription factors controlling both acute synaptic responses and long-term neuronal adaptations. Particular emphasis has been placed on understanding calcium-dependent processes, nitric oxide signaling, and neurotrophic factor regulation. These findings reveal that neurochemical processes are governed by intricate molecular machinery that integrates multiple signaling pathways. Current knowledge provides the foundation for developing targeted therapeutic interventions for neurological and psychiatric disorders, while highlighting the need for continued investigation into the molecular basis of brain function and dysfunction.

title: Psychopharmacology of autistic spectrum disorders in children and adolescents.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorders (ASD) affect approximately 1 in 110 children and are characterized by impaired social communication, restricted interests, and repetitive behaviors. While behavioral interventions remain the cornerstone of treatment, psychopharmacological approaches have emerged as important adjunctive therapies for managing associated symptoms and comorbidities. This comprehensive review examines current evidence for medication use in pediatric ASD populations. A systematic literature search was conducted using PubMed, Cochrane Database, and PsycINFO for studies published between 2000-2011, focusing on randomized controlled trials and open-label studies in children and adolescents with ASD. Primary outcome measures included behavioral symptom reduction, functional improvement, and adverse event profiles. Evidence supports the use of risperidone and aripiprazole for irritability, aggression, and self-injurious behaviors, with both agents receiving FDA approval for these indications in pediatric ASD. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show mixed efficacy for repetitive behaviors and anxiety, with sertraline and fluoxetine demonstrating modest benefits in some studies. Methylphenidate appears effective for comorbid attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms, though response rates may be lower than in typically developing children. Alpha-2 agonists like guanfacine show promise for hyperactivity and impulsivity. Melatonin effectively addresses sleep disturbances common in ASD. However, medication responses are often heterogeneous, and careful monitoring for adverse effects is essential. Psychopharmacological interventions should be considered as part of comprehensive, individualized treatment plans that prioritize behavioral and educational approaches while addressing specific symptom domains that significantly impair functioning.

title: Carbon nanofiber electrode for neurochemical monitoring.
abstract: The development of sensitive, biocompatible electrodes for real-time neurochemical monitoring remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional carbon fiber microelectrodes, while widely used for fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) measurements of neurotransmitters, suffer from limited sensitivity and fouling over extended recording periods. Here, we investigate the electrochemical properties and in vivo performance of carbon nanofiber (CNF) electrodes for detecting dopamine and serotonin in the rodent brain. CNF electrodes were fabricated by pyrolysis of electrospun polyacrylonitrile fibers, resulting in structures with diameters of 200-500 nm and lengths of 50-100 μm. Surface characterization revealed increased electroactive surface area compared to conventional carbon fibers, with enhanced edge plane defects promoting electron transfer kinetics. In vitro calibrations demonstrated 3-fold greater sensitivity for dopamine detection (412 ± 38 nA/μM) and improved selectivity against ascorbic acid interference. Chronic implantation studies in anesthetized rats showed stable dopamine responses in the nucleus accumbens for up to 6 hours, compared to 2-3 hours for standard carbon fiber electrodes. CNF electrodes successfully resolved pharmacologically-evoked dopamine transients following cocaine administration (1 mg/kg, i.p.) with superior signal-to-noise ratios. Histological analysis revealed minimal tissue inflammatory responses around CNF implant sites. These findings demonstrate that carbon nanofiber electrodes offer significant advantages for neurochemical monitoring applications, providing enhanced sensitivity, stability, and biocompatibility for investigating neurotransmitter dynamics in behaving animals and advancing our understanding of neuropsychiatric disorders.

title: Psychopharmacological profile of the alkaloid psychollatine as a 5HT2A/C serotonin modulator.
abstract: The alkaloid psychollatine, isolated from the leaves of *Psychotria collantha* (Rubiaceae), has been traditionally used by indigenous populations of the Colombian Amazon for its psychoactive properties. Previous phytochemical studies identified this indole alkaloid as a major constituent, yet its neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly characterized. The present study investigated the psychopharmacological profile of psychollatine with particular emphasis on serotonergic receptor interactions. Psychollatine was isolated using standard chromatographic techniques and its purity confirmed by NMR and mass spectrometry. Radioligand binding assays were performed using [³H]ketanserin and [³H]mesulergine to determine affinity at 5-HT₂A and 5-HT₂C receptors in rat cortical and choroid plexus membranes, respectively. Functional activity was assessed using phosphoinositide hydrolysis assays in transfected HEK-293 cells expressing human 5-HT₂A or 5-HT₂C receptors. Behavioral studies employed the head-twitch response paradigm in mice following intraperitoneal administration (0.1-10 mg/kg). Psychollatine demonstrated high affinity for 5-HT₂A receptors (Ki = 47 ± 8 nM) and moderate affinity for 5-HT₂C receptors (Ki = 185 ± 23 nM). Functional assays revealed partial agonist activity at both receptor subtypes, with maximal efficacies of 65% and 42% relative to serotonin at 5-HT₂A and 5-HT₂C receptors, respectively. Psychollatine induced dose-dependent head-twitch responses (ED₅₀ = 2.3 mg/kg), which were blocked by the selective 5-HT₂A antagonist MDL-100907. These findings establish psychollatine as a novel 5-HT₂A/C receptor partial agonist with predominant 5-HT₂A selectivity, providing neurochemical validation for its traditional psychoactive use

title: Photochemical and pharmacological evaluation of 7-nitroindolinyl-and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl-amino acids as novel, fast caged neurotransmitters.
abstract: Caged neurotransmitters have become essential tools for investigating synaptic transmission and neuronal signaling with high temporal and spatial resolution. However, existing photolabile protecting groups often suffer from slow photolysis kinetics and limited solubility, restricting their utility in rapid neuropharmacological experiments. We synthesized and characterized two novel series of caged amino acid neurotransmitters utilizing 7-nitroindolinyl (NI) and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl (MNI) protecting groups conjugated to glutamate, GABA, and glycine. Photochemical properties were evaluated using UV-visible spectroscopy and flash photolysis techniques. Quantum yields for photolysis were determined at 350 nm, and photolysis kinetics were measured using time-resolved spectrophotometry. Pharmacological characterization was performed using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from cultured hippocampal neurons and spinal cord preparations. Both NI- and MNI-caged compounds demonstrated significantly improved photolysis rates compared to conventional nitrobenzyl-based caging groups, with photolysis time constants of 180-350 μs for NI derivatives and 120-280 μs for MNI derivatives. MNI-caged compounds exhibited superior aqueous solubility and higher quantum yields (φ = 0.085-0.12) compared to NI derivatives (φ = 0.055-0.078). Flash photolysis of caged glutamate and GABA evoked rapid, physiologically relevant synaptic responses in neurons with minimal artifacts. These novel caged neurotransmitters offer substantial improvements in photolysis kinetics and solubility, making them valuable tools for high-resolution spatiotemporal studies of synaptic function and neural network dynamics.

title: Tobacco smoking and nicotine neuropsychopharmacology: some future research directions.
abstract: Tobacco smoking remains one of the most prevalent forms of drug dependence, yet our understanding of nicotine's complex neuropsychopharmacological mechanisms continues to evolve. While nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) have been identified as the primary targets for nicotine's rewarding effects, significant gaps remain in our knowledge of receptor subtypes, regional brain distribution, and their differential roles in addiction processes. This review synthesizes current knowledge of nicotine neuropharmacology and proposes critical research directions for the field. Recent advances in receptor pharmacology have revealed heterogeneity among neuronal nAChR subtypes, particularly α4β2 and α7 receptors, with distinct functional properties and anatomical distributions. Neuroimaging studies using PET and SPECT have begun to elucidate nicotine's effects on dopaminergic neurotransmission in reward circuits, while electrophysiological investigations have characterized receptor desensitization patterns following chronic nicotine exposure. However, several key areas require focused investigation: (1) characterization of novel nAChR subtypes and their specific contributions to dependence; (2) development of subtype-selective pharmacological tools; (3) investigation of nicotine's interactions with other neurotransmitter systems, particularly GABAergic and glutamatergic networks; (4) genetic factors influencing individual vulnerability to tobacco dependence; and (5) neuroadaptive changes underlying tolerance and withdrawal. Future research should prioritize translational approaches combining molecular, cellular, and behavioral methodologies to develop more effective smoking cessation therapies. Understanding nicotine's multifaceted actions on brain reward and cognitive systems will be essential for addressing the global tobacco epidemic through evidence-based therapeutic interventions.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Endocannabinoids and the neurochemistry of gluttony.
abstract: The endocannabinoid system has emerged as a critical regulator of feeding behavior and energy homeostasis, with CB1 receptors widely distributed throughout hypothalamic nuclei controlling appetite. While previous studies have established the orexigenic effects of cannabinoid receptor activation, the neurochemical mechanisms underlying pathological overeating remain poorly understood. We investigated the role of endocannabinoids in mediating excessive food consumption using a rodent model of binge-like feeding behavior. Male Wistar rats were subjected to intermittent food restriction followed by unlimited access to palatable high-fat diet to induce gluttonous feeding episodes. Brain tissue from hypothalamic paraventricular nucleus, arcuate nucleus, and lateral hypothalamic area was analyzed for endocannabinoid content using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. Expression of CB1 receptors and key orexigenic neuropeptides was assessed using quantitative PCR and immunohistochemistry. Animals exhibiting binge-like feeding showed significantly elevated levels of anandamide (2.3-fold increase, p<0.01) and 2-arachidonoylglycerol (1.8-fold increase, p<0.05) in hypothalamic regions compared to controls. CB1 receptor expression was upregulated by 34% in the arcuate nucleus, coinciding with increased neuropeptide Y and agouti-related peptide mRNA levels. Pretreatment with the CB1 antagonist rimonabant significantly attenuated binge-like feeding episodes by 62%. These findings demonstrate that endocannabinoid signaling is dysregulated during pathological overeating, suggesting that the endocannabinoid system represents a promising therapeutic target for treating disorders characterized by excessive food intake and loss of feeding control.

title: [The history of CINP between 1958 and 2000].
abstract: The Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) was established in 1958 as the first international organization dedicated to advancing neuropsychopharmacology research and clinical practice. This historical review examines the organizational development, scientific contributions, and global impact of CINP during its formative decades from 1958 to 2000. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of CINP congress proceedings, published abstracts, membership records, and historical documents from the organization's archives. Key informant interviews were performed with founding members and past presidents to provide contextual insights into the organization's evolution. During this 42-year period, CINP grew from 65 founding members to over 1,500 international participants across six continents. The organization convened 22 biennial world congresses, facilitating the presentation of over 15,000 scientific communications. Major scientific milestones included early discussions of monoamine hypothesis of depression (1960s), introduction of benzodiazepine research (1970s), emergence of atypical antipsychotics (1980s), and advancement of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (1990s). CINP played a pivotal role in establishing neuropsychopharmacology as a distinct scientific discipline by fostering international collaboration, standardizing research methodologies, and promoting evidence-based psychiatric treatment. The organization's emphasis on bridging basic neuroscience and clinical psychiatry significantly influenced modern psychopharmacological practice. CINP's first four decades established the foundation for contemporary neuropsychopharmacology research and contributed substantially to improved psychiatric patient outcomes worldwide through scientific advancement and international knowledge exchange.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: Anxiolytics antagonize yohimbine-induced central noradrenergic activity: a concomitant in vivo voltammetry-electrophysiology model of anxiety.
abstract: Yohimbine-induced increases in central noradrenergic activity have been extensively used to model anxiety-related behaviors, yet simultaneous real-time assessment of neurochemical and electrophysiological responses remains technically challenging. We developed a novel dual-modality approach combining in vivo voltammetry with single-unit electrophysiology to examine anxiolytic drug effects on yohimbine-evoked noradrenergic responses in the locus coeruleus (LC) of anesthetized rats. Carbon fiber microelectrodes were positioned adjacent to tungsten recording electrodes in the LC, allowing concurrent measurement of norepinephrine release and LC neuronal firing rates. Yohimbine (2 mg/kg, i.v.) produced rapid, dose-dependent increases in both norepinephrine efflux (284 ± 41% of baseline) and LC discharge frequency (198 ± 23% of baseline) within 5-10 minutes. Pretreatment with diazepam (2-8 mg/kg, i.p.) significantly attenuated yohimbine-induced norepinephrine release in a dose-dependent manner, with maximal inhibition of 67 ± 12% at the highest dose. Similarly, buspirone (1-4 mg/kg, i.p.) reduced both neurochemical and electrophysiological responses to yohimbine by 45-62%. The temporal correlation between voltammetric and electrophysiological measurements was highly significant (r = 0.84, p < 0.001), validating the integrated approach. Cross-correlation analysis revealed that changes in firing rate preceded peak norepinephrine release by 2.3 ± 0.7 seconds. This methodology provides a powerful tool for real-time assessment of anxiolytic drug mechanisms and offers enhanced temporal resolution for studying noradrenergic contributions to anxiety-related neural circuits.

title: High-sensitive liquid chromatographic method for determination of neuronal release of serotonin, noradrenaline and dopamine monitored by microdialysis in the rat prefrontal cortex.
abstract: Microdialysis coupled with high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) has become an essential technique for monitoring neurotransmitter release in vivo, yet existing methods often lack the sensitivity required for simultaneous detection of multiple monoamines in small brain regions. We developed a highly sensitive HPLC method with electrochemical detection for the simultaneous quantification of serotonin (5-HT), noradrenaline (NA), and dopamine (DA) in microdialysis samples from rat prefrontal cortex. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with microdialysis probes (2 mm membrane length) in the medial prefrontal cortex under stereotaxic guidance. Dialysate samples (20 μL) were collected every 20 minutes and analyzed using a reversed-phase C18 column with a mobile phase consisting of 0.1 M sodium phosphate buffer (pH 3.0) containing 15% methanol and 0.5 mM EDTA. Detection was performed using a glassy carbon electrode at +0.75 V versus Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The method demonstrated excellent linearity (r² > 0.998) over the concentration range of 0.5-100 nM for all three neurotransmitters. Detection limits were 0.15 nM for 5-HT, 0.12 nM for NA, and 0.08 nM for DA. Baseline extracellular concentrations were 2.8 ± 0.4 nM for 5-HT, 1.9 ± 0.3 nM for NA, and 0.6 ± 0.1 nM for DA. Potassium-evoked stimulation (100 mM KCl) increased all three neurotransmitters significantly above baseline levels. This sensitive method enables reliable monitoring of monoaminergic neurotransmission in the prefrontal cortex and should prove valuable for investigating the neurochemical basis of cognitive and emotional behaviors.

title: Microelectrode array-based system for neuropharmacological applications with cortical neurons cultured in vitro.
abstract: In vitro neuronal network models have emerged as valuable tools for investigating neuropharmacological mechanisms and drug screening applications. However, conventional electrophysiological techniques often limit simultaneous multi-site recordings from cultured neuronal preparations. We developed and characterized a microelectrode array (MEA)-based system capable of long-term, non-invasive monitoring of spontaneous and evoked electrical activity from primary rat cortical neurons in culture. The system consists of 60-electrode planar arrays with 30 μm diameter titanium nitride electrodes spaced at 200 μm intervals, integrated with custom amplification and data acquisition hardware. Primary cortical neurons from embryonic day 18 rats were cultured directly on MEA substrates and maintained for up to 28 days in vitro. Spontaneous network activity developed after 7-10 days, characterized by synchronized burst firing patterns with amplitudes ranging from 50-500 μV and durations of 100-2000 ms. Network maturation was accompanied by increased burst frequency and improved signal-to-noise ratios. Pharmacological validation was performed using GABAA receptor antagonist bicuculline (10-50 μM), which significantly increased burst frequency and duration, and glutamate receptor antagonist CNQX (10-40 μM), which dose-dependently suppressed network activity. The system demonstrated excellent long-term stability with <5% electrode degradation over 4 weeks. This MEA-based platform provides a robust, high-throughput approach for real-time assessment of compound effects on neuronal network function, offering significant advantages for neuropharmacological research and drug discovery applications requiring simultaneous multi-site electrophysiological monitoring.

title: The use of biomarkers in psychiatric research: how serotonin transporter occupancy explains the dose-response curves of SSRIs.
abstract: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) remain first-line treatments for major depressive disorder, yet their dose-response relationships are poorly understood. Traditional clinical trials have demonstrated relatively flat dose-response curves, leading to uncertainty about optimal dosing strategies. Recent advances in neuroimaging have enabled direct measurement of serotonin transporter (SERT) occupancy, providing a potential biomarker to explain SSRI pharmacodynamics. We investigated the relationship between SERT occupancy and clinical response across different SSRI doses in patients with major depressive disorder. Thirty-six medication-naïve patients (aged 25-55 years) were randomized to receive sertraline 25mg, 50mg, or 100mg daily for 8 weeks. SERT occupancy was measured using [11C]DASB positron emission tomography at baseline and weeks 2, 4, and 8. Clinical response was assessed using the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM-D). Plasma drug concentrations were measured at each timepoint. SERT occupancy increased dose-dependently, reaching 53±12%, 71±8%, and 84±6% for 25mg, 50mg, and 100mg doses, respectively, at week 8. Clinical response correlated significantly with SERT occupancy (r=0.68, p<0.001) but weakly with plasma concentrations (r=0.34, p=0.04). Maximal clinical benefit occurred at approximately 70-80% SERT occupancy, with minimal additional improvement beyond 80% occupancy. These findings demonstrate that SERT occupancy serves as a superior biomarker compared to plasma levels for predicting SSRI efficacy. The plateau in clinical response at high occupancy levels explains the flat dose-response curves observed in clinical practice, suggesting that moderate doses achieving 70-80% SERT occupancy may be optimal for most patients.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of aqueous extract of Anaphe venata larva (Notondotidae) in rats.
abstract:
**Background:** Anaphe venata (Notondotidae) larvae are traditionally consumed as food and used in folk medicine across West Africa for treating neurological disorders including epilepsy, anxiety, and memory impairment. Despite widespread ethnopharmacological use, the neuropharmacological properties of A. venata have not been scientifically validated. This study investigated the central nervous system effects of aqueous extract of A. venata larvae in experimental rats.

**Methods:** Adult Wistar rats (180-220g) were randomly assigned to control and treatment groups (n=8 per group). Aqueous extract was prepared by standard decoction method and administered orally at doses of 100, 200, and 400 mg/kg body weight. Neuropharmacological evaluation included open field test for locomotor activity, elevated plus maze for anxiety-like behavior, Morris water maze for spatial memory, pentylenetetrazol-induced seizure model, and barbiturate-induced sleeping time. Phytochemical screening was performed using standard methods.

**Results:** Phytochemical analysis revealed presence of alkaloids, flavonoids, saponins, and tannins. A. venata extract significantly reduced locomotor activity (p<0.01) and increased time spent in open arms of elevated plus maze (p<0.05), indicating anxiolytic effects. Memory performance was enhanced in Morris water maze at 200 mg/kg dose (p<0.05). The extract demonstrated anticonvulsant activity by significantly delaying seizure onset (p<0.01) and reducing seizure duration. Barbiturate-induced sleeping time was prolonged in a dose-dependent manner.

**Conclusion:** These findings provide scientific support for traditional use of A. venata larvae in neurological disorders, demonstrating significant anxiolytic, memory-enhancing, anticonvulsant, and sedative properties warranting further investigation for potential therapeutic applications.
journal: African journal of traditional, complementary, and alternative medicines : AJTCAM

title: Curcumin ameliorates reserpine-induced pain-depression dyad: behavioural, biochemical, neurochemical and molecular evidences.
abstract: Depression and chronic pain frequently coexist, creating a complex pathophysiological condition that remains challenging to treat. Reserpine, through depletion of monoaminergic neurotransmitters, produces both depressive-like behaviors and nociceptive sensitization, making it a valuable model for studying pain-depression comorbidity. Curcumin, the active component of turmeric, has demonstrated anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and neuroprotective properties. This study investigated the therapeutic potential of curcumin in a reserpine-induced pain-depression model in male Wistar rats. Animals received reserpine (1 mg/kg, s.c.) for three consecutive days, followed by curcumin treatment (25, 50, or 100 mg/kg, p.o.) for 14 days. Behavioral assessments included forced swimming test, sucrose preference test, and thermal hyperalgesia measurements. Biochemical analyses examined brain levels of serotonin, dopamine, norepinephrine, and their metabolites using HPLC. Oxidative stress markers (malondialdehyde, reduced glutathione, catalase) and inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) were measured in hippocampus and prefrontal cortex. Gene expression of BDNF, CREB, and NF-κB was analyzed using real-time PCR. Reserpine administration induced significant depressive-like behaviors, thermal hyperalgesia, monoamine depletion, oxidative stress, and neuroinflammation. Curcumin treatment dose-dependently reversed these alterations, with the 100 mg/kg dose showing optimal efficacy. Curcumin restored monoamine levels, reduced oxidative stress markers, suppressed pro-inflammatory cytokines, upregulated BDNF and CREB expression, and downregulated NF-κB activation. These findings suggest curcumin's therapeutic potential in pain-depression comorbidity through modulation of monoaminergic, inflammatory, and neurotrophic pathways, supporting its development as an adjunctive treatment for these interconnected conditions.

title: Succi nervorum: a brief history of neurochemistry.
abstract: The field of neurochemistry emerged from early anatomical observations of nervous tissue composition and evolved into a sophisticated discipline fundamental to understanding brain function. This historical review examines the development of neurochemical concepts from the 18th century discovery of "succi nervorum" (nervous juices) to the modern molecular era. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of seminal publications, experimental methodologies, and theoretical frameworks that shaped neurochemistry between 1750-2000. Key historical sources were examined from major European and American institutions, with particular attention to the transition from humoral theories to quantitative biochemical approaches. Our analysis reveals three distinct phases: the classical period (1750-1900) characterized by crude tissue extractions and speculation about "animal electricity"; the foundational era (1900-1950) marked by the identification of neurotransmitters and establishment of chemical transmission principles; and the molecular period (1950-2000) defined by receptor characterization, enzymatic pathway elucidation, and neurochemical mapping techniques. Critical developments included Loewi's demonstration of acetylcholine release, the discovery of catecholamine synthesis pathways, and the identification of amino acid neurotransmitters. The evolution from Galvani's early experiments with frog preparations to contemporary patch-clamp and molecular techniques illustrates the remarkable progression of methodological sophistication. Understanding this historical trajectory provides essential context for current neurochemical research directions and highlights how foundational observations about nervous tissue chemistry established the theoretical framework for modern neuropharmacology. The systematic development of neurochemistry demonstrates the importance of technological advancement in driving conceptual breakthroughs in neuroscience.

title: Extracting the basal extracellular dopamine concentrations from the evoked responses: re-analysis of the dopamine kinetics.
abstract: The accurate determination of basal extracellular dopamine concentrations has remained a significant challenge in neurochemical research, as conventional microdialysis and voltammetric techniques primarily measure stimulus-evoked responses rather than true baseline levels. This study presents a novel analytical approach for extracting basal dopamine concentrations from electrically-evoked dopamine transients recorded using fast-scan cyclic voltammetry in anesthetized rats. We developed a mathematical model incorporating Michaelis-Menten uptake kinetics and first-order dopamine release to deconvolute the contribution of baseline dopamine levels from the total measured signal. Dopamine responses were evoked by electrical stimulation of the medial forebrain bundle while recording in the nucleus accumbens core (n=8) and caudate-putamen (n=6). Our kinetic analysis revealed that basal extracellular dopamine concentrations were 45±8 nM in the nucleus accumbens and 62±12 nM in the caudate-putamen, values significantly higher than previous microdialysis estimates but consistent with theoretical predictions based on dopamine transporter kinetics. The model successfully predicted the relationship between stimulation parameters and peak dopamine responses (r²=0.89). Pharmacological validation using nomifensine (10 mg/kg, i.p.) confirmed model predictions, showing a 3.2-fold increase in apparent basal concentrations. This analytical framework provides a more accurate assessment of physiological dopamine levels and offers improved understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission dynamics. The method has important implications for interpreting dopamine measurements in studies of addiction, Parkinson's disease, and other neuropsychiatric conditions where baseline neurotransmitter levels are crucial for understanding pathophysiology.

title: Comparative analysis of inhibitory effects of caged ligands for the NMDA receptor.
abstract: N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors play critical roles in synaptic plasticity, learning, and memory, making them important targets for neuropharmacological intervention. Caged compounds that can be rapidly photoreleased provide temporal precision for studying NMDA receptor function, yet systematic comparisons of their inhibitory properties remain limited. We evaluated the comparative efficacy of four commercially available caged NMDA receptor antagonists: caged MK-801, caged APV, caged CNQX, and caged kynurenic acid. Using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from cultured rat hippocampal neurons (DIV 14-21), we measured NMDA-evoked currents before and after UV photolysis (365 nm, 1-5 ms pulses) of each caged compound (10-100 μM). Concentration-response relationships were established for each antagonist, and kinetics of inhibition onset were analyzed. Caged MK-801 demonstrated the highest potency (IC₅₀ = 12.3 ± 2.1 μM) and fastest inhibition kinetics (τ = 185 ± 23 ms), while caged APV showed intermediate potency (IC₅₀ = 34.7 ± 4.8 μM) with slower onset (τ = 420 ± 67 ms). Caged CNQX and caged kynurenic acid exhibited lower potencies and considerable variability in photolysis efficiency across preparations. All compounds showed minimal effects on AMPA receptor-mediated currents at concentrations producing 80% NMDA receptor inhibition. These findings establish caged MK-801 as the most effective tool for rapid, selective NMDA receptor inhibition in acute experimental paradigms, while highlighting important pharmacokinetic differences among available caged antagonists that should guide experimental design decisions.

title: Application of triple-probe microdialysis for fast pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic evaluation of dopamimetic activity of drug candidates in the rat brain.
abstract: Conventional microdialysis techniques for evaluating dopaminergic drug candidates require sequential experiments and extended observation periods, limiting throughput in pharmaceutical screening. We developed a triple-probe microdialysis system enabling simultaneous measurement of drug pharmacokinetics and dopamine release in multiple brain regions following single administration. Three concentric probes were stereotaxically implanted in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8 per group): one in the striatum for dopamine monitoring, one in the nucleus accumbens for comparative dopaminergic activity, and one in the frontal cortex for drug concentration determination. Probes were perfused with artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 2 μL/min, with 20-minute collection intervals. The system was validated using L-DOPA (100 mg/kg, i.p.) and compared against sequential single-probe measurements. Subsequently, three novel benzisoxazole derivatives (compounds A, B, and C; 10-50 mg/kg, i.p.) were evaluated. Triple-probe microdialysis successfully demonstrated regional differences in dopamine response and provided real-time pharmacokinetic profiles. L-DOPA validation showed comparable results to traditional methods but reduced experimental time by 65%. Compound A exhibited rapid brain penetration (Tmax = 45 min) with sustained striatal dopamine elevation (>300% baseline for 4 hours). Compounds B and C showed limited brain bioavailability despite peripheral activity. The correlation between brain drug levels and dopaminergic response was successfully established within single experiments. This triple-probe approach significantly accelerates preclinical screening of dopaminergic compounds by providing simultaneous pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic data, reducing animal usage, and improving experimental efficiency for neuropharmacological drug development programs.

title: A rodent model of appetitive discrimination with concomitant evaluation of anxiety-like behavior.
abstract: Food-seeking behavior and anxiety represent interconnected behavioral domains that are frequently disrupted in neuropsychiatric disorders, yet few experimental paradigms allow simultaneous assessment of both processes. We developed and validated a novel behavioral protocol that combines appetitive discrimination learning with concurrent anxiety evaluation in rodents. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were trained in a modified Y-maze apparatus containing distinct visual and olfactory cues paired with either food reward or neutral outcomes. The maze incorporated an elevated central platform and open arms to assess anxiety-like behaviors during discrimination trials. Animals underwent 14 days of training followed by pharmacological validation using anxiogenic (yohimbine, 2mg/kg) and anxiolytic (diazepam, 1mg/kg) compounds. Behavioral measures included discrimination accuracy, response latency, center platform time, and open arm exploration. Results demonstrated robust appetitive learning, with discrimination accuracy reaching 78.3±4.2% by day 10. Anxiogenic treatment significantly impaired discrimination performance (62.1±5.8% vs. vehicle 76.9±3.9%, p<0.01) while reducing center platform time and open arm entries. Conversely, anxiolytic treatment maintained discrimination accuracy while increasing exploratory behavior in anxiety-provoking zones. Individual discrimination performance correlated negatively with baseline anxiety measures (r=-0.61, p<0.001), suggesting trait-dependent interactions between learning and emotional processing. This paradigm provides a time-efficient method for evaluating the bidirectional relationship between appetitive cognition and anxiety-related behaviors, offering applications for screening anxiolytic compounds and investigating neural mechanisms underlying motivation-anxiety interactions in neuropharmacological research.

title: Effect of ethanolic extract of Rubia peregrina L. (Rubiaceae) on monoamine-mediated behaviour.
abstract: Rubia peregrina L. (Rubiaceae), commonly known as wild madder, has been traditionally used in Mediterranean folk medicine for treating various neurological disorders, yet its neuropsychopharmacological properties remain poorly characterized. This study investigated the effects of ethanolic extract of R. peregrina aerial parts on monoamine-mediated behaviors in experimental animals. Male Swiss albino mice (n=8 per group) received oral administration of R. peregrina extract (100, 200, and 400 mg/kg) or vehicle control. Behavioral assessments were conducted using forced swimming test, tail suspension test, open field test, and elevated plus maze. Neurochemical analysis of brain monoamines (dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin) and their metabolites was performed using HPLC with electrochemical detection in frontal cortex, hippocampus, and striatum. Acute treatment with R. peregrina extract (200 and 400 mg/kg) significantly reduced immobility time in both forced swimming test (p<0.01) and tail suspension test (p<0.05), suggesting antidepressant-like activity. In the open field test, the extract increased locomotor activity and center exploration at 400 mg/kg (p<0.05). Neurochemical analysis revealed dose-dependent increases in dopamine and norepinephrine levels in frontal cortex and hippocampus, while serotonin concentrations were elevated specifically in hippocampus. The dopamine metabolite DOPAC was significantly reduced, indicating decreased dopamine turnover. These findings demonstrate that R. peregrina extract exhibits antidepressant-like properties mediated through modulation of central monoaminergic systems, particularly dopaminergic and noradrenergic neurotransmission, supporting its traditional neurotherapeutic applications.

title: Corticosterone urinalysis and nicotinic receptor modulation in rats.
abstract: Chronic stress and nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) dysfunction are implicated in numerous neuropsychiatric disorders, yet methodological limitations have hindered comprehensive investigation of their interactions. Non-invasive biomarkers for stress assessment combined with pharmacological tools for nAChR modulation could advance translational research in this field. We developed and validated a combined approach using urinary corticosterone measurement and selective nicotinic receptor ligands to examine stress-receptor interactions in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48). Animals were subjected to chronic unpredictable stress (CUS) for 14 days, with concurrent administration of either nicotine (0.4 mg/kg), mecamylamine (2.0 mg/kg), or saline via osmotic minipumps. Urine samples were collected every 48 hours using metabolic cages, and corticosterone levels were quantified using enzyme immunoassay. Nicotinic receptor binding was assessed using [³H]-epibatidine autoradiography in prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and striatum. CUS significantly elevated urinary corticosterone levels (285% increase, p<0.001), which correlated strongly with plasma measurements (r=0.87, p<0.001). Chronic nicotine treatment attenuated stress-induced corticosterone elevation by 40% (p<0.01), while mecamylamine exacerbated the response by 60% (p<0.001). Autoradiographic analysis revealed region-specific alterations in nAChR density following stress exposure, with significant reductions in hippocampal CA1 and increases in prefrontal cortical regions. These findings demonstrate that urinary corticosterone provides a reliable, non-invasive biomarker for chronic stress assessment and reveal bidirectional modulation between stress systems and nicotinic receptors, supporting the utility of this methodological approach for investigating stress-addiction comorbidity.

title: Measurement of kinetically resolved vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux using rotating disk electrode voltammetry.
abstract: Understanding the kinetic properties of vesicular dopamine transport is essential for elucidating the mechanisms underlying dopamine storage and release in presynaptic terminals. Traditional methods for measuring vesicular monoamine transporter (VMAT2) activity rely on radiotracer uptake assays that lack temporal resolution and cannot distinguish between uptake and efflux processes in real-time. We developed a novel electrochemical approach using rotating disk electrode (RDE) voltammetry to directly monitor dopamine uptake and efflux from isolated synaptic vesicles with subsecond temporal resolution. Synaptic vesicles were isolated from rat striatum using standard differential centrifugation and incubated in ATP-containing buffer. A glassy carbon RDE was positioned in the vesicle suspension and rotated at controlled speeds (400-1600 rpm) while applying potential sweeps to detect dopamine oxidation currents. Vesicular dopamine uptake was initiated by ATP addition and monitored as decreased free dopamine concentration in the medium. Efflux was measured following addition of the VMAT2 inhibitor reserpine or the protonophore FCCP. This method successfully resolved biphasic dopamine uptake kinetics, revealing a rapid initial phase (Km = 0.8 ± 0.2 μM) and a slower secondary component. Reserpine treatment resulted in dose-dependent inhibition of uptake (IC50 = 45 nM) and stimulated dopamine efflux with kinetics consistent with simple diffusion. RDE voltammetry provides unprecedented temporal resolution for studying vesicular dopamine transport and offers significant advantages over conventional approaches for investigating the pharmacology and regulation of VMAT2.

title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning II: nicotinic and muscarinic modulations of atropine-induced disruption of the degraded contingency effect.
abstract: The cholinergic system plays a critical role in associative learning and memory consolidation, yet the differential contributions of nicotinic and muscarinic receptors to fear conditioning remain poorly understood. Previous work demonstrated that atropine, a muscarinic antagonist, disrupts the degraded contingency effect—a phenomenon where unpaired unconditioned stimulus presentations during the intertrial interval reduce conditioned responding. The present study examined whether selective cholinergic agonists could rescue atropine-induced deficits in contingency learning. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received fear conditioning with either standard paired conditioning or degraded contingency training, where additional footshocks were presented during intertrial intervals. Animals were pretreated with atropine (50 mg/kg, i.p.) alone or in combination with either nicotine (0.4 mg/kg, s.c.) or the muscarinic agonist oxotremorine (0.1 mg/kg, s.c.) 30 minutes prior to training. Freezing behavior was assessed during tone presentations 24 hours later. Atropine eliminated the normal reduction in conditioned freezing observed with degraded contingency training compared to standard conditioning. Nicotine co-administration partially restored contingency sensitivity, producing intermediate freezing levels between standard and degraded groups. In contrast, oxotremorine fully reversed atropine's effects, restoring normal contingency discrimination. These findings suggest that muscarinic receptors are primarily responsible for processing temporal relationships between conditioned and unconditioned stimuli, while nicotinic mechanisms provide modulatory support. The results have implications for understanding cholinergic contributions to fear learning and may inform therapeutic approaches for anxiety disorders involving dysfunctional fear conditioning.

title: Trends in use of psychotropic medications among patients treated with cholinesterase inhibitors in Japan from 2002 to 2010.
abstract: **Background:** Cholinesterase inhibitors (ChEIs) are widely prescribed for dementia treatment, but concurrent psychotropic medication use remains poorly characterized in Japanese populations. This study examined temporal trends in psychotropic medication prescribing patterns among Japanese patients receiving ChEIs over an 8-year period. **Methods:** We conducted a retrospective analysis using the Japanese National Database of Health Insurance Claims from 2002 to 2010. Patients aged ≥65 years with at least one ChEI prescription were included (n=147,892). Psychotropic medications were categorized as antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, and hypnotics. Temporal trends were analyzed using joinpoint regression, and logistic regression models examined factors associated with psychotropic co-prescribing. **Results:** The proportion of ChEI users receiving concurrent psychotropic medications increased from 43.2% in 2002 to 61.7% in 2010 (p<0.001). Antipsychotic use showed the steepest increase (18.3% to 35.9%), followed by antidepressants (12.1% to 24.3%). Traditional antipsychotics decreased from 78.4% to 45.2% of all antipsychotic prescriptions, while atypical agents increased correspondingly. Anxiolytic and hypnotic prescribing remained relatively stable. Factors independently associated with psychotropic use included younger age, female gender, urban residence, and hospital-based care. Polypharmacy (≥5 psychotropic classes) affected 8.7% of patients by 2010. **Conclusions:** Psychotropic medication use among Japanese ChEI recipients increased substantially from 2002 to 2010, particularly antipsychotics and antidepressants. The shift toward atypical antipsychotics suggests evolving prescribing practices, though clinical appropriateness requires further evaluation. These findings highlight the need for evidence-based guidelines regarding psychotropic co-prescribing in dementia care.

title: Investigation of Potential Antioxidant, Thrombolytic and Neuropharmacological Activities of Homalomena aromatica Leaves Using Experimental and In Silico Approaches.
abstract: Homalomena aromatica (Schott) Hook.f., a medicinal plant traditionally used in Southeast Asian folk medicine, has been reported to possess various therapeutic properties, yet its neuropharmacological potential remains largely unexplored. This study aimed to investigate the antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological activities of H. aromatica leaf extracts using both experimental and computational approaches. Methanolic extracts of H. aromatica leaves were prepared and subjected to phytochemical screening, followed by in vitro antioxidant assays including DPPH, ABTS, and ferric reducing antioxidant power (FRAP) methods. Thrombolytic activity was evaluated using the clot lysis assay, while neuropharmacological effects were assessed through hole-board, open field, elevated plus maze, and tail suspension tests in Swiss albino mice. Additionally, molecular docking studies were performed against key neurological targets including acetylcholinesterase, monoamine oxidase-A, and GABA-A receptors. The methanolic extract demonstrated significant antioxidant activity with IC₅₀ values of 24.31 ± 1.42 μg/mL and 31.67 ± 2.15 μg/mL for DPPH and ABTS assays, respectively. Thrombolytic activity showed 47.23 ± 3.21% clot lysis at 500 μg/mL concentration. Behavioral studies revealed dose-dependent anxiolytic and antidepressant-like effects at 200 and 400 mg/kg body weight. Molecular docking analysis identified several bioactive compounds with favorable binding affinities to neurological targets, supporting the observed pharmacological activities. These findings suggest that H. aromatica leaves possess promising antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological properties, warranting further investigation for potential therapeutic applications in neurological disorders.

title: [Research progress in neuropsychopharmacology updated for the post-genomic era].
abstract: The completion of the Human Genome Project in 2003 has fundamentally transformed neuropsychopharmacological research, necessitating a comprehensive evaluation of current methodological approaches and therapeutic targets. Traditional pharmacological strategies based on single-target hypotheses are increasingly supplemented by systems-level approaches that incorporate genomic, proteomic, and metabolomic data. This review examines key developments in neuropsychopharmacology from 2003 to 2009, focusing on how post-genomic technologies have influenced drug discovery and clinical practice. We conducted a systematic analysis of peer-reviewed publications, clinical trial databases, and regulatory approvals during this period, emphasizing advances in psychiatric and neurological disorders. Major findings include the identification of novel genetic variants associated with drug metabolism and efficacy, particularly in cytochrome P450 enzymes and neurotransmitter receptors. Pharmacogenomic approaches have revealed significant inter-individual variability in responses to antidepressants, antipsychotics, and anticonvulsants, with response rates varying 3-5 fold based on genetic polymorphisms. Additionally, genome-wide association studies have identified previously unknown molecular pathways involved in schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and Alzheimer's disease, leading to 12 new therapeutic targets currently in Phase II clinical trials. Proteomic analyses have enhanced our understanding of synaptic dysfunction and neuroinflammation mechanisms. These post-genomic insights are driving the development of personalized medicine approaches in neuropsychopharmacology, though significant challenges remain in translating genomic discoveries into clinical applications. The integration of multi-omics data represents a promising frontier for next-generation neuropsychiatric therapeutics.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: Validation of a fluorescence-based high-throughput assay for the measurement of neurotransmitter transporter uptake activity.
abstract: Current methods for measuring neurotransmitter transporter uptake activity rely primarily on radioactive substrates, which present safety concerns and disposal challenges while limiting throughput capacity. We developed and validated a fluorescence-based assay for high-throughput measurement of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine transporter activity using the fluorescent substrate 4-(4-(dimethylamino)styryl)-N-methylpyridinium (ASP+). HEK293 cells stably expressing human DAT, SERT, or NET were cultured in 96-well plates and incubated with ASP+ in the presence or absence of specific transporter inhibitors. Fluorescence accumulation was measured using a microplate reader with excitation at 475 nm and emission at 605 nm. The assay demonstrated excellent correlation with traditional [³H]-substrate uptake methods (r² > 0.92 for all three transporters). IC₅₀ values for cocaine, GBR12909, paroxetine, and desipramine obtained using the fluorescence assay were within 2-fold of published radioligand binding values. The assay exhibited high signal-to-background ratios (>10-fold), low inter- and intra-assay variability (CV < 15%), and Z'-factors consistently above 0.6, indicating excellent assay quality for screening applications. Linear uptake kinetics were observed for 30 minutes at 37°C, with Km values of 8.2 ± 1.4 μM, 12.1 ± 2.3 μM, and 9.7 ± 1.8 μM for DAT, SERT, and NET, respectively. This fluorescence-based method provides a safer, more cost-effective alternative to radioactive approaches while enabling high-throughput screening of neurotransmitter transporter modulators for drug discovery applications.

title: The rotational model and microdialysis: Significance for dopamine signalling, clinical studies, and beyond.
abstract: The rotational model using unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) lesions has served as a cornerstone of Parkinson's disease research for over four decades, while in vivo microdialysis has emerged as the gold standard for monitoring extracellular neurotransmitter dynamics. However, the integration of these methodologies and their translational relevance to clinical dopaminergic dysfunction remains incompletely characterized. We systematically examined dopamine signaling patterns using concurrent rotational behavioral testing and striatal microdialysis in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) following unilateral 6-OHDA lesions. Microdialysis probes were implanted bilaterally in the dorsal striatum, and extracellular dopamine levels were monitored during baseline conditions and following pharmacological challenges with apomorphine (0.05-0.5 mg/kg, s.c.) and L-DOPA (25-100 mg/kg, i.p.). Lesioned animals exhibited >95% dopamine depletion on the lesioned side and developed robust ipsilateral rotation following apomorphine administration. Microdialysis revealed a 20-fold increase in dopamine sensitivity on the denervated side, with paradoxically enhanced extracellular dopamine responses despite massive terminal loss. Contralateral rotational behavior correlated strongly with the interhemispheric dopamine gradient (r=0.84, p<0.001) rather than absolute dopamine levels. L-DOPA treatment normalized both rotational asymmetry and dopamine imbalance in a dose-dependent manner. These findings demonstrate that the rotational model reflects dynamic dopaminergic imbalances rather than static dopamine deficiency, supporting its continued utility for evaluating therapeutic interventions. The enhanced dopamine sensitivity observed in denervated striatum parallels clinical observations of dyskinesia development, validating this model's translational relevance for understanding both Parkinson's disease pathophysiology and treatment-related complications.

title: CSF neurochemicals during tryptophan depletion in individuals with remitted depression and healthy controls.
abstract: Tryptophan depletion has been widely used to investigate serotonergic mechanisms underlying depression, yet direct measurement of central serotonergic metabolites during depletion remains limited. This study examined cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) neurochemical changes following acute tryptophan depletion (ATD) in individuals with remitted major depression and healthy controls. Twenty-four medication-free participants with remitted depression (≥6 months) and 22 matched healthy controls underwent lumbar puncture before and 5 hours after consuming either a tryptophan-depleting amino acid mixture or balanced control mixture in a double-blind, crossover design. CSF levels of 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), homovanillic acid (HVA), and 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycol (MHPG) were measured using HPLC. Plasma tryptophan levels decreased by 85% following ATD in both groups. CSF 5-HIAA concentrations were significantly reduced after ATD compared to control conditions (p<0.001), with a greater magnitude of reduction in remitted depressed participants (-42%) versus controls (-28%, p=0.024). CSF HVA levels showed modest but significant decreases following ATD (p=0.031), while MHPG remained unchanged. The degree of CSF 5-HIAA reduction correlated with transient mood deterioration scores in the remitted depression group (r=0.52, p=0.009). These findings provide direct neurochemical evidence that ATD substantially reduces central serotonin turnover, with enhanced vulnerability in individuals with depression history. The differential CSF response may reflect underlying serotonergic dysfunction that persists during clinical remission, supporting theories of trait-dependent serotonergic abnormalities in recurrent depression.
journal: European neuropsychopharmacology : the journal of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: In vivo autofluorescence spectrofluorometry of central serotonin.
abstract: Serotonin (5-HT) plays crucial roles in mood regulation, sleep, and cognition, yet current methods for monitoring central 5-HT in living brain tissue remain limited by invasiveness or temporal resolution. While microdialysis provides accurate neurochemical measurements, it requires tissue disruption and offers poor temporal dynamics. We developed a novel approach utilizing in vivo autofluorescence spectrofluorometry to monitor endogenous serotonin levels in real-time within intact brain tissue. Male Spritz-Dawley rats (n=24, 250-300g) were anesthetized and implanted with custom fiber-optic probes (200μm core diameter) targeting the dorsal raphe nucleus and hippocampus. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using 280nm excitation with emission detection at 330-350nm, corresponding to tryptophan/5-HT spectral characteristics. Pharmacological validation employed systemic administration of fluoxetine (10mg/kg), p-chlorophenylalanine (150mg/kg), and 5-hydroxytryptophan (100mg/kg). Simultaneous microdialysis confirmed neurochemical changes. Autofluorescence signals showed characteristic peaks at 340nm that correlated significantly with microdialysis 5-HT measurements (r=0.78, p<0.001). Fluoxetine administration produced sustained fluorescence increases (142±18% of baseline), while p-chlorophenylalanine caused progressive signal reduction (67±12% at 24h). 5-HTP injection rapidly enhanced fluorescence intensity within 15 minutes. Signal stability was maintained for >6 hours with minimal photobleaching. These findings demonstrate that in vivo autofluorescence spectrofluorometry provides a minimally invasive method for real-time monitoring of central serotonin dynamics, offering superior temporal resolution compared to conventional techniques while preserving tissue integrity for chronic neurochemical studies.

title: Comparison of the effects of clozapine and 8-hydroxy-2-(di-n-propylamino)tetralin (8-OH-DPAT) on progressive ratio schedule performance: evidence against the involvement of 5-HT1A receptors in the behavioural effects of clozapine.
abstract: **Background and rationale:** Clozapine's unique clinical efficacy in treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been attributed to its complex pharmacological profile, including interactions with serotonergic systems. The 5-HT1A receptor has been proposed as a key mediator of clozapine's therapeutic and side effect profiles. However, direct behavioral comparisons between clozapine and selective 5-HT1A agonists remain limited. **Objectives:** We compared the effects of clozapine and the prototypical 5-HT1A receptor agonist 8-OH-DPAT on operant behavior using a progressive ratio (PR) schedule to assess motivational processes potentially relevant to negative symptoms of schizophrenia. **Methods:** Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) were trained on a PR schedule for food reinforcement. Following stable baseline performance, dose-response curves were established for clozapine (1.25-10 mg/kg, i.p.) and 8-OH-DPAT (0.03-1.0 mg/kg, s.c.). Breakpoints, response rates, and post-reinforcement pause durations were analyzed. Additional experiments examined whether the 5-HT1A antagonist WAY-100635 (0.3 mg/kg, s.c.) could attenuate clozapine's effects. **Results:** Clozapine dose-dependently decreased breakpoints and response rates while increasing post-reinforcement pauses. In contrast, 8-OH-DPAT produced a distinct behavioral profile, initially increasing response rates at low doses before causing decrements at higher doses. WAY-100635 pretreatment failed to significantly alter clozapine's effects on any measure. **Conclusions:** The differential behavioral profiles of clozapine and 8-OH-DPAT, combined with the lack of antagonism by WAY-100635, suggest that 5-HT1A receptor activation does not primarily mediate clozapine's effects on motivated behavior in this paradigm.

title: Determination of high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) and choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) activity in the same population of cultured cells.
abstract: Cholinergic neurons rely on high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) for acetylcholine synthesis, with choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) catalyzing the final synthetic step. While both processes are critical for cholinergic function, technical limitations have prevented simultaneous measurement in identical cell populations, potentially obscuring important regulatory relationships. We developed a novel dual-assay protocol to sequentially measure HACU and ChAT activity in the same cultured cholinergic neurons, enabling direct correlation of these functionally linked processes. Primary septal cholinergic neurons were cultured from embryonic day 18 rat brains and maintained for 7-14 days in vitro. HACU was first assessed using [³H]-choline uptake assays in the presence or absence of hemicholinium-3 to determine high-affinity transport. Following washout, cells were harvested and ChAT activity measured using standard radioenzymatic assays with [¹⁴C]-acetyl-CoA and choline substrates. Protein content was determined for normalization. The sequential protocol yielded reproducible measurements with minimal cross-contamination between assays. HACU rates correlated positively with ChAT activity (r=0.78, p<0.001) across different culture conditions and developmental time points. Chronic exposure to nerve growth factor increased both HACU (2.3-fold) and ChAT activity (2.8-fold) proportionally, while muscarinic receptor stimulation selectively enhanced HACU without affecting ChAT levels. This methodology provides a valuable tool for investigating cholinergic regulation and demonstrates coordinate but distinct control mechanisms governing choline transport and acetylcholine synthetic capacity in individual neuronal populations.

title: In vivo monitoring of extracellular glutamate in the brain with a microsensor.
abstract: Glutamate is the primary excitatory neurotransmitter in the mammalian central nervous system, and alterations in extracellular glutamate concentrations are implicated in various neurological disorders including stroke, epilepsy, and neurodegenerative diseases. Traditional methods for measuring extracellular glutamate, such as microdialysis, suffer from poor temporal resolution and tissue damage. We developed and characterized a novel glutamate microsensor based on glutamate oxidase immobilized on a platinum microelectrode for real-time monitoring of extracellular glutamate in the living brain. The microsensor exhibited high sensitivity (detection limit 0.5 μM), selectivity for glutamate over other amino acids (>95%), and rapid response time (<2 seconds). In anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8), we implanted the microsensor into the hippocampal CA1 region and monitored basal glutamate levels and stimulus-evoked responses. Basal extracellular glutamate concentrations were 2.3 ± 0.4 μM, consistent with previous microdialysis studies. High-frequency electrical stimulation of the Schaffer collaterals produced rapid, transient increases in glutamate concentration (peak amplitude 8.2 ± 1.1 μM, duration 12.4 ± 2.1 seconds). Administration of the glutamate uptake inhibitor PDC (1 mM) via reverse microdialysis resulted in a 3-fold increase in both basal and evoked glutamate responses. The microsensor remained stable for recordings lasting up to 6 hours with minimal tissue reaction. These results demonstrate that glutamate microsensors provide a valuable tool for investigating glutamatergic neurotransmission with high spatial and temporal resolution in vivo, offering new opportunities for understanding glutamate dynamics in physiological and pathological conditions.

title: Dextromethorphan-quinidine-responsive pseudobulbar affect (PBA): psychopharmacological model for wide-ranging disorders of emotional expression?
abstract: Pseudobulbar affect (PBA) is characterized by involuntary, exaggerated episodes of laughing and/or crying that are incongruent with underlying emotional state, commonly occurring in patients with neurological conditions including amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, multiple sclerosis, and traumatic brain injury. The recent FDA approval of dextromethorphan-quinidine (DM/Q) for PBA treatment provides new insights into the neurochemical basis of emotional dysregulation. We conducted a comprehensive review of clinical trials and mechanistic studies to evaluate whether DM/Q's therapeutic effects in PBA might inform treatment approaches for broader disorders of emotional expression. Analysis of randomized controlled trials (n=326 patients) demonstrated significant reductions in PBA episode frequency (45-49% decrease vs placebo, p<0.001) and severity scores.
