Mechanistically, dextromethorphan's activity as an NMDA receptor antagonist and sigma-1 receptor agonist, combined with quinidine's role in inhibiting cytochrome P450 2D6 metabolism, appears to modulate glutamatergic and monoaminergic pathways involved in emotional processing and motor expression. The drug's effects on cortico-pontine-cerebellar circuits implicated in emotional motor control may extend beyond classical PBA presentations. Emerging case reports suggest potential efficacy in pathological laughing and crying associated with psychiatric conditions, post-stroke emotional incontinence, and treatment-resistant mood disorders with emotional lability. These findings support a hypothesis that dysregulation of glutamate-mediated emotional expression networks represents a common pathway across diverse neuropsychiatric conditions. DM/Q may serve as a pharmacological probe for investigating emotional dysregulation mechanisms and could potentially expand therapeutic options for disorders characterized by inappropriate emotional expression beyond traditional PBA indications.

title: Evaluation of an osmotic pump for microdialysis sampling in an awake and untethered rat.
abstract: Microdialysis sampling in freely moving animals remains technically challenging due to the need for continuous perfusion while maintaining behavioral mobility. Traditional tethering systems limit natural movement patterns and may introduce stress artifacts that confound neurochemical measurements. We developed and validated an osmotic pump-based microdialysis system for continuous sampling in awake, untethered rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with guide cannulae targeting the nucleus accumbens shell. Alzet osmotic pumps (model 2ML1, 10 μL/h) containing artificial cerebrospinal fluid were subcutaneously implanted and connected via polyethylene tubing to CMA/11 microdialysis probes. Dialysate collection was achieved using miniaturized fraction collectors attached to the animals. The system was validated by measuring basal extracellular dopamine and dopamine metabolite levels over 24-hour periods and comparing responses to systemic amphetamine (2 mg/kg, i.p.) in both tethered and untethered conditions. The osmotic pump system maintained stable flow rates (9.8 ± 0.4 μL/h) throughout the experimental period. Basal dopamine levels in untethered animals (2.1 ± 0.3 nM) were significantly lower than in tethered controls (3.7 ± 0.5 nM, p<0.01), suggesting reduced stress-induced release. Amphetamine-stimulated dopamine release showed similar peak responses between conditions but more rapid recovery in untethered animals. Locomotor activity patterns were markedly more natural in untethered subjects. This osmotic pump-based approach provides a reliable method for extended microdialysis sampling in freely moving rats, reducing stress-related confounds while maintaining temporal resolution suitable for pharmacological studies.

title: The role of 5-HT₁A receptors in fish oil-mediated increased BDNF expression in the rat hippocampus and cortex: a possible antidepressant mechanism.
abstract: Fish oil supplementation has demonstrated antidepressant-like effects in preclinical and clinical studies, yet the underlying neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. Brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) upregulation represents a key mechanism of antidepressant action, while 5-HT₁A receptors play crucial roles in mood regulation and neuroplasticity. This study investigated whether 5-HT₁A receptors mediate fish oil-induced BDNF expression in limbic brain regions. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10/group) received daily fish oil supplementation (300 mg/kg, p.o.) or vehicle for 21 days, with or without co-administration of the selective 5-HT₁A receptor antagonist WAY-100635 (0.3 mg/kg, s.c.). BDNF protein and mRNA levels were quantified in hippocampus and prefrontal cortex using Western blotting and quantitative RT-PCR. 5-HT₁A receptor binding was assessed by autoradiography using [³H]8-OH-DPAT. Fish oil treatment significantly increased BDNF protein expression in CA1 (142% of control, p<0.01), CA3 (135% of control, p<0.05), and dentate gyrus (156% of control, p<0.001) hippocampal subregions, as well as in prefrontal cortex (128% of control, p<0.05). Corresponding increases in BDNF mRNA were observed in these regions. WAY-100635 co-treatment completely blocked fish oil-induced BDNF upregulation in all brain areas examined without affecting basal BDNF levels. Fish oil supplementation did not alter 5-HT₁A receptor binding density. These findings demonstrate that 5-HT₁A receptor activation is necessary for fish oil-mediated BDNF enhancement in rat limbic structures, suggesting a novel mechanism underlying the antidepressant properties of omega-3 fatty acids involving serotonergic-neurotrophic factor interactions.

title: Validation of a robust and sensitive method for detecting hydroxyl radical formation together with evoked neurotransmitter release in brain microdialysis.
abstract: The simultaneous detection of hydroxyl radical formation and neurotransmitter release in vivo remains technically challenging due to the short half-life of hydroxyl radicals and the requirement for sensitive analytical methods. We developed and validated a microdialysis approach combining salicylate trapping with high-performance liquid chromatography-electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) to concurrently measure hydroxyl radical production and dopamine release in the rat striatum. Male Spritz-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with microdialysis probes perfused with artificial cerebrospinal fluid containing 2.0 mM salicylate. Hydroxyl radical formation was quantified by measuring 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid (2,3-DHBA), the hydroxylated product of salicylate, while dopamine was simultaneously analyzed using HPLC-ECD. Method validation demonstrated linear responses for both 2,3-DHBA (r²=0.998, range 0.5-50 nM) and dopamine (r²=0.995, range 1-100 nM) with detection limits of 0.2 nM and 0.5 nM, respectively. Intraday and interday coefficients of variation were <8% for both analytes. Potassium-evoked depolarization (100 mM KCl) increased dopamine release by 450±65% above baseline while simultaneously elevating 2,3-DHBA formation by 180±25%. Iron-induced oxidative stress (10 μM FeCl₂) produced a 300±40% increase in hydroxyl radical formation with minimal effect on basal dopamine levels. The method demonstrated excellent temporal resolution (5-minute sampling) and reproducibility across multiple experimental sessions. This validated approach provides a robust tool for investigating the relationship between oxidative stress and neurotransmission in neurodegeneration models and therapeutic interventions.

title: Transport mechanisms governing serotonin clearance in vivo revealed by high-speed chronoamperometry.
abstract: The clearance of synaptically released serotonin (5-HT) is primarily mediated by the serotonin transporter (SERT), though other mechanisms may contribute to neurotransmitter homeostasis. Despite extensive pharmacological characterization of SERT in vitro, the kinetics and relative contributions of different clearance mechanisms in vivo remain poorly understood. We employed high-speed chronoamperometry with carbon fiber microelectrodes to examine 5-HT clearance dynamics in the rat dorsal raphe nucleus and projection regions following local pressure ejection of exogenous 5-HT. Clearance kinetics were analyzed using Michaelis-Menten modeling under control conditions and following pharmacological manipulation of putative clearance mechanisms. Under control conditions, 5-HT clearance exhibited saturable kinetics with an apparent Km of 1.8 ± 0.3 μM and Vmax of 0.45 ± 0.08 μM/s in the dorsal raphe. The SERT-selective inhibitor citalopram (10 mg/kg, i.p.) reduced clearance rate by 89 ± 4%, while the norepinephrine transporter blocker desipramine had no significant effect. 6-Nitroquipazine, a SERT inhibitor, produced dose-dependent reductions in clearance (IC50 = 2.1 μM). Destruction of 5-HT terminals with 5,7-dihydroxytryptamine reduced clearance capacity by 78 ± 6%. Mathematical modeling indicated that SERT accounts for >85% of 5-HT clearance under physiological conditions, with minor contributions from non-specific uptake mechanisms. These findings provide the first direct kinetic characterization of 5-HT clearance mechanisms in vivo and establish high-speed chronoamperometry as a valuable tool for studying monoamine transport dynamics in intact neural tissue.

title: The footfault test as a screening tool in the 6-hydroxydopamine rat model of Parkinson's disease.
abstract: The 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) rat model is widely used to study Parkinson's disease pathophysiology and evaluate potential therapeutics. However, current behavioral assessments often lack sensitivity to detect mild motor deficits or require extensive training and equipment. The footfault test, which measures limb placement errors during locomotion across a grid, represents a potentially useful screening tool for dopaminergic dysfunction. We evaluated the sensitivity and reliability of the footfault test in detecting motor impairments following unilateral 6-OHDA lesions in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24). Animals received either 6-OHDA (8 μg in 4 μl) or vehicle injections into the medial forebrain bundle. Footfault testing was performed weekly for 4 weeks post-lesion alongside rotational behavior assessment and cylinder test. Tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry confirmed striatal dopamine depletion. 6-OHDA-lesioned animals showed significant increases in contralateral forelimb footfaults compared to controls, with deficits emerging at 1 week post-lesion (vehicle: 12.3±2.1% vs. 6-OHDA: 28.7±4.2%, p<0.01). Footfault scores correlated strongly with amphetamine-induced rotations (r=0.78, p<0.001) and striatal dopamine depletion (r=-0.72, p<0.001). Test-retest reliability was high (r=0.89), and the procedure required minimal training compared to other motor assessments. These findings demonstrate that the footfault test provides a sensitive, reliable, and easily implemented screening tool for motor dysfunction in the 6-OHDA rat model, making it valuable for both research applications and therapeutic screening protocols in Parkinson's disease studies.

title: Name that neurotransmitter: using music to teach psychopharmacology concepts.
abstract:
Psychopharmacology education presents significant challenges for nursing students due to the complexity of neurotransmitter systems and drug mechanisms. Traditional didactic approaches often result in poor retention and difficulty applying concepts to clinical practice. This study evaluated the effectiveness of a novel musical mnemonic intervention for teaching neurotransmitter functions and associated psychotropic medications in undergraduate nursing education.

A randomized controlled trial was conducted with 120 junior-level nursing students enrolled in psychiatric-mental health nursing courses at two universities. Participants were randomly assigned to either a traditional lecture-based control group or an experimental group receiving the "Name That Neurotransmitter" musical intervention, which incorporated original songs linking neurotransmitter functions, pathways, and corresponding medications. Pre- and post-intervention assessments measured knowledge retention using a validated 40-item psychopharmacology examination. Student engagement and perceived learning effectiveness were evaluated through Likert-scale surveys and focus groups.

Students in the musical intervention group demonstrated significantly higher post-test scores compared to controls (82.3 ± 8.7 vs. 74.1 ± 9.2, p < 0.001). Knowledge retention at 6-week follow-up remained superior in the intervention group (79.8 ± 8.1 vs. 71.5 ± 10.3, p < 0.01). Qualitative analysis revealed increased student engagement, reduced anxiety about psychopharmacology content, and improved confidence in medication teaching abilities. Students reported the musical approach enhanced their ability to remember complex neurotransmitter-drug relationships during clinical rotations.

Musical mnemonics represent a promising pedagogical strategy for psychopharmacology education in nursing curricula. This innovative approach may improve knowledge retention and clinical application of complex neuropharmacological concepts, ultimately enhancing patient safety and medication management competencies.

title: Can psychopharmacological treatment change personality traits in patients with panic disorder?
abstract: **Background:** Panic disorder (PD) is frequently associated with specific personality traits, particularly neuroticism and harm avoidance, which may influence treatment outcomes and long-term prognosis. While psychopharmacological interventions effectively reduce panic symptoms, their impact on underlying personality dimensions remains poorly understood. **Objective:** To investigate whether successful pharmacological treatment of panic disorder produces measurable changes in personality traits. **Methods:** We conducted a 12-week prospective study of 84 patients diagnosed with PD according to DSM-IV criteria. Participants were randomized to receive either sertraline (n=42, 50-200mg/day) or clonazepam (n=42, 1-4mg/day). Personality traits were assessed using the NEO-PI-R and Temperament and Character Inventory (TCI) at baseline, 6 weeks, and 12 weeks. Panic severity was measured using the Panic Disorder Severity Scale (PDSS). **Results:** Both treatment groups showed significant reductions in PDSS scores (p<0.001). Patients receiving sertraline demonstrated significant decreases in neuroticism scores (baseline: 142.3±18.7 vs. 12-week: 128.6±20.1, p=0.003) and TCI harm avoidance (baseline: 22.8±5.2 vs. 12-week: 18.9±6.1, p=0.007). The clonazepam group showed modest but non-significant personality changes. Improvement in panic symptoms correlated with personality trait changes (r=0.52, p<0.01). Patients achieving clinical remission (PDSS≤7) showed greater personality trait improvements compared to partial responders. **Conclusions:** Successful antidepressant treatment of panic disorder appears to facilitate adaptive changes in personality traits, particularly reducing neuroticism and harm avoidance. These findings suggest that effective pharmacotherapy may produce benefits extending beyond symptom reduction to fundamental aspects of personality structure.
journal: Revista brasileira de psiquiatria (Sao Paulo, Brazil : 1999)

title: Neurochemical signatures revealed by glutamine labeling in the chicken retina.
abstract: The chicken retina serves as an excellent model system for understanding retinal neurochemistry due to its well-characterized laminar organization and absence of retinal vasculature. Glutamine, synthesized exclusively by Müller glial cells, represents a critical metabolic precursor for neurotransmitter glutamate and GABA synthesis in retinal neurons. To investigate the spatial distribution and metabolic fate of glutamine in retinal circuitry, we employed [³H]glutamine autoradiography combined with pharmacological manipulations in isolated chicken retina preparations. Retinal explants were incubated with 50 μCi/ml [³H]glutamine for 30-120 minutes under various conditions, including co-incubation with glutamine synthetase inhibitors (methionine sulfoximine) and glutaminase blockers (6-diazo-5-oxo-L-norleucine). Autoradiographic analysis revealed intense labeling in Müller cell bodies and processes, with prominent accumulation in the inner nuclear layer and around photoreceptor inner segments. Glutamine synthetase inhibition resulted in 78% reduction in glial labeling, while glutaminase blockade increased glutamine accumulation by 145% in ganglion cells and amacrine cells. Double-labeling experiments with cell-specific markers demonstrated preferential glutamine uptake by GABAergic amacrine cells compared to glycinergic subtypes. Time-course analysis showed rapid conversion of glutamine to glutamate within 15 minutes in bipolar and ganglion cells. These findings establish distinct neurochemical compartments within the chicken retina and demonstrate the central role of glutamine in maintaining excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitter pools, providing crucial insights into retinal metabolic organization.

title: Extracellular trypsin increases ASIC1a selectivity for monovalent versus divalent cations.
abstract: Acid-sensing ion channels (ASICs) are proton-gated sodium channels that play critical roles in synaptic transmission, pain perception, and neurodegeneration. ASIC1a, the predominant neuronal subtype, exhibits modest selectivity for monovalent over divalent cations under physiological conditions. Recent evidence suggests that extracellular proteases may modulate ASIC function, though the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. We investigated whether trypsin treatment alters the ionic selectivity properties of heterologously expressed ASIC1a channels in Chinese hamster ovary cells using whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology. ASIC1a currents were recorded in response to pH drops from 7.4 to 6.0 before and after exposure to 10 μg/ml trypsin for 5 minutes. Ion selectivity was assessed by measuring reversal potentials and relative permeabilities using bi-ionic substitution protocols with Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+. Untreated ASIC1a channels showed PNa/PCa and PNa/PMg ratios of 3.2 ± 0.4 and 4.1 ± 0.5, respectively (n=12). Following trypsin treatment, these ratios increased significantly to 8.7 ± 1.2 and 10.3 ± 1.4 (p<0.001), indicating enhanced discrimination against divalent cations. Trypsin also reduced steady-state inactivation and increased the pH50 for activation from 6.2 to 6.0. The selectivity changes were prevented by the serine protease inhibitor PMSF and were irreversible upon washout. These findings demonstrate that extracellular trypsin proteolytically modifies ASIC1a channels to enhance monovalent cation selectivity, potentially representing a novel mechanism for activity-dependent modulation of acid-sensing channel function in the nervous system.

title: From systems biology to dynamical neuropharmacology: proposal for a new methodology.
abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches have relied heavily on reductionist methodologies that examine individual molecular targets in isolation, often failing to capture the complex, nonlinear dynamics of neural networks and their responses to pharmacological interventions. The emerging field of systems biology offers powerful computational and experimental tools that could revolutionize drug discovery and therapeutic strategies for neurological and psychiatric disorders. We propose a novel methodological framework that integrates high-throughput genomic and proteomic data with mathematical modeling of neural circuit dynamics to create a comprehensive "dynamical neuropharmacology" approach. Our methodology combines real-time monitoring of gene expression patterns using microarray analysis, protein interaction network mapping, and computational modeling of synaptic plasticity in response to drug treatments. We applied this framework to analyze the effects of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) in primary hippocampal cultures and validated findings in a chronic mild stress rodent model of depression. Results demonstrate that SSRI action involves time-dependent reorganization of transcriptional networks affecting not only serotonergic signaling but also GABA-ergic and glutamatergic systems, with distinct temporal phases occurring over minutes to weeks. Network analysis revealed previously unidentified hub proteins that may represent novel therapeutic targets. This systems-level approach identified biomarkers predictive of treatment response and revealed mechanistic insights into delayed therapeutic onset. Our dynamical neuropharmacology methodology provides a paradigm shift toward understanding drug action as emergent properties of complex biological networks rather than simple receptor-ligand interactions.

title: Proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy detection of neurotransmitters in dorsomedial medulla correlate with spontaneous baroreceptor reflex function.
abstract: The dorsomedial medulla (DMM) contains critical cardiovascular regulatory neurons, but the neurochemical basis of spontaneous baroreceptor reflex (BRR) function remains poorly understood. We hypothesized that neurotransmitter concentrations in the DMM would correlate with spontaneous BRR sensitivity in conscious rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24, 300-350g) were implanted with arterial catheters for continuous blood pressure monitoring. After 5 days recovery, spontaneous BRR sensitivity was assessed using sequence analysis of spontaneous fluctuations in systolic blood pressure and heart rate over 60 minutes. Animals were then anesthetized and underwent proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (¹H-MRS) using a 7.0-Tesla scanner with a custom-built surface coil positioned over the DMM (2×2×2 mm voxel). Spectra were acquired using PRESS sequence (TR=3000ms, TE=30ms) and analyzed for glutamate, GABA, glycine, and N-acetylaspartate concentrations using LCModel software. Spontaneous BRR sensitivity ranged from 0.8 to 3.2 ms/mmHg. DMM glutamate concentrations showed significant positive correlation with BRR sensitivity (r=0.67, P<0.01), while GABA concentrations demonstrated negative correlation (r=-0.54, P<0.05). Glycine levels were not significantly correlated with BRR function (r=0.23, P=0.28). The glutamate/GABA ratio strongly predicted BRR sensitivity (r=0.74, P<0.001). N-acetylaspartate concentrations remained stable across animals, confirming tissue viability. These findings demonstrate that excitatory/inhibitory neurotransmitter balance in the DMM directly relates to spontaneous cardiovascular reflex function, supporting the critical role of this brainstem region in blood pressure regulation and suggesting potential therapeutic targets for hypertension.

title: Developmental exposure to a serotonin agonist produces subsequent behavioral and neurochemical changes in the adult male prairie vole.
abstract: Prairie voles (Microtus ochrogaster) exhibit socially monogamous behavior regulated by complex neurochemical systems, including serotonergic pathways that undergo critical developmental programming during early postnatal life. The present study examined whether developmental exposure to the selective serotonin receptor agonist 8-OH-DPAT alters subsequent adult social behavior and neurochemistry in male prairie voles. Male pups received daily subcutaneous injections of 8-OH-DPAT (0.5 mg/kg) or saline vehicle from postnatal day 1-14. At 60-70 days of age, subjects underwent standard partner preference testing to assess pair bonding behavior, followed by elevated plus maze testing for anxiety-like behavior. Brains were subsequently processed for measurement of serotonin transporter (SERT) binding density via autoradiography and 5-HT1A receptor expression in the paraventricular nucleus, nucleus accumbens, and prefrontal cortex using quantitative immunohistochemistry. Males exposed to developmental 8-OH-DPAT showed significantly reduced partner preference scores compared to controls (p<0.01) and spent less time in side-by-side contact during testing. 8-OH-DPAT-treated males also exhibited increased anxiety-like behavior, spending significantly less time in open arms of the elevated plus maze (p<0.05). Neurochemical analysis revealed decreased SERT binding density in the nucleus accumbens (-23%, p<0.05) and reduced 5-HT1A receptor immunoreactivity in the paraventricular nucleus (-18%, p<0.05) in treated animals. These findings demonstrate that early developmental serotonergic manipulations produce lasting alterations in adult social bonding capacity and associated neural substrates in prairie voles, suggesting critical periods for serotonergic programming of social behavior.

title: Analysis of neurotransmitter tissue content of Drosophila melanogaster in different life stages.
abstract: Neurotransmitter systems undergo dynamic changes during development, yet comprehensive analyses across complete life cycles remain limited in established model organisms. Drosophila melanogaster provides an ideal system to investigate developmental neurotransmitter profiles due to its well-characterized life stages and conserved neurochemical pathways. We quantified tissue concentrations of major neurotransmitters—dopamine, serotonin, octopamine, tyramine, GABA, and acetylcholine—across four distinct developmental stages: third-instar larvae, white pupae, pharate adults, and 3-day-old adults using high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) and enzymatic assays. Whole-organism extracts were analyzed from synchronized populations (n=8-12 per stage) of wild-type Canton-S flies. Dopamine levels increased dramatically during metamorphosis, rising 4.2-fold from larval to adult stages (p<0.001), with the steepest increase occurring during pupal development. Serotonin showed a biphasic pattern, peaking in white pupae (2.1-fold higher than larvae) before declining in adults. Octopamine and tyramine exhibited coordinated increases throughout development, reaching maximum concentrations in pharate adults. GABA levels remained relatively stable across stages, while acetylcholine showed moderate but significant increases during pupal stages (1.6-fold, p<0.05). These findings reveal stage-specific neurotransmitter profiles that likely reflect the extensive neural remodeling occurring during Drosophila metamorphosis. The dramatic dopamine increase coincides with adult-specific behaviors and motor control requirements, while the serotonin peak during pupation may regulate metamorphic processes. This comprehensive developmental neurotransmitter atlas provides a foundation for investigating how genetic mutations and environmental factors influence neurochemical maturation in this important model organism.

title: Advancing equity, diversity, and inclusion in the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP): advances, challenges, and opportunities to accelerate progress.
abstract: The American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP) has recognized the critical importance of advancing equity, diversity, and inclusion (EDI) within the field of neuropsychopharmacology to foster scientific excellence and address health disparities. This analysis examines the current state of EDI initiatives within ACNP and identifies strategies to accelerate progress. We conducted a comprehensive assessment of ACNP membership demographics, leadership representation, and EDI programming from 2015-2020, comparing these metrics to national benchmarks in biomedical sciences. Additionally, we surveyed ACNP members (n=847, 52% response rate) regarding perceived barriers to inclusion and effectiveness of existing EDI initiatives. Our analysis revealed persistent underrepresentation of women (32% of membership vs. 47% in biomedical sciences overall) and racial/ethnic minorities (18% vs. 28% nationally) within ACNP membership and leadership positions. However, recent EDI initiatives, including mentorship programs and diversity travel awards, showed positive trends with a 23% increase in underrepresented minority membership since 2018. Survey responses highlighted key barriers including limited networking opportunities (64%), inadequate mentorship (58%), and workplace bias (41%). Members rated ACNP's diversity programming favorably (mean score 4.2/5.0) but identified needs for enhanced career development resources and institutional policy advocacy. These findings demonstrate ACNP's commitment to EDI while revealing ongoing challenges. Recommendations include expanding mentorship networks, implementing bias training, and establishing measurable diversity targets. Sustained efforts to promote EDI are essential for advancing neuropsychopharmacology research and ensuring equitable representation in scientific leadership.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Cocaine increases stimulated dopamine release more in periadolescent than adult rats.
abstract: The adolescent brain exhibits heightened vulnerability to drug abuse, yet the neurochemical mechanisms underlying age-related differences in cocaine sensitivity remain poorly understood. Previous studies have demonstrated that adolescent rats show enhanced behavioral responses to psychostimulants compared to adults, suggesting developmental differences in dopaminergic signaling. This study examined whether cocaine differentially affects stimulated dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens of periadolescent (postnatal day 35-42) versus adult (postnatal day 70-85) male Sprague-Dawley rats. Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry was used to measure electrically-evoked dopamine release and uptake in acute brain slices before and after cocaine administration (1-30 μM). Baseline evoked dopamine release and uptake kinetics did not differ significantly between age groups. However, cocaine produced markedly greater enhancement of stimulated dopamine release in periadolescent compared to adult rats across all concentrations tested. At 10 μM cocaine, peak dopamine levels increased 285 ± 32% above baseline in periadolescent rats versus 198 ± 28% in adults (p < 0.01). This age-related difference was attributed primarily to greater cocaine-induced inhibition of the dopamine transporter in younger animals, as evidenced by prolonged dopamine clearance times. Maximal transport inhibition occurred at lower cocaine concentrations in periadolescent rats (IC50 = 4.2 μM) compared to adults (IC50 = 7.8 μM). These findings demonstrate that periadolescent rats exhibit enhanced dopaminergic responses to cocaine at the synaptic level, which may contribute to increased vulnerability to cocaine's reinforcing effects during adolescence and inform prevention strategies targeting this critical developmental period.

title: Role of GIRK channels on the noradrenergic transmission in vivo: an electrophysiological and neurochemical study on GIRK2 mutant mice.
abstract: G-protein-activated inwardly rectifying potassium (GIRK) channels mediate the inhibitory effects of various neurotransmitters and are abundantly expressed in noradrenergic neurons of the locus coeruleus. The GIRK2 subunit is essential for functional GIRK channel assembly, yet its specific role in noradrenergic transmission remains poorly understood. Here we investigated the contribution of GIRK2 channels to norepinephrine (NE) signaling using GIRK2 knockout mice (Girk2−/−) through combined electrophysiological and neurochemical approaches. In vivo single-unit recordings from locus coeruleus neurons revealed significantly increased spontaneous firing rates in Girk2−/− mice compared to wild-type controls (4.2 ± 0.3 Hz vs 2.8 ± 0.2 Hz, p<0.01). The α2-adrenergic agonist clonidine produced markedly reduced inhibition of firing in knockout animals (23% vs 71% reduction in wild-type, p<0.001). Microdialysis measurements in the prefrontal cortex showed elevated baseline NE levels in Girk2−/− mice (245 ± 18% of wild-type values) and enhanced NE release following acute stress exposure. High-performance liquid chromatography analysis revealed increased NE content in cortical and hippocampal tissues from knockout animals. Behaviorally, Girk2−/− mice exhibited hyperactivity and reduced anxiety-like behaviors consistent with enhanced noradrenergic tone. Western blot analysis confirmed the absence of GIRK2 protein in knockout tissue while showing compensatory increases in GIRK1 and GIRK3 subunit expression. These findings demonstrate that GIRK2-containing channels are critical regulators of noradrenergic neuron excitability and NE release, providing important insights into the molecular mechanisms controlling central noradrenergic transmission and its behavioral consequences.

title: [New directions in GABAergic pharmacology of anxiety and depression].
abstract: GABAergic neurotransmission dysfunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of anxiety and depressive disorders, yet conventional benzodiazepine therapeutics are associated with significant limitations including tolerance, dependence, and cognitive impairment. This study investigated novel pharmacological approaches targeting specific GABA receptor subtypes and allosteric modulation sites to develop more selective anxiolytic and antidepressant compounds. We synthesized and evaluated a series of α2/α3-selective GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulators and examined their behavioral effects in established rodent models. Male Wistar rats (n=8-12 per group) underwent elevated plus maze, forced swim test, and chronic mild stress paradigms following acute and chronic administration of test compounds (0.5-5.0 mg/kg, i.p.) compared to diazepam (2 mg/kg) and vehicle controls. Electrophysiological recordings from hippocampal and amygdalar slices assessed synaptic transmission changes. The α2/α3-selective compounds demonstrated significant anxiolytic efficacy comparable to diazepam in elevated plus maze testing (p<0.01) while showing reduced sedative and motor impairment effects. In depression models, chronic treatment (14 days) with lead compounds significantly reduced immobility time in forced swim tests and reversed anhedonic behaviors in stressed animals (p<0.05). Electrophysiological analysis revealed enhanced inhibitory postsynaptic currents in amygdalar neurons without tolerance development observed with classical benzodiazepines. These findings suggest that subtype-selective GABAergic modulation represents a promising therapeutic strategy for anxiety and depression treatment, potentially offering improved safety profiles and sustained efficacy compared to conventional anxiolytics.

title: Microdialysis in freely moving mice: determination of acetylcholine, serotonin and noradrenaline release in galanin transgenic mice.
abstract: Galanin, a 29-amino acid neuropeptide, has been implicated in the modulation of cholinergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic neurotransmission, but its precise role in regulating neurotransmitter release remains poorly understood. To investigate the effects of galanin overexpression on central neurotransmitter systems, we employed in vivo microdialysis to simultaneously monitor acetylcholine, serotonin, and noradrenaline release in freely moving transgenic mice expressing human galanin under the dopamine β-hydroxylase promoter. Concentric microdialysis probes were stereotaxically implanted into the hippocampus and frontal cortex of transgenic mice and wild-type littermates. Dialysate samples were collected every 20 minutes and analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection for monoamines and a chemiluminescent assay for acetylcholine. Baseline extracellular acetylcholine levels were significantly reduced by 35% in the hippocampus and 28% in the frontal cortex of galanin transgenic mice compared to controls. Potassium-evoked acetylcholine release was similarly attenuated in transgenic animals. Conversely, basal serotonin concentrations were elevated by 42% in the hippocampus of transgenic mice, while noradrenaline levels showed no significant differences between groups. Pharmacological challenge with the acetylcholinesterase inhibitor physostigmine produced a blunted response in transgenic mice, suggesting impaired cholinergic function. These findings demonstrate that chronic galanin overexpression selectively modulates neurotransmitter release in a region- and transmitter-specific manner, providing evidence for galanin's inhibitory influence on cholinergic transmission while facilitating serotonergic activity. This microdialysis approach in transgenic mice offers a valuable tool for investigating neuropeptide-neurotransmitter interactions in vivo.

title: Neuropathology in Alzheimer's disease: target of pharmacotherapy.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) represents the most prevalent form of dementia, affecting approximately 4.5 million Americans with projections indicating exponential growth as the population ages. The characteristic neuropathological hallmarks of AD include extracellular amyloid-beta (Aβ) plaques, intracellular neurofibrillary tangles composed of hyperphosphorylated tau protein, and progressive neuronal loss particularly in memory-related brain regions. Current pharmacotherapeutic approaches primarily target cholinergic dysfunction through acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and NMDA receptor modulation, yet these treatments provide only modest symptomatic relief without addressing underlying pathogenic mechanisms. This review examines emerging therapeutic strategies that directly target AD neuropathology, including anti-amyloid agents, tau-directed therapies, and neuroprotective compounds. Recent clinical trials investigating secretase inhibitors and immunotherapeutic approaches to reduce Aβ burden have shown mixed results, with some studies demonstrating reduced plaque load but limited cognitive improvement. Novel tau-targeting strategies, including kinase inhibitors and aggregation modulators, represent promising avenues for intervention. Additionally, antioxidant therapies and anti-inflammatory agents address the oxidative stress and neuroinflammation components of AD pathogenesis. While traditional symptomatic treatments remain the current standard of care, disease-modifying therapies targeting specific neuropathological features offer hope for more effective interventions. Future therapeutic success will likely require combination approaches addressing multiple pathogenic pathways simultaneously, along with earlier intervention during preclinical disease stages. Advanced understanding of AD neuropathology continues to inform rational drug design and optimize therapeutic targeting strategies.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Nurse Practitioners

title: Effects of the mGluR2/3 agonist LY379268 on ketamine-evoked behaviours and neurochemical changes in the dentate gyrus of the rat.
abstract: Metabotropic glutamate receptors (mGluRs) have emerged as potential therapeutic targets for schizophrenia, with group II mGluRs (mGluR2/3) showing particular promise in preclinical models. The NMDA receptor hypofunction hypothesis of schizophrenia suggests that ketamine-induced behavioral and neurochemical alterations may be attenuated by mGluR2/3 activation. The present study investigated whether the selective mGluR2/3 agonist LY379268 could reverse ketamine-evoked behavioral abnormalities and associated neurochemical changes in the rat dentate gyrus. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received subcutaneous injections of ketamine (10 mg/kg), LY379268 (3 mg/kg), or vehicle, either alone or in combination. Locomotor activity was assessed in automated activity chambers for 120 minutes post-injection. Microdialysis was performed in the dentate gyrus to measure extracellular glutamate and GABA levels. Ketamine administration significantly increased locomotor activity and elevated extracellular glutamate concentrations by 180% compared to controls, while reducing GABA levels by 35%. Pretreatment with LY379268 dose-dependently attenuated ketamine-induced hyperlocomotion by 65% and normalized glutamate efflux in the dentate gyrus. The mGluR2/3 agonist also partially restored GABA levels to near-baseline values. LY379268 alone had no significant effect on locomotor behavior or neurotransmitter levels. These findings demonstrate that mGluR2/3 activation can effectively counteract ketamine-induced behavioral and neurochemical alterations in the dentate gyrus, supporting the therapeutic potential of group II mGluR agonists for treating NMDA receptor hypofunction-related psychiatric disorders.

title: Neurochemically distinct myenteric neurone populations containing calbindin have specific distribution patterns around the circumference of the gastric corpus.
abstract: The myenteric plexus of the gastric corpus contains morphologically and neurochemically heterogeneous populations of neurons that regulate gastric motility and secretion. While previous studies have identified calbindin-immunoreactive neurons within the enteric nervous system, their precise distribution patterns and neurochemical phenotypes remain poorly characterized. We investigated the circumferential organization and neurochemical properties of calbindin-positive myenteric neurons in the rat gastric corpus using immunohistochemistry and confocal microscopy. Whole-mount preparations of gastric muscle layers from adult Wistar rats (n=8) were processed for multiple immunofluorescence labeling using antibodies against calbindin-D28K, choline acetyltransferase, nitric oxide synthase, vasoactive intestinal peptide, and substance P. Quantitative analysis revealed that calbindin-immunoreactive neurons comprised 23.4 ± 2.1% of total myenteric neurons and exhibited distinct regional variations around the gastric circumference. The highest density occurred along the greater curvature (34.2 ± 3.8 neurons/cm²), while the lowest density was observed near the lesser curvature (18.7 ± 2.3 neurons/cm²). Double-labeling experiments demonstrated three neurochemically distinct calbindin-positive populations: cholinergic motor neurons (41.2%), nitrergic inhibitory neurons (35.8%), and a population co-expressing vasoactive intestinal peptide (23.0%). Each subpopulation showed characteristic circumferential distribution patterns that correlated with regional differences in gastric wall innervation density. These findings reveal a previously unrecognized organizational principle of the gastric myenteric plexus and suggest that calbindin-containing neurons contribute to region-specific control of gastric function through distinct neurochemical mechanisms.

title: Toward a neuropsychological theory of antidepressant drug action: increase in positive emotional bias after potentiation of norepinephrine activity.
abstract: Current theories of antidepressant action focus primarily on monoamine neurotransmitter systems, yet the precise neuropsychological mechanisms underlying therapeutic efficacy remain poorly understood. We hypothesized that potentiation of norepinephrine activity produces acute changes in emotional processing that may precede clinical improvement. Twenty-four healthy volunteers participated in a double-blind, placebo-controlled, crossover study examining the effects of reboxetine (4 mg), a selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor, on emotional bias during cognitive tasks. Participants completed computerized assessments of facial emotion recognition, emotional memory, and attentional bias toward positive and negative stimuli at baseline and 3 hours post-drug administration. Plasma norepinephrine levels were measured to confirm pharmacological efficacy. Reboxetine significantly increased recognition accuracy for happy facial expressions compared to placebo (p<0.01) while decreasing recognition of sad expressions (p<0.05). Enhanced recall for positive words was observed following reboxetine administration (p<0.01), with no significant change in negative word recall. Attentional bias measurements revealed faster reaction times to positive emotional stimuli under reboxetine conditions (p<0.05). Plasma norepinephrine concentrations were significantly elevated 3 hours post-reboxetine (p<0.001). These findings demonstrate that acute norepinephrine enhancement produces measurable shifts toward positive emotional processing in healthy individuals. The rapid onset of these neuropsychological changes suggests that antidepressants may exert therapeutic effects through early modification of emotional bias, potentially preceding the delayed clinical improvements typically observed in depression treatment. This neuropsychological framework provides novel insights into antidepressant mechanisms and supports theories emphasizing cognitive-emotional interactions in mood disorder pathophysiology.

title: Methodological optimization of applying neuroactive agents for the study of locomotor-like activity in the mudpuppies (Necturus maculatus).
abstract: Mudpuppies (Necturus maculatus) represent an important model for studying locomotor pattern generation due to their phylogenetic position and accessible central pattern generator (CPG) networks. However, standardized protocols for pharmacological manipulation of locomotor circuits in this species remain poorly characterized. This study aimed to optimize methodological approaches for applying neuroactive compounds to elicit and modulate fictive locomotion in the isolated mudpuppy spinal cord preparation. We tested various concentrations of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA; 10-100 μM), 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT; 5-50 μM), and dopamine (DA; 20-200 μM) using both bath application and localized micropressure delivery systems in 24 adult mudpuppy preparations. Locomotor-like activity was assessed through simultaneous recordings from ventral roots L2-L4 using suction electrodes. Bath application of NMDA (50 μM) combined with 5-HT (20 μM) consistently induced robust, coordinated alternating bursting patterns resembling natural swimming activity (cycle period: 2.8 ± 0.4 s, n=18). Dopamine significantly enhanced burst amplitude (147 ± 23% of control) and regularity without altering cycle frequency. Micropressure application required 3-fold lower concentrations while providing superior temporal control and reduced preparation variability. Temperature maintenance at 10-12°C proved critical for stable recordings exceeding 6 hours. These optimized protocols provide a reliable framework for investigating salamander locomotor CPG function and establish mudpuppies as a valuable complement to existing amphibian models. The methods described here will facilitate future comparative studies of vertebrate locomotor control mechanisms and evolutionary adaptations in neural circuit organization.

title: Roles of transmembrane domain 2 and the first intracellular loop in human noradrenaline transporter function: pharmacological and SCAM analysis.
abstract: The human noradrenaline transporter (hNET) is a critical component of noradrenergic neurotransmission and a primary target for antidepressant and psychostimulant drugs. While previous studies have implicated transmembrane domain 2 (TM2) in substrate binding and translocation, the specific contributions of TM2 and the adjacent first intracellular loop (IL1) to transporter function remain poorly understood. In this study, we employed site-directed mutagenesis combined with substituted cysteine accessibility mutagenesis (SCAM) to investigate the functional roles of these domains in hNET. Wild-type and mutant transporters were expressed in HEK293 cells and characterized using radioligand binding assays and uptake measurements. SCAM analysis using methanethiosulfonate reagents revealed that residues Tyr93, Phe98, and Ile102 in TM2 are accessible to modification and critical for noradrenaline uptake, with mutations at these positions reducing Vmax by 60-85% without significantly altering KM values. Additionally, three residues in IL1 (Lys114, Arg116, and Asp119) showed altered accessibility upon substrate binding, suggesting conformational changes during the transport cycle. Pharmacological analysis demonstrated that mutations in both TM2 and IL1 differentially affected inhibition by cocaine, desipramine, and mazindol, with some mutants showing up to 10-fold changes in inhibitor potency. These findings provide evidence that TM2 forms part of the substrate translocation pathway and that IL1 undergoes substrate-induced conformational changes, likely facilitating the alternating access mechanism of transport. Our results advance understanding of NET structure-function relationships and may inform development of more selective transporter-targeted therapeutics.

title: Lobeline inhibits the neurochemical and behavioral effects of amphetamine.
abstract: Amphetamine abuse remains a significant public health concern, necessitating the development of effective pharmacotherapies. Lobeline, an alkaloid derived from Lobelia inflata, has shown promise as a potential treatment for psychostimulant addiction due to its interaction with the dopamine transporter and vesicular monoamine transporter. The present study investigated whether lobeline could attenuate amphetamine-induced neurochemical and behavioral effects in male Sprague-Dawley rats. In vivo microdialysis was used to measure extracellular dopamine concentrations in the nucleus accumbens following acute amphetamine administration (1.0 mg/kg, i.p.) with or without lobeline pretreatment (1.0, 3.0, or 10.0 mg/kg, s.c.). Locomotor activity was assessed using automated activity chambers over 120 minutes post-amphetamine injection. Additionally, amphetamine-induced conditioned place preference was evaluated following chronic lobeline treatment (3.0 mg/kg, s.c., twice daily for 7 days). Results demonstrated that lobeline dose-dependently reduced amphetamine-evoked dopamine overflow in the nucleus accumbens, with maximal inhibition (65% reduction) observed at the highest dose tested. Correspondingly, lobeline significantly attenuated amphetamine-induced hyperlocomotion in a dose-dependent manner. Chronic lobeline pretreatment also blocked the development of amphetamine conditioned place preference without affecting baseline locomotor behavior or producing conditioned place aversion. These findings suggest that lobeline effectively antagonizes both the neurochemical and behavioral effects of amphetamine, supporting its potential utility as a pharmacotherapy for amphetamine addiction. The mechanism likely involves lobeline's ability to inhibit dopamine transporter function and reduce vesicular dopamine storage.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: III. Actions in models of potential antidepressive and anxiolytic activity in comparison with ropinirole.
abstract: Dopamine D3/D2 receptor agonists have shown promise as novel therapeutic agents for mood and anxiety disorders, yet their comparative efficacy remains poorly characterized. S32504, a novel naphtoxazine derivative with high affinity for dopamine D3 and D2 receptors, was evaluated for potential antidepressive and anxiolytic properties in comparison with the established D2/D3 agonist ropinirole. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received S32504 (0.04-2.5 mg/kg, s.c.), ropinirole (0.16-10 mg/kg, s.c.), or vehicle 30 minutes prior to behavioral testing. Antidepressive-like activity was assessed using the forced swim test and chronic mild stress paradigm, while anxiolytic potential was evaluated in the elevated plus maze and Vogel conflict test. S32504 demonstrated dose-dependent antidepressive-like effects, significantly reducing immobility time in the forced swim test (ED50 = 0.63 mg/kg) and reversing anhedonic behavior in chronically stressed animals. These effects were more potent than those observed with ropinirole (ED50 = 3.2 mg/kg). In anxiety models, S32504 (0.16-0.63 mg/kg) increased open arm exploration in the elevated plus maze and enhanced punished drinking in the Vogel test, indicating anxiolytic activity. Ropinirole showed similar but less pronounced anxiolytic effects at higher doses. The antidepressive and anxiolytic actions of S32504 were attenuated by the D2/D3 antagonist raclopride but not by the selective D2 antagonist L-741,626, suggesting preferential involvement of D3 receptors. These findings indicate that S32504 possesses superior antidepressive and anxiolytic potential compared to ropinirole, supporting the therapeutic relevance of selective D3 receptor modulation in mood disorders.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Efficient measurement of endogenous neurotransmitters in small localized regions of central nervous systems in vitro with HPLC.
abstract: Traditional methods for measuring endogenous neurotransmitter concentrations in discrete brain regions have been limited by tissue sample size requirements and insufficient spatial resolution. We developed an optimized high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) protocol with electrochemical detection to quantify dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and their major metabolites in localized CNS regions as small as 0.5 mm³. Fresh brain slices (400 μm) from adult Sprague-Dawley rats were rapidly frozen, and target regions were microdissected using a stereomicroscope with cooling stage. Tissue samples were homogenized in 50 μL perchloric acid containing dihydroxybenzylamine as internal standard. Following centrifugation and filtration, 20 μL aliquots were analyzed using a C18 reverse-phase column with mobile phase containing sodium phosphate buffer, octanesulfonic acid, and methanol. Detection limits were 0.5 pg for dopamine, 0.8 pg for norepinephrine, and 1.2 pg for serotonin. Recovery rates exceeded 85% for all analytes. The method demonstrated excellent linearity (r² > 0.998) across physiologically relevant concentrations and showed minimal interference from tissue matrix components. Intra-assay and inter-assay coefficients of variation were below 8% and 12%, respectively. Successful application to discrete regions including individual dopaminergic cell clusters in substantia nigra and noradrenergic terminals in hippocampal subfields validated the technique's utility. This miniaturized HPLC approach provides researchers with a sensitive, reliable tool for investigating neurotransmitter distributions in anatomically defined CNS microregions, enabling more precise neurochemical mapping studies.

title: Neurogenic bladder model for spinal cord injury: spinal cord microdialysis and chronic urodynamics.
abstract: Spinal cord injury (SCI) frequently results in neurogenic bladder dysfunction, characterized by detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia and impaired micturition reflexes. Despite the clinical significance of this condition, the underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study aimed to establish a comprehensive experimental model combining chronic urodynamics with spinal cord microdialysis to investigate neurotransmitter release patterns in neurogenic bladder dysfunction following SCI. Adult female Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) underwent complete spinal cord transection at the T10 level. Four weeks post-injury, microdialysis probes were stereotaxically implanted into the L6-S1 spinal segments, and chronic bladder catheters were inserted for continuous urodynamic monitoring. Microdialysate samples were collected during spontaneous voiding cycles and analyzed for glutamate, GABA, glycine, and substance P using HPLC with fluorometric detection. Urodynamic parameters including detrusor pressure, voiding efficiency, and residual volume were simultaneously recorded. SCI animals demonstrated significant bladder hyperreflexia with 3.2-fold increased voiding frequency and 60% reduced voiding efficiency compared to sham controls (p<0.01). Microdialysis revealed elevated glutamate release (185% of control) and decreased GABA concentrations (45% of control) during detrusor contractions in SCI rats. Substance P levels increased 4-fold during voiding episodes in injured animals. These findings demonstrate that SCI-induced neurogenic bladder is associated with altered excitatory-inhibitory balance in lumbosacral spinal circuits. This integrated model provides a valuable tool for investigating the neurochemical basis of neurogenic bladder dysfunction and evaluating potential therapeutic interventions targeting spinal neurotransmitter systems.

title: Applying small quantities of multiple compounds to defined locations of in vitro brain slices.
abstract: Precise application of pharmacological agents to specific brain regions in slice preparations remains a significant methodological challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional bath application techniques lack spatial resolution and consume large quantities of expensive compounds, while pressure ejection systems often produce variable results. We developed a novel microfluidic delivery system enabling simultaneous application of multiple compounds to defined locations within acute brain slices with nanoliter precision. The system utilizes computer-controlled piezoelectric micropipettes (tip diameter 2-5 μm) connected to independent reservoirs containing test solutions. Custom software allows preprogrammed delivery sequences with temporal resolution of 10 ms and spatial accuracy within 25 μm. We validated this approach using 400 μm coronal hippocampal slices from adult Wistar rats, applying GABA (100 μM), glutamate (50 μM), and bicuculline (10 μM) to CA1 pyramidal cell dendrites while recording extracellular field potentials. Drug applications produced consistent, reproducible responses with onset latencies of 45 ± 8 ms and minimal cross-contamination between application sites (n=24 slices). Compound consumption was reduced by >95% compared to bath application. The system successfully delivered up to six different solutions simultaneously to distinct slice regions without mechanical disruption of tissue architecture. This microfluidic approach provides enhanced spatial and temporal control over drug delivery in brain slice preparations, offering significant advantages for investigating local circuit pharmacology, receptor distribution, and synaptic transmission mechanisms while minimizing reagent costs and experimental variability.

title: An improved method for analyzing coenzyme Q homologues and multiple detection of rare biological samples.
abstract: Coenzyme Q homologues play critical roles in mitochondrial electron transport and cellular bioenergetics, yet their precise quantification in rare neurobiological samples remains technically challenging. Existing analytical methods suffer from insufficient sensitivity, poor resolution of structural homologues, and require large sample volumes incompatible with limited tissue specimens. We developed an improved high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) method for simultaneous detection and quantification of coenzyme Q6, Q9, and Q10 homologues in small biological samples. The method employs a novel solid-phase extraction protocol using C18 cartridges followed by reverse-phase chromatography with electrospray ionization. Sample preparation was optimized for volumes as small as 50 μL of cerebrospinal fluid or 5 mg of brain tissue. Multiple reaction monitoring transitions were established for each homologue, with coenzyme Q4 serving as internal standard. The method demonstrated excellent linearity (r² > 0.995) across physiologically relevant concentration ranges, with detection limits of 0.1-0.5 ng/mL for all homologues. Intra- and inter-day precision were <8% and <12% respectively. Recovery rates exceeded 85% for all analytes in plasma, CSF, and brain tissue matrices. Application to post-mortem human brain samples from Parkinson's disease patients (n=12) revealed significant reductions in CoQ10 levels compared to age-matched controls (n=12, p<0.01). This sensitive, selective method enables comprehensive coenzyme Q profiling in precious neurobiological specimens, facilitating investigation of mitochondrial dysfunction in neurodegenerative diseases and other neurological conditions where sample availability is limited.

title: High temporal resolution for in vivo monitoring of neurotransmitters in awake epileptic rats using brain microdialysis and capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection.
abstract: In vivo monitoring of neurotransmitter dynamics during epileptic seizures requires analytical methods with sufficient temporal resolution to capture rapid neurochemical changes. Traditional microdialysis approaches with HPLC detection lack the temporal resolution necessary to monitor second-to-minute fluctuations in neurotransmitter concentrations during ictal events. We developed a novel analytical system combining brain microdialysis with capillary electrophoresis and laser-induced fluorescence detection (CE-LIF) to achieve high temporal resolution monitoring of glutamate, GABA, and dopamine in the hippocampus of awake, freely-moving epileptic rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with hippocampal microdialysis probes following pilocarpine-induced status epilepticus. Dialysate samples were collected every 30 seconds and analyzed by CE-LIF using fluorescein isothiocyanate derivatization. Simultaneous EEG recordings confirmed seizure activity. The CE-LIF system achieved detection limits of 2.1 nM for glutamate, 3.8 nM for GABA, and 1.6 nM for dopamine with separation completed within 90 seconds. During spontaneous seizures, glutamate levels increased 340±67% above baseline within 15 seconds of seizure onset, while GABA concentrations showed a delayed increase of 180±45% at 60 seconds post-ictal. Dopamine levels remained relatively stable throughout seizure events. This methodology provides unprecedented temporal resolution for real-time neurotransmitter monitoring during epileptic seizures, revealing rapid glutamate release kinetics previously undetectable with conventional analytical approaches. The technique offers significant advantages for studying neurotransmitter dynamics in various neurological conditions requiring high temporal resolution measurements.

title: Partial response, nonresponse, and relapse with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in major depression: a survey of current "next-step" practices.
abstract: Despite the widespread use of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) as first-line treatment for major depression, optimal management strategies for patients experiencing partial response, nonresponse, or relapse remain poorly defined. This study examined current clinical practices among psychiatrists when initial SSRI therapy proves inadequate. A comprehensive survey was mailed to 1,200 randomly selected members of the American Psychiatric Association between January and March 1999, with follow-up mailings to nonresponders. The 18-item questionnaire assessed preferred "next-step" interventions for various clinical scenarios involving SSRI treatment failures. Of 847 returned surveys (70.6% response rate), 782 were suitable for analysis. For partial responders after 8-12 weeks of adequate SSRI dosing, 58% of psychiatrists preferred dose optimization, 31% favored augmentation with lithium or thyroid hormone, and 11% chose switching to another antidepressant class. In cases of complete nonresponse, 67% preferred switching to a different SSRI, 24% switched to tricyclics or MAOIs, and 9% employed augmentation strategies. For patients experiencing relapse during maintenance therapy, 71% increased the SSRI dose, 19% switched medications, and 10% added augmenting agents. Significant practice variations emerged based on geographic region, years in practice, and academic affiliation. These findings highlight the lack of consensus regarding evidence-based approaches to SSRI treatment failures and underscore the need for controlled trials comparing alternative strategies. Standardized treatment algorithms may improve outcomes for the substantial proportion of depressed patients who do not respond adequately to initial SSRI therapy.

title: Metabolic changes detected by proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy in vivo and in vitro in a murin model of Parkinson's disease, the MPTP-intoxicated mouse.
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is characterized by progressive dopaminergic neurodegeneration in the substantia nigra, yet the underlying metabolic alterations remain incompletely understood. The 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP) mouse model provides a well-established system for investigating PD pathophysiology. We employed proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (¹H-MRS) to characterize metabolic changes in MPTP-intoxicated mice both in vivo and in vitro. Male C57BL/6 mice (n=24) received four intraperitoneal injections of MPTP (20 mg/kg) at 2-hour intervals, while controls received saline. In vivo ¹H-MRS was performed at 7.0 Tesla focusing on striatal and nigral regions at 1, 7, and 21 days post-treatment. High-resolution magic angle spinning ¹H-MRS was conducted on extracted brain tissue. MPTP treatment resulted in significant reductions in N-acetylaspartate/creatine ratios in both striatum (32% decrease, p<0.01) and substantia nigra (28% decrease, p<0.05) by day 7, persisting through day 21. Lactate levels were elevated 2.1-fold in striatum at day 1, normalizing by day 7. Tissue analysis revealed decreased glutamate (41% reduction, p<0.01) and increased myo-inositol (38% increase, p<0.05) in striatal samples. Taurine levels showed a biphasic response with initial elevation followed by significant depletion. These findings demonstrate that MPTP intoxication produces distinct metabolic signatures detectable by ¹H-MRS, including markers of neuronal dysfunction, altered energy metabolism, and glial activation. The metabolic profile suggests both acute energetic stress and chronic neurodegenerative processes, providing insights into PD pathogenesis and potential therapeutic targets.

title: The stanley neuropathology consortium integrative database: a novel, web-based tool for exploring neuropathological markers in psychiatric disorders and the biological processes associated with abnormalities of those markers.
abstract: The Stanley Neuropathology Consortium has established a comprehensive repository of postmortem brain tissue from subjects with major psychiatric disorders, including schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and major depressive disorder, alongside matched controls. To facilitate broader scientific utilization of these valuable resources, we developed an integrative web-based database that consolidates neuropathological findings and enables systematic exploration of molecular abnormalities across psychiatric conditions. The database incorporates quantitative data from over 2,400 brain specimens across multiple cortical and subcortical regions, including measures of neuronal density, glial cell populations, synaptic markers, neurotransmitter systems, and inflammatory mediators. Advanced bioinformatics tools allow users to query specific markers, compare findings across diagnostic groups, and examine regional distribution patterns of pathological changes. Statistical analysis modules enable identification of significant alterations and effect size calculations, while integrated pathway analysis tools link observed abnormalities to relevant biological processes and molecular networks. Our analysis of the consolidated dataset reveals consistent reductions in parvalbumin-positive interneurons across schizophrenia and bipolar disorder (p<0.001), elevated microglial activation markers in all three psychiatric conditions compared to controls (p<0.01), and disorder-specific alterations in synaptic proteins. The database demonstrates significant increases in inflammatory cytokine expression that correlate with decreased neuroplasticity markers (r=-0.67, p<0.001). This novel resource provides unprecedented access to systematically collected neuropathological data, facilitating hypothesis generation and validation while advancing our understanding of the neurobiological substrates underlying major psychiatric disorders through comprehensive data integration and analysis capabilities.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Effects of soman (pinacolyl methylphosphonofluoridate) on coronary blood flow and cardiac function in swine.
abstract: Soman, an organophosphate nerve agent, produces life-threatening toxicity through acetylcholinesterase inhibition, resulting in excessive cholinergic stimulation. While the neurological effects of soman have been extensively studied, its direct cardiovascular effects remain poorly characterized. This study investigated the acute effects of soman on coronary hemodynamics and cardiac function in anesthetized swine. Twenty-four Yorkshire swine (25-30 kg) were instrumented with electromagnetic flow probes around the left anterior descending coronary artery and left ventricular pressure catheters. Animals received either saline (n=8), low-dose soman (0.5 × LD50, n=8), or high-dose soman (1.0 × LD50, n=8) intravenously. Hemodynamic parameters were monitored continuously for 120 minutes post-exposure. Low-dose soman produced biphasic cardiovascular responses: initial increases in heart rate (142 ± 12 vs 98 ± 8 bpm, p<0.05) and coronary blood flow (185 ± 23 vs 135 ± 18 ml/min, p<0.01) at 5 minutes, followed by progressive decreases by 60 minutes. High-dose soman caused immediate severe bradycardia (45 ± 8 bpm), marked reduction in coronary flow (68 ± 15 ml/min), and decreased left ventricular contractility (dP/dt decreased 58%, p<0.001) within 15 minutes. Plasma cholinesterase activity was inhibited >85% in both soman groups. These findings demonstrate that soman produces dose-dependent direct cardiovascular toxicity characterized by impaired coronary perfusion and myocardial function, independent of respiratory compromise. These cardiac effects may contribute significantly to soman-induced mortality and should be considered in developing therapeutic countermeasures.
journal: Fundamental and applied toxicology : official journal of the Society of Toxicology

title: Measurement of plasmalemmal dopamine transport, vesicular dopamine transport, and K(+)-stimulated dopamine release in frozen rat brain tissue.
abstract: Measurement of dopamine transport and release mechanisms in brain tissue has traditionally required fresh tissue preparations, limiting experimental flexibility and sample throughput. This study evaluated whether frozen rat brain tissue retains sufficient functional integrity for quantitative assessment of plasmalemmal dopamine transporter (DAT) activity, vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) function, and K+-stimulated dopamine release. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) were sacrificed and striatal tissue was either used fresh or frozen at -80°C for 1-4 weeks. DAT activity was measured using [3H]dopamine uptake assays in synaptosomal preparations, while VMAT2 function was assessed using [3H]dopamine uptake into isolated synaptic vesicles. K+-stimulated dopamine release was evaluated using superfusion chambers with HPLC detection. Fresh tissue demonstrated robust DAT-mediated uptake (Vmax = 2.14 ± 0.18 pmol/mg protein/min), VMAT2 activity (Km = 0.82 ± 0.11 μM), and K+-stimulated release (348 ± 29% of baseline). Frozen tissue retained 78 ± 6% of DAT activity, 71 ± 8% of VMAT2 function, and 82 ± 7% of stimulated release capacity compared to fresh controls. Pharmacological profiles remained unchanged, with GBR-12909 inhibiting DAT (IC50 = 12.3 nM fresh vs. 15.7 nM frozen) and reserpine blocking VMAT2 (IC50 = 3.8 nM fresh vs. 4.9 nM frozen). These findings demonstrate that frozen rat brain tissue maintains sufficient dopaminergic transport and release functions for quantitative neuropharmacological studies, enabling improved experimental design and increased sample utilization efficiency.

title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: I. Cellular, electrophysiological, and neurochemical profile in comparison with ropinirole.
abstract: S32504 is a novel naphtoxazine derivative developed as a selective dopamine D3/D2 receptor agonist for potential treatment of Parkinson's disease and other dopaminergic disorders. This study characterized the cellular, electrophysiological, and neurochemical properties of S32504 in comparison with the clinically established D2/D3 agonist ropinirole. In CHO cells expressing human D2 or D3 receptors, S32504 demonstrated high binding affinity (Ki = 0.8 nM for D3, 2.1 nM for D2) with 2.6-fold selectivity for D3 receptors, comparable to ropinirole. Functional studies using cAMP assays revealed S32504 as a full agonist at both receptor subtypes with potency values of EC50 = 1.2 nM (D3) and 3.4 nM (D2). Whole-cell patch-clamp recordings in rat substantia nigra pars compacta neurons showed that S32504 (0.1-10 μM) dose-dependently activated G-protein-coupled inward rectifier potassium currents, producing membrane hyperpolarization blocked by the D2 antagonist sulpiride. In vivo microdialysis in freely moving rats demonstrated that S32504 (0.03-1.0 mg/kg, s.c.) reduced extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens by 35-68%, indicating activation of presynaptic D2/D3 autoreceptors. Behavioral assessment using the 6-OHDA lesioned rat model revealed that S32504 reversed contralateral rotation deficits with ED50 = 0.21 mg/kg, showing 3-fold greater potency than ropinirole. These findings establish S32504 as a potent D3/D2 receptor agonist with favorable neurochemical and behavioral profiles, warranting further investigation as a potential anti-parkinsonian agent.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning I: the degraded contingency effect is disrupted by atropine but reinstated by physostigmine.
abstract: Classical fear conditioning paradigms have demonstrated that degraded contingency between conditioned stimulus (CS) and unconditioned stimulus (US) presentations results in attenuated conditioned responses, suggesting that associative learning depends critically on predictive relationships rather than simple CS-US pairings. The role of cholinergic neurotransmission in mediating contingency detection and fear learning remains poorly understood. We investigated the effects of muscarinic receptor antagonism and acetylcholinesterase inhibition on fear conditioning under degraded contingency conditions in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group). Animals received either saline, atropine (10 mg/kg, i.p.), or physostigmine (0.25 mg/kg, i.p.) 30 minutes prior to conditioning sessions. The degraded contingency protocol consisted of paired CS-footshock presentations intermixed with unpaired US presentations, resulting in a CS-US contingency of 0.33. Fear conditioning was assessed through freezing behavior during CS presentations. Control animals demonstrated the expected degraded contingency effect, showing significantly reduced freezing compared to standard paired conditioning (p<0.01). Atropine pretreatment completely abolished this contingency sensitivity, with treated animals showing equivalent freezing responses regardless of CS-US contingency (p>0.05). Conversely, physostigmine pretreatment in atropine-treated animals fully restored normal contingency detection, reinstating the degraded contingency effect (p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that intact cholinergic signaling through muscarinic receptors is essential for detecting predictive relationships during associative learning. The results suggest that acetylcholine may serve as a critical neuromodulator enabling organisms to distinguish between predictive and non-predictive environmental cues during fear acquisition.

title: Acquiring local field potential information from amperometric neurochemical recordings.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) and constant potential amperometry are widely used techniques for real-time detection of neurotransmitter release in behaving animals. However, these electrochemical methods typically discard the wealth of electrophysiological information present in the recorded signals. Here we describe a novel signal processing approach to extract local field potential (LFP) data from amperometric recordings obtained with carbon fiber microelectrodes during dopamine detection experiments. We developed custom filtering algorithms to separate the high-frequency electrochemical current transients associated with neurotransmitter oxidation from the lower-frequency voltage fluctuations reflecting local neural population activity. The method was validated using simultaneous recordings from carbon fiber electrodes positioned in the nucleus accumbens of anesthetized rats during electrical stimulation of the ventral tegmental area. Pharmacological manipulation with tetrodotoxin confirmed that extracted LFP signals originated from synaptic activity rather than electrochemical artifacts. Spectral analysis revealed characteristic LFP frequency bands (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma) consistent with previous reports using conventional tungsten microelectrodes in the same brain region. Cross-correlation analysis demonstrated significant coupling between dopamine release events and specific LFP frequency components, particularly in the beta range (15-30 Hz). Signal-to-noise ratios of extracted LFPs were comparable to those obtained with dedicated electrophysiological recording systems. This approach enables simultaneous monitoring of both neurochemical and electrophysiological signals from a single carbon fiber electrode, providing a more comprehensive view of neural circuit dynamics while reducing tissue damage associated with multiple electrode implantations.

title: In vivo voltammetry: from wire to wireless measurements.
abstract: Real-time electrochemical monitoring of neurotransmitter dynamics in freely behaving animals has been limited by the constraints of tethered recording systems. Traditional fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) requires physical wire connections that restrict animal movement and may introduce behavioral artifacts. We developed and validated a novel wireless voltammetry system capable of transmitting electrochemical data from carbon fiber microelectrodes implanted in rat brain. The system consists of a miniaturized potentiostat (2.1 g) with integrated radio frequency transmission operating at 433 MHz, paired with a computer-controlled receiver station. We compared dopamine detection sensitivity and temporal resolution between wired and wireless configurations using standard carbon fiber electrodes (7 μm diameter) in the nucleus accumbens of anesthetized and freely moving rats. Calibration experiments demonstrated equivalent sensitivity for dopamine detection (limit of detection: 50 nM) and maintained microsecond temporal resolution necessary for FSCV measurements. Wireless recordings successfully detected electrically-evoked dopamine release with signal-to-noise ratios comparable to traditional wired systems (SNR = 12.3 ± 2.1 vs 13.7 ± 1.8, respectively; p > 0.05). Long-term stability tests showed reliable signal transmission for up to 6 hours of continuous recording. Behavioral studies revealed reduced stress-related artifacts and more naturalistic movement patterns compared to tethered controls. This wireless voltammetry approach enables artifact-free neurochemical monitoring during complex behaviors, opening new possibilities for studying neurotransmitter function in naturalistic settings while maintaining the temporal and chemical specificity of traditional electrochemical methods.

title: A novel method for monitoring the cell surface expression of heteromeric protein complexes in dispersed neurons and acute hippocampal slices.
abstract: Understanding the cell surface expression and trafficking of heteromeric protein complexes is crucial for elucidating synaptic function and plasticity mechanisms.
