Current methodologies for monitoring surface expression of multisubunit receptors and channels in native neuronal preparations remain technically challenging and often lack the specificity required to distinguish heteromeric assemblies from homomeric complexes. Here we describe a novel dual-epitope labeling approach that enables real-time monitoring of heteromeric protein complex surface expression in both dispersed cultured neurons and acute brain slices. Our method utilizes simultaneous immunofluorescent detection of extracellular epitopes on different subunits of the target complex, combined with proximity-based fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) analysis to confirm heteromeric assembly at the cell surface. We validated this technique using NMDA receptors containing NR1 and NR2B subunits in primary hippocampal neurons and acute hippocampal slices from postnatal day 14-21 rats. Surface-expressed heteromeric NR1/NR2B complexes showed characteristic FRET efficiency of 0.23 ± 0.03, significantly higher than background levels (0.08 ± 0.02, p<0.001). The method successfully detected dynamic changes in surface expression following pharmacological treatments, including a 40% increase in surface heteromers after forskolin application and a 25% decrease following NMDA treatment. This approach provides superior spatial resolution compared to biochemical methods and maintains tissue architecture integrity compared to dissociated cell preparations. The technique is applicable to various heteromeric complexes and offers a valuable tool for investigating synaptic protein trafficking in physiologically relevant preparations.

title: From the search for a molecular code of memory to the role of neurotransmitters: a historical perspective.
abstract: The quest to understand the molecular basis of memory formation has undergone significant paradigmatic shifts over the past five decades. Early investigations focused primarily on structural modifications at synapses, while contemporary research has increasingly emphasized the critical role of neurotransmitter systems in memory consolidation and retrieval. This historical review examines the evolution of memory research from initial attempts to identify a putative "molecular code" to current understanding of neurotransmitter-mediated mechanisms. We analyzed seminal publications from 1950-2003 using systematic literature review methodology, categorizing findings into distinct conceptual frameworks and identifying key theoretical transitions. Our analysis reveals three major phases: the initial search for memory-specific molecules (1950s-1970s), the emergence of synaptic plasticity models emphasizing structural changes (1980s-1990s), and the recent integration of neurotransmitter dynamics with molecular mechanisms (1990s-present). Particularly significant was the recognition that acetylcholine, dopamine, and glutamate systems provide both the immediate signaling required for memory formation and the modulatory influences necessary for memory strength and persistence. The convergence of molecular biology techniques with pharmacological approaches has demonstrated that memory formation involves complex interactions between neurotransmitter release, receptor activation, and downstream signaling cascades rather than simple structural encoding. These findings suggest that future therapeutic interventions for memory disorders should target neurotransmitter system dysfunction rather than solely focusing on synaptic structural modifications, representing a fundamental shift in translational memory research strategies.

title: Can voltammetry measure nitrogen monoxide (NO) and/or nitrites?
abstract: Nitrogen monoxide (NO) plays crucial roles as a neurotransmitter and signaling molecule in the central nervous system, yet direct electrochemical detection remains challenging due to its short half-life and low concentrations. This study evaluated the feasibility of using fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) to detect NO and its stable metabolite nitrite (NO2-) in brain tissue preparations. Carbon fiber microelectrodes (7 μm diameter) were fabricated and characterized using standard techniques. Voltammetric measurements were performed in artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF) containing known concentrations of NO (10 nM to 10 μM) generated by NONOate donors and sodium nitrite (1 μM to 1 mM). Rat hippocampal slices were used to assess detectability in biological preparations following stimulation with N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA, 100 μM). Direct NO detection proved difficult with FSCV due to rapid oxidation and interference from oxygen reduction currents. However, DPV successfully detected nitrite with a detection limit of 2.1 μM and linear response up to 500 μM (R² = 0.94). In hippocampal slices, NMDA stimulation produced detectable increases in nitrite-like signals (127 ± 23% of baseline, n=8, p<0.01) that were blocked by the NOS inhibitor L-NAME (100 μM). Background subtraction algorithms improved signal resolution but could not overcome fundamental limitations for direct NO detection. These findings suggest that while direct voltammetric NO measurement remains problematic, nitrite detection may serve as a viable indirect approach for monitoring NO production in neural tissue preparations.

title: [Use of the antisense oligonucleotide technique in neuropsychopharmacology research].
abstract: The antisense oligonucleotide technique has emerged as a powerful molecular tool for investigating gene function in the central nervous system, offering unique advantages over traditional pharmacological approaches for neuropsychopharmacology research. This study examined the efficacy and specificity of phosphorothioate-modified antisense oligodeoxynucleotides in modulating dopamine D2 receptor expression in rat striatum and their behavioral consequences. Male Spritz-Dawley rats received bilateral intracerebroventricular injections of either antisense oligonucleotides complementary to D2 receptor mRNA (20 μg/5 μl), corresponding sense oligonucleotides, or vehicle control twice daily for 4 consecutive days. D2 receptor protein levels were assessed using Western blot analysis and [³H]spiperone binding assays 24 hours after the final injection. Behavioral responses to apomorphine (0.5 mg/kg, s.c.) were evaluated using automated locomotor activity chambers. Antisense treatment resulted in a significant 65% reduction in striatal D2 receptor protein levels compared to controls (p<0.001), while sense oligonucleotides produced no significant changes. Correspondingly, [³H]spiperone binding was reduced by 58% in antisense-treated animals. Apomorphine-induced stereotypic behavior was markedly attenuated in the antisense group, with locomotor counts decreased by 72% relative to controls (p<0.001). No significant changes in D1 receptor binding or general motor function were observed, confirming the specificity of the antisense approach. These findings demonstrate that antisense oligonucleotides provide a selective and temporally controlled method for studying dopaminergic neurotransmission and represent a valuable complement to conventional pharmacological tools in neuropsychopharmacology research.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: Photolysis of caged neurotransmitters: theory and procedures for light delivery.
abstract: Caged neurotransmitters have emerged as powerful tools for investigating synaptic transmission and neural circuit function with high spatial and temporal precision. However, optimal photolysis requires careful consideration of light delivery parameters, photochemical properties, and tissue penetration characteristics. We present comprehensive theoretical frameworks and practical protocols for effective uncaging of neurotransmitters in brain slice preparations and cultured neurons. Our approach combines Monte Carlo simulations of photon propagation with experimental validation using caged glutamate (MNI-glutamate) and GABA (CNB-GABA) in acute hippocampal slices from P14-21 rats. We systematically characterized the relationship between laser power (1-50 mW), pulse duration (0.1-10 ms), and effective uncaging volume using two-photon microscopy at 720 nm and UV flash photolysis at 355 nm. Patch-clamp recordings from CA1 pyramidal neurons revealed that optimal glutamate uncaging occurred with 2-5 ms pulses at 15-25 mW, producing peak currents of 200-800 pA with rise times of 1.2±0.3 ms. Depth penetration analysis demonstrated 50% signal attenuation at 180±25 μm for two-photon and 45±8 μm for UV photolysis. We provide detailed protocols for calibrating light delivery systems, determining photolysis efficiency, and minimizing photodamage. These standardized procedures enable reproducible spatial mapping of synaptic responses and kinetic analysis of neurotransmitter receptor dynamics. The methods described here establish essential guidelines for quantitative photolysis experiments in neuropharmacological research.

title: European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) - 23rd Congress.
abstract: The 23rd Congress of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) convened in Amsterdam from August 28-September 1, 2010, bringing together over 5,000 researchers, clinicians, and industry professionals to discuss advances in neuropsychopharmacology and drug development. This comprehensive review analyzes key presentations and symposia focusing on emerging therapeutic targets and novel compounds in various stages of clinical development. The congress featured 847 scientific presentations, including 156 symposia, 98 poster sessions, and 45 industry-sponsored satellite meetings covering major neuropsychiatric disorders. Significant attention was devoted to innovative mechanisms of action in depression treatment, including triple monoamine reuptake inhibitors and glutamatergic modulators such as ketamine analogs. Notable developments in schizophrenia therapeutics highlighted partial dopamine agonists and phosphodiesterase inhibitors showing promise in Phase II trials. Addiction medicine sessions emphasized novel approaches targeting nicotinic acetylcholine receptors and GABA-B modulators. Alzheimer's disease research showcased gamma-secretase modulators and tau protein aggregation inhibitors, though several high-profile compounds reported disappointing Phase III results. Neuroinflammation emerged as a critical therapeutic target across multiple disorders, with microglial activation inhibitors generating considerable interest. The congress demonstrated robust pipeline activity in neuropsychopharmacology, with over 200 investigational compounds discussed across various development phases. However, the high attrition rates in late-stage clinical trials underscored the continued challenges in translating preclinical findings to clinical efficacy. These findings highlight both the promising therapeutic landscape and the persistent hurdles in developing effective neuropsychiatric medications.

title: An in vivo technique for pharmacological manipulation of Drosophila brain during optical recording.
abstract: The combination of pharmacological manipulation with simultaneous optical recording in living Drosophila represents a powerful approach for dissecting neural circuit function, yet technical challenges have limited its application. Traditional methods for drug delivery to the adult fly brain require invasive procedures that disrupt normal physiology and are incompatible with high-resolution imaging. Here we describe a novel in vivo preparation that enables controlled pharmacological manipulation of the Drosophila central nervous system during concurrent optical recording of neural activity. We developed a semi-intact preparation where the head capsule is opened and the brain exposed while maintaining physiological saline perfusion and minimizing mechanical damage to neural tissue. A custom perfusion chamber allows rapid exchange of solutions containing neuroactive compounds while the preparation remains stable under a two-photon microscope. We validated this technique using genetically-encoded calcium indicators (GCaMP2.0) expressed in specific neuronal populations and tested responses to GABA, acetylcholine, dopamine, and octopamine at physiologically relevant concentrations (10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁴ M). Pharmacological responses were detected within 30-60 seconds of compound application, with full reversibility achieved within 5-10 minutes of washout. The preparation remained viable for optical recordings up to 4 hours, enabling detailed pharmacological dose-response studies. This method provides temporal and spatial resolution superior to feeding-based drug delivery while preserving the genetic advantages of the Drosophila model system. The technique should facilitate mechanistic studies of neurotransmitter signaling and screening of neuroactive compounds in an intact nervous system context.

title: Pitfalls in presenting and interpreting clinical trial data.
abstract: Clinical trials in psychopharmacology have become increasingly sophisticated, yet systematic biases in data presentation and interpretation continue to compromise the validity of research findings. This review examines common methodological pitfalls encountered in the analysis and reporting of clinical trial outcomes in neuropsychiatric drug development. We analyzed 147 published clinical trials of antidepressants, anxiolytics, and antipsychotics from 1990-1994, identifying recurring patterns of data misrepresentation and interpretive errors. Systematic evaluation revealed several critical issues: selective reporting of favorable outcome measures while omitting negative findings (observed in 34% of studies), inappropriate use of multiple statistical comparisons without correction (28%), premature termination of trials showing unfavorable trends (12%), and misleading graphical representations that exaggerate treatment effects (41%). Post-hoc subgroup analyses were frequently employed to salvage non-significant primary endpoints, with insufficient justification in 23% of reviewed studies. Additionally, clinically insignificant but statistically significant differences were often overinterpreted as evidence of therapeutic efficacy. Industry-sponsored trials showed higher rates of these problematic practices compared to investigator-initiated studies (p<0.01). These findings highlight the need for more rigorous standards in clinical trial design, analysis, and reporting within psychopharmacology research. We recommend mandatory registration of study protocols, standardized outcome measures, and independent statistical review to minimize bias and improve the reliability of clinical evidence used to guide therapeutic decisions in psychiatric medicine.

title: [Current trends in clinical psychiatric research].
abstract: The landscape of clinical psychiatric research has undergone significant transformation during the 1990s, necessitating a comprehensive evaluation of emerging methodological approaches and therapeutic targets. This review examines current trends in psychiatric clinical research based on analysis of 847 published studies from 1995-1997 indexed in major psychiatric journals. We conducted a systematic review of research methodologies, study populations, and therapeutic interventions reported in clinical trials for major depressive disorder, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and anxiety disorders. Placebo-controlled, double-blind designs remained the gold standard, representing 68% of therapeutic trials. However, active comparator studies increased by 23% compared to the early 1990s. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors dominated antidepressant research (45% of studies), while atypical antipsychotics emerged as a major focus in schizophrenia trials (38% increase). Notably, combination therapy protocols and augmentation strategies showed marked expansion, particularly lithium augmentation in treatment-resistant depression. Neuroimaging integration increased substantially, with 15% of studies incorporating SPECT or PET methodology. Quality of life measures and functional outcomes gained prominence alongside traditional symptom rating scales. Patient populations showed greater diagnostic specificity, with comorbidity exclusions becoming more stringent. The trend toward shorter trial durations (mean 8.2 weeks vs. 12.1 weeks in 1990-1992) raised concerns about long-term efficacy assessment. These findings indicate that contemporary psychiatric research emphasizes mechanistic understanding, functional outcomes, and therapeutic optimization while maintaining rigorous methodological standards essential for evidence-based clinical practice.

title: A pre-clinical study showing how dopaminergic drugs administered during pre-exposure can impair or facilitate latent inhibition.
abstract: Latent inhibition (LI), the phenomenon whereby pre-exposure to a stimulus retards subsequent associative learning, serves as an important model for understanding attentional processes and schizophrenia-related cognitive deficits. Dopaminergic dysfunction has been implicated in LI disruption, yet the specific effects of dopaminergic manipulations during the pre-exposure phase remain poorly characterized. The present study examined how dopamine D1 and D2 receptor ligands administered during pre-exposure affect LI expression in rats. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received vehicle, the D1 agonist SKF-38393 (1.0 or 3.0 mg/kg), D1 antagonist SCH-23390 (0.01 or 0.03 mg/kg), D2 agonist quinpirole (0.5 or 1.0 mg/kg), or D2 antagonist haloperidol (0.05 or 0.1 mg/kg) 30 minutes before each pre-exposure session. Following standard conditioning and testing phases conducted drug-free, suppression ratios were calculated to assess LI. Control animals demonstrated robust LI, with pre-exposed subjects showing significantly less conditioned suppression than non-pre-exposed controls. High-dose SKF-38393 (3.0 mg/kg) and haloperidol (0.1 mg/kg) completely abolished LI, while low-dose quinpirole (0.5 mg/kg) enhanced LI expression beyond control levels. SCH-23390 had no significant effect at either dose tested. These findings demonstrate that dopaminergic manipulations during pre-exposure can bidirectionally modulate LI, with D1 receptor overstimulation and D2 receptor blockade impairing LI formation, while moderate D2 receptor activation facilitates it. These results provide important insights into the neurochemical mechanisms underlying attentional learning and may inform therapeutic approaches for disorders characterized by LI deficits.

title: Lactate dehydrogenase release is facilitated by brief sonication of rat hippocampal slices and isolated retinas following acute neuronal damage.
abstract: Assessment of neuronal viability following acute injury requires reliable and quantitative methods for detecting cell death in brain tissue preparations. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release has emerged as a useful marker of cellular membrane integrity, but conventional protocols may underestimate total damage due to incomplete enzyme release from compromised cells. We investigated whether brief sonication enhances LDH release detection in two commonly used ex vivo preparations: rat hippocampal slices and isolated retinas. Adult Sprague-Dawley rats were sacrificed and 400-μm hippocampal slices or whole retinas were prepared using standard techniques. Acute neuronal damage was induced by oxygen-glucose deprivation (OGD) for 30 or 60 minutes, or by exposure to 100 μM glutamate for 2 hours. Following injury protocols, tissues were either processed immediately for LDH assay or subjected to brief sonication (3 × 10-second pulses at 20% amplitude) before enzyme measurement. Sonication significantly increased detectable LDH release in both preparations compared to non-sonicated controls. In hippocampal slices, sonication revealed 2.3-fold higher LDH levels following 60-minute OGD and 1.8-fold higher levels after glutamate exposure. Similarly, sonicated retinas showed 2.7-fold increased LDH release following OGD treatment. Microscopic examination confirmed that sonication parameters did not cause additional tissue damage in uninjured preparations. These findings demonstrate that brief sonication substantially improves the sensitivity of LDH-based viability assays by facilitating complete enzyme release from damaged neurons while preserving tissue architecture, providing a more accurate assessment of neuronal injury in slice preparations.

title: Fast Hadamard transform capillary electrophoresis for on-line, time-resolved chemical monitoring.
abstract: Chemical monitoring of neurotransmitter dynamics in biological systems requires analytical methods capable of high temporal resolution and sensitivity. Traditional capillary electrophoresis (CE) methods, while offering excellent separation efficiency, are limited by sequential injection protocols that preclude real-time monitoring of rapidly changing concentrations. We present a novel approach utilizing fast Hadamard transform (FHT) multiplexing with capillary electrophoresis for continuous, time-resolved analysis of neurochemical systems. The FHT-CE system employs pseudorandom binary sequence injection patterns combined with mathematical deconvolution to achieve simultaneous monitoring of multiple analytes with sub-second temporal resolution. Method validation was performed using standard solutions of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine, demonstrating linear responses (r² > 0.998) across physiologically relevant concentrations (10 nM to 10 μM). Detection limits of 2.1 nM, 3.4 nM, and 2.8 nM were achieved for dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine, respectively, representing a 15-fold improvement in sensitivity compared to conventional CE methods. The system was successfully applied to monitor stimulated neurotransmitter release from PC12 cell cultures, revealing previously unresolved kinetic features of dopamine efflux with 0.3 s temporal resolution over 30-minute experimental periods. Signal-to-noise ratios improved by an average factor of 12 through Hadamard multiplexing, while maintaining separation resolution (Rs > 1.5) for all tested analyte pairs. This FHT-CE approach provides a powerful new tool for real-time neurochemical analysis, enabling detailed investigation of neurotransmitter dynamics in complex biological systems.

title: [The way out from crisis--from the closing of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI) to Semmelweis University].
abstract: The closure of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI) in 2007 created an unprecedented crisis in Hungarian psychiatric research and clinical care, threatening decades of accumulated expertise in neuropharmacological research. This study examines the transitional process and outcomes following the integration of OPNI's psychiatric departments into Semmelweis University's medical faculty between 2007-2009. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of research productivity, patient care metrics, and institutional capacity before, during, and after the transition period. Data were collected from publication databases, clinical records, and structured interviews with 45 faculty members and researchers. Patient treatment continuity was assessed through medical records analysis of 1,247 patients with major psychiatric disorders. Results demonstrated an initial 40% decline in research output during 2007-2008, with publications dropping from 89 to 53 annually. However, by 2009, research productivity recovered to 78% of pre-crisis levels, accompanied by improved funding acquisition (€2.3M vs. €1.8M in 2006). Patient care quality indicators remained stable throughout the transition, with readmission rates showing no significant increase (p>0.05). The integration preserved critical neuropharmacological research programs, including ongoing clinical trials for novel antipsychotic compounds. Most importantly, the transition resulted in enhanced academic infrastructure and expanded international collaborations. The successful transformation from OPNI to Semmelweis University demonstrates that institutional crises, while initially disruptive, can catalyze positive structural changes that ultimately strengthen psychiatric research capacity and clinical excellence in Hungary.
journal: Psychiatria Hungarica : A Magyar Pszichiatriai Tarsasag tudomanyos folyoirata

title: Volume versus wiring transmission in the brain: a new theoretical frame for neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The traditional view of neural communication has focused primarily on point-to-point synaptic transmission, yet accumulating evidence suggests that diffuse chemical signaling plays an equally important role in brain function. We propose a comprehensive theoretical framework distinguishing between "wiring transmission" (classical synaptic communication) and "volume transmission" (extrasynaptic diffusion of neurotransmitters through brain parenchyma). This framework was developed through integration of neuroanatomical, neurochemical, and pharmacological data from multiple experimental paradigms, including microdialysis studies, electron microscopy of synaptic and non-synaptic release sites, and analysis of neurotransmitter diffusion patterns in brain tissue. Our analysis reveals that monoaminergic, cholinergic, and peptidergic systems operate predominantly through volume transmission, with neurotransmitters diffusing distances of 10-100 micrometers from release sites to reach extrasynaptic receptors. In contrast, glutamatergic and GABAergic systems rely primarily on wiring transmission for rapid, precise signaling. Pharmacokinetic modeling demonstrates that drugs affecting volume transmission systems exhibit distinct dose-response relationships and temporal dynamics compared to those targeting wiring transmission. This dual-mode framework provides new insights into psychopharmacological mechanisms, explaining why certain therapeutic agents require days to weeks for clinical efficacy and why regional brain concentrations often correlate poorly with synaptic activity. The volume/wiring transmission paradigm offers a novel conceptual basis for understanding drug action in the central nervous system and may guide development of more selective neuropsychiatric therapeutics targeting specific transmission modes.

title: Quantitative assessment of the microstructure of rat behavior: I, f(d), the extension of the scaling hypothesis.
abstract: The scaling hypothesis proposes that temporal patterns in operant behavior exhibit fractal-like properties across multiple time scales, suggesting underlying neural mechanisms may operate according to power-law distributions. Previous qualitative analyses have supported this framework, but quantitative methods for characterizing behavioral microstructure remain limited. We developed a novel analytical approach, f(d), to quantify the fractal dimension of inter-response time distributions in rat operant behavior and test extensions of the scaling hypothesis. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) were trained on variable-interval schedules (VI-30, VI-60, VI-120 seconds) in operant chambers. Inter-response times were recorded with millisecond precision across 2-hour sessions. The f(d) function was calculated using box-counting algorithms applied to log-log plots of inter-response time distributions across temporal scales from 100 ms to 10 minutes. Statistical analysis employed ANOVA and regression techniques to assess scaling exponents. Results demonstrated robust fractal scaling across all VI schedules, with dimension values ranging from 1.23 to 1.67. The f(d) function revealed self-similar patterns extending across four orders of magnitude in time scale. VI-30 schedules produced significantly higher fractal dimensions than VI-120 schedules (p<0.01), indicating schedule-dependent modulation of temporal organization. Within-subject variability in scaling exponents was minimal (CV<0.15), suggesting stable individual differences in behavioral microstructure. These findings provide quantitative support for the extended scaling hypothesis and establish f(d) as a sensitive measure of temporal organization in operant behavior, with potential applications for characterizing drug effects on behavioral dynamics.

title: Psychopharmacology in fragile X syndrome--present and future.
abstract: Fragile X syndrome (FXS), the most common inherited cause of mental retardation and autism spectrum disorders, results from mutations in the FMR1 gene leading to loss of FMRP protein expression. Current psychopharmacological interventions for FXS rely primarily on symptomatic management using conventional psychiatric medications, with limited understanding of syndrome-specific therapeutic targets. This review synthesizes current evidence for psychopharmacological treatments in FXS and explores emerging therapeutic strategies based on recent advances in understanding FXS pathophysiology. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of published studies on medication use in FXS populations, including both controlled trials and case series from 1991-2003, and examined preclinical findings from knockout mouse models. Current evidence supports the use of stimulants for attention deficits, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors for anxiety and repetitive behaviors, and anticonvulsants for seizure management, though systematic controlled trials remain limited. Folic acid supplementation shows promise in preliminary studies, particularly in patients with folate-sensitive sites on molecular testing. Emerging therapeutic targets based on FMRP function include mGluR5 antagonists to normalize excessive protein synthesis, GABA receptor modulators to address excitatory-inhibitory imbalance, and ampakines to enhance synaptic plasticity. Animal model studies suggest these targeted approaches may address core neurobiological deficits rather than merely managing behavioral symptoms. Future psychopharmacological research in FXS should prioritize mechanism-based drug development, incorporating biomarkers of treatment response and conducting adequately powered controlled trials to establish evidence-based treatment guidelines for this vulnerable population.
journal: Mental retardation and developmental disabilities research reviews

title: The future of pharmacotherapy for paediatric anxiety.
abstract: Anxiety disorders affect approximately 10-20% of children and adolescents, yet pharmacological treatment options remain limited due to insufficient pediatric clinical trial data and concerns regarding developmental neurotoxicity. Current off-label prescribing practices rely heavily on adult efficacy data, despite significant differences in pediatric pharmacokinetics and neurobiological development. This review examines emerging pharmacotherapeutic approaches and regulatory developments that may transform pediatric anxiety treatment over the next decade. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases (1990-2002), focusing on pediatric clinical trials, developmental neuropharmacology studies, and regulatory policy analyses. Recent developments include the FDA's proposed Pediatric Exclusivity provisions, which provide market incentives for conducting pediatric trials of existing medications. Promising pharmacological advances include selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors with improved pediatric safety profiles, novel neurokinin-1 receptor antagonists, and gamma-aminobutyric acid modulators specifically designed for developing neural systems. Preliminary data suggest that buspirone analogues and certain atypical antipsychotics may offer anxiolytic benefits with reduced metabolic side effects in children. Additionally, pharmacogenomic approaches are beginning to identify genetic polymorphisms affecting drug metabolism in pediatric populations, potentially enabling personalized dosing strategies. The integration of neuroimaging biomarkers with clinical outcomes may facilitate more targeted drug development. Future pediatric anxiety pharmacotherapy will likely emphasize mechanism-specific agents with established developmental safety profiles, supported by robust clinical trial infrastructure and regulatory frameworks specifically designed for vulnerable populations. Enhanced collaboration between academic researchers, pharmaceutical companies, and regulatory agencies remains essential for advancing this critical therapeutic area.

title: The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
abstract: The 5-HT3 receptor represents a novel class of serotonin receptors that differs fundamentally from the previously characterized 5-HT1 and 5-HT2 receptor subtypes. Unlike these G-protein coupled receptors, the 5-HT3 receptor functions as a ligand-gated cation channel, mediating rapid synaptic transmission in the central and peripheral nervous systems. This review examines the emerging psychopharmacological profile of 5-HT3 receptors based on recent radioligand binding studies, electrophysiological recordings, and behavioral investigations using selective antagonists. Pharmacological characterization reveals high-affinity binding sites for [3H]GR65630 and [3H]zacopride in brain regions including the area postrema, nucleus tractus solitarius, and hippocampus. The selective 5-HT3 antagonists ondansetron, granisetron, and tropisetron demonstrate nanomolar affinities and exhibit distinct behavioral effects in animal models. Administration of these compounds produces anxiolytic-like effects in conflict paradigms, reduces ethanol consumption in alcohol-preferring rat strains, and blocks cocaine-induced locomotor activation without affecting basal motor activity. Electrophysiological studies confirm that 5-HT3 receptor activation produces rapid membrane depolarization through increased sodium and calcium conductance. The 5-HT3 receptor appears critically involved in mediating nausea and emesis, with clinical trials demonstrating remarkable efficacy of 5-HT3 antagonists in chemotherapy-induced emesis. These findings suggest that 5-HT3 receptors represent important therapeutic targets for anxiety disorders, substance abuse, and gastrointestinal dysfunction, warranting continued investigation of their psychopharmacological properties.

title: Plate reader-based assays for measuring cell viability, neuroprotection and calcium in primary neuronal cultures.
abstract: Primary neuronal cultures represent essential models for investigating neuroprotective mechanisms and neurotoxicity, yet standardized high-throughput methodologies for assessing multiple cellular parameters remain limited. We developed and validated a comprehensive plate reader-based assay platform for simultaneous measurement of cell viability, neuroprotection, and intracellular calcium dynamics in primary cortical and hippocampal neuronal cultures. Primary neurons were isolated from embryonic day 18 Sprague-Dawley rats and cultured in 96-well microplates. Cell viability was assessed using MTT and alamarBlue reduction assays, while neuroprotection was evaluated following glutamate- and NMDA-induced excitotoxicity. Intracellular calcium measurements employed Fura-2 AM loading with ratiometric fluorescence detection. Assay parameters were optimized for signal-to-noise ratio, temporal stability, and reproducibility across multiple culture preparations. The MTT and alamarBlue assays demonstrated excellent correlation (r = 0.94, p < 0.001) and detected dose-dependent neuronal death following excitotoxic challenge. Neuroprotective effects of MK-801, memantine, and brain-derived neurotrophic factor were readily quantifiable with Z'-factors exceeding 0.6. Calcium imaging revealed robust, reproducible responses to KCl depolarization and glutamate receptor activation with temporal resolution suitable for kinetic analysis. Assay variability remained below 15% coefficient of variation within and between experimental sessions. This integrated platform provides a reliable, cost-effective approach for screening neuroprotective compounds and investigating calcium-dependent mechanisms in primary neuronal cultures, facilitating translational neuropharmacology research with improved throughput and statistical power compared to conventional microscopy-based methods.

title: Blended learning: how can we optimise undergraduate student engagement?
abstract:
**Background:** Traditional lecture-based teaching methods in undergraduate neuropharmacology education often result in passive learning and suboptimal student engagement. Blended learning approaches, combining face-to-face instruction with online components, have emerged as promising pedagogical strategies. However, optimal implementation methods for maximizing student engagement in neuropharmacology curricula remain poorly defined.

**Methods:** We conducted a randomized controlled study involving 184 third-year medical students enrolled in a neuropharmacology course at two academic institutions. Students were assigned to either traditional lecture-based learning (control, n=92) or blended learning intervention (n=92). The blended approach incorporated pre-class online modules, interactive case-based discussions, and post-class digital assessments. Student engagement was measured using validated questionnaires, attendance tracking, online participation metrics, and focus group interviews. Academic performance was assessed through standardized examinations and practical assessments.

**Results:** Students in the blended learning group demonstrated significantly higher engagement scores compared to controls (mean engagement index: 4.2±0.8 vs 3.1±0.9, p<0.001). Online module completion rates averaged 87%, with students spending mean 45 minutes per session. Examination scores were significantly improved in the intervention group (78.3±12.4 vs 71.2±14.1, p=0.002). Qualitative analysis revealed enhanced perceived relevance, increased active participation, and improved self-directed learning capabilities. Student satisfaction scores were notably higher for blended learning (4.4/5.0 vs 3.2/5.0, p<0.001).

**Conclusions:** Blended learning approaches significantly optimize undergraduate student engagement in neuropharmacology education while improving academic outcomes. Implementation of structured online components alongside traditional teaching enhances active learning and should be considered for curriculum integration in medical education programs.

title: Molecular Signaling Pathways Behind the Biological Effects of Salvia Species Diterpenes in Neuropharmacology and Cardiology.
abstract: Salvia species have been traditionally used for cognitive enhancement and cardiovascular protection, with their diterpene constituents representing key bioactive compounds. However, the molecular mechanisms underlying their neuroprotective and cardioprotective effects remain poorly characterized. This study investigated the cellular signaling pathways activated by major Salvia diterpenes, including tanshinone IIA, cryptotanshinone, and salvianolic acids, in neuronal and cardiac cell models. Primary rat cortical neurons and H9c2 cardiomyocytes were treated with purified diterpenes (1-100 μM) and subjected to oxidative stress or hypoxia-reoxygenation injury. Cell viability, reactive oxygen species levels, and protein expression of key signaling molecules were assessed using MTT assays, fluorometric analysis, and Western blotting. Diterpene treatment significantly enhanced neuronal survival (72-85% vs. 45% in controls) and preserved cardiac cell viability (78-89% vs. 38% in controls) following injury. Mechanistic studies revealed activation of the PI3K/Akt survival pathway, upregulation of Nrf2-mediated antioxidant responses, and modulation of calcium homeostasis through L-type calcium channel inhibition. Tanshinone IIA demonstrated the most potent neuroprotective effects, while salvianolic acids showed superior cardioprotective activity. Additionally, diterpenes enhanced CREB phosphorylation and BDNF expression in neurons, and activated AMPK signaling in cardiomyocytes. These findings provide mechanistic evidence supporting the traditional use of Salvia species and identify specific molecular targets for the development of diterpene-based therapeutics in neurodegenerative and cardiovascular diseases.

title: The Neuropharmacology of Cluster Headache and other Trigeminal Autonomic Cephalalgias.
abstract: Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias (TACs), including cluster headache, paroxysmal hemicrania, and short-lasting unilateral neuralgiform headache attacks, represent a distinct group of primary headache disorders characterized by severe unilateral pain and prominent cranial autonomic features. Despite significant advances in understanding their clinical presentation, the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain incompletely elucidated. This review synthesizes current knowledge of TAC pathophysiology and examines emerging therapeutic targets. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies investigating neurotransmitter systems, neuropeptide pathways, and pharmacological interventions in TACs published between 2000-2014. Evidence suggests that TACs involve dysfunction of the trigeminovascular system, with aberrant activation of trigeminal nociceptors and disruption of cranial parasympathetic outflow via the sphenopalatine ganglion. Key findings indicate that calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP), and substance P play critical roles in TAC pathogenesis. Hypothalamic involvement, particularly posterior hypothalamic gray matter, appears central to cluster headache periodicity through disrupted circadian mechanisms. Current pharmacological approaches target serotonin (5-HT1B/1D) receptors for acute treatment, while preventive strategies involve calcium channel blockers, corticosteroids, and lithium. Novel therapeutic avenues include CGRP receptor antagonists, selective 5-HT1F receptor agonists, and neuromodulation techniques. Understanding the distinct neuropharmacological profiles of individual TACs may facilitate development of more targeted, effective treatments. Future research should focus on elucidating hypothalamic-trigeminovascular interactions and identifying biomarkers for personalized therapeutic approaches in these debilitating conditions.

title: William L. Woolverton: a case history in unraveling the behavioral pharmacology of stimulants.
abstract: The behavioral pharmacology of psychomotor stimulants has been fundamentally shaped by the pioneering work of William L. Woolverton over three decades of research. This retrospective analysis examines Woolverton's contributions to understanding stimulant mechanisms through systematic application of operant conditioning paradigms and drug discrimination procedures. We reviewed 127 peer-reviewed publications (1982-2013) from Woolverton's laboratory, categorizing methodological approaches and key findings that advanced stimulant pharmacology. His early work established the predictive validity of rhesus monkey self-administration models for assessing abuse liability of novel stimulants, demonstrating that compounds sharing dopamine transporter binding properties with cocaine maintained robust self-administration behavior. Subsequent studies revealed critical distinctions between dopamine and norepinephrine transporter selectivity in determining reinforcing efficacy. Woolverton's drug discrimination studies provided the first systematic characterization of stimulant interoceptive effects, establishing cocaine, d-amphetamine, and methylphenidate as prototypical training drugs with distinct subjective profiles. His laboratory demonstrated that DAT/NET selectivity ratios could predict both discriminative stimulus effects and abuse potential. Later investigations examined environmental and pharmacological factors modulating stimulant self-administration, including alternative reinforcer availability and pretreatment with monoamine uptake inhibitors. These findings directly informed medication development strategies for cocaine use disorder. Woolverton's systematic approach exemplifies how rigorous behavioral pharmacology can elucidate drug mechanisms while maintaining translational relevance. His work established fundamental principles linking neurochemical selectivity to behavioral outcomes that continue to guide contemporary stimulant research and therapeutic development efforts.

title: The 20th scientific conference of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology.
abstract: The 20th Annual Scientific Conference of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology convened leading researchers to present cutting-edge findings in neuroinflammation and neuroimmune therapeutic interventions. This comprehensive review synthesizes key presentations addressing novel pharmacological targets in neurodegenerative diseases, HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders, and substance abuse-related neuroinflammation. Featured research encompassed both preclinical studies utilizing transgenic mouse models and clinical trials investigating neuroprotective agents. Methodological approaches included advanced neuroimaging techniques, cerebrospinal fluid biomarker analysis, and high-throughput screening of anti-inflammatory compounds. Particularly noteworthy presentations demonstrated the therapeutic potential of microglial modulation through TLR4 antagonists in Alzheimer's disease models, resulting in 40-60% reductions in amyloid plaque burden and improved cognitive performance. Clinical data revealed promising outcomes for minocycline treatment in HIV patients, showing significant improvements in neurocognitive test scores and reduced CSF inflammatory markers. Novel findings on the gut-brain axis highlighted how peripheral immune activation influences central nervous system inflammation, with specific emphasis on the role of cytokine trafficking across the blood-brain barrier. Emerging research on epigenetic modifications in neuroinflammation provided new insights into chromatin remodeling mechanisms. The conference underscored the growing recognition that neuroimmune interactions represent critical therapeutic targets for treating neuropsychiatric and neurodegenerative conditions. These collaborative efforts continue to advance our understanding of neuroinflammation's role in disease pathogenesis and highlight promising avenues for developing next-generation neuroimmune pharmacotherapies with enhanced specificity and reduced systemic toxicity.
journal: Journal of neuroimmune pharmacology : the official journal of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology

title: Discovery of novel tetrahydro-pyrazolo [4,3-c] pyridines for the treatment of neuropathic pain: synthesis and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Neuropathic pain represents a significant clinical challenge with current therapeutic options providing limited efficacy and substantial side effects. Voltage-gated sodium channels, particularly Nav1.7, Nav1.8, and Nav1.9, have emerged as promising targets for neuropathic pain management due to their selective expression in nociceptive neurons. In this study, we describe the synthesis and neuropharmacological evaluation of a novel series of tetrahydro-pyrazolo[4,3-c]pyridine derivatives as potential analgesics for neuropathic pain treatment. A focused library of 32 compounds was synthesized through a convergent synthetic approach involving cyclization of appropriately substituted hydrazines with β-keto esters, followed by selective reduction and functional group modifications. The compounds were evaluated for their ability to block voltage-gated sodium channels using whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology in DRG neurons and recombinant Nav channel subtypes. Lead compound 15c demonstrated potent and selective inhibition of Nav1.7 (IC₅₀ = 180 nM) and Nav1.8 (IC₅₀ = 95 nM) with >50-fold selectivity over cardiac Nav1.5 channels. In vivo efficacy was assessed using the chronic constriction injury model in rats, where compound 15c exhibited dose-dependent antinociceptive effects with an ED₅₀ of 12 mg/kg (p.o.) without affecting motor coordination or cardiovascular parameters. Structure-activity relationship analysis revealed that 4-fluorophenyl substitution at the C3 position and methoxy substitution at the pyridine ring were crucial for optimal activity. These findings identify tetrahydro-pyrazolo[4,3-c]pyridines as a promising new chemotype for developing safer and more effective neuropathic pain therapeutics.

title: Neuropharmacological properties of Trichosanthes dioica root.
abstract: **Background:** Trichosanthes dioica Roxb., commonly known as pointed gourd, has been traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine for treating neurological disorders. However, the neuropharmacological properties of its root extract remain poorly characterized. This study aimed to evaluate the central nervous system activities of T. dioica root extract and identify potential bioactive compounds responsible for these effects. **Methods:** Hydroalcoholic extract of T. dioica root was prepared and subjected to preliminary phytochemical screening. Neuropharmacological activities were assessed using established behavioral models in Swiss albino mice (n=6 per group). Motor coordination was evaluated using rotarod test, anxiolytic activity by elevated plus maze and light-dark box tests, anticonvulsant activity using pentylenetetrazol and maximal electroshock seizure models, and analgesic activity through hot plate and acetic acid-induced writhing tests. Extract doses of 100, 200, and 400 mg/kg were administered orally. Standard drugs diazepam, phenytoin, and morphine served as positive controls. **Results:** Phytochemical analysis revealed presence of alkaloids, flavonoids, saponins, and triterpenoids. T. dioica root extract (200-400 mg/kg) significantly increased time spent in open arms of elevated plus maze (P<0.01) and light compartment duration (P<0.05), indicating anxiolytic activity. The extract dose-dependently protected against pentylenetetrazol-induced seizures (P<0.001) and increased seizure latency in electroshock model. Significant analgesic effects were observed in both pain models without affecting motor coordination. **Conclusions:** These findings demonstrate that T. dioica root possesses anxiolytic, anticonvulsant, and analgesic properties, supporting its traditional use in neurological disorders and warranting further investigation of its bioactive constituents.

title: [Nanopsychiatry. The potential role of nanotechnologies in the future of psychiatry. A systematic review].
abstract: The convergence of nanotechnology and psychiatry represents an emerging frontier with potential to revolutionize therapeutic approaches for mental disorders. Current limitations in psychopharmacology include poor blood-brain barrier penetration, systemic side effects, and inadequate drug delivery to specific brain regions. This systematic review examined the potential applications of nanotechnology-based systems in psychiatric treatment. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of PubMed, Embase, and Web of Science databases from 2000 to 2012, using terms related to nanotechnology, drug delivery systems, and psychiatric disorders. Studies investigating nanocarriers, targeted drug delivery, and novel therapeutic approaches for depression, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and anxiety disorders were included. Twenty-three preclinical studies and four theoretical frameworks met inclusion criteria. Nanoparticle-based delivery systems demonstrated enhanced bioavailability of conventional psychotropic medications, with liposomal formulations showing 3-5 fold improvement in brain penetration compared to standard preparations. Polymeric nanoparticles loaded with antipsychotics achieved sustained release profiles extending beyond 30 days in animal models. Targeted nanocarriers utilizing transferrin and lactoferrin receptors successfully delivered therapeutics across the blood-brain barrier with minimal systemic exposure. Gold nanoparticles conjugated with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors showed preferential accumulation in limbic structures. While preliminary results suggest significant therapeutic potential, current research remains largely confined to preclinical stages. Future clinical translation will require extensive safety evaluation and optimization of targeting mechanisms. Nanopsychiatry may ultimately enable personalized, region-specific treatment approaches with improved efficacy and reduced adverse effects.

title: Principles of motivation revealed by the diverse functions of neuropharmacological and neuroanatomical substrates underlying feeding behavior.
abstract: The neural mechanisms underlying feeding behavior represent a complex interplay of motivational, reward, and homeostatic systems that have evolved to ensure energy balance and survival. While extensive research has identified key neuropharmacological and neuroanatomical substrates of feeding, the fundamental principles governing motivation across these diverse systems remain poorly understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge of feeding-related neural circuits to extract overarching motivational principles applicable to broader behavioral contexts. We analyzed findings from pharmacological studies targeting dopaminergic, serotonergic, GABAergic, and neuropeptidergic systems, alongside neuroanatomical investigations of hypothalamic, limbic, and brainstem structures involved in feeding regulation. Our analysis reveals three core motivational principles: (1) hierarchical processing, wherein homeostatic signals from hypothalamic nuclei modulate reward-processing regions including the ventral tegmental area and nucleus accumbens; (2) opponent-process dynamics, demonstrated by the reciprocal relationship between orexigenic and anorexigenic peptide systems; and (3) contextual integration, whereby environmental cues are processed through prefrontal-limbic circuits to modify feeding motivation. Pharmacological manipulations consistently show that disruption of any single system produces compensatory changes in others, suggesting robust motivational redundancy. These findings demonstrate that feeding behavior exemplifies fundamental organizational principles of motivated behavior, including distributed processing, dynamic opponent systems, and adaptive flexibility. Understanding these principles provides a framework for interpreting motivation across diverse behavioral domains and offers insights for developing therapeutic interventions for disorders of motivation and reward processing.

title: A conversation with Paul Greengard. Interview by Eric J Nestler.
abstract: This interview explores the remarkable scientific journey of Paul Greengard, Nobel laureate and pioneer in neuropharmacology, whose groundbreaking discoveries fundamentally transformed our understanding of synaptic transmission and neuronal signaling. Greengard's seminal work established that neurotransmitters function through complex intracellular signaling cascades involving protein phosphorylation, revolutionizing the field from earlier models of simple excitatory and inhibitory mechanisms. His identification of DARPP-32 (dopamine- and cAMP-regulated phosphoprotein of 32 kDa) as a key integrator of dopaminergic and glutamatergic signaling in medium spiny neurons provided crucial insights into basal ganglia function and dysfunction. The conversation traces Greengard's transition from early pharmaceutical industry research to academic pursuits, highlighting his systematic approach to understanding how psychoactive drugs modify neuronal function at the molecular level. Particular emphasis is placed on his laboratory's elucidation of protein kinase A and protein phosphatase-1 regulatory networks, which established the conceptual framework for modern neuropharmacology. Greengard discusses the clinical implications of his research for treating neuropsychiatric disorders, including Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and drug addiction, where disrupted protein phosphorylation cascades play central roles. The interview also addresses his perspectives on mentorship, interdisciplinary collaboration, and the evolution of neuroscience research methodologies. This retrospective provides valuable insights into the development of molecular neuropharmacology and emphasizes how basic mechanistic research continues to inform therapeutic strategies for central nervous system disorders.
journal: Annual review of pharmacology and toxicology

title: Future treatments for Parkinson's disease: surfing the PD pipeline.
abstract: Current therapeutic approaches for Parkinson's disease (PD) remain largely symptomatic, providing temporary relief while the underlying neurodegenerative process continues. This comprehensive review examines emerging therapeutic strategies in the PD drug development pipeline, analyzing their mechanisms of action and clinical potential. We systematically reviewed preclinical and early clinical data from 2008-2011 for novel PD therapeutics, focusing on neuroprotective and disease-modifying agents. Database searches identified 127 compounds in various developmental stages, from which we selected 34 promising candidates for detailed analysis. The pipeline encompasses diverse mechanistic approaches including α-synuclein aggregation inhibitors, mitochondrial enhancers, anti-inflammatory agents, and neurotrophic factors. Notable developments include rasagiline's potential neuroprotective effects, the emergence of adenosine A2A receptor antagonists such as istradefylline, and promising results with the iron chelator deferiprone. Gene therapy approaches using viral vectors to deliver AADC, GAD, or GDNF show encouraging preliminary results in small patient cohorts. Cell replacement strategies utilizing embryonic stem cells and induced pluripotent stem cells have demonstrated proof-of-concept in animal models. However, significant challenges remain in translating preclinical efficacy to clinical success, with several high-profile failures including coenzyme Q10 and creatine in recent trials. The evolving understanding of PD pathogenesis continues to reveal new therapeutic targets, suggesting that the next decade may witness a paradigm shift from purely symptomatic treatments toward genuine disease modification. Success will likely require combination therapies targeting multiple pathogenic pathways simultaneously.

title: The FGIN period: electrophysiological studies.
abstract: Recent investigations into FGIN-1-27 (N,N-dihexyl-2-(4-fluorophenyl)indole-3-acetamide), a synthetic ligand with high affinity for the peripheral benzodiazepine receptor, have revealed intriguing neurosteroidogenic properties that may influence neuronal excitability. However, the acute electrophysiological effects of FGIN-1-27 on central nervous system neurons remain poorly characterized. We investigated the direct electrophysiological actions of FGIN-1-27 on hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings in acute rat brain slices. FGIN-1-27 (1-100 μM) produced concentration-dependent effects on neuronal membrane properties, including a significant hyperpolarization of resting membrane potential (-2.8 ± 0.4 mV at 10 μM, p<0.01) and reduction in input resistance (18.3 ± 3.2% decrease, p<0.05). Action potential threshold was shifted to more depolarized potentials (+3.6 ± 0.8 mV at 50 μM), while action potential amplitude and half-width remained unchanged. FGIN-1-27 also enhanced the medium afterhyperpolarization following action potentials, suggesting modulation of calcium-activated potassium channels. These effects were partially reversed by the neurosteroid synthesis inhibitor finasteride (50 μM) but were insensitive to the GABAA receptor antagonist bicuculline (10 μM), indicating both neurosteroid-dependent and independent mechanisms. Field potential recordings demonstrated that FGIN-1-27 reduced population spike amplitude and delayed peak latency in a concentration-dependent manner. These findings suggest that FGIN-1-27 exerts complex modulatory effects on hippocampal neuronal excitability through multiple mechanisms, providing new insights into peripheral benzodiazepine receptor ligand actions in the central nervous system.

title: Cognitive and neurodevelopmental effects of antiepileptic drugs.
abstract: Antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) are essential for seizure control but may adversely affect cognitive function and neurodevelopment, particularly in children. This study examined the differential cognitive effects of commonly prescribed AEDs in pediatric epilepsy patients. We conducted a prospective, multicenter study of 248 children (ages 6-16 years) with newly diagnosed epilepsy who were randomized to receive monotherapy with valproate (n=83), carbamazepine (n=82), or lamotrigine (n=83). Comprehensive neuropsychological assessments were performed at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months, evaluating attention, memory, executive function, and processing speed. Plasma drug concentrations were monitored to ensure therapeutic levels. After 12 months of treatment, significant between-group differences emerged in cognitive outcomes. Children receiving valproate showed greater declines in verbal IQ (-6.2 points, p<0.001) and working memory performance compared to other groups. Carbamazepine was associated with modest impairments in attention and processing speed (-4.1 points, p=0.02). Lamotrigine demonstrated the most favorable cognitive profile, with minimal changes from baseline across all domains. Higher plasma concentrations of valproate correlated with greater cognitive impairment (r=-0.34, p<0.01). Younger children (ages 6-10) were more susceptible to AED-related cognitive effects than adolescents. These findings suggest that lamotrigine may be preferable for pediatric patients when cognitive preservation is prioritized, while valproate carries the highest risk for neurocognitive adverse effects. Clinicians should consider these differential cognitive impacts when selecting AEDs for children, particularly those with pre-existing learning difficulties or during critical developmental periods.

title: A new cross-correlation algorithm for the analysis of "in vitro" neuronal network activity aimed at pharmacological studies.
abstract: Quantitative analysis of neuronal network activity patterns is essential for understanding drug effects on synaptic transmission and network dynamics. Traditional cross-correlation methods for analyzing multi-electrode array (MEA) recordings often suffer from computational limitations and artifacts when applied to complex pharmacological datasets. We developed a novel cross-correlation algorithm optimized for analyzing spontaneous and evoked neuronal activity in cultured networks during drug applications. The algorithm incorporates adaptive time-window selection, noise reduction through iterative filtering, and statistical significance testing based on surrogate data analysis. We validated the method using MEA recordings from primary cortical neuron cultures (DIV 14-21) treated with various pharmacological agents including GABA_A receptor modulators (bicuculline, 10-50 μM; muscimol, 1-10 μM), glutamate receptor antagonists (CNQX, 10-20 μM; APV, 50-100 μM), and sodium channel blockers (TTX, 0.1-1 μM). Compared to conventional cross-correlation analysis, our algorithm demonstrated superior temporal resolution (±1 ms precision) and reduced false-positive detection rates by 60%. The method successfully detected drug-induced changes in functional connectivity patterns, revealing distinct network reorganization signatures for GABAergic versus glutamatergic modulation. Bicuculline treatment increased cross-correlation coefficients by 180% within 15 minutes, while CNQX reduced connectivity strength by 70%. Processing time was reduced 3-fold compared to existing methods while maintaining statistical robustness. This algorithm provides an improved analytical framework for pharmacological studies investigating drug effects on neuronal network dynamics and may facilitate high-throughput screening applications in neuropharmacology research.

title: Mentored and inspired by Mimo: a tribute to Erminio Costa.
abstract: Dr. Erminio Costa's pioneering contributions to neuropharmacology have profoundly shaped our understanding of GABAergic neurotransmission and neurosteroid biosynthesis over five decades of distinguished research. This tribute examines the lasting impact of Costa's mentorship and scientific innovations on contemporary neuropharmacological research through analysis of his seminal publications, collaborative networks, and influence on current therapeutic approaches. We conducted a comprehensive bibliometric analysis of Costa's 400+ peer-reviewed publications from 1960-2010, identifying key research themes and their evolution over time. Additionally, we surveyed 127 former trainees and collaborators regarding their experiences in Costa's laboratory and subsequent career trajectories. Costa's research fundamentally advanced three major areas: the role of GABA in anxiety and mood disorders, the discovery of endozepines and their interaction with benzodiazepine receptors, and the characterization of neurosteroid synthesis in brain tissue. His work on allopregnanolone and related compounds established the foundation for understanding steroid modulation of GABA-A receptors. Bibliometric analysis revealed over 15,000 citations to his work in the past decade alone, with particular impact in psychopharmacology and neurodegenerative disease research. Survey responses highlighted Costa's unique mentoring philosophy emphasizing rigorous experimental design, interdisciplinary collaboration, and translational relevance. His former trainees now lead major research programs worldwide, continuing his tradition of mechanistic neuropharmacology. Costa's scientific legacy demonstrates how visionary research coupled with exceptional mentorship can catalyze decades of scientific progress, establishing him as one of neuropharmacology's most influential figures.

title: New treatment strategies in multiple sclerosis.
abstract: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system affecting over 2.5 million people worldwide. Current therapies primarily target inflammatory processes but provide limited neuroprotection and functional recovery. This study investigated novel therapeutic approaches combining immunomodulation with neuroprotective agents in experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE), the principal animal model of MS. Female C57BL/6 mice were immunized with myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein peptide 35-55 and treated with either conventional interferon-β, a combination of low-dose interferon-β plus the neuroprotective agent erythropoietin (EPO), or EPO alone. Clinical scores, histopathological analysis, and magnetic resonance imaging were performed over 30 days post-immunization. Flow cytometry assessed peripheral immune cell populations, while Western blot analysis evaluated myelin proteins and neurotrophin expression in spinal cord tissue. Combined interferon-β/EPO treatment significantly reduced clinical severity compared to interferon-β monotherapy (mean maximum clinical score 1.8 ± 0.4 vs 3.2 ± 0.6, p<0.01). This combination preserved myelin basic protein levels, increased brain-derived neurotrophic factor expression by 2.3-fold, and promoted oligodendrocyte survival. EPO monotherapy showed modest clinical benefit but enhanced remyelination markers. The combination therapy reduced CD4+ T cell infiltration while maintaining regulatory T cell populations in the CNS. These findings suggest that combining traditional immunomodulatory approaches with neuroprotective agents represents a promising strategy for MS treatment, potentially addressing both inflammatory and degenerative disease components simultaneously.

title: Charles Darwin's emotional expression "experiment" and his contribution to modern neuropharmacology.
abstract: The study of emotional expression has deep historical roots in neuroscience, with Charles Darwin's seminal work "The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals" (1872) representing a pioneering contribution to understanding the neurobiological basis of affect. While Darwin is primarily remembered for evolutionary theory, his systematic investigation of emotional expression established foundational principles that would later influence modern neuropharmacological research. This historical analysis examines Darwin's empirical methodology and its relationship to contemporary approaches in affective neuroscience and psychopharmacology. We reviewed Darwin's original correspondence, photographic evidence, and experimental notes alongside modern neuropharmacological literature to identify conceptual continuities. Darwin's use of galvanic stimulation to elicit facial expressions, his cross-cultural observational studies, and his comparative approach across species established three key methodological precedents: the use of controlled stimulation to study neural circuits, the recognition of universal emotional patterns suggesting conserved neurobiological mechanisms, and the evolutionary framework for understanding affective systems. These approaches directly parallel modern techniques including optogenetics, cross-cultural psychopharmacological studies, and translational research using animal models. Darwin's hypothesis that emotional expressions reflect underlying "nerve-force" patterns anticipated current understanding of neurotransmitter systems and neural networks governing affect. His integration of physiological, behavioral, and evolutionary perspectives established a multidisciplinary framework that remains central to neuropharmacological research. Darwin's contributions to emotional expression research represent an underappreciated foundation for modern neuropharmacology, demonstrating the value of historical perspective in understanding scientific progress in affective neuroscience.
journal: Journal of the history of the neurosciences

title: Operant self-administration models for testing the neuropharmacological basis of ethanol consumption in rats.
abstract: Operant self-administration paradigms represent the gold standard for investigating the neurobiological mechanisms underlying ethanol consumption and addiction liability in preclinical models. These procedures allow researchers to assess voluntary ethanol intake under controlled conditions while examining the pharmacological modulation of drinking behavior. This protocol describes standardized methods for establishing ethanol self-administration in male Sprague-Dawley rats using operant conditioning chambers equipped with two response levers and fluid delivery systems. Following initial training with sweetened ethanol solutions (10% w/v with 5% sucrose), subjects progressively transition to unsweetened ethanol while maintaining stable responding on fixed-ratio and progressive-ratio schedules. Key methodological considerations include appropriate fluid concentrations, session duration (typically 30-60 minutes), training criteria for acquisition, and baseline stability requirements. The protocol enables pharmacological testing of compounds targeting various neurotransmitter systems implicated in ethanol reinforcement, including dopaminergic, GABAergic, glutamatergic, and opioidergic pathways. Validation studies demonstrate that rats reliably acquire ethanol self-administration, with intake levels correlating with blood ethanol concentrations of 50-150 mg/dl. Response patterns exhibit characteristic features of operant behavior, including extinction when ethanol is removed and reinstatement following stress or drug priming. This methodology provides a translational platform for screening potential pharmacotherapies for alcohol use disorders and investigating the neural circuitry mediating ethanol's reinforcing properties, offering significant advantages over passive administration models in terms of face and predictive validity.

title: Using a rate-frequency curve method to assess the rewarding properties of morphine in the intracranial self-stimulation paradigm in rats.
abstract: Intracranial self-stimulation (ICSS) is a widely used behavioral paradigm for assessing drug reward and abuse liability. Traditional ICSS methods analyzing response rates at fixed stimulation parameters may lack sensitivity to detect subtle drug effects. We developed and validated a rate-frequency curve method to evaluate morphine's rewarding properties with enhanced sensitivity and reproducibility. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) were implanted with bipolar electrodes in the medial forebrain bundle and trained to lever-press for electrical brain stimulation. Following stable baseline performance, rate-frequency curves were generated using a descending series of stimulation frequencies (158-25 Hz) while maintaining constant current intensity. Morphine (1.0, 3.0, 5.6, and 10.0 mg/kg, i.p.) or saline vehicle was administered 30 minutes prior to testing sessions. The rate-frequency curve method yielded three dependent measures: maximum response rate (Rmax), frequency supporting half-maximal responding (EF50), and threshold frequency. Morphine produced dose-dependent leftward and upward shifts in rate-frequency curves, significantly decreasing EF50 values at 3.0 mg/kg (p<0.01) and above, while increasing Rmax at the highest dose tested (p<0.05). These effects were naloxone-reversible (1.0 mg/kg, s.c.), confirming opioid receptor mediation. The rate-frequency curve approach demonstrated superior sensitivity compared to traditional fixed-frequency methods and reduced within-subject variability by 35%. This method provides a robust, quantitative tool for characterizing drug reward effects in ICSS paradigms and may facilitate more precise assessment of abuse liability and therapeutic potential of psychoactive compounds.

title: Single-fiber recordings of unmyelinated afferents in pig.
abstract: Recent advances in single-fiber recording techniques have enabled detailed characterization of unmyelinated afferent fibers in various mammalian species, yet comprehensive data from porcine models remain limited despite their increasing use in translational neuroscience research. The present study aimed to characterize the electrophysiological properties and response characteristics of unmyelinated C-fibers in domestic pig using in vivo single-fiber recording techniques. Adult Yorkshire pigs (n=8, 25-30 kg) were anesthetized with isoflurane and prepared for saphenous nerve recordings. Single C-fiber activity was recorded using tungsten microelectrodes positioned in nerve fascicles, with mechanical and thermal stimuli applied to identified receptive fields on the hindlimb. Conduction velocities, mechanical thresholds, and thermal response properties were quantified for 47 individual C-fibers. Mean conduction velocity was 0.89 ± 0.31 m/s, consistent with unmyelinated fiber classification. Mechanical thresholds ranged from 2.1 to 158.3 mN, with 68% of fibers classified as high-threshold mechanoreceptors. Thermal testing revealed 34% of fibers responded to noxious heat (>43°C), with mean heat thresholds of 45.2 ± 2.8°C. Response latencies and adaptation patterns were similar to those reported in other mammalian species. These findings provide the first comprehensive characterization of porcine unmyelinated afferent properties and support the utility of pig models for studying peripheral nociceptor function. The electrophysiological characteristics closely parallel those observed in human studies, reinforcing the translational relevance of porcine preparations for pain research and therapeutic development.

title: Chronic infusion of PCP via osmotic mini-pumps: a new rodent model of cognitive deficit in schizophrenia characterized by impaired attentional set-shifting (ID/ED) performance.
abstract: Current rodent models of schizophrenia-related cognitive dysfunction rely predominantly on acute drug administration or genetic manipulations, limiting their translational validity for studying chronic pathophysiology. N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction is implicated in schizophrenia cognitive deficits, particularly executive dysfunction. We developed a chronic phencyclidine (PCP) infusion model using osmotic mini-pumps to investigate sustained NMDA receptor antagonism effects on cognitive flexibility. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) received subcutaneous osmotic pumps delivering either PCP (5 mg/kg/day) or saline vehicle for 14 days. Following a 7-day washout period, animals underwent intra-dimensional/extra-dimensional (ID/ED) set-shifting testing, a rodent analog of the Wisconsin Card Sorting Task sensitive to prefrontal cortical function. Chronic PCP-treated rats demonstrated significantly impaired performance specifically during the extra-dimensional shift phase (p<0.01), requiring 68% more trials to reach criterion compared to controls, while showing normal acquisition during simple discrimination and intra-dimensional phases. Histological analysis revealed reduced parvalbumin-positive interneuron density in medial prefrontal cortex (32% reduction, p<0.05) without gross neuronal loss. Plasma PCP levels were undetectable at behavioral testing, confirming that deficits reflected neuroadaptive changes rather than acute drug effects. This chronic infusion model recapitulates core features of schizophrenia cognitive pathology, including selective executive dysfunction and prefrontal GABAergic interneuron alterations, while avoiding confounding acute drug effects. The model provides a valuable tool for investigating mechanisms underlying cognitive symptoms and evaluating potential therapeutic interventions targeting NMDA receptor hypofunction in schizophrenia.

title: Alotamide A, a novel neuropharmacological agent from the marine cyanobacterium Lyngbya bouillonii.
abstract: Marine cyanobacteria represent a rich source of bioactive natural products with potential therapeutic applications in neurological disorders. While screening extracts from Pacific marine cyanobacteria for neuroactive compounds, we isolated a novel cyclic peptide, alotamide A, from Lyngbya bouillonii collected off the coast of Palau. The structure of alotamide A was elucidated using a combination of 1D and 2D NMR spectroscopy, high-resolution mass spectrometry, and amino acid analysis, revealing a unique 14-membered cyclic peptide containing four non-proteinogenic amino acids including 3-amino-6-hydroxy-2-piperidone and N-methylated residues. Initial neuropharmacological screening demonstrated significant activity in rat cortical neuron cultures, with alotamide A exhibiting potent neuroprotective effects against glutamate-induced excitotoxicity at nanomolar concentrations (IC₅₀ = 45 nM). Patch-clamp electrophysiology studies revealed that alotamide A selectively modulates voltage-gated sodium channels, showing preferential binding to the inactivated state with a Kd of 23 nM. In vivo studies using a rat model of focal cerebral ischemia demonstrated that alotamide A (0.5 mg/kg, i.v.) significantly reduced infarct volume by 42% compared to vehicle controls when administered 30 minutes post-occlusion. The compound showed excellent blood-brain barrier penetration and minimal systemic toxicity at therapeutic doses.
