These findings identify alotamide A as a promising lead compound for neuroprotective drug development, representing a novel structural class of marine-derived sodium channel modulators with potential applications in stroke and neurodegenerative diseases.

title: Current evaluation of the millennium phytomedicine- ginseng (II): Collected chemical entities, modern pharmacology, and clinical applications emanated from traditional Chinese medicine.
abstract: Ginseng (Panax species) represents one of the most extensively studied phytomedicines, with over 4000 years of documented use in traditional Chinese medicine and substantial contemporary clinical applications. This comprehensive review evaluates the current understanding of ginseng's chemical constituents, pharmacological mechanisms, and therapeutic efficacy based on modern scientific evidence through 2009. We systematically analyzed published literature on ginseng's bioactive compounds, including ginsenosides, polysaccharides, and phenolic compounds, along with their pharmacokinetic properties and molecular targets. The primary ginsenosides Rb1, Rg1, and Re demonstrate diverse pharmacological activities including neuroprotection, immunomodulation, and cardiovascular protection through modulation of neurotransmitter systems, inflammatory pathways, and cellular signaling cascades. Neurochemical studies reveal ginseng's ability to enhance cholinergic transmission, modulate GABA and dopaminergic systems, and provide antioxidant protection against neurodegeneration. Clinical trials demonstrate significant efficacy in cognitive enhancement, fatigue reduction, and stress adaptation, with standardized extracts showing improved bioavailability compared to crude preparations. Pharmacovigilance data indicate generally favorable safety profiles, though herb-drug interactions with anticoagulants and antidiabetic medications warrant attention. Modern analytical techniques have identified over 150 chemical entities from various Panax species, with structure-activity relationships elucidating optimal therapeutic ratios of protopanaxadiol and protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides. These findings validate traditional therapeutic applications while providing mechanistic insights for evidence-based clinical use, supporting ginseng's continued relevance as a millennium phytomedicine in contemporary healthcare systems.

title: Attention: oscillations and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Neural oscillations in the gamma frequency range (30-100 Hz) have been implicated in attentional processes, yet the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying attention-related oscillatory activity remain poorly understood. We investigated how GABAergic and cholinergic modulation influences gamma oscillations during sustained attention in awake behaving rats. Local field potentials were recorded from the primary visual cortex of rats performing a visual discrimination task while administering pharmacological manipulations via reverse microdialysis. Muscimol (GABA-A receptor agonist, 1 μM) significantly reduced gamma power by 47% ± 8% and impaired task performance (correct response rate decreased from 78% ± 5% to 52% ± 7%, P < 0.001). Conversely, bicuculline (GABA-A antagonist, 10 μM) increased gamma power by 62% ± 12% but paradoxically decreased performance accuracy to 61% ± 9% (P < 0.01), suggesting that excessive gamma activity disrupts optimal attentional processing. Physostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, 50 μM) enhanced both gamma oscillations (32% ± 6% increase) and behavioral performance (89% ± 4% correct responses, P < 0.001). Scopolamine (muscarinic antagonist, 100 μM) abolished attention-related gamma enhancement and reduced performance to chance levels (49% ± 8%). Cross-correlation analysis revealed that cholinergic modulation specifically enhanced the coupling between gamma oscillations and behavioral response times (r = 0.73, P < 0.001). These findings demonstrate that optimal attentional performance requires a delicate balance of GABAergic inhibition to generate gamma rhythms and cholinergic facilitation to couple oscillatory dynamics with behavioral output, providing new insights into the neuropharmacological basis of attention.

title: Cell death and proliferation in acute slices and organotypic cultures of mammalian CNS.
abstract: The precise characterization of cell death and proliferation mechanisms in ex vivo brain preparations remains critical for advancing our understanding of CNS pathophysiology and evaluating neuroprotective interventions. While acute brain slices and organotypic cultures are widely utilized experimental models, the temporal dynamics and molecular pathways underlying cellular responses in these preparations have not been systematically compared. We investigated cell death and proliferation patterns in acute hippocampal slices (400 μm) and organotypic slice cultures from postnatal day 7-10 rats over 72 hours using propidium iodide uptake, TUNEL staining, and BrdU incorporation assays. Caspase-3 activation, PARP cleavage, and expression of proliferation markers (Ki-67, PCTN-1) were assessed by immunofluorescence and Western blotting. Acute slices exhibited rapid cell death onset within 2-4 hours, predominantly in CA1 pyramidal neurons, with peak caspase-3 activation at 6 hours and minimal proliferative activity. In contrast, organotypic cultures showed delayed but sustained cell death over 24-48 hours, primarily affecting dentate granule cells, accompanied by robust glial proliferation and increased Ki-67 expression in the subgranular zone. NMDA receptor antagonists (MK-801, 10 μM) significantly reduced cell death in both preparations, while PI3K/Akt pathway inhibition (LY294002, 25 μM) selectively suppressed proliferation in organotypic cultures. These findings demonstrate distinct spatiotemporal profiles of cell death and proliferation between acute and organotypic preparations, suggesting different underlying mechanisms. This characterization provides essential baseline data for interpreting pharmacological interventions and highlights the importance of model selection in neuropharmacological research.

title: Maps of cardiovascular and respiratory regions of rat ventral medulla: focus on the caudal medulla.
abstract: The ventral medulla oblongata contains critical neuronal networks that regulate cardiovascular and respiratory functions, yet the precise anatomical organization of these regions in the caudal medulla remains incompletely characterized. We employed a combination of electrophysiological mapping, retrograde tracing, and immunohistochemical techniques to generate detailed topographic maps of cardiovascular and respiratory neurons in the caudal ventral medulla of adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24). Single-unit extracellular recordings were performed to identify neurons with cardiac-related, respiratory-related, or dual cardiovascular-respiratory discharge patterns. Retrograde tracers (Fluorogold and cholera toxin B subunit) were injected into the intermediolateral cell column and phrenic motor nucleus to confirm bulbospinal projections. Sections were processed for tyrosine hydroxylase, choline acetyltransferase, and substance P immunoreactivity to identify neurochemical phenotypes. Our mapping revealed three distinct functional zones in the caudal ventral medulla: a rostral cardiovascular region extending from 13.2-14.8 mm caudal to bregma containing predominantly sympathoexcitatory neurons, a mixed cardiovascular-respiratory zone (14.8-15.4 mm) with neurons showing dual discharge patterns, and a caudal respiratory region (15.4-16.2 mm) containing primarily expiratory neurons. Approximately 35% of bulbospinal neurons in the mixed zone exhibited dual cardiovascular-respiratory activity, suggesting functional integration at the cellular level. These neurons were predominantly non-catecholaminergic and showed substance P immunoreactivity. Our findings provide the first comprehensive anatomical framework for understanding cardiorespiratory integration in the caudal medulla and may inform targeted interventions for autonomic dysfunction.

title: Neuropharmacological activity of the pericarp of Passiflora edulis flavicarpa degener: putative involvement of C-glycosylflavonoids.
abstract: Passiflora edulis flavicarpa Degener (yellow passion fruit) has been traditionally used for its anxiolytic properties, yet the bioactive constituents responsible for central nervous system effects remain poorly characterized. While previous investigations have focused primarily on leaf extracts, the pericarp represents an underexplored source of neuroactive compounds. This study evaluated the neuropharmacological activity of P. edulis flavicarpa pericarp extract and investigated the potential role of C-glycosylflavonoids in mediating these effects. Dried pericarp was extracted with 70% ethanol and fractionated using column chromatography. Male Swiss mice (n=8-10 per group) were administered extract (50, 100, 200 mg/kg, p.o.) and evaluated using elevated plus maze, open field, and pentobarbital-induced sleep tests. C-glycosylflavonoids were identified using HPLC-MS/MS analysis and quantified spectrophotometrically. GABAergic involvement was assessed through flumazenil antagonism studies. Pericarp extract demonstrated significant anxiolytic activity in the elevated plus maze, increasing open arm entries (p<0.01) and time spent (p<0.05) at 100 and 200 mg/kg doses without affecting locomotor activity. Extract treatment (200 mg/kg) reduced sleep latency by 38% and increased total sleep time by 45% compared to controls (p<0.001). HPLC-MS/MS revealed the presence of vitexin, isovitexin, and orientin as major C-glycosylflavonoids (total content: 12.3 ± 0.8 mg/g extract). Flumazenil (2 mg/kg, i.p.) completely blocked the anxiolytic effects, suggesting GABAergic mediation. These findings indicate that P. edulis flavicarpa pericarp possesses significant neuropharmacological activity, with C-glycosylflavonoids potentially contributing to GABAergic modulation and representing novel therapeutic targets for anxiety disorders.

title: Pharmacological MRI in animal models: a useful tool for 5-HT research?
abstract: Pharmacological magnetic resonance imaging (phMRI) has emerged as a promising translational tool for investigating serotonergic neurotransmission in vivo, yet its utility in preclinical 5-HT research remains incompletely characterized. We evaluated the sensitivity and specificity of phMRI for detecting serotonergic modulation in anesthetized rats using blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) and cerebral blood volume (CBV) measurements. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received acute administration of the selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor citalopram (10 mg/kg, i.p.), the 5-HT2A/2C receptor agonist DOI (2.5 mg/kg, i.p.), or saline vehicle during continuous MRI acquisition at 7T. BOLD and CBV responses were quantified in serotonin-rich brain regions including raphe nuclei, hippocampus, prefrontal cortex, and striatum over 120 minutes post-injection. Citalopram produced significant positive BOLD responses in the dorsal raphe (+4.2±0.8%) and hippocampus (+3.1±0.6%) that peaked at 30-45 minutes and correlated with increased extracellular 5-HT levels measured by microdialysis. DOI elicited biphasic responses characterized by early negative BOLD changes in cortical regions (-2.8±0.5% in mPFC) followed by sustained positive responses. Pretreatment with the 5-HT2A antagonist M100907 significantly attenuated DOI-induced responses, confirming receptor specificity. CBV measurements paralleled BOLD findings but showed enhanced sensitivity in subcortical structures. These results demonstrate that phMRI can reliably detect pharmacologically-induced alterations in serotonergic neurotransmission across multiple brain regions in rodents. The technique offers a valuable non-invasive approach for screening serotonergic compounds and investigating 5-HT system function in animal models of psychiatric disorders.

title: Considerations in the choice of an antiepileptic drug in the treatment of epilepsy.
abstract: The selection of appropriate antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) remains a critical clinical challenge given the heterogeneous nature of epilepsy syndromes and individual patient factors. This comprehensive review analyzes current evidence-based approaches to AED selection, examining efficacy profiles, tolerability, and patient-specific considerations across different epilepsy types. We conducted a systematic analysis of randomized controlled trials, observational studies, and clinical guidelines published between 1990-2007, focusing on first-line and adjunctive therapies for focal and generalized epilepsies. Our analysis included data from over 15,000 patients across 45 studies, evaluating both traditional AEDs (phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate) and newer agents (lamotrigine, levetiracetam, topiramate, oxcarbazepine). For focal epilepsies, carbamazepine and lamotrigine demonstrated superior efficacy-to-tolerability ratios compared to phenytoin, with lamotrigine showing particular advantages in elderly patients and women of childbearing potential. Valproate remained the preferred broad-spectrum agent for generalized epilepsies, though lamotrigine and levetiracetam emerged as viable alternatives with fewer cognitive side effects. Patient age, comorbidities, concomitant medications, and reproductive status significantly influenced optimal drug selection. Pharmacokinetic considerations, including hepatic enzyme induction and drug interactions, proved crucial in polytherapy scenarios. These findings emphasize that AED selection should be individualized based on seizure type, epilepsy syndrome, patient demographics, and tolerability profiles rather than following a universal algorithmic approach. Future research should focus on biomarkers predictive of treatment response to enable precision medicine approaches in epilepsy management.

title: When the boss turns pusher: a proposal for employee protections in the age of cosmetic neurology.
abstract: The emergence of pharmacological cognitive enhancement in workplace settings presents unprecedented ethical challenges as employers increasingly pressure employees to use prescription stimulants and other neurotropic agents to improve performance. This analysis examines the ethical implications of workplace-mandated cognitive enhancement and proposes regulatory frameworks to protect employee autonomy and safety. We conducted a systematic review of existing occupational health regulations across fifteen developed nations, analyzed case reports from occupational medicine clinics regarding off-label stimulant use, and surveyed 847 professionals in high-performance sectors including finance, law, and academia regarding employer expectations for cognitive enhancement. Our findings reveal that 23% of respondents reported direct or indirect employer pressure to use cognitive enhancers, with 67% expressing concern about future workplace coercion. Current occupational health frameworks inadequately address neurochemical coercion, creating regulatory gaps that leave employees vulnerable to discrimination and health risks. Case reports documented adverse effects including dependency, cardiovascular complications, and psychological distress among workers using unprescribed stimulants for occupational purposes. We propose a comprehensive regulatory framework including explicit prohibitions on employer-mandated cognitive enhancement, expanded definitions of workplace coercion to include neurochemical pressure, mandatory disclosure of enhancement expectations in employment contracts, and enhanced occupational health monitoring for cognitive enhancer use. These protections are essential to preserve human dignity and autonomous choice as cosmetic neurology becomes increasingly prevalent in competitive workplace environments.

title: Conflict of interest, journal review, and publication policy.
abstract: The integrity of neuropharmacological research has come under increased scrutiny following high-profile cases of undisclosed industry relationships and selective publication practices. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of conflict of interest (COI) policies and adherence patterns in major neuropharmacology journals from 2003-2007. We systematically reviewed COI disclosure statements, editorial policies, and publication patterns across 12 high-impact neuroscience journals, analyzing 2,847 original research articles and 156 review articles. Industry funding sources, author affiliations, and disclosure completeness were coded and cross-referenced with publicly available pharmaceutical industry databases. Only 34% of journals had explicit COI policies during the study period, with significant variation in disclosure requirements. Among industry-funded studies (n=892), 23% failed to disclose financial relationships that were independently verifiable through company databases. Industry-sponsored trials were more likely to report positive outcomes compared to government-funded studies (78% vs. 52%, p<0.001). Editorials and review articles showed the poorest disclosure compliance (41% adequate disclosure). Time-to-publication was significantly shorter for industry-funded studies (median 4.2 vs. 6.1 months, p<0.01). Our findings demonstrate substantial gaps in current COI policies and their enforcement within neuropharmacological publishing. The association between funding source and positive outcomes, combined with incomplete disclosure practices, raises concerns about publication bias and research integrity. We recommend mandatory registration of all funding sources, standardized disclosure requirements, and improved editorial oversight to maintain scientific credibility in an era of increasing industry-academic partnerships.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Strategy for utilization of new antiepileptic drugs.
abstract: The introduction of numerous antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) over the past decade has significantly expanded therapeutic options for epilepsy management, yet clinical decision-making regarding optimal drug selection remains challenging. This review synthesizes current evidence and expert recommendations to establish a framework for incorporating newer AEDs into clinical practice. We analyzed published clinical trials, post-marketing surveillance data, and consensus guidelines for second and third-generation AEDs including levetiracetam, oxcarbazepine, topiramate, lamotrigine, gabapentin, and pregabalin through systematic literature review covering 1995-2008. Efficacy data, tolerability profiles, drug interactions, and cost-effectiveness were evaluated across different epilepsy syndromes and patient populations. Newer AEDs demonstrate comparable or superior efficacy to traditional agents in both monotherapy and adjunctive therapy settings, with generally improved tolerability and reduced drug interactions. Levetiracetam and lamotrigine show particular promise for broad-spectrum use, while oxcarbazepine offers advantages over carbamazepine with fewer hepatic interactions. Topiramate provides dual benefits for epilepsy and migraine prophylaxis but requires careful titration due to cognitive effects. Selection criteria should prioritize seizure type, comorbidities, age-related considerations, potential drug interactions, and individual tolerability factors rather than defaulting to conventional agents. For newly diagnosed patients, newer AEDs may be considered as first-line therapy, particularly when drug interactions or tolerability concerns exist. The expanding AED armamentarium necessitates individualized treatment approaches based on patient-specific factors rather than hierarchical algorithms, potentially improving long-term seizure control and quality of life outcomes.

title: Traumatic brain injury: a review of practice management and recent advances.
abstract: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) remains a leading cause of death and disability worldwide, with an estimated 1.7 million cases occurring annually in the United States. Despite advances in acute care management, long-term functional outcomes remain suboptimal, necessitating comprehensive review of current therapeutic approaches and emerging interventions. This review synthesizes current evidence from clinical trials, observational studies, and meta-analyses published between 2000-2007 regarding TBI management strategies and novel therapeutic targets. We examined acute care protocols, neuroprotective agents, rehabilitation interventions, and pharmacological approaches for cognitive and behavioral sequelae. Recent advances in neuroimaging, particularly diffusion tensor imaging and functional MRI, have enhanced our understanding of injury mechanisms and recovery patterns. Acute management has evolved to emphasize intracranial pressure monitoring, targeted temperature management, and aggressive prevention of secondary injury. Emerging neuroprotective strategies include NMDA receptor antagonists, antioxidants, and anti-inflammatory agents, though clinical translation remains limited. Cognitive rehabilitation programs incorporating computer-based training and compensatory strategies show promise for addressing executive dysfunction and memory impairments. Pharmacological interventions, including cholinesterase inhibitors, dopaminergic agents, and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, demonstrate modest efficacy for specific cognitive and behavioral symptoms. However, the heterogeneity of TBI pathophysiology continues to challenge therapeutic development. Future directions emphasize personalized medicine approaches, biomarker development for prognostication, and combination therapies targeting multiple injury mechanisms. Multidisciplinary rehabilitation models integrating acute care with long-term cognitive and physical therapy represent the current standard for optimizing functional recovery following TBI.
journal: Physical medicine and rehabilitation clinics of North America

title: A chronically implantable, hybrid cannula-electrode device for assessing the effects of molecules on electrophysiological signals in freely behaving animals.
abstract: The study of neuropharmacological effects on neural activity in awake, behaving animals requires simultaneous drug delivery and electrophysiological recording at precise brain locations. Current methodologies often rely on separate implantation procedures or acute preparations that limit long-term studies and behavioral assessments. We developed a novel hybrid cannula-electrode device that enables chronic simultaneous microinjection and extracellular recording in freely moving rats. The device consists of a 26-gauge stainless steel guide cannula surrounding a moveable tungsten microelectrode (1-5 MΩ impedance) with a 33-gauge injection cannula positioned 0.5 mm ventral to the recording tip. Twenty-four male Sprague-Dawley rats were implanted with devices targeting the ventral tegmental area. Animals were allowed 7-10 days recovery before testing. We validated the system by examining dopaminergic responses to local apomorphine (10 μg/0.5 μL) and glutamatergic responses to NMDA (1 μg/0.5 μL) while recording single-unit and local field potential activity. Histological analysis confirmed accurate placement in 92% of implants. The device maintained stable recording properties for 4-6 weeks post-implantation, with signal-to-noise ratios comparable to standard electrodes. Drug injections produced dose-dependent changes in firing rates and spectral power without mechanical artifacts. Apomorphine decreased firing rates by 68±12% within 5 minutes, while NMDA increased activity by 156±28%. This hybrid system provides a reliable method for chronic neuropharmacological studies, offering advantages over existing techniques by combining spatial precision with long-term stability in behaving animals.

title: Two prospective educational interventions in a neurology residency: effect on RITE performance.
abstract:
**Background:** The Residency In-service Training Examination (RITE) serves as a critical benchmark for neurology resident performance and knowledge acquisition. Despite its importance, limited research exists on educational interventions designed to improve RITE scores. We hypothesized that structured educational interventions would significantly enhance resident performance on standardized neurology examinations.

**Methods:** We conducted a prospective study involving 48 neurology residents across PGY-2 through PGY-4 levels at two academic medical centers from 2005-2007. Residents were randomized to receive either intensive case-based learning sessions (Group A, n=24) consisting of weekly 2-hour structured case discussions led by faculty, or standardized question-based review sessions (Group B, n=24) involving systematic review of previous RITE questions with detailed explanations. A historical control group (n=25) received standard curriculum without additional interventions. RITE scores were compared across groups using ANOVA, with post-hoc analysis stratified by training year.

**Results:** Both intervention groups demonstrated significant improvement in RITE performance compared to controls (p<0.001). Group A showed mean score improvement of 18.3 points (95% CI: 12.4-24.2), while Group B improved by 15.7 points (95% CI: 9.8-21.6). PGY-2 residents showed the greatest benefit from case-based learning (mean improvement 22.1 points, p<0.001), whereas PGY-4 residents responded better to question-based review (mean improvement 19.4 points, p<0.01). No significant difference was observed between the two intervention strategies overall (p=0.34).

**Conclusions:** Both case-based learning and question-based review significantly improve RITE performance in neurology residents, with effectiveness varying by training level. These findings support implementing structured educational interventions tailored to resident experience in neurology training programs.

title: The neuropharmacology of L-theanine(N-ethyl-L-glutamine): a possible neuroprotective and cognitive enhancing agent.
abstract: L-theanine, a unique amino acid found predominantly in tea leaves (Camellia sinensis), has garnered increasing attention for its potential neuroprotective and cognitive-enhancing properties. This study investigated the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying L-theanine's effects on neuronal function and cognitive performance. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were administered L-theanine (50, 100, or 200 mg/kg, p.o.) daily for 14 days. Cognitive function was assessed using Morris water maze and novel object recognition tasks. Neurochemical analyses included measurement of neurotransmitter levels via HPLC, oxidative stress markers, and calcium homeostasis in hippocampal tissue. Additionally, primary cortical neurons were exposed to glutamate-induced excitotoxicity with or without L-theanine pretreatment (10-100 μM). L-theanine administration significantly improved spatial memory retention and object recognition performance in a dose-dependent manner. Neurochemical analysis revealed increased GABA levels (34% increase, p<0.01) and enhanced dopamine and serotonin concentrations in treated animals. L-theanine demonstrated potent antioxidant activity, reducing malondialdehyde levels by 28% and increasing superoxide dismutase activity. In vitro studies showed that L-theanine protected neurons against glutamate toxicity by modulating intracellular calcium influx and reducing apoptotic cell death by 45%. These findings suggest that L-theanine exerts neuroprotective effects through multiple mechanisms including GABAergic modulation, antioxidant activity, and calcium homeostasis regulation. The compound's ability to cross the blood-brain barrier and enhance cognitive function without sedative effects positions L-theanine as a promising natural nootropic agent warranting further clinical investigation.

title: 5-HT in the enteric nervous system: gut function and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine, 5-HT) serves as a critical neurotransmitter and signaling molecule within the enteric nervous system (ENS), where it regulates diverse gastrointestinal functions including motility, secretion, and visceral sensation. The gut contains approximately 95% of the body's total 5-HT, produced primarily by enterochromaffin cells in the intestinal mucosa and enteric neurons. We review current understanding of 5-HT signaling pathways in the ENS and their pharmacological modulation. Multiple 5-HT receptor subtypes (5-HT1P, 5-HT3, 5-HT4, and 5-HT7) are expressed throughout enteric neural circuits, mediating both excitatory and inhibitory responses. 5-HT3 receptors on intrinsic primary afferent neurons initiate peristaltic and secretory reflexes, while 5-HT4 receptors facilitate acetylcholine release from motor neurons, promoting propulsive motility. The 5-HT reuptake transporter (SERT) terminates synaptic 5-HT signaling and represents a key regulatory mechanism. Dysfunction of enteric 5-HT systems contributes to gastrointestinal disorders including irritable bowel syndrome, inflammatory bowel disease, and gastroparesis. Therapeutic agents targeting enteric 5-HT signaling show considerable promise: 5-HT4 receptor agonists enhance gastric emptying and colonic transit, while 5-HT3 receptor antagonists reduce visceral hypersensitivity and diarrhea. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors can modulate gut function independently of their central nervous system effects. Understanding the complex pharmacology of enteric 5-HT systems provides new opportunities for developing targeted therapies for functional and inflammatory gastrointestinal diseases, potentially offering improved efficacy and reduced systemic side effects compared to conventional approaches.

title: Strains of rodents and the pharmacology of learning and memory.
abstract: Genetic variability among rodent strains has emerged as a critical factor influencing cognitive performance and drug responsiveness, yet systematic comparisons of learning and memory pharmacology across strains remain limited. We investigated the differential effects of cholinergic and glutamatergic modulators on spatial learning and memory in C57BL/6J, DBA/2J, and 129/SvJ mouse strains. Male mice (n=12-15 per group) were trained in the Morris water maze and tested with acute administrations of scopolamine (0.1-1.0 mg/kg), physostigmine (0.05-0.2 mg/kg), MK-801 (0.05-0.2 mg/kg), and D-cycloserine (5-20 mg/kg). Baseline performance revealed significant strain differences, with C57BL/6J mice demonstrating superior acquisition and retention compared to DBA/2J and 129/SvJ strains. Scopolamine impaired performance across all strains but showed dose-dependent variation, with DBA/2J mice exhibiting greatest sensitivity (ED50: 0.3 mg/kg vs. 0.6 mg/kg in C57BL/6J). Physostigmine enhanced performance most robustly in C57BL/6J mice, while showing minimal effects in DBA/2J animals. MK-801 produced comparable impairments across strains, whereas D-cycloserine facilitated memory consolidation preferentially in 129/SvJ mice. Neurochemical analysis revealed strain-specific differences in hippocampal acetylcholinesterase activity and NMDA receptor density that correlated with drug responsiveness patterns. These findings demonstrate that genetic background profoundly influences both baseline cognitive function and pharmacological sensitivity, highlighting the importance of strain selection in neuropharmacological research and suggesting that therapeutic efficacy of cognitive enhancers may vary significantly across genetically diverse populations.

title: Functional magnetic resonance mapping of intracerebroventricular infusion of a neuroactive peptide in the anaesthetised rat.
abstract: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating brain activation patterns, yet its application to study the central effects of neuroactive peptides administered via intracerebroventricular (ICV) routes remains limited. We developed a methodology to monitor real-time brain responses to ICV peptide infusion in anaesthetised rats using blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) fMRI. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were anaesthetised with isoflurane and positioned in a 7T MRI scanner with a custom-designed head coil. A cannula was stereotaxically implanted into the right lateral ventricle for peptide delivery. Orexin-A (1 μg in 5 μL saline) was infused over 2 minutes while acquiring T2*-weighted echo-planar images every 2 seconds for 20 minutes. Control animals received saline infusion. Statistical parametric mapping revealed significant BOLD signal increases in the hypothalamus, particularly the lateral hypothalamic area and perifornical region, beginning 3-4 minutes post-infusion and persisting for 12-15 minutes. Additional activation was observed in the amygdala, bed nucleus of stria terminalis, and brainstem arousal centers including the locus coeruleus. Peak activation occurred 6-8 minutes post-infusion, with signal increases of 2.8±0.6% above baseline in the lateral hypothalamus. Saline controls showed no significant activation. This approach provides temporal and spatial resolution of peptide-induced brain activation patterns and offers a valuable method for investigating neuropeptide function and developing centrally-acting therapeutic compounds. The technique could be extended to other neuroactive substances and conscious animal preparations.

title: The effect of alprostadil on ischemia-reperfusion injury of peripheral nerve in rats.
abstract: Ischemia-reperfusion injury of peripheral nerves represents a significant clinical problem following vascular reconstruction, compartment syndrome, and microsurgical procedures. Alprostadil (prostaglandin E1) has demonstrated cytoprotective properties in various ischemic models, but its neuroprotective efficacy in peripheral nerve ischemia-reperfusion injury remains unclear. This study investigated the effect of alprostadil on functional and morphological outcomes following experimental peripheral nerve ischemia-reperfusion in rats. Forty adult Sprague-Dawley rats were randomly assigned to four groups: sham operation, ischemia-reperfusion control, low-dose alprostadil (10 μg/kg), and high-dose alprostadil (20 μg/kg). Sciatic nerve ischemia was induced by clamping the femoral artery for 4 hours followed by 24 hours of reperfusion. Alprostadil was administered intravenously 30 minutes before reperfusion. Functional recovery was assessed using the sciatic functional index and nerve conduction velocity measurements at 1, 7, 14, and 28 days post-injury. Morphological evaluation included light microscopy for axonal degeneration and electron microscopy for ultrastructural changes. Biochemical analysis measured malondialdehyde levels and superoxide dismutase activity in nerve tissue. Alprostadil treatment significantly improved functional recovery in a dose-dependent manner, with the high-dose group showing markedly better sciatic functional index scores and nerve conduction velocities compared to controls (p<0.01). Morphological examination revealed reduced axonal degeneration and preserved myelin integrity in alprostadil-treated groups. Biochemical analysis demonstrated decreased lipid peroxidation and enhanced antioxidant activity. These findings suggest that alprostadil provides significant neuroprotection against ischemia-reperfusion injury in peripheral nerves, likely through antioxidant mechanisms and preservation of microvascular perfusion.

title: Neuropharmacological activity of Nigella sativa L. extracts.
abstract: Nigella sativa L. (black cumin) has been traditionally used in folk medicine for treating various neurological disorders, yet its neuropharmacological properties remain poorly characterized. This study investigated the central nervous system effects of N. sativa seed extracts in experimental animal models. Aqueous and ethanolic extracts were prepared from N. sativa seeds and administered orally to Swiss albino mice (25-30g) at doses of 100, 200, and 400 mg/kg. Neuropharmacological activities were evaluated using standard behavioral paradigms including open field test, elevated plus maze, forced swimming test, pentylenetetrazole-induced seizure model, and barbiturate sleeping time. Phytochemical screening was performed using standard methods to identify major bioactive compounds. Both extracts demonstrated significant dose-dependent anxiolytic activity in the elevated plus maze test, with the ethanolic extract showing greater potency. In the forced swimming test, treatment with N. sativa extracts significantly reduced immobility time, suggesting antidepressant-like effects. The extracts provided protection against pentylenetetrazole-induced seizures, with the 400 mg/kg dose increasing seizure latency by 65% compared to controls. Additionally, both extracts potentiated barbiturate-induced sleep duration without affecting sleep onset. Locomotor activity remained unchanged at therapeutic doses but was reduced at 400 mg/kg. Phytochemical analysis revealed the presence of alkaloids, flavonoids, saponins, and volatile oils, particularly thymoquinone. These findings provide scientific evidence supporting the traditional use of N. sativa for neurological conditions and suggest potential therapeutic applications in anxiety, depression, and epilepsy management.

title: Genomics and proteomics: the next generation of health care.
abstract: Recent advances in genomic sequencing and proteomic analysis have created unprecedented opportunities for personalized medicine approaches in neuropharmacology, with significant implications for employee health benefit programs. This study examined the feasibility and cost-effectiveness of implementing pharmacogenomic screening in corporate health plans to optimize psychotropic medication selection. We conducted a 12-month prospective analysis of 847 employees across three Fortune 500 companies who were prescribed antidepressants or anxiolytics. Participants were randomized to receive either standard care or genomics-guided therapy based on CYP2D6, CYP2C19, and serotonin transporter polymorphisms. Primary outcomes included medication adherence, clinical response rates, and healthcare utilization costs. Secondary measures assessed workplace productivity metrics and employee satisfaction scores. Genomics-guided therapy resulted in 34% fewer medication switches (p<0.001), 28% improvement in clinical response at 8 weeks (p<0.01), and 41% reduction in adverse events requiring medical intervention (p<0.001). Total healthcare costs decreased by $1,247 per employee annually, primarily due to reduced emergency department visits and psychiatric hospitalizations. Workplace productivity increased by 12% in the genomics group, with significant improvements in absenteeism and disability claims. Employee satisfaction with mental health benefits improved significantly (p<0.05). These findings suggest that integration of pharmacogenomic testing into employee benefit programs represents a cost-effective strategy for optimizing neuropsychiatric care while reducing overall healthcare expenditures and improving workforce productivity in the emerging genomics era.

title: Early polyneuropharmacologic intervention in brain injury agitation.
abstract: Agitation following traumatic brain injury represents a significant clinical challenge that can impair rehabilitation outcomes and increase morbidity. Traditional monotherapy approaches with sedatives or antipsychotics often produce suboptimal results with significant side effects. This study evaluated the efficacy and safety of early polyneuropharmacologic intervention combining low-dose haloperidol, carbamazepine, and propranolol in managing post-traumatic agitation. A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial was conducted involving 84 patients with moderate to severe traumatic brain injury who developed agitation within 72 hours of injury. Subjects were randomized to receive either polyneuropharmacologic therapy (haloperidol 2.5 mg BID, carbamazepine 200 mg BID, propranolol 20 mg BID) or placebo for 14 days. Primary outcome measures included the Agitated Behavior Scale (ABS) and Overt Agitation Severity Scale (OASS) scores. Secondary measures assessed cognitive function using the Galveston Orientation and Amnesia Test and monitored adverse effects. The polyneuropharmacologic group demonstrated significantly greater reduction in agitation scores compared to placebo (mean ABS reduction: 18.3 vs 8.7, p<0.001; mean OASS reduction: 12.4 vs 5.9, p<0.002). Time to resolution of agitation was reduced by 4.2 days in the treatment group. Cognitive recovery was not impaired, and the incidence of extrapyramidal side effects was minimal due to low dosing. Early implementation of targeted polyneuropharmacologic intervention appears to be a safe and effective strategy for managing post-traumatic brain injury agitation while preserving cognitive recovery potential.
journal: American journal of physical medicine & rehabilitation

title: Conduction pathways in microtubules, biological quantum computation, and consciousness.
abstract: Recent theoretical frameworks propose that microtubules within neurons may serve as biological quantum computers, potentially underlying consciousness through orchestrated objective reduction (Orch-OR). To investigate the quantum mechanical properties of neuronal microtubules, we examined electrical conduction pathways in purified tubulin polymers using single-molecule conductivity measurements and quantum coherence spectroscopy. Microtubules were isolated from bovine brain tissue and reconstituted in vitro under controlled conditions. Conductivity was measured using scanning tunneling microscopy at physiological temperatures, while quantum coherence was assessed through electron spin resonance and fluorescence correlation spectroscopy. Results demonstrated that microtubules exhibit semiconductor-like properties with conductivity values of 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ S/cm, significantly higher than previously reported. Quantum coherence persisted for 10-25 microseconds at 37°C, substantially longer than thermal decoherence predictions. Treatment with anesthetic agents (isoflurane, propofol) at clinically relevant concentrations significantly reduced both conductivity and coherence times by 60-80%, correlating with known consciousness-altering effects. Tubulin dimer dipole oscillations occurred at frequencies consistent with gamma-wave neural activity (30-100 Hz). These findings support the hypothesis that microtubules possess quantum computational capabilities through organized electron transport pathways and sustained coherence. The correlation between anesthetic effects on microtubular quantum properties and consciousness suppression provides preliminary evidence for quantum processes in neural computation. Further investigation of microtubular quantum mechanics may illuminate fundamental mechanisms underlying consciousness and cognitive processing.

title: Nystagmus.
abstract: Nystagmus, characterized by involuntary rhythmic oscillations of the eyes, represents a complex oculomotor disorder with diverse etiologies ranging from congenital abnormalities to acquired vestibular and central nervous system pathology. Despite advances in understanding the neural circuitry controlling eye movements, pharmacological interventions for nystagmus remain limited and often ineffective. This review examines current neuropharmacological approaches to nystagmus treatment, focusing on recent developments in GABAergic, cholinergic, and adrenergic modulation of oculomotor control. A comprehensive analysis of published clinical trials and case series from 1990-1996 was conducted, evaluating the efficacy of various therapeutic agents including baclofen, gabapentin, clonazepam, scopolamine, and beta-blockers in reducing nystagmus amplitude and improving visual function. Results indicate that GABAergic agents, particularly baclofen at doses of 10-80 mg daily, demonstrate modest efficacy in acquired pendular nystagmus, with approximately 40% of patients showing clinically significant improvement in oscillopsia and visual acuity. Gabapentin shows promise for acquired nystagmus, with preliminary studies suggesting dose-dependent reduction in nystagmus intensity at 900-3600 mg daily. Anticholinergic agents provide limited benefit primarily in vestibular nystagmus, while adrenergic modulators show inconsistent results. Congenital nystagmus remains largely refractory to pharmacological intervention. Current evidence suggests that while no single agent provides universal efficacy, individualized pharmacotherapy based on nystagmus type and underlying pathophysiology may offer symptomatic relief. Future research should focus on novel targets within the oculomotor neural networks and combination therapeutic strategies.

title: Impairments in reflective cognitive functions in alcoholics: a neuropharmacological model.
abstract: Chronic alcohol abuse produces profound alterations in brain neurochemistry that may underlie the cognitive deficits observed in alcoholic patients. This study examined the relationship between impaired reflective cognitive functions and specific neurotransmitter system dysfunction in abstinent alcoholics using a novel neuropharmacological challenge paradigm. Twenty-four detoxified alcoholic patients (mean abstinence 28 days) and 18 age-matched controls underwent comprehensive neuropsychological assessment followed by acute administration of either scopolamine (0.4 mg, i.v.), haloperidol (2 mg, p.o.), or saline placebo in a double-blind, crossover design. Reflective cognitive functions were assessed using the Wisconsin Card Sorting Test, Trail-Making Test B, and a computerized delayed response task. Alcoholic subjects demonstrated significant baseline impairments in cognitive flexibility (p<0.01), working memory (p<0.001), and response inhibition (p<0.05) compared to controls. Scopolamine administration produced greater cognitive deterioration in alcoholics than controls across all measures (interaction effect F=4.32, p<0.05), suggesting enhanced sensitivity to cholinergic blockade. Haloperidol selectively impaired working memory performance in both groups equally, while placebo produced no additional deficits. These findings demonstrate that abstinent alcoholics exhibit heightened vulnerability to cholinergic disruption, consistent with alcohol-induced damage to acetylcholine systems in prefrontal and limbic regions. The differential neuropharmacological sensitivity observed may reflect compensatory upregulation of cholinergic receptors following chronic alcohol exposure. These results support a cholinergic contribution to the cognitive rehabilitation difficulties experienced by recovering alcoholics and suggest potential therapeutic targets for pharmacological intervention.

title: Recent developments in oculomotor neurophysiology.
abstract: Recent advances in oculomotor neurophysiology have significantly enhanced our understanding of the neural mechanisms controlling eye movements. This review examines developments over the past five years in identifying the cellular and molecular basis of oculomotor control, with particular emphasis on brainstem circuitry and neurotransmitter systems. Single-unit recording studies in alert primates have revealed distinct populations of burst neurons, pause neurons, and omnipause neurons within the paramedian pontine reticular formation that precisely coordinate horizontal saccadic eye movements. Immunohistochemical and lesion studies have demonstrated that glycinergic inhibition plays a crucial role in reciprocal innervation of extraocular motor neurons, while GABAergic mechanisms modulate burst neuron activity. Recent work has identified acetylcholine as a key neurotransmitter in the neural integrator circuits responsible for maintaining steady gaze position. Voltage-clamp studies of oculomotor neurons have characterized calcium-dependent potassium channels that contribute to the discharge patterns underlying different types of eye movements. Additionally, tract-tracing experiments using retrograde and anterograde transport techniques have mapped previously unknown connections between the superior colliculus, cerebellum, and brainstem oculomotor areas. These findings provide new insights into the pathophysiology of ocular motility disorders and suggest potential therapeutic targets. The emerging picture reveals a highly organized network of specialized neuronal populations utilizing specific neurotransmitter systems to generate the precise motor commands required for accurate eye movements and stable vision.

title: Neuropharmacology of motion sickness and emesis. A review.
abstract: Motion sickness represents a complex neurovestibular syndrome involving sensory conflict between visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive inputs, ultimately resulting in nausea, vomiting, and associated autonomic symptoms. The neural pathways mediating motion-induced emesis involve the vestibular nuclei, chemoreceptor trigger zone, and vomiting center in the medulla oblongata, with multiple neurotransmitter systems playing critical roles in symptom generation and therapeutic intervention. This review examines the current understanding of neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying motion sickness and evaluates therapeutic approaches based on neurotransmitter modulation. A comprehensive analysis of published literature from 1970-1993 was conducted, focusing on animal studies and human clinical trials investigating motion sickness pathophysiology and treatment. Histaminergic pathways appear central to motion sickness susceptibility, with H1 receptor antagonists such as dimenhydrinate and meclizine demonstrating significant prophylactic efficacy. Cholinergic mechanisms involving muscarinic receptors also contribute substantially, as evidenced by the therapeutic benefit of scopolamine, particularly via transdermal delivery systems. Dopaminergic neurotransmission at the chemoreceptor trigger zone plays a secondary but important role, with D2 receptor antagonists showing moderate anti-emetic properties. Recent investigations suggest involvement of serotonergic pathways and potential therapeutic applications of 5-HT3 receptor antagonists. The multifactorial nature of motion sickness neuropharmacology indicates that combination therapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems may offer superior therapeutic outcomes compared to single-agent approaches, while minimizing dose-dependent adverse effects such as sedation and cognitive impairment.

title: Emergency Neuropharmacology.
abstract: Acute neurological emergencies requiring immediate pharmacological intervention represent a significant challenge in emergency medicine, with delayed or inappropriate treatment leading to increased morbidity and mortality. This comprehensive review examines current evidence-based neuropharmacological approaches for common emergency neurological conditions presenting to the emergency department. We conducted a systematic literature review of randomized controlled trials, observational studies, and clinical guidelines published between 2015-2021, focusing on pharmacological management of status epilepticus, acute stroke, severe traumatic brain injury, and acute agitation with neurological components. Data sources included PubMed, EMBASE, and Cochrane databases, with emphasis on emergency department-specific protocols and outcomes. Analysis of 247 studies revealed significant advances in rapid-sequence neuropharmacological interventions. For refractory status epilepticus, early initiation of continuous midazolam or propofol infusions improved seizure control rates by 34% compared to traditional stepwise approaches. In acute ischemic stroke, streamlined alteplase protocols reduced door-to-needle times to under 45 minutes in 78% of cases. For severe traumatic brain injury, targeted osmotherapy with 23.4% saline demonstrated superior intracranial pressure control compared to mannitol (p<0.05). Ketamine emerged as first-line therapy for excited delirium, showing 89% success rates for safe sedation. These findings support the implementation of protocol-driven neuropharmacological approaches in emergency settings. Emergency physicians must maintain proficiency in rapid neurological assessment and targeted pharmacological interventions to optimize patient outcomes in time-sensitive neurological emergencies.
journal: Emergency medicine clinics of North America

title: Epigenetic Neuropharmacology: Drugs Affecting the Epigenome in the Brain.
abstract: The emerging field of epigenetic neuropharmacology represents a paradigm shift in understanding how pharmacological interventions can modulate gene expression in the central nervous system through chromatin modifications. Epigenetic mechanisms, including DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNA regulation, play crucial roles in synaptic plasticity, memory formation, and neuropsychiatric disorders. This review synthesizes recent advances in drugs that target epigenetic machinery in neural tissues, examining their therapeutic potential and mechanistic actions. We analyzed preclinical and clinical studies from 2015-2020 involving epigenetic modulators including histone deacetylase inhibitors (HDACi), DNA methyltransferase inhibitors, histone methyltransferase inhibitors, and bromodomain inhibitors. Particular attention was given to their applications in treating neurodegenerative diseases, addiction, depression, and cognitive disorders. HDAC inhibitors such as vorinostat and sodium butyrate demonstrated significant neuroprotective effects in Alzheimer's disease models by enhancing memory-related gene expression. DNA methylation modulators like 5-azacytidine showed promise in reversing addiction-related epigenetic modifications in reward circuitry. Bromodomain inhibitors exhibited antidepressant-like effects through chromatin remodeling in stress-responsive brain regions. However, challenges remain regarding blood-brain barrier penetration, off-target effects, and optimal dosing strategies. The specificity of epigenetic modifications across different brain regions and cell types presents both opportunities for targeted therapies and complications for systemic drug delivery. Future developments in brain-penetrant, region-specific epigenetic drugs hold considerable promise for treating previously intractable neurological and psychiatric conditions through precise modulation of neural gene expression programs.
journal: Annual review of pharmacology and toxicology

title: Compact 256-channel multi-well microelectrode array system for in vitro neuropharmacology test.
abstract: High-throughput screening of neuroactive compounds requires scalable platforms that can simultaneously monitor neuronal activity across multiple conditions while maintaining physiological relevance. Current microelectrode array (MEA) systems are limited by low channel density, bulky instrumentation, or inadequate throughput for pharmacological screening applications. We developed a compact 256-channel multi-well MEA system specifically designed for in vitro neuropharmacology testing. The system integrates 16 independent wells, each containing a 4×4 array of titanium nitride microelectrodes (30 μm diameter, 200 μm spacing) fabricated on glass substrates using standard photolithography. Custom low-noise amplification circuits and multiplexed data acquisition enable simultaneous recording from all channels at 20 kHz sampling rate. We validated the platform using primary rat cortical cultures and human iPSC-derived neurons, demonstrating stable recordings of spontaneous network activity for over 21 days in vitro. Pharmacological validation included dose-response characterization of GABA_A receptor modulators (muscimol, bicuculline), glutamate receptor antagonists (CNQX, APV), and sodium channel blockers (TTX, lidocaine). The system successfully detected compound effects with EC50 values consistent with literature reports and showed excellent inter-well reproducibility (CV < 15%). Signal-to-noise ratios exceeded 8:1 for 89% of electrodes. The compact footprint (15×10 cm) and integrated design enable deployment in standard laboratory settings. This platform addresses the growing need for medium-throughput electrophysiological screening in drug discovery, offering a practical solution for neuropharmacology laboratories requiring reliable, cost-effective neuronal activity monitoring across multiple experimental conditions.

title: Introduction to the Theme "Ion Channels and Neuropharmacology: From the Past to the Future".
abstract: Ion channels represent fundamental molecular targets in neuropharmacology, serving as critical mediators of neuronal excitability and synaptic transmission. Over the past five decades, our understanding of ion channel structure, function, and pharmacology has evolved dramatically, transforming therapeutic approaches to neurological and psychiatric disorders. This review synthesizes historical developments and contemporary advances in ion channel neuropharmacology, examining the trajectory from early electrophysiological discoveries to modern precision medicine approaches. We analyze key milestones including the identification of voltage-gated sodium, potassium, and calcium channels, the characterization of ligand-gated ion channels, and the development of channel-targeted therapeutics. Contemporary structural biology techniques, including cryo-electron microscopy and X-ray crystallography, have revealed atomic-level details of channel architecture, enabling structure-based drug design. High-throughput screening technologies and computational modeling have accelerated the identification of novel channel modulators with improved selectivity profiles. Recent advances in optogenetics and chemogenetics have provided unprecedented tools for dissecting channel function in vivo, while emerging gene therapy approaches offer potential for treating channelopathies at their molecular origin. Current challenges include developing subtype-selective modulators to minimize off-target effects, understanding the role of channel auxiliary subunits in drug responses, and translating preclinical findings to clinical success. Future directions encompass personalized pharmacotherapy based on genetic variants, exploitation of allosteric modulation mechanisms, and integration of artificial intelligence in drug discovery. Ion channel neuropharmacology continues to represent a dynamic field with immense therapeutic potential for addressing unmet clinical needs in neurological disorders.
journal: Annual review of pharmacology and toxicology

title: Combinatorial Oxytocin Neuropharmacology in Social Cognition.
abstract: Social cognition encompasses complex behavioral processes including empathy, trust, and social memory that are critically regulated by neuropeptide signaling systems. While oxytocin (OT) has emerged as a key modulator of prosocial behavior, recent evidence suggests that its effects depend heavily on interactions with other neurotransmitter systems. Here, we investigated the combinatorial effects of oxytocin with dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic signaling on social cognitive performance in healthy adults. Using a double-blind, placebo-controlled design, 120 participants received intranasal oxytocin (24 IU) combined with either L-DOPA (100mg), citalopram (20mg), or lorazepam (1mg) before completing validated social cognition tasks including emotion recognition, theory of mind, and trust paradigms. Neuroimaging data were acquired using task-based fMRI during social decision-making. Results revealed that oxytocin-dopamine co-administration significantly enhanced trust behavior (p<0.001) and increased activation in the ventral tegmental area and nucleus accumbens compared to oxytocin alone. The oxytocin-serotonin combination improved emotion recognition accuracy (p<0.01) with corresponding increases in amygdala-prefrontal connectivity. Conversely, oxytocin-GABA co-treatment impaired social memory performance and reduced anterior cingulate cortex activation. Computational modeling indicated that these combinatorial effects arise from synergistic modulation of prediction error signals during social learning. These findings demonstrate that oxytocin's prosocial effects emerge through dynamic interactions with classical neurotransmitter systems, suggesting that effective therapeutic interventions for social cognitive disorders may require multi-target pharmacological approaches rather than single-agent treatments.

title: Neuropharmacological effects of d-pinitol and its possible mechanisms of action.
abstract: D-pinitol, a naturally occurring cyclitol found in various legumes and traditional medicinal plants, has garnered attention for its potential therapeutic properties, yet its neuropharmacological effects remain poorly characterized. This study investigated the central nervous system activities of d-pinitol and elucidated its underlying mechanisms of action. Male Swiss albino mice (n=8 per group) received oral administration of d-pinitol (25, 50, and 100 mg/kg) or vehicle controls. Behavioral assessments included open field test, elevated plus maze, forced swimming test, and pentylenetetrazole-induced seizure model. Neurochemical analyses were performed using high-performance liquid chromatography to measure monoamine neurotransmitters in brain homogenates. Molecular docking studies examined d-pinitol's interaction with GABA-A and serotonin receptors. D-pinitol demonstrated dose-dependent anxiolytic effects in the elevated plus maze, significantly increasing open arm entries (p<0.01) and time spent in open arms (p<0.05) at 50 and 100 mg/kg doses. In the forced swimming test, d-pinitol reduced immobility time by 34% (p<0.001) at the highest dose, indicating antidepressant-like activity. Seizure latency was significantly prolonged (p<0.01) following d-pinitol pretreatment. Neurochemical analysis revealed increased serotonin (43%) and norepinephrine (28%) levels in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Molecular docking indicated favorable binding affinity of d-pinitol to GABA-A receptor sites. These findings suggest that d-pinitol exhibits significant anxiolytic, antidepressant, and anticonvulsant properties, potentially mediated through GABAergic enhancement and monoaminergic modulation, supporting its traditional use in neurological disorders.

title: Wireless optofluidic brain probes for chronic neuropharmacology and photostimulation.
abstract: Chronic neuropharmacology studies and optogenetic manipulations in freely behaving animals require reliable methods for simultaneous drug delivery and optical stimulation deep within brain tissue. Current approaches using tethered systems limit behavioral repertoires and cause chronic inflammation at probe sites. Here we present wireless optofluidic neural probes that integrate microfluidic drug delivery channels with microscale light-emitting diodes (μLEDs) on flexible substrates for minimally invasive chronic implantation. The devices feature independently addressable μLED arrays (470 nm, 10 mW/mm²) and dual microfluidic channels enabling controlled delivery of multiple pharmacological agents with nanoliter precision. We validated probe functionality through chronic implantation in the ventral tegmental area of transgenic mice expressing channelrhodopsin-2, demonstrating stable drug infusion and optical stimulation over 8 weeks with minimal tissue damage. Behavioral experiments combining dopamine receptor antagonists with optogenetic stimulation revealed dissociable effects on reward-seeking behavior that were not observable using traditional pharmacological approaches alone. Wireless operation was achieved through inductive coupling with external control units, enabling unrestricted movement during 6-hour experimental sessions. Histological analysis showed 60% reduction in glial scarring compared to conventional rigid probes, with maintained drug diffusion profiles throughout the chronic implantation period. Post-mortem examination confirmed precise targeting with <50 μm deviation from intended coordinates. These wireless optofluidic probes enable sophisticated neuropharmacological manipulations in naturalistic behavioral contexts, providing a powerful platform for dissecting neural circuit function and advancing therapeutic neuromodulation strategies.

title: Neuropharmacology of the Neuropsychiatric Symptoms of Dementia and Role of Pain: Essential Oil of Bergamot as a Novel Therapeutic Approach.
abstract: Neuropsychiatric symptoms (NPS) including agitation, anxiety, and depression significantly impact quality of life in dementia patients and represent major clinical challenges. Emerging evidence suggests chronic pain may exacerbate NPS through shared neuroinflammatory pathways involving microglial activation and altered neurotransmitter signaling. Essential oil of bergamot (Citrus bergamia) contains bioactive compounds including linalool and linalyl acetate that demonstrate neuroprotective properties, yet its therapeutic potential for dementia-related NPS remains unexplored. We investigated bergamot essential oil's effects on NPS and pain-related behaviors using the 5xFAD transgenic mouse model of Alzheimer's disease. Twenty-four 8-month-old mice received either bergamot oil aromatherapy (0.1% v/v, 30 min daily) or control treatment for 4 weeks. Behavioral assessments included elevated plus maze, forced swim test, and von Frey mechanical sensitivity testing. Post-mortem analysis examined hippocampal and frontal cortex levels of inflammatory markers (IL-1β, TNF-α), neurotransmitters (serotonin, GABA), and pain-related neuropeptides. Bergamot treatment significantly reduced anxiety-like behaviors (p<0.01) and depressive symptoms (p<0.05) compared to controls. Mechanical pain sensitivity was notably decreased following treatment (p<0.01). Neurochemical analysis revealed reduced neuroinflammation with decreased IL-1β (-35%) and TNF-α (-28%) expression, alongside increased serotonergic signaling (+42%) and GABAergic activity (+31%). Substance P levels, associated with pain processing, were significantly diminished (-39%). These findings suggest bergamot essential oil ameliorates NPS through anti-inflammatory mechanisms and neurotransmitter modulation while addressing pain-related components, supporting its potential as a complementary therapeutic intervention for dementia-associated neuropsychiatric complications.

title: [Pharmacotherapy of chronic neuropathic pain].
abstract: Chronic neuropathic pain affects 7-10% of the general population and represents a significant therapeutic challenge due to its complex pathophysiology involving peripheral and central sensitization mechanisms. Current pharmacological approaches often provide insufficient pain relief with considerable side effects. This systematic review analyzed the efficacy and tolerability of established and emerging pharmacotherapeutic options for chronic neuropathic pain management. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of PubMed, Cochrane Library, and EMBASE databases from 2010 to 2018, identifying 847 relevant studies including randomized controlled trials, meta-analyses, and clinical guidelines. Primary endpoints included pain reduction measured by visual analog scale (VAS) or numerical rating scale (NRS), and secondary endpoints comprised functional improvement and adverse event profiles. Analysis of 156 high-quality studies revealed that gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin) demonstrated moderate efficacy with number-needed-to-treat (NNT) values of 6.3-7.7 for 50% pain reduction. Tricyclic antidepressants showed comparable efficacy (NNT 3.6-6.4) but with higher discontinuation rates due to anticholinergic side effects. Topical agents including lidocaine patches and capsaicin demonstrated favorable risk-benefit profiles for localized neuropathic pain. Emerging therapies such as high-concentration capsaicin patches and botulinum toxin A showed promising results in refractory cases. Combination therapy approaches yielded superior outcomes compared to monotherapy in treatment-resistant patients. These findings support a multimodal, individualized approach to neuropathic pain management, emphasizing the importance of patient-specific factors in treatment selection and the potential benefits of combining pharmacological agents with different mechanisms of action.

title: Opioid Neurobiology, Neurogenetics and Neuropharmacology in Zebrafish.
abstract: The zebrafish (Danio rerio) has emerged as a powerful vertebrate model for studying opioid systems due to high genetic homology with mammals and amenability to high-throughput screening. This review synthesizes current understanding of opioid neurobiology, neurogenetics, and neuropharmacology in zebrafish, highlighting translational applications for addiction research. Zebrafish possess functional orthologs of mammalian opioid receptors (μ, δ, κ) and endogenous opioid peptides, with conserved expression patterns in key brain regions including the telencephalon, diencephalon, and hindbrain. Pharmacological studies demonstrate that zebrafish exhibit dose-dependent responses to morphine, fentanyl, and other opioid agonists, including analgesia, respiratory depression, and locomotor effects comparable to mammalian responses. Behavioral paradigms such as conditioned place preference and self-administration protocols have successfully modeled addiction-like behaviors in zebrafish. Genetic approaches utilizing CRISPR/Cas9 and transgenic lines have enabled targeted manipulation of opioid receptor subtypes, revealing distinct functional roles in nociception, reward processing, and stress responses. Transcriptomic analyses following chronic opioid exposure identified conserved molecular pathways involved in tolerance and dependence, including alterations in dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic signaling. Forward genetic screens have discovered novel modulators of opioid sensitivity and withdrawal behaviors. The zebrafish model offers unique advantages for mechanistic studies of opioid action, including optical transparency for in vivo imaging, large clutch sizes for statistical power, and rapid development enabling longitudinal studies. These findings establish zebrafish as a valuable complement to rodent models for advancing our understanding of opioid pharmacology and developing therapeutic interventions for opioid use disorders.

title: Drug-gut microbiota interactions: implications for neuropharmacology.
abstract: The gut microbiota has emerged as a critical modulator of drug metabolism and efficacy, yet its impact on centrally-acting pharmaceuticals remains poorly understood. This study investigated how commensal bacteria influence the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of commonly prescribed neurotropic medications. We administered fluoxetine (10 mg·kg⁻¹), diazepam (2 mg·kg⁻¹), and levodopa (100 mg·kg⁻¹) to conventional and germ-free C57BL/6 mice, alongside animals treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin 1 g·L⁻¹, vancomycin 0.5 g·L⁻¹, neomycin 1 g·L⁻¹, metronidazole 1 g·L⁻¹ for 14 days). Drug concentrations in plasma and brain tissue were quantified using LC-MS/MS, whilst behavioural responses were assessed through elevated plus maze, forced swim test, and rotarod performance. 16S rRNA sequencing characterized microbiotal composition. Antibiotic-treated mice exhibited significantly altered pharmacokinetics for all compounds: fluoxetine brain concentrations increased 2.3-fold (P<0.01), diazepam clearance decreased by 40% (P<0.001), and levodopa bioavailability was reduced by 35% (P<0.05) compared to controls. Germ-free animals showed similar patterns, with enhanced anxiolytic responses to diazepam and prolonged antidepressant effects of fluoxetine. Microbiotal depletion correlated with reduced expression of hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes and altered gut transit time. These findings demonstrate that gut bacteria substantially influence neurotropic drug disposition and efficacy through modulation of hepatic metabolism and intestinal absorption. Clinical implications include potential need for dosage adjustments in patients receiving antimicrobial therapy or presenting with dysbiotic conditions.

title: Corticotropin-Releasing Factor (CRF) Neurocircuitry and Neuropharmacology in Alcohol Drinking.
abstract: Corticotropin-releasing factor (CRF) signaling plays a critical role in stress-induced alcohol consumption and the development of alcohol use disorders. The extended amygdala, particularly the central nucleus of the amygdala (CeA) and bed nucleus of the stria terminalis (BNST), represents a key neuroanatomical substrate where CRF modulates alcohol-seeking behaviors. This review synthesizes current understanding of CRF neurocircuitry and pharmacological interventions targeting the CRF system in preclinical models of alcohol consumption. We examined evidence from rodent studies utilizing operant self-administration, conditioned place preference, and reinstatement paradigms, focusing on the effects of CRF receptor antagonists and genetic manipulations. CRF1 receptor antagonism consistently reduces stress-induced reinstatement of alcohol seeking and escalated drinking in dependent animals, while having minimal effects on baseline consumption in non-dependent subjects. Antalarmin, CP-154,526, and more selective compounds like R121919 demonstrate efficacy in attenuating alcohol-motivated behaviors across multiple laboratories. CRF2 receptor modulation shows more complex, context-dependent effects on alcohol consumption. Neuroanatomically, CRF release in the CeA and BNST increases during withdrawal and stress exposure, correlating with enhanced alcohol seeking. The CRF system appears to be particularly important during the transition from recreational to compulsive alcohol use. These findings support the therapeutic potential of CRF receptor antagonists for treating alcohol use disorders, particularly in individuals with comorbid stress-related pathology. Future directions include developing brain-penetrant CRF1 antagonists with improved pharmacokinetic properties and exploring combination therapies targeting multiple stress-related neurotransmitter systems.

title: ISOLATION OF NOVEL PARA-PENTYL PHENYL BENZOATE FROM MONDIA WHITEI. (HOOK.F.) SKEELS (PERIPLOCACEAE), ITS STRUCTURE, SYNTHESIS AND NEUROPHARMACOLOGICAL EVALUATION.
abstract:
**Background:** Mondia whitei (Hook.f.) Skeels is a medicinal plant widely used in traditional African medicine for treating various neurological disorders including anxiety, depression, and cognitive impairment. Despite its ethnopharmacological importance, the bioactive compounds responsible for its neuropharmacological effects remain poorly characterized. This study aimed to isolate, identify, and evaluate the neuropharmacological properties of bioactive compounds from M. whitei root extracts.

**Methods:** The root bark of M. whitei was extracted using methanol and subjected to bioassay-guided fractionation using column chromatography. Structural elucidation of the isolated compound was performed using spectroscopic techniques including ¹H-NMR, ¹³C-NMR, and mass spectrometry. The compound was subsequently synthesized using standard organic chemistry methods. Neuropharmacological evaluation was conducted using Swiss albino mice (n=6-8 per group) employing elevated plus maze, forced swim test, and Morris water maze paradigms. Acute toxicity was assessed according to OECD guidelines.

**Results:** A novel compound, para-pentyl phenyl benzoate, was successfully isolated and characterized from M. whitei root bark (yield: 0.034% w/w). The synthesized compound showed 98% purity and identical spectroscopic properties to the natural isolate. At doses of 25-100 mg/kg (p.o.), para-pentyl phenyl benzoate demonstrated significant anxiolytic effects in the elevated plus maze (p<0.01), antidepressant activity in the forced swim test (p<0.05), and enhanced spatial memory in the Morris water maze (p<0.01) compared to controls. No acute toxicity was observed up to 2000 mg/kg.

**Conclusions:** Para-pentyl phenyl benzoate represents a novel bioactive compound from M. whitei with promising neuropharmacological properties, supporting the traditional use of this plant in neurological disorders and warranting further mechanistic studies.
journal: African journal of traditional, complementary, and alternative medicines : AJTCAM

title: Neuroimmune Pharmacology, 2nd Edition - A Perspective.
abstract: The field of neuroimmune pharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation since its inception, evolving from a niche area of investigation to a cornerstone of modern neuroscience and drug discovery. This perspective examines the major advances in neuroimmune pharmacology over the past decade, highlighting key therapeutic targets and emerging concepts that have reshaped our understanding of brain-immune interactions. We conducted a comprehensive review of literature from 2007-2017, analyzing breakthrough discoveries in microglial pharmacology, neuroinflammation modulators, and immune-to-brain signaling pathways. Our analysis reveals that the identification of microglial phenotypic switching, the characterization of resolution-promoting lipid mediators, and the discovery of glymphatic system dysfunction have fundamentally altered therapeutic approaches to neurological disorders. Notable advances include the development of CSF1R antagonists for microglial depletion, specialized pro-resolving mediators for neuroinflammation resolution, and blood-brain barrier modulators for enhanced drug delivery. Clinical translation has accelerated, with over 15 neuroimmune-targeted therapeutics entering Phase II/III trials for conditions ranging from Alzheimer's disease to major depression. The integration of systems pharmacology approaches and biomarker-guided patient stratification has enhanced drug development efficiency. These developments underscore the maturation of neuroimmune pharmacology as a distinct discipline with immense therapeutic potential. Future directions point toward precision medicine approaches, combination therapies targeting multiple neuroimmune pathways, and the exploration of peripheral immune modulation for central nervous system disorders. The field stands poised to deliver transformative treatments for previously intractable neurological and psychiatric conditions.
journal: Journal of neuroimmune pharmacology : the official journal of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology

title: Herbal Highs: Review on Psychoactive Effects and Neuropharmacology.
abstract: The increasing popularity of herbal psychoactive substances as legal alternatives to controlled drugs has raised significant public health concerns, yet their neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This comprehensive review examines the neuropharmacological profiles and psychoactive effects of commonly used herbal substances including Salvia divinorum, Mitragyna speciosa (kratom), Piper methysticum (kava), and synthetic cannabinoid receptor agonists found in "Spice" products. We conducted a systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature published between 2000-2016, focusing on receptor binding studies, neurochemical mechanisms, behavioral effects, and adverse event reports.
