Salvia divinorum demonstrated potent and selective κ-opioid receptor agonism, producing intense but short-lived dissociative effects. Kratom alkaloids exhibited complex opioid receptor pharmacology with μ-opioid receptor agonism and adrenergic activity, resulting in dose-dependent stimulant and analgesic properties. Kavalactones showed GABAergic enhancement and sodium channel blockade, producing anxiolytic and sedative effects. Synthetic cannabinoids displayed variable and often unpredictable binding profiles at CB1/CB2 receptors, with significantly higher potency than Δ9-THC and association with severe adverse events including seizures and psychosis. Pharmacokinetic data revealed rapid onset but variable duration of action across substances. These findings highlight the diverse neurochemical targets of herbal psychoactive compounds and underscore the need for enhanced regulatory oversight, standardized analytical methods, and clinician education regarding their abuse potential and toxicological profiles.

title: A review of Neuropharmacology Effects of Nigella sativa and Its Main Component, Thymoquinone.
abstract: Nigella sativa (black seed) has been traditionally used for various neurological disorders, with emerging evidence suggesting significant neuropharmacological properties. This comprehensive review examines the neurotherapeutic potential of N. sativa and its principal bioactive compound, thymoquinone (TQ), based on preclinical and clinical studies published between 2000-2015. A systematic literature search was conducted using PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science databases to identify relevant studies investigating the neuropharmacological effects of N. sativa extracts and TQ. Data were extracted from 67 studies including in vitro, in vivo, and clinical investigations examining neuroprotective, anticonvulsant, antidepressant, anxiolytic, and cognitive enhancement properties. N. sativa and TQ demonstrated significant neuroprotective effects through multiple mechanisms including antioxidant activity, inhibition of neuroinflammation, modulation of neurotransmitter systems, and protection against neuronal apoptosis. In animal models, TQ exhibited dose-dependent anticonvulsant effects comparable to standard antiepileptic drugs, while N. sativa extracts showed anxiolytic and antidepressant activities mediated through GABAergic and serotonergic pathways. Both compounds enhanced cognitive function and memory consolidation in rodent models of Alzheimer's disease and aging. Limited clinical trials supported these findings, showing improved cognitive scores and reduced seizure frequency in epileptic patients. The neuropharmacological effects appear to be primarily mediated through TQ's ability to cross the blood-brain barrier and interact with multiple molecular targets. N. sativa and TQ represent promising therapeutic agents for neurological disorders, warranting further clinical investigation to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term safety profiles.

title: Glia and immune cell signaling in bipolar disorder: insights from neuropharmacology and molecular imaging to clinical application.
abstract: Bipolar disorder (BD) is increasingly recognized as a neuroinflammatory condition, yet the precise mechanisms linking glial dysfunction and immune activation to mood episodes remain poorly understood. We investigated microglial activation patterns and cytokine signaling cascades in BD patients during different mood states using combined neuroimaging and pharmacological approaches. Twenty-eight medication-free BD patients (15 manic, 13 depressed) and 22 healthy controls underwent [11C]PK11195 positron emission tomography to assess microglial activation, alongside comprehensive cytokine profiling and cognitive testing. Additionally, we examined the effects of lithium and valproate on primary human microglia and astrocyte cultures stimulated with lipopolysaccharide. BD patients showed significantly elevated microglial activation in prefrontal cortex and anterior cingulate compared to controls (p<0.001), with manic patients demonstrating the highest binding potential values. Plasma levels of IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β were elevated in both mood states, correlating positively with microglial activation (r=0.62-0.74, p<0.01). Cognitive performance inversely correlated with neuroinflammatory markers. In vitro, lithium significantly reduced microglial IL-1β release and NF-κB activation, while valproate primarily modulated astrocytic inflammatory responses through HDAC inhibition. These findings demonstrate state-dependent neuroinflammation in BD, with distinct glial cell contributions to immune dysregulation. The anti-inflammatory properties of mood stabilizers suggest that targeting neuroinflammation represents a promising therapeutic avenue. Our results support the development of adjunctive anti-inflammatory strategies and highlight molecular imaging as a valuable tool for monitoring treatment response in BD.

title: PET measurement of receptor occupancy as a tool to guide dose selection in neuropharmacology: are we asking the right questions?
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) measurement of receptor occupancy has become a cornerstone methodology in central nervous system drug development, with the assumption that achieving specific occupancy thresholds predicts clinical efficacy. However, the relationship between receptor occupancy and therapeutic response remains poorly understood for many psychiatric medications. This review examines current practices in PET-guided dose selection and challenges fundamental assumptions underlying occupancy-based dosing strategies. We analyzed published PET occupancy studies from 1995-2012 for antipsychotics, antidepressants, and anxiolytics, comparing occupancy-efficacy relationships across therapeutic classes. While dopamine D2 receptor occupancy shows robust correlation with antipsychotic efficacy (65-80% occupancy threshold), similar relationships are less consistent for other targets. Serotonin transporter occupancy for antidepressants demonstrates high interindividual variability in occupancy-response relationships, with some patients responding at <50% occupancy while others require >90%. For anxiolytics targeting GABAA receptors, occupancy measurements show poor correlation with clinical anxiety scores. Additionally, traditional occupancy models fail to account for functional selectivity, allosteric modulation, and downstream signaling cascades that may be more relevant to therapeutic outcomes than simple receptor binding. These findings suggest that current PET occupancy paradigms may be overly simplistic for complex psychiatric disorders. Future neuropharmacological research should integrate occupancy data with functional neuroimaging, pharmacogenomics, and circuit-based measures to develop more sophisticated biomarkers for dose optimization. The field must evolve beyond the "one-size-fits-all" occupancy approach toward personalized, mechanistically-informed dosing strategies that better reflect the complexity of brain function and psychiatric pathophysiology.

title: Resting state functional connectivity: its physiological basis and application in neuropharmacology.
abstract: Resting state functional connectivity (rsFC) has emerged as a powerful neuroimaging approach for investigating intrinsic brain network organization and its modulation by pharmacological interventions. This review synthesizes current understanding of the physiological mechanisms underlying rsFC and examines its applications in neuropharmacological research. Resting state networks reflect spontaneous, low-frequency (<0.1 Hz) fluctuations in blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signal that are temporally correlated across spatially distributed brain regions. These intrinsic connectivity patterns arise from the interplay of neuronal oscillations, neurotransmitter systems, and structural white matter architecture. Growing evidence indicates that rsFC measurements are sensitive to acute and chronic drug effects across multiple neurotransmitter systems, including dopaminergic, serotonergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic pathways. Pharmacological neuroimaging studies utilizing rsFC have provided novel insights into drug mechanisms of action, dose-response relationships, and therapeutic efficacy in psychiatric and neurological disorders. For instance, antidepressant medications consistently alter connectivity within the default mode network, while antipsychotics modulate fronto-striatal and thalamo-cortical connectivity patterns. Additionally, rsFC serves as a translational bridge between preclinical animal models and human studies, enabling cross-species validation of drug effects. The non-invasive nature and high test-retest reliability of rsFC measures make them particularly suitable for longitudinal pharmacological studies and clinical trials. Future applications include the development of connectivity-based biomarkers for drug response prediction, optimization of dosing regimens, and identification of novel therapeutic targets. As methodological advances continue to improve the specificity and interpretability of rsFC measures, this approach promises to accelerate neuropharmacological discovery and personalized medicine approaches.

title: The first Insubria autumn school on neuroimmunopharmacology: challenging paradigms beyond boundaries.
abstract: The inaugural Insubria Autumn School on Neuroimmunopharmacology convened leading experts and emerging researchers to address critical knowledge gaps at the intersection of neuroscience, immunology, and pharmacology. This intensive educational program was designed to challenge conventional paradigms and foster interdisciplinary collaboration in understanding neuroinflammatory processes and their therapeutic implications. The school featured comprehensive lectures, interactive workshops, and case-based discussions covering emerging topics including microglial activation dynamics, blood-brain barrier modulation, cytokine signaling cascades in neurodegeneration, and novel immunomodulatory therapeutic approaches. Particular emphasis was placed on translational research methodologies bridging preclinical models and clinical applications. Sixty-three participants from 18 countries engaged in structured learning modules addressing current controversies in neuroimmune interactions, including the dual role of neuroinflammation in neuroprotection versus neurodegeneration, and the therapeutic potential of targeting specific immune pathways in CNS disorders. Interactive sessions revealed significant knowledge synthesis regarding microglial phenotypic plasticity, astrocyte-mediated immune responses, and peripheral immune cell infiltration mechanisms. Participant feedback demonstrated enhanced understanding of complex neuroimmune networks and increased confidence in applying interdisciplinary approaches to research design. Post-program evaluations indicated 89% of attendees reported improved ability to integrate immunological concepts into neuropharmacological research frameworks. The school successfully established a foundation for ongoing international collaboration and identified priority areas for future investigation, including personalized neuroimmune therapies and biomarker development. This educational initiative represents a crucial step toward advancing the neuroimmunopharmacology field through enhanced cross-disciplinary training and knowledge exchange.
journal: Journal of neuroimmune pharmacology : the official journal of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology

title: Behavioral barcoding in the cloud: embracing data-intensive digital phenotyping in neuropharmacology.
abstract: The exponential growth of digital data collection technologies presents unprecedented opportunities for neuropharmacological research through comprehensive behavioral phenotyping. Traditional methods for assessing drug effects on behavior rely on limited observational windows and subjective scoring systems, constraining both throughput and reproducibility. We developed a cloud-based behavioral barcoding platform that integrates high-resolution video tracking, automated feature extraction, and machine learning algorithms to create standardized digital phenotypes for drug discovery applications. Our system captures multi-dimensional behavioral parameters from rodent models across extended timeframes, generating terabyte-scale datasets that are processed using distributed computing architectures. We validated this approach using established psychoactive compounds including amphetamine, diazepam, and haloperidol across multiple behavioral paradigms (n=240 animals). The platform identified 847 distinct behavioral features and generated reproducible "behavioral barcodes" - unique digital signatures that distinguished drug classes with 94% accuracy. Cloud-based analysis reduced processing time from weeks to hours while enabling real-time collaboration across research sites. Notably, our unsupervised clustering algorithms revealed previously uncharacterized behavioral subtypes within treatment groups, suggesting novel mechanisms of drug action. Integration with pharmacokinetic modeling demonstrated strong correlations between behavioral barcode dynamics and plasma drug concentrations (r=0.82, p<0.001). This data-intensive approach represents a paradigm shift toward precision neuropharmacology, offering enhanced statistical power, reduced animal usage through optimized experimental design, and accelerated translation of behavioral insights into therapeutic applications. The scalable cloud infrastructure positions behavioral barcoding as an essential tool for next-generation drug discovery pipelines.

title: The development of an implantable catheter system for chronic or intermittent convection-enhanced delivery.
abstract: Convection-enhanced delivery (CED) has emerged as a promising technique for bypassing the blood-brain barrier and achieving targeted drug distribution within the central nervous system. However, current methods are limited by the need for repeated surgical procedures and inconsistent catheter placement for chronic therapeutic regimens. We developed and characterized a novel implantable catheter system designed for long-term, programmable CED in rodent models. The system consists of a biocompatible titanium port connected to a flexible polyurethane catheter with a customized tip geometry optimized for uniform infusate distribution. Twenty-four adult Sprague-Dawley rats were implanted with the device targeting the striatum, and system performance was evaluated over 8 weeks using fluorescent tracers, MRI-compatible contrast agents, and therapeutic compounds. Catheter patency was maintained in 92% of animals throughout the study period, with no evidence of significant tissue inflammation or foreign body reaction. Infusion volumes of 2-50 μL delivered at rates of 0.2-2.0 μL/min achieved consistent distribution patterns with minimal backflow along the catheter track. Real-time MRI monitoring confirmed reproducible targeting accuracy within 200 μm across multiple treatment sessions. Drug pharmacokinetics demonstrated sustained therapeutic concentrations in target tissues with minimal systemic exposure compared to conventional delivery methods. This implantable CED system provides a reliable platform for chronic neurotherapeutic studies and shows potential for clinical translation in treating neurodegenerative diseases, brain tumors, and other CNS disorders requiring repeated or continuous drug delivery.

title: Stress-free microinjections in conscious rats.
abstract: Microinjections into discrete brain regions of conscious animals represent a fundamental technique in neuropharmacology research, yet conventional restraint methods induce significant stress responses that can confound experimental outcomes. Elevated corticosterone levels and behavioral alterations associated with physical restraint may mask or amplify drug effects, particularly in studies examining stress-sensitive neurotransmitter systems. We developed and validated a novel habituation protocol combined with a custom-designed, lightweight injection apparatus to minimize stress during intracerebroventricular and intracerebral microinjections in conscious rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) underwent progressive habituation sessions over 7 days using a modified stereotaxic frame with reduced contact points and a flexible tether system. Stress responses were assessed by measuring plasma corticosterone, heart rate variability, and behavioral indicators compared to conventional restraint methods. Our approach reduced baseline corticosterone levels by 73% (p<0.001) and eliminated stress-induced locomotor suppression observed with traditional restraint techniques. Heart rate variability analysis revealed maintenance of parasympathetic tone, indicating minimal autonomic stress activation. The injection accuracy remained equivalent to conventional methods (±0.2mm), with no increase in tissue damage or inflammatory markers. Successful microinjections were completed in 95% of attempts within 2 minutes. This stress-free microinjection technique provides a valuable methodological advancement for neuropharmacological studies requiring precise drug delivery to conscious animals while preserving physiological baseline conditions. The protocol is particularly beneficial for investigations of stress-responsive neural circuits, anxiolytic compounds, and studies requiring repeated sampling in the same subjects.

title: Understanding the effects of stimulant medications on cognition in individuals with attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder: a decade of progress.
abstract: Over the past decade, substantial progress has been made in elucidating the cognitive mechanisms underlying stimulant medication efficacy in attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). While methylphenidate and amphetamine-based treatments have demonstrated clear clinical benefits, their specific effects on discrete cognitive domains remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes findings from 127 studies published between 2001-2011 examining stimulant effects on cognition in ADHD populations. We conducted a systematic analysis of neuropsychological, neuroimaging, and electrophysiological studies investigating working memory, inhibitory control, sustained attention, and reward processing in medicated versus unmedicated ADHD patients. Converging evidence indicates that stimulants primarily enhance working memory capacity and reduce reaction time variability, with effect sizes ranging from 0.4-0.8 across studies. Neuroimaging data reveal normalized activation patterns in prefrontal-striatal circuits, particularly within the anterior cingulate cortex and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex during executive function tasks. However, improvements in inhibitory control show more modest and inconsistent effects (d=0.2-0.5), suggesting this domain may be less responsive to dopaminergic enhancement. Individual differences in treatment response correlate significantly with baseline dopamine transporter availability and genetic polymorphisms in catecholaminergic systems. Notably, cognitive benefits appear most pronounced in children and adolescents compared to adults, potentially reflecting developmental differences in prefrontal maturation. These findings provide crucial insights into the neurobiological basis of ADHD pharmacotherapy and highlight the need for personalized treatment approaches based on individual cognitive profiles and neurochemical characteristics.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Pharmacological enhancement of memory and executive functioning in laboratory animals.
abstract: Memory and executive functioning deficits represent core features of numerous neuropsychiatric disorders, yet effective pharmacological interventions remain limited. This study investigated the cognitive-enhancing effects of modafinil, a wake-promoting agent with dopaminergic and noradrenergic activity, on memory consolidation and executive performance in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received acute modafinil (32, 64, or 128 mg/kg, i.p.) or vehicle 30 minutes prior to behavioral testing. Memory performance was assessed using the novel object recognition task and contextual fear conditioning, while executive functioning was evaluated through the attentional set-shifting task and five-choice serial reaction time task. Neurochemical analyses measured dopamine, norepinephrine, and acetylcholine levels in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus using high-performance liquid chromatography. Modafinil dose-dependently enhanced novel object recognition at 24-hour retention (64 mg/kg: discrimination ratio 0.72 ± 0.06 vs. vehicle 0.54 ± 0.04, p < 0.01) and improved contextual fear memory (64 mg/kg: 68 ± 5% freezing vs. vehicle 42 ± 4%, p < 0.05). Executive performance was significantly enhanced, with reduced extra-dimensional shift errors (64 mg/kg: 8.2 ± 1.3 vs. vehicle 14.7 ± 2.1, p < 0.01) and improved attentional accuracy (64 mg/kg: 82 ± 3% vs. vehicle 71 ± 4%, p < 0.05). Neurochemical analysis revealed dose-dependent increases in prefrontal dopamine (2.1-fold) and norepinephrine (1.8-fold) with 64 mg/kg modafinil. These findings demonstrate that modafinil enhances both memory consolidation and executive functioning through monoaminergic mechanisms, supporting its potential therapeutic application in cognitive disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Addiction: decreased reward sensitivity and increased expectation sensitivity conspire to overwhelm the brain's control circuit.
abstract: The neurobiological basis of addiction involves complex alterations in reward processing and cognitive control systems that perpetuate compulsive drug-seeking behavior despite negative consequences. While previous research has focused on individual components of addiction pathology, the integrative mechanisms by which reward dysfunction and enhanced drug expectancy jointly compromise prefrontal control remain poorly understood. We propose a novel framework wherein addiction emerges from the convergence of two key neuroadaptations: diminished sensitivity to natural rewards in the ventral striatum and heightened expectation-driven responses in the anterior cingulate and orbitofrontal cortex. Using functional magnetic resonance imaging and pharmacological challenge studies in cocaine-dependent individuals (n=45) and matched controls (n=38), we demonstrate that blunted dopaminergic signaling in reward circuits correlates with enhanced anticipatory responses to drug-related cues. Critically, these opposing neuroadaptations create an imbalanced state where weakened reward processing reduces the motivational salience of adaptive behaviors while amplified expectancy responses increasingly monopolize limited cognitive control resources in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex. Computational modeling reveals that this dual dysfunction creates a positive feedback loop, progressively eroding the capacity for executive override of drug-seeking impulses. Our findings suggest that effective addiction treatments must simultaneously address reward deficits and expectation sensitization rather than targeting either component in isolation. This mechanistic insight provides a neurobiological foundation for combination therapies that restore reward sensitivity while dampening cue reactivity to rebalance the brain's control architecture.
journal: BioEssays : news and reviews in molecular, cellular and developmental biology

title: New therapeutic developments in chronic migraine.
abstract: Chronic migraine affects approximately 2% of the global population and represents a significant burden on healthcare systems worldwide. Traditional preventive therapies, including antiepileptics, antidepressants, and beta-blockers, demonstrate limited efficacy and substantial side effect profiles in many patients. Recent advances in understanding migraine pathophysiology have identified novel therapeutic targets, particularly focusing on calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) pathways and neuromodulation approaches. This review examines emerging treatment modalities for chronic migraine based on clinical trial data from 2008-2010. We analyzed published Phase II and III clinical trials investigating CGRP receptor antagonists, including telcagepant and olcegepant, as well as neuromodulation techniques such as occipital nerve stimulation and transcranial magnetic stimulation. Additionally, we reviewed data on botulinum toxin type A injections and topiramate extended-release formulations. CGRP antagonists demonstrated significant efficacy in reducing headache frequency and intensity compared to placebo, with telcagepant showing particular promise in Phase II trials. Botulinum toxin type A injections provided meaningful headache reduction in 60-70% of patients with chronic migraine, with effects lasting 12-16 weeks. Occipital nerve stimulation showed favorable outcomes in refractory cases, though patient selection criteria remain under investigation. These therapeutic developments represent a paradigm shift toward mechanism-based treatments for chronic migraine. The emergence of CGRP-targeted therapies and evidence-based neuromodulation techniques offers new hope for patients with treatment-resistant chronic migraine, potentially improving quality of life while reducing healthcare utilization.

title: Cluster headache: diagnosis and treatment.
abstract: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder characterized by severe, unilateral periorbital pain occurring in cyclical patterns with associated autonomic features. Despite being one of the most painful conditions known to medicine, cluster headache remains underdiagnosed and undertreated due to limited physician awareness and diagnostic challenges. This comprehensive review examines current diagnostic criteria and evidence-based treatment approaches for cluster headache management. We analyzed recent clinical trials, case series, and systematic reviews published between 2000-2010 to evaluate diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic efficacy. The International Classification of Headache Disorders (ICHD-2) criteria demonstrate high specificity (>95%) when properly applied, though initial misdiagnosis rates remain elevated at 75-80%, often mistaken for migraine or trigeminal neuralgia. Neuroimaging is typically unnecessary except to exclude secondary causes in atypical presentations. For acute treatment, high-flow oxygen (12-15 L/min) provides relief in 78% of attacks within 15 minutes, while subcutaneous sumatriptan 6mg demonstrates 75% efficacy at 15 minutes. Prophylactic therapy with verapamil (240-960mg daily) shows superior efficacy compared to placebo, with 71% of patients achieving >50% reduction in attack frequency. Lithium carbonate and topiramate represent second-line options. Deep brain stimulation of the posterior hypothalamus has emerged as a promising treatment for medically refractory cases. Early recognition and appropriate treatment significantly improve quality of life and reduce disability in cluster headache patients. Increased physician education and adherence to established guidelines remain critical for optimal patient outcomes.

title: Acute treatment of migraine headaches.
abstract: Migraine remains a highly prevalent neurological disorder affecting approximately 12% of the population, with current acute treatments showing variable efficacy and significant limitations. Triptans, while effective, are contraindicated in patients with cardiovascular disease, and traditional analgesics often provide insufficient relief. This randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluated the efficacy and safety of frovatriptan 2.5 mg versus sumatriptan 100 mg and placebo for acute migraine treatment in 384 patients diagnosed with migraine with or without aura according to International Headache Society criteria. Patients were randomized to receive frovatriptan (n=128), sumatriptan (n=128), or placebo (n=128) within 4 hours of moderate to severe headache onset. Primary endpoints included headache relief at 2 hours and pain-free response at 2 hours, with secondary measures including sustained pain relief at 24 hours and functional disability scores. Frovatriptan demonstrated superior 2-hour headache relief compared to placebo (68% vs 32%, p<0.001) and comparable efficacy to sumatriptan (68% vs 71%, p=0.61). Notably, frovatriptan showed significantly better sustained relief at 24 hours versus sumatriptan (78% vs 62%, p=0.008) with lower headache recurrence rates (14% vs 28%, p=0.012). Adverse events were mild and transient across all groups, with frovatriptan showing the most favorable tolerability profile. These findings suggest frovatriptan represents an effective acute migraine treatment with enhanced sustainability of therapeutic response, offering clinicians an important therapeutic option for patients requiring prolonged headache relief.

title: Contributions of different modes of TRPV1 activation to TRPV1 antagonist-induced hyperthermia.
abstract: TRPV1 antagonists have shown promise as analgesics but frequently cause hyperthermia in clinical trials, limiting their therapeutic utility. The mechanisms underlying this adverse effect remain poorly understood, particularly regarding how different modes of TRPV1 activation contribute to thermoregulation. We investigated the relative contributions of capsaicin-sensitive versus heat-activated TRPV1 channels to antagonist-induced hyperthermia using pharmacological and genetic approaches in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Core body temperature was continuously monitored via radiotelemetry following administration of selective TRPV1 antagonists AMG9810 (10 mg/kg, i.p.) and A-425619 (30 mg/kg, i.p.). To dissect activation mechanisms, we employed capsaicin pretreatment (50 mg/kg, s.c.) to selectively desensitize vanilloid-sensitive TRPV1 channels while preserving heat sensitivity. Additionally, we used TRPV1 knockout mice and wild-type littermates to confirm receptor specificity. Both TRPV1 antagonists produced significant hyperthermia in control animals (peak increases: AMG9810, 2.1 ± 0.3°C; A-425619, 1.8 ± 0.4°C at 4-6 hours post-injection). Capsaicin pretreatment completely abolished antagonist-induced hyperthermia despite preserving TRPV1 protein expression in hypothalamic thermoregulatory centers. Heat-shock protein analysis revealed that capsaicin pretreatment selectively eliminated vanilloid sensitivity while maintaining thermosensitive channel function. TRPV1 knockout mice showed no hyperthermic response to either antagonist. These findings demonstrate that TRPV1 antagonist-induced hyperthermia results primarily from blockade of tonically active, capsaicin-sensitive TRPV1 channels rather than thermosensitive channels, suggesting that subtype-selective antagonists targeting heat activation might avoid this liability while retaining analgesic efficacy.
journal: The Journal of neuroscience : the official journal of the Society for Neuroscience

title: Neuropharmacology of the essential oil of bergamot.
abstract: Essential oils from Citrus bergamia Risso et Poiteau (bergamot) have been traditionally used in folk medicine for their anxiolytic and sedative properties, yet their neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly characterized. This study investigated the effects of bergamot essential oil (BEO) on central nervous system activity using established behavioral and biochemical models. BEO was obtained by cold-pressing and analyzed by GC-MS, revealing limonene (43.2%), linalyl acetate (26.8%), and linalool (8.3%) as major components. Male Swiss mice (n=8-10/group) received BEO (50, 100, 200 mg/kg, i.p.) or vehicle 30 min before behavioral testing. Anxiolytic activity was evaluated using elevated plus-maze and light-dark box tests, while sedative effects were assessed by open-field and rotarod performance. GABA_A receptor involvement was examined using flumazenil pretreatment, and monoamine levels were measured in brain homogenates by HPLC. BEO dose-dependently increased open-arm exploration in the elevated plus-maze (p<0.01 at 100-200 mg/kg) and light compartment time (p<0.05 at 200 mg/kg) without affecting locomotor coordination. These anxiolytic-like effects were completely reversed by flumazenil (2 mg/kg, i.p.), suggesting GABA_A receptor mediation. BEO treatment significantly increased brain GABA levels (34% at 200 mg/kg, p<0.01) while reducing cortical norepinephrine concentrations (23% decrease, p<0.05). These findings provide pharmacological evidence supporting the traditional anxiolytic use of bergamot essential oil and suggest its therapeutic potential involves GABAergic neurotransmission modulation.

title: A method for the evaluation of intracranial tetrodotoxin injections.
abstract: Tetrodotoxin (TTX) is widely used in neuroscience research to selectively block voltage-gated sodium channels and create reversible neural lesions. However, current methods for evaluating the efficacy and spatial extent of intracranial TTX injections rely primarily on behavioral assessments or post-mortem histological analysis, limiting real-time verification of injection success. We developed a novel electrophysiological approach to assess TTX delivery and effectiveness in vivo using multi-electrode arrays positioned adjacent to injection sites in anesthetized rats. Following stereotaxic placement of injection cannulae in the motor cortex (n=18) or hippocampus (n=12), we recorded local field potentials and multi-unit activity before, during, and after TTX administration (10-50 ng in 0.5 μL artificial cerebrospinal fluid). TTX injection produced rapid suppression of neural activity within 2-5 minutes, with complete silencing achieved at distances up to 800 μm from the injection site. The effective radius of neural suppression correlated positively with TTX concentration (r=0.78, p<0.001) and injection volume (r=0.71, p<0.01). Recovery of neural activity occurred 4-6 hours post-injection, following a predictable time course that varied with distance from the injection site. Control injections of vehicle alone produced minimal changes in neural activity. Post-mortem fluorescent tracer analysis confirmed the spatial distribution of injected solutions matched electrophysiological predictions. This method provides real-time verification of TTX injection efficacy and enables precise determination of affected tissue volume, offering significant advantages over existing approaches for studies requiring reversible neural inactivation.

title: Characterization of rat behavior in the elevated plus-maze using a directed graph.
abstract: The elevated plus-maze (EPM) is a widely used behavioral paradigm for assessing anxiety-like behavior in rodents, traditionally analyzed through time-based and frequency measures in open versus closed arms. However, conventional analyses may overlook important temporal dynamics and sequential patterns of exploratory behavior. We developed a novel analytical approach using directed graph theory to characterize the spatiotemporal organization of rat locomotor behavior in the EPM. Male Wistar rats (n=48) were subjected to standard EPM testing under control conditions or following administration of diazepam (1 mg/kg, i.p.) or yohimbine (3 mg/kg, i.p.). Behavioral trajectories were digitized and transformed into directed graphs where nodes represented spatial zones and edges indicated transition frequencies between zones. Graph-theoretical measures including node centrality, clustering coefficients, and path lengths were computed to quantify behavioral organization. Traditional anxiety measures correlated significantly with graph parameters: animals spending more time in open arms exhibited higher centrality values for open arm nodes (r=0.74, p<0.001) and increased inter-arm transition frequencies. Diazepam-treated rats showed enhanced graph connectivity and reduced path lengths compared to controls, while yohimbine produced the opposite pattern. Cluster analysis of graph metrics successfully classified animals into anxiety phenotypes with 87% accuracy, superior to conventional measures alone (69% accuracy). Our directed graph approach reveals previously undetected aspects of anxiety-related behavior, providing a more comprehensive framework for analyzing exploratory patterns in the EPM. This methodology may enhance sensitivity for detecting subtle behavioral changes in neuropharmacological studies and facilitate automated behavioral phenotyping.

title: The neuropsychopharmacology of fronto-executive function: monoaminergic modulation.
abstract: Executive functions encompass higher-order cognitive processes including working memory, cognitive flexibility, and inhibitory control, which are critically dependent on prefrontal cortical circuits. Accumulating evidence indicates that monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems—dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin—play fundamental roles in modulating fronto-executive performance through distinct but overlapping mechanisms. This review synthesizes recent advances in understanding how monoaminergic signaling influences executive control processes. Dopaminergic modulation of prefrontal function follows an inverted-U shaped dose-response relationship, with optimal performance occurring at intermediate levels of D1 receptor activation. Working memory capacity and cognitive flexibility are particularly sensitive to dopaminergic tone, with both hypo- and hyperdopaminergic states impairing performance. Noradrenergic influences on executive function are mediated primarily through α2A-adrenoceptors in prefrontal cortex, where moderate activation enhances working memory and attentional control while excessive stimulation produces deficits. The norepinephrine system also modulates the balance between focused attention and cognitive flexibility through differential activation of α2- versus α1-adrenoceptors. Serotonergic modulation of executive function occurs principally through 5-HT2A and 5-HT1A receptors, influencing behavioral inhibition and cognitive flexibility. Interactions between monoaminergic systems add further complexity, with dopamine-norepinephrine and dopamine-serotonin interactions being particularly important for executive control. These findings have significant implications for understanding psychiatric disorders characterized by executive dysfunction, including schizophrenia, ADHD, and depression, and suggest that optimal executive performance requires precise balance among monoaminergic systems rather than simply increased or decreased activity in any single system.

title: From real-world events to psychosis: the emerging neuropharmacology of delusions.
abstract: Delusions represent a core feature of psychotic disorders, yet their neurobiological underpinnings remain poorly understood. Recent evidence suggests that delusional beliefs may emerge from dysregulated neurotransmitter systems that normally process environmental salience and prediction errors. This study investigated the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying delusion formation using a novel translational approach combining clinical assessment, neuroimaging, and pharmacological challenge paradigms. We recruited 45 patients with schizophrenia experiencing active delusions, 32 patients in remission, and 38 healthy controls. Participants underwent functional magnetic resonance imaging during a probabilistic learning task designed to elicit prediction errors, followed by acute administration of either haloperidol (2mg), amisulpride (400mg), or placebo in a double-blind, randomized design. Delusional severity was assessed using the Peters Delusions Inventory and correlated with neural activation patterns. Patients with active delusions showed hyperactivation in the ventral striatum and reduced prefrontal cortical engagement during unexpected events compared to controls (p<0.001). Haloperidol administration normalized striatal hyperactivity and improved performance on the prediction error task, while amisulpride selectively enhanced prefrontal activation. Critically, the degree of striatal normalization following D2 blockade correlated significantly with reductions in delusional conviction scores (r=0.67, p<0.001). These findings provide direct evidence that delusions arise from aberrant dopaminergic signaling of environmental salience, leading to inappropriate assignment of significance to neutral stimuli. The differential effects of D2 versus D2/D3 antagonism suggest multiple therapeutic targets for delusion-specific interventions, potentially informing more precise pharmacological approaches to psychotic symptoms.

title: Acute and long-term treatment of mania.
abstract: Bipolar disorder affects approximately 1-2% of the global population, with acute manic episodes representing a critical phase requiring immediate pharmacological intervention. While lithium has remained the gold standard for long-term prophylaxis, the emergence of atypical antipsychotics and anticonvulsants has expanded treatment options for both acute stabilization and maintenance therapy. This comprehensive review examines current evidence for pharmacological management strategies across both phases of treatment. We analyzed data from 47 randomized controlled trials published between 1995-2007, encompassing 12,847 patients with DSM-IV-diagnosed bipolar I disorder experiencing acute manic episodes. Primary outcome measures included Young Mania Rating Scale scores, time to response, and relapse rates during maintenance phases. For acute treatment, atypical antipsychotics demonstrated superior efficacy compared to placebo, with olanzapine, risperidone, and aripiprazole showing significant symptom reduction within 1-2 weeks (effect sizes 0.43-0.65). Divalproex sodium exhibited comparable acute antimanic efficacy to lithium but with faster onset of action. Long-term maintenance data revealed lithium's continued superiority in preventing both manic and depressive relapses over 18-month follow-up periods. However, combination therapy utilizing mood stabilizers with atypical antipsychotics showed enhanced prophylactic benefits, particularly in patients with mixed episodes or rapid cycling. Tolerability profiles varied significantly, with metabolic concerns emerging for certain atypical antipsychotics. These findings support a personalized approach to bipolar disorder management, integrating rapid-acting agents for acute stabilization with evidence-based maintenance strategies tailored to individual patient characteristics and treatment response patterns.

title: The use of imaging in the early development of neuropharmacological drugs: a survey of approved NDAs.
abstract: The integration of neuroimaging techniques into early-phase drug development has emerged as a critical tool for advancing neuropharmacological therapeutics, yet systematic evaluation of its regulatory impact remains limited. We conducted a comprehensive survey of New Drug Applications (NDAs) approved by the FDA between 2000-2007 for central nervous system indications to assess the utilization and influence of imaging data in regulatory decision-making. All publicly available FDA review documents for approved neurological and psychiatric medications (n=47) were systematically analyzed for inclusion of neuroimaging studies, imaging modalities employed, and stated regulatory relevance. Positron emission tomography (PET) was incorporated in 68% of submissions, functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) in 34%, and single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) in 23%. Imaging data most frequently supported proof-of-mechanism (72% of cases), dose selection (45%), and biomarker validation (38%). PET studies demonstrating target engagement were particularly influential in antipsychotic and antidepressant approvals, with receptor occupancy data directly informing dosing recommendations in 15 applications. Imaging-derived biomarkers showed significant correlation with clinical efficacy measures in Phase II trials (r=0.67, p<0.001) and were associated with reduced late-stage attrition rates compared to programs without imaging components (18% vs. 34%, p=0.02). These findings demonstrate that neuroimaging has become integral to contemporary neuropharmaceutical development, providing mechanistic insights that enhance regulatory confidence and optimize clinical trial design. Strategic implementation of imaging biomarkers early in development programs may substantially improve success rates for neurotherapeutics.

title: Systematic review of therapeutic interventions in human prion disease.
abstract: Human prion diseases represent a group of rare, invariably fatal neurodegenerative disorders characterized by the accumulation of misfolded prion protein (PrPSc). Despite decades of research, no curative treatments exist, and therapeutic options remain limited. We conducted a systematic review to evaluate the efficacy of pharmacological and non-pharmacological interventions reported in human prion disease cases. We searched MEDLINE, EMBASE, and Cochrane databases from 1990 to 2007 for studies reporting therapeutic interventions in patients with confirmed or probable prion disease, including Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, fatal familial insomnia, and Gerstmann-Sträussler-Scheinker syndrome. Case reports, case series, and clinical trials were included. Data extraction focused on intervention type, dosage, duration, clinical outcomes, and survival. From 1,847 initially identified articles, 43 studies met inclusion criteria, encompassing 312 patients. Quinacrine was the most frequently reported intervention (18 studies, 156 patients), showing no significant improvement in survival or clinical progression. Other antiprion compounds including fluphenazine, chlorpromazine, and pentosan polysulfate demonstrated similarly disappointing results. Symptomatic treatments with antiepileptics, antipsychotics, and sleep medications provided modest symptomatic relief in some cases. Two small trials of immunomodulatory approaches showed preliminary signals of potential benefit but lacked statistical power. Our systematic review reveals that despite numerous therapeutic attempts, no intervention has demonstrated clear efficacy in altering the natural history of human prion diseases. Current evidence supports only symptomatic management. Further research into novel therapeutic targets and larger controlled trials are urgently needed for these devastating disorders.

title: Recent progress in the research field of neuropharmacology in China.
abstract: China has emerged as a significant contributor to neuropharmacology research over the past decade, with substantial advances in both traditional medicine applications and modern drug discovery approaches. This review synthesizes recent developments in Chinese neuropharmacology research from 2000-2007, examining publications in major international journals and national databases. We analyzed 847 peer-reviewed articles from Chinese institutions focusing on central nervous system drug development, neuroprotective agents, and traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) applications in neurological disorders. Key research areas include the development of novel neuroprotective compounds derived from natural products, particularly ginsenosides and flavonoids, with demonstrated efficacy in experimental models of stroke, Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson's disease. Chinese researchers have made notable contributions to understanding GABAergic and glutamatergic neurotransmission, with several laboratories identifying new modulators of NMDA and AMPA receptors. Significant progress has been observed in translating TCM principles into mechanistically-defined therapeutic approaches, including standardized extracts of Ginkgo biloba and Panax ginseng showing clinical promise in neurodegenerative diseases. Advanced techniques including patch-clamp electrophysiology, transgenic animal models, and proteomics have been increasingly adopted by Chinese research institutions. International collaborations have expanded substantially, with joint research programs focusing on drug discovery and clinical translation. These developments position China as an increasingly important player in global neuropharmacology research, combining traditional medicinal knowledge with cutting-edge molecular approaches to advance our understanding of neurological therapeutics.

title: [Intervening in the neural basis of one's personality: a practice-oriented ethical analysis of neuropharmacology and deep-brain stimulation].
abstract: The rapid advancement of neuropharmacological interventions and deep brain stimulation (DBS) techniques has raised fundamental questions about the ethical boundaries of personality modification through direct neural manipulation. While these technologies offer promising therapeutic applications for psychiatric disorders, their potential to alter core personality traits necessitates careful ethical examination from a clinical practice perspective. This analysis synthesizes current neuropharmacological literature with established bioethical frameworks to evaluate the moral implications of personality-altering neural interventions. We conducted a systematic review of 127 published studies on neuropharmacological personality modification and 43 case reports of DBS applications between 2000-2006, analyzing outcomes through the lens of four principal ethical considerations: patient autonomy, therapeutic beneficence, identity preservation, and societal implications. Our analysis reveals significant gaps between technological capabilities and ethical guidelines, particularly regarding informed consent for personality-altering effects and long-term identity consequences. Neuropharmacological interventions showed measurable personality changes in 68% of cases, while DBS demonstrated alterations in core personality dimensions in 34% of patients. Critical ethical concerns emerged regarding the distinction between therapeutic restoration and enhancement, the reversibility of personality changes, and the potential for coercive applications. We propose a structured ethical framework emphasizing rigorous informed consent protocols, mandatory psychological evaluation, and enhanced patient autonomy protection. These findings suggest that while neural personality interventions hold therapeutic promise, their implementation requires strengthened ethical safeguards and interdisciplinary oversight to protect patient welfare and preserve human agency.

title: Stimulating the development of drug treatments to improve cognition in schizophrenia.
abstract: Cognitive deficits represent a core feature of schizophrenia, affecting up to 85% of patients and serving as a primary predictor of functional outcome. Despite advances in antipsychotic medications, current treatments show limited efficacy for cognitive symptoms, creating an urgent need for novel therapeutic approaches. This review examines emerging pharmacological strategies targeting cognitive enhancement in schizophrenia, with particular focus on glutamatergic, cholinergic, and GABAergic systems. We analyzed data from 47 clinical trials conducted between 2002-2006, encompassing 3,247 patients with schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder. Primary outcome measures included standardized neuropsychological batteries assessing working memory, attention, executive function, and verbal learning. Promising results emerged from trials targeting α7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, with partial agonists demonstrating modest but significant improvements in working memory (effect size d=0.34, p<0.05) and attention (d=0.28, p<0.01). AMPA receptor positive allosteric modulators showed comparable benefits for verbal learning and memory (d=0.31, p<0.05). Glycine transport inhibitors yielded mixed results, with efficacy potentially modulated by baseline glycine levels. Notably, combination approaches targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems showed enhanced therapeutic potential compared to single-target interventions. However, effect sizes remained modest, and functional translation of cognitive improvements proved inconsistent. These findings suggest that while several novel pharmacological targets hold promise for cognitive enhancement in schizophrenia, future research must prioritize identification of patient subgroups most likely to benefit and development of personalized treatment algorithms to optimize therapeutic outcomes.

title: Electrophysiological and pharmacological validation of vagal afferent fiber type of neurons enzymatically isolated from rat nodose ganglia.
abstract: The nodose ganglion contains heterogeneous populations of vagal afferent neurons that mediate diverse physiological functions, yet reliable methods for identifying specific fiber types in isolated preparations remain limited. This study aimed to develop and validate electrophysiological and pharmacological criteria for classifying vagal afferent fiber types in enzymatically dissociated rat nodose ganglion neurons. Nodose ganglia were harvested from adult Sprague-Dawley rats and neurons isolated using sequential collagenase and trypsin digestion. Whole-cell patch-clamp recordings were performed to characterize action potential properties, including duration, amplitude, and afterhyperpolarization kinetics. Neurons were classified as A- or C-type based on action potential duration (<2 ms or >2 ms, respectively) and tetrodotoxin sensitivity. Pharmacological validation employed capsaicin (1 μM), ATP (100 μM), and bradykinin (1 μM) to assess nociceptive C-fiber properties, while baroreceptor A-fibers were identified by their sensitivity to phenylephrine (10 μM) and insensitivity to capsaicin. Of 347 recorded neurons, 62% exhibited C-fiber characteristics with broad action potentials (mean duration 4.8 ± 0.3 ms) and capsaicin sensitivity, while 38% showed A-fiber properties with narrow spikes (1.4 ± 0.1 ms) and capsaicin insensitivity. Pharmacological responses correlated strongly with electrophysiological classification (r = 0.89, p < 0.001). These findings establish reliable electrophysiological and pharmacological markers for identifying vagal afferent fiber types in vitro, providing a valuable tool for investigating the cellular mechanisms underlying visceral sensory transduction and enabling targeted studies of specific vagal afferent populations.

title: Pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain.
abstract: Neuropathic pain affects millions of patients worldwide and remains a significant therapeutic challenge due to its complex pathophysiology and limited response to conventional analgesics. Current evidence-based treatments show modest efficacy, necessitating systematic evaluation of pharmacological options. We conducted a comprehensive review of randomized controlled trials published between 1995-2005 examining pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain conditions including diabetic neuropathy, postherpetic neuralgia, and central pain syndromes. Database searches identified 47 eligible studies encompassing 6,242 patients treated with anticonvulsants, antidepressants, topical agents, opioids, and NMDA receptor antagonists. Primary outcome measures included pain intensity reduction using visual analog scales and categorical improvement ratings. Tricyclic antidepressants demonstrated consistent efficacy with numbers-needed-to-treat (NNT) of 2.1-3.4 for achieving 50% pain reduction, though limited by anticholinergic side effects. Gabapentin showed moderate effectiveness (NNT 4.3-6.8) with favorable tolerability profiles. Topical lidocaine provided localized relief for postherpetic neuralgia (NNT 4.4) with minimal systemic effects. Controlled-release opioids achieved significant pain reduction but carried risks of dependence and tolerance. NMDA antagonists showed promise in small studies but require further investigation. Combination therapy appeared superior to monotherapy in treatment-refractory cases. Current evidence supports a multimodal approach to neuropathic pain management, with treatment selection guided by pain etiology, patient comorbidities, and individual drug tolerance. Future research should focus on mechanistic subtyping of neuropathic pain to optimize therapeutic targeting and improve patient outcomes.
journal: Pain practice : the official journal of World Institute of Pain

title: Neuropharmacology of drugs and alcohol in mother and fetus.
abstract: Maternal substance use during pregnancy poses significant risks to fetal neurodevelopment through direct pharmacological effects and altered intrauterine environment. This review examines the neuropharmacological mechanisms by which commonly abused substances cross the placental barrier and affect the developing central nervous system. We analyzed current literature on placental transfer kinetics, fetal drug metabolism, and neurodevelopmental consequences of prenatal exposure to alcohol, cocaine, opioids, nicotine, and cannabis. Systematic review of clinical studies and animal models published between 1990-2006 was conducted using MEDLINE and EMBASE databases. Alcohol readily crosses the placenta and disrupts neuronal migration, synaptogenesis, and myelination, particularly affecting the developing hippocampus and cortex through NMDA receptor antagonism and GABA receptor enhancement. Cocaine blocks dopamine reuptake in fetal brain regions, leading to altered neurotransmitter development and increased risk of cerebral infarction. Opioids cross the placenta efficiently, causing fetal physical dependence and affecting endogenous opioid systems crucial for stress response and behavioral regulation. Nicotine exposure alters acetylcholine receptor development, affecting attention and learning pathways. Cannabis compounds accumulate in fetal brain tissue, disrupting endocannabinoid signaling essential for neural connectivity. Fetal pharmacokinetics differ significantly from maternal due to immature hepatic metabolism and blood-brain barrier properties. These findings emphasize that maternal substance use during pregnancy results in direct fetal neuropharmacological effects that can lead to long-term cognitive, behavioral, and developmental impairments, highlighting the critical need for early intervention and prevention strategies.

title: Applications of fMRI in translational medicine and clinical practice.
abstract: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has emerged as a pivotal neuroimaging technique with significant potential for translational medicine and clinical applications. While traditionally confined to basic neuroscience research, recent technological advances and methodological refinements have positioned fMRI as a valuable tool for understanding disease mechanisms, evaluating therapeutic interventions, and informing clinical decision-making. This review examines current applications of fMRI across multiple clinical domains, including psychiatry, neurology, and neurosurgery. We analyzed 127 clinical studies published between 2000-2006 that utilized task-based and resting-state fMRI paradigms to investigate brain function in patient populations. Our analysis reveals substantial progress in several key areas: presurgical mapping for tumor resection and epilepsy surgery, with sensitivity rates exceeding 85% for language and motor localization; pharmacological studies demonstrating drug-induced neural changes in depression, schizophrenia, and Alzheimer's disease; and diagnostic applications showing promise for early detection of cognitive decline and psychiatric disorders. Resting-state fMRI has proven particularly valuable, revealing altered default mode networks in conditions ranging from autism to chronic pain. However, significant challenges remain, including standardization of acquisition protocols, validation of clinical biomarkers, and integration with existing diagnostic frameworks. Despite these limitations, fMRI's non-invasive nature and ability to probe neural circuits in vivo position it as an increasingly important bridge between basic neuroscience discoveries and clinical practice, with potential to revolutionize personalized medicine approaches in neurological and psychiatric care.

title: Validation of a mechanical apparatus for the measurement of avian walking.
abstract: Quantitative assessment of avian locomotion has been limited by the lack of standardized apparatus capable of accurately measuring multiple gait parameters simultaneously. While treadmill-based systems have been developed for mammalian studies, the unique biomechanical properties of avian bipedal locomotion require specialized instrumentation. We developed and validated a novel mechanical apparatus designed specifically for comprehensive measurement of walking parameters in medium-sized birds (100-500g). The system consists of a variable-speed motorized walkway (120 cm length) equipped with force-sensitive platforms, infrared motion sensors, and high-speed video capture capabilities. Validation studies were conducted using adult domestic chickens (Gallus gallus domesticus, n=12) and zebra finches (Taeniopygia guttata, n=8) across multiple walking speeds (0.1-0.8 m/s). Ground reaction forces, stride length, step frequency, and stance duration were measured and compared with manual video analysis and previously published data. Strong correlations were observed between automated measurements and manual analysis (r>0.92 for all parameters, p<0.001). The apparatus demonstrated high test-retest reliability (ICC=0.89-0.95) and inter-observer agreement (κ=0.91). Measurements of ground reaction forces showed excellent agreement with force plate data (mean difference <3%). The system successfully detected subtle gait alterations following administration of motor-affecting compounds, demonstrating sensitivity for pharmacological studies. This validated apparatus provides a standardized, reproducible method for quantitative assessment of avian locomotion, offering significant advantages for neuropharmacological research investigating motor function, drug effects, and neurological disorders in avian models.

title: From neuroscience to application in neuropharmacology: A generation of progress in electrophysiology.
abstract: Over the past generation, advances in electrophysiological techniques have fundamentally transformed neuropharmacological research and clinical applications. This review synthesizes key developments from 1980-2005, examining how technological innovations in EEG, event-related potentials (ERPs), and single-cell recording have enhanced drug discovery and therapeutic monitoring. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of 847 peer-reviewed studies, focusing on electrophysiological biomarkers in psychiatric and neurological disorders. Additionally, we analyzed clinical trial data from 23 pharmaceutical compounds where electrophysiological endpoints were employed as primary or secondary outcome measures. Our analysis reveals that quantitative EEG (qEEG) has emerged as a reliable predictor of antidepressant response, with theta cordance showing 74% accuracy in treatment outcome prediction. Event-related potential components, particularly P300 amplitude and latency, have become established surrogate markers for cognitive enhancement drugs, with effect sizes ranging from 0.4-0.8 across cholinesterase inhibitor trials. Single-cell patch-clamp techniques have elucidated ion channel mechanisms for over 200 neurotropic compounds, directly informing rational drug design. High-throughput electrophysiological screening platforms now enable testing of 10,000+ compounds weekly, accelerating early-phase drug discovery. These electrophysiological advances have shortened average CNS drug development timelines by approximately 18 months and improved Phase II success rates from 28% to 41%. The integration of electrophysiology into neuropharmacology represents a paradigm shift toward biomarker-driven, personalized neurotherapeutics, establishing the foundation for precision medicine approaches in neuropsychiatry.

title: Computational neuropharmacology: dynamical approaches in drug discovery.
abstract: The traditional paradigm of drug discovery has relied heavily on static receptor binding assays and linear dose-response relationships, often failing to capture the complex temporal dynamics underlying neural circuit function and pathophysiology. Recent advances in computational neuroscience and systems biology have enabled the development of dynamical modeling approaches that incorporate time-dependent drug-target interactions, network-level effects, and emergent properties of neural systems. We review emerging computational frameworks that integrate pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic modeling with detailed neural network simulations to predict drug efficacy and side effects. These approaches utilize ordinary and stochastic differential equations to model drug distribution, receptor occupancy dynamics, and downstream signaling cascades within realistic neural circuit architectures. Case studies in antidepressant and antipsychotic drug development demonstrate that dynamical models can successfully predict therapeutic windows, explain delayed onset of action, and identify potential off-target effects that static models miss. Specifically, simulations of serotonin reuptake inhibition revealed that therapeutic efficacy emerges from sustained network-level changes in limbic circuits rather than simple receptor occupancy. Similarly, dopamine receptor antagonist models showed that motor side effects result from differential temporal dynamics in nigrostriatal versus mesolimbic pathways. These computational approaches also enable virtual screening of drug combinations and optimization of dosing regimens. We propose that integrating dynamical modeling into early-stage drug discovery pipelines will improve success rates by providing mechanistic insights into complex neuropsychiatric disorders and enabling more rational therapeutic strategies.

title: The cell cycle in Alzheimer disease: a unique target for neuropharmacology.
abstract: Recent evidence suggests that aberrant cell cycle reactivation in post-mitotic neurons contributes to neurodegeneration in Alzheimer disease (AD), representing a novel therapeutic target. We investigated the relationship between cell cycle protein expression and neuronal vulnerability in AD brain tissue and evaluated the neuroprotective effects of cell cycle inhibitors in experimental models. Post-mortem brain samples from 24 AD patients and 18 age-matched controls were analyzed using immunohistochemistry and Western blotting for cyclin D1, cyclin E, CDK4, CDK2, and p53 expression. Primary cortical neurons from embryonic day 18 rat brains were exposed to amyloid-β₁₋₄₂ (Aβ) oligomers with or without the CDK inhibitors roscovitine and flavopiridol. Neuronal viability, DNA fragmentation, and caspase-3 activation were assessed. AD brain tissue showed significant upregulation of cyclin D1 (3.2-fold), cyclin E (2.8-fold), and CDK4 (2.1-fold) in vulnerable hippocampal and cortical regions compared to controls (p<0.01). Double-labeling revealed cell cycle protein expression predominantly in neurons containing hyperphosphorylated tau. In vitro, Aβ exposure induced cell cycle protein expression within 6 hours, preceding neuronal death by 18-24 hours. Pretreatment with roscovitine (10 μM) or flavopiridol (0.5 μM) significantly reduced Aβ-induced neuronal death by 65% and 58%, respectively, and prevented DNA fragmentation. These findings demonstrate that inappropriate cell cycle reactivation is a critical early event in AD neurodegeneration. Cell cycle inhibitors represent a promising neuroprotective strategy that targets upstream mechanisms of neuronal death, potentially offering therapeutic benefits for AD treatment.

title: Drug discovery in neurodegenerative diseases.
abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases represent a growing public health challenge as populations age worldwide, yet therapeutic options remain severely limited. Current treatments for Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and related disorders provide only symptomatic relief without addressing underlying pathogenic mechanisms. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of drug discovery approaches in neurodegeneration, examining both traditional and emerging strategies employed between 1995-2004. We reviewed 347 clinical trials from ClinicalTrials.gov and analyzed preclinical research trends through systematic literature searches of major databases. Our analysis reveals that conventional single-target approaches have yielded disappointing results, with over 85% of Phase II/III trials failing to meet primary endpoints. Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors remain the primary approved therapeutics for Alzheimer's disease, while dopamine replacement therapy continues to dominate Parkinson's treatment. However, emerging strategies show promise, including disease-modifying approaches targeting protein aggregation, neuroinflammation, and oxidative stress. Novel drug delivery systems, particularly those addressing blood-brain barrier penetration, have increased CNS bioavailability by 3-8 fold in preclinical models. High-throughput screening of natural products and combinatorial libraries has identified several lead compounds currently advancing to clinical trials. Notably, multi-target drug design and systems biology approaches are gaining traction, recognizing the complex, interconnected nature of neurodegenerative pathology. These findings suggest that future therapeutic breakthroughs will likely emerge from innovative approaches that address disease complexity rather than single molecular targets, emphasizing the critical need for continued investment in translational neuropharmacology research.
journal: Science of aging knowledge environment : SAGE KE

title: Quantitative in vitro phosphor imaging using [3H] and [18F] radioligands: the effects of chronic desipramine treatment on serotonin 5-HT2 receptors.
abstract: Chronic antidepressant treatment is known to induce adaptive changes in serotonin receptor systems, yet quantitative assessment of these alterations remains technically challenging. The present study describes a novel dual-radioligand phosphor imaging approach to examine 5-HT2 receptor binding changes following chronic desipramine administration. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) received either desipramine (10 mg/kg, i.p.) or saline vehicle daily for 21 days. Brain sections (20 μm) were prepared and incubated with either [3H]ketanserin (2.5 nM) or [18F]setoperone (1.8 nM) to label 5-HT2A receptors, with parallel sections processed for 5-HT2C receptor binding using [3H]mesulergine (3.2 nM). Sections were exposed to phosphor imaging plates for quantitative autoradiography, with binding densities measured in frontal cortex, parietal cortex, caudate-putamen, and hippocampus. Both radioligands demonstrated excellent correlation (r=0.94) in control conditions, validating the dual-tracer methodology. Chronic desipramine treatment significantly reduced 5-HT2A receptor binding in frontal cortex (23% decrease, p<0.01) and parietal cortex (18% decrease, p<0.05) compared to controls, with no significant changes observed in subcortical regions. 5-HT2C receptor binding remained unchanged across all brain regions examined. Scatchard analysis revealed decreased Bmax values without alterations in KD, indicating reduced receptor density rather than altered affinity. These findings demonstrate that chronic tricyclic antidepressant treatment selectively downregulates cortical 5-HT2A receptors and establish phosphor imaging with multiple radioligands as a valuable quantitative approach for neuropharmacological investigations of receptor plasticity.

title: The application of functional magnetic resonance imaging to neuropharmacology.
abstract: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has emerged as a powerful non-invasive technique for investigating drug effects on brain function in both preclinical and clinical neuropharmacology research. This review examines recent advances in applying fMRI to understand mechanisms of psychoactive drugs and their therapeutic targets. We analyzed peer-reviewed studies from 1998-2004 that employed fMRI to assess acute and chronic drug effects on neural activity patterns, focusing on studies of antidepressants, antipsychotics, and drugs of abuse. Blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) fMRI has successfully detected dose-dependent changes in regional brain activation following administration of serotonin reuptake inhibitors, with consistent findings of altered limbic and prefrontal cortex activity correlating with clinical efficacy measures. Pharmacological fMRI studies of dopaminergic agents have revealed distinct activation patterns in striatal and mesolimbic circuits, providing insights into both therapeutic mechanisms and addiction liability. Methodological advances include improved temporal resolution, pharmacokinetic modeling of drug distribution, and combined fMRI-pharmacokinetic approaches that better correlate brain activity with plasma drug concentrations. Challenges remain in standardizing acquisition protocols, accounting for neurovascular coupling effects of vasoactive drugs, and translating findings between rodent and human studies. Despite these limitations, fMRI represents a significant advancement in neuropharmacology research, offering unprecedented ability to visualize drug action in the living brain and potentially accelerate drug development through early identification of central nervous system target engagement and biomarkers of therapeutic response.

title: Transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS).
abstract: Transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) represents a non-invasive method of modulating cortical excitability through the application of weak direct current to the human brain. While previous studies have demonstrated that tDCS can induce polarity-dependent changes in neuronal activity, the underlying neurophysiological mechanisms and optimal stimulation parameters remain poorly understood. We investigated the effects of tDCS on cortical excitability and plasticity using transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) and electroencephalography (EEG) in twelve healthy volunteers. Participants received anodal, cathodal, or sham tDCS (1 mA, 13 minutes) applied over the left primary motor cortex (M1) using saline-soaked electrodes. Motor evoked potentials (MEPs) were recorded from the contralateral first dorsal interosseous muscle before, during, and up to 60 minutes following stimulation. EEG was simultaneously recorded to assess changes in cortical rhythms. Anodal tDCS significantly increased MEP amplitudes by 35±12% that persisted for at least 30 minutes post-stimulation, while cathodal stimulation decreased MEP amplitudes by 28±9% for up to 45 minutes. EEG analysis revealed increased alpha power (8-12 Hz) following anodal stimulation and decreased alpha activity after cathodal stimulation. Sham stimulation produced no significant changes. These findings demonstrate that tDCS induces polarity-specific, sustained modifications of cortical excitability that outlast the stimulation period. The prolonged after-effects suggest involvement of synaptic plasticity mechanisms, potentially mediated by NMDA receptor-dependent processes. These results support tDCS as a promising tool for investigating cortical plasticity and developing therapeutic interventions for neurological disorders.

title: Neuropharmacology and drug development.
abstract: The development of novel neurotherapeutics remains a critical challenge in modern medicine, with traditional drug discovery approaches yielding limited success rates and prolonged development timelines. Current pharmaceutical strategies rely heavily on receptor-based screening and animal models that often fail to translate to human efficacy. This review examines emerging neuropharmacological approaches and their implications for accelerating drug development in neurological and psychiatric disorders. We analysed recent advances in molecular target identification, including genomics-based approaches, proteomics, and functional neuroimaging techniques. Additionally, we evaluated novel screening methodologies, including high-throughput electrophysiology, transgenic animal models, and early-phase biomarker development. Recent progress in understanding neurotransmitter systems has identified several promising therapeutic targets, particularly in glutamate and GABA signalling pathways. Advances in pharmacokinetic modelling have improved brain penetration predictions, whilst genotype-phenotype correlations are enabling more targeted therapeutic approaches. High-throughput screening technologies have increased compound throughput by 300-fold compared to traditional methods, though hit-to-lead conversion rates remain challenging at approximately 0.1%. Novel biomarkers, including cerebrospinal fluid protein profiles and functional connectivity patterns, show promise for early efficacy assessment. The integration of systems biology approaches with traditional pharmacology offers significant potential for improving drug development success rates. However, regulatory frameworks must evolve to accommodate these methodological advances. Future neuropharmacological research should prioritise translational validity and patient stratification strategies to enhance therapeutic outcomes whilst reducing development costs and timelines.

title: Techniques: applications of the nerve-bouton preparation in neuropharmacology.
abstract: The nerve-bouton preparation has emerged as a powerful experimental model for investigating presynaptic mechanisms and neurotransmitter release dynamics in neuropharmacological research. This preparation preserves the functional integrity of nerve terminals while providing exceptional accessibility for direct pharmacological manipulation and electrophysiological recording. We present a comprehensive evaluation of methodological approaches utilizing the nerve-bouton preparation across multiple experimental paradigms. Freshly isolated nerve terminals from rat hippocampus and cortex were maintained in physiological saline and subjected to patch-clamp recording, calcium imaging, and neurotransmitter release assays. Pharmacological agents including calcium channel blockers (ω-conotoxin GVIA, nimodipine), vesicle fusion inhibitors (botulinum toxin A), and monoamine uptake blockers were applied via microperfusion systems. The preparation demonstrated robust calcium-dependent exocytosis with pharmacological profiles consistent with native synapses. Voltage-clamp recordings revealed distinct calcium channel subtypes mediating transmitter release, with N-type channels contributing 65% and P/Q-type channels 30% of total calcium influx. Real-time monitoring of vesicle dynamics using FM dyes showed differential effects of various pharmacological modulators on vesicle pool mobilization. The nerve-bouton preparation proved particularly valuable for studying presynaptic plasticity mechanisms and drug actions at concentrations difficult to achieve in intact tissue preparations.
