This study aimed to provide a comprehensive morphometric analysis of key brain regions involved in autonomic control in SHR compared to normotensive Wistar-Kyoto (WKY) controls, and to evaluate the implications for neuropharmacological research applications. Adult male SHR (n=24) and WKY rats (n=24) were examined using standard histological techniques, immunohistochemistry for tyrosine hydroxylase and choline acetyltransferase, and stereological methods. Morphometric analyses focused on the rostral ventrolateral medulla (RVLM), nucleus tractus solitarius (NTS), paraventricular nucleus (PVN), and locus coeruleus. SHR demonstrated significantly reduced neuronal density in the NTS (23% decrease, P<0.01) and altered dendritic arborization patterns in RVLM neurons compared to WKY controls. Tyrosine hydroxylase-positive neurons showed increased soma size in the locus coeruleus of SHR (+18%, P<0.05), while PVN exhibited enhanced vasopressinergic immunoreactivity. Ultrastructural analysis revealed increased synaptic density in RVLM of hypertensive animals (+31%, P<0.01). These neuroanatomical differences correlate with known functional alterations in autonomic regulation and provide important considerations for interpreting pharmacological interventions targeting central cardiovascular control mechanisms. The observed structural modifications in SHR brain regions suggest that therapeutic strategies should account for these baseline morphological differences when evaluating drug efficacy and mechanism of action in hypertension research.
journal: Italian journal of anatomy and embryology = Archivio italiano di anatomia ed embriologia

title: What have positron emission tomography and 'Zippy' told us about the neuropharmacology of drug addiction?
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful neuroimaging technique for investigating the neuropharmacology of drug addiction, providing unique insights into receptor occupancy, neurotransmitter function, and neural circuitry in both preclinical and clinical settings. This review synthesizes findings from PET studies conducted over the past decade, with particular emphasis on data obtained using the synthetic cocaine analogue 'Zippy' ([¹¹C]cocaine) as a tracer for dopamine transporter (DAT) imaging. We examined PET studies investigating dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic systems in addiction, focusing on receptor availability, binding kinetics, and regional brain activation patterns in drug-dependent individuals compared to healthy controls. Key findings demonstrate significant reductions in striatal DAT availability in chronic cocaine users, with 20-30% decreases in caudate and putamen observed across multiple cohorts. Similar patterns emerged for other psychostimulants, suggesting common pathophysiological mechanisms. PET studies using [¹¹C]raclopride revealed blunted dopamine release in response to pharmacological challenges in addiction, correlating with anhedonia and craving severity. Serotonergic imaging showed altered 5-HT₂A receptor binding in cortical regions, particularly in individuals with comorbid mood disorders. Longitudinal studies indicated partial recovery of transporter function following extended abstinence, though deficits persisted beyond 12 months. These neuroimaging advances have elucidated the neurochemical substrates of addiction, identified potential therapeutic targets, and provided biomarkers for treatment response. Future applications of PET technology promise to further refine our understanding of addiction neuropharmacology and guide evidence-based therapeutic interventions.

title: EFNS guidelines for the diagnosis and management of Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) represents the most common cause of dementia in Europe, affecting approximately 5.4 million individuals, with prevalence expected to double by 2030. The European Federation of Neurological Societies (EFNS) convened an expert panel to develop evidence-based guidelines for AD diagnosis and management, addressing current diagnostic challenges and therapeutic approaches. A comprehensive literature review was conducted using MEDLINE, Cochrane Database, and relevant European databases from 1990-2009. Evidence was classified according to EFNS criteria, and recommendations were graded based on strength of available data. The guidelines emphasize early clinical recognition using standardized cognitive assessments, including Mini-Mental State Examination and clock drawing tests, combined with neuroimaging approaches. Cerebrospinal fluid biomarkers (Aβ42, total tau, phospho-tau) show promise for differential diagnosis, particularly in mild cognitive impairment cases. For pharmacological management, acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) demonstrate modest but clinically meaningful benefits in mild-to-moderate AD, with memantine recommended for moderate-to-severe stages. Non-pharmacological interventions, including cognitive stimulation and structured physical activity, provide additional therapeutic value. The guidelines stress importance of multidisciplinary care approaches, caregiver support, and regular monitoring for treatment response and adverse effects. These EFNS recommendations provide European neurologists with standardized, evidence-based protocols for AD diagnosis and management, emphasizing early detection strategies and comprehensive therapeutic approaches. Implementation of these guidelines should improve diagnostic accuracy and optimize patient outcomes across European healthcare systems.

title: TARPs differentially decorate AMPA receptors to specify neuropharmacology.
abstract: AMPA receptors mediate fast excitatory synaptic transmission in the central nervous system and represent critical targets for neurological and psychiatric therapeutics. Transmembrane AMPA receptor regulatory proteins (TARPs) have emerged as essential auxiliary subunits that profoundly influence AMPA receptor trafficking, gating, and pharmacology. Here, we investigated how different TARP isoforms confer distinct pharmacological profiles to AMPA receptors using heterologous expression systems and acute hippocampal slices. We systematically examined the effects of stargazin (γ-2), γ-3, γ-4, and γ-8 TARPs on AMPA receptor sensitivity to positive allosteric modulators, competitive antagonists, and use-dependent blockers. Our results demonstrate that TARP association dramatically alters AMPA receptor pharmacology in an isoform-specific manner. Stargazin-containing receptors showed 5-fold increased sensitivity to the potentiator cyclothiazide compared to undecorated receptors, while γ-8-associated receptors exhibited only 2-fold enhancement. Conversely, γ-8 TARPs conferred greater resistance to NBQX inhibition (IC50 = 890 nM) compared to stargazin complexes (IC50 = 340 nM). TARP γ-4 uniquely enhanced receptor sensitivity to kainate while reducing desensitization rates. Pharmacological profiling in hippocampal neurons confirmed that endogenous TARP expression patterns determine region-specific drug sensitivity. These findings reveal that the native heterogeneity of TARP-AMPA receptor complexes creates a pharmacological landscape where identical glutamate receptor subunits display markedly different drug responses. Understanding TARP-dependent pharmacological diversity is essential for developing subtype-selective AMPA receptor modulators and predicting therapeutic efficacy across different brain regions and pathological states.

title: Endogenous nociceptin/orphanin FQ (N/OFQ) contributes to haloperidol-induced changes of nigral amino acid transmission and parkinsonism: a combined microdialysis and behavioral study in naïve and nociceptin/orphanin FQ receptor knockout mice.
abstract: The nociceptin/orphanin FQ (N/OFQ) system has emerged as a modulator of dopaminergic neurotransmission, but its role in antipsychotic-induced parkinsonism remains unclear. We investigated whether endogenous N/OFQ contributes to haloperidol-induced alterations in nigral amino acid transmission and motor dysfunction using wild-type and N/OFQ receptor (NOP) knockout mice. Male C57BL/6 wild-type and NOP knockout mice received acute haloperidol (1 mg/kg, i.p.) or vehicle. In vivo microdialysis was performed in the substantia nigra to measure extracellular glutamate and GABA levels. Motor function was assessed using catalepsy bar test and locomotor activity measurements. Haloperidol significantly increased nigral glutamate levels by 180% ± 22% in wild-type mice, accompanied by a 45% ± 8% decrease in GABA concentrations. These neurochemical changes were associated with pronounced catalepsy (latency: 24.3 ± 3.1 s vs. 2.1 ± 0.4 s in controls) and reduced locomotor activity. In NOP knockout mice, haloperidol-induced glutamate elevation was attenuated to 110% ± 15%, while the GABA reduction was less pronounced (25% ± 6% decrease). Correspondingly, NOP knockout mice showed reduced cataleptic responses (12.7 ± 2.8 s) and better preserved locomotor function compared to wild-type littermates. These findings demonstrate that endogenous N/OFQ signaling contributes to haloperidol-induced disruption of nigral excitatory/inhibitory balance and motor impairment. The N/OFQ system represents a potential therapeutic target for reducing extrapyramidal side effects associated with antipsychotic treatment while maintaining their therapeutic efficacy.

title: 3D-QSAR study of corticotropin-releasing factor 1 antagonists and pharmacophore-based drug design.
abstract: Corticotropin-releasing factor (CRF) plays a pivotal role in the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and stress response, making CRF1 receptor antagonists promising therapeutic targets for anxiety, depression, and stress-related disorders. Despite significant research efforts, the structural requirements for potent CRF1 antagonism remain poorly understood. In this study, we performed a comprehensive three-dimensional quantitative structure-activity relationship (3D-QSAR) analysis on a dataset of 87 structurally diverse CRF1 antagonists to elucidate key molecular determinants of binding affinity and selectivity. Comparative molecular field analysis (CoMFA) and comparative molecular similarity indices analysis (CoMSIA) models were constructed using binding affinity data (Ki values ranging from 0.8 to 2,450 nM) obtained from radioligand displacement assays. The optimized CoMFA model demonstrated excellent statistical parameters (r² = 0.891, q² = 0.667, r²pred = 0.724), while the CoMSIA model incorporating steric, electrostatic, hydrophobic, and hydrogen-bond donor fields yielded comparable predictive ability (r² = 0.903, q² = 0.671, r²pred = 0.738). Contour map analysis revealed that bulky hydrophobic substituents at specific positions enhance binding affinity, while positive electrostatic fields in the central aromatic region are detrimental to activity. Based on these findings, a comprehensive pharmacophore model was developed featuring four hydrophobic regions, two hydrogen-bond acceptors, and one aromatic ring. Virtual screening of commercial databases using this pharmacophore identified 23 novel compounds with predicted high CRF1 binding affinity. These results provide valuable insights for structure-based optimization of CRF1 antagonists and accelerate the discovery of next-generation therapeutics for stress-related psychiatric disorders.
